<step n="17" type="derived" from="16,5">
<text>Using the fact that honey is not aromatic together with the disjunction that honey is aromatic or precise, we deduce that honey is precise.</text>
<logic>precise(honey)</logic>
</step>

<step n="18" type="derived" from="17,3">
<text>From honey being precise we infer that honey is zesty.</text>
<logic>zesty(honey)</logic>
</step>

<step n="19" type="derived" from="18,2">
<text>Because honey is zesty, we infer that honey is neutral-flavored.</text>
<logic>neutral_flavor(honey)</logic>
</step>

We derived Honey is neutral flavor, so the conclusion Honey is not neutral flavor is False.
</think>
Answer: False

examples.context: In the adventure game, the character Hero Dakota is in good shape; specifically, Hero Dakota is healthy. The non‑player character NPC Kai is also healthy, and NPC Kai feels at ease, so NPC Kai is relaxed. The game’s rule set works as follows: whenever a character is not knowledgeable, that character becomes confident; if a character is healthy, then the character is experienced; if a character is relaxed, then the character is patient; if a character is prepared, then the character is relaxed; and whenever a character is knowledgeable, that character is not patient. Finally, the system knows that either Hero Dakota is not experienced or Hero Dakota is prepared.
examples.question: Based on the game rules and the given facts, is Hero Dakota confident?
examples.completion:
<think>
We need to determine whether Hero Dakota ends up confident given the game’s facts and rules. The goal is to chain the provided premises together until we can apply the confidence rule.

The confidence rule is crucial because it tells us exactly how confidence can be obtained – only through the lack of knowledge. Keeping this in mind will guide us toward showing Dakota is not knowledgeable later on.
<step n="1" type="premise">
<text>The rule says that any character who is not knowledgeable will be confident.</text>
<logic>is_confident(hero_dakota) :- ~is_knowledgeable(hero_dakota)</logic>
</step>

The health‑to‑experience rule is important because we already know Dakota is healthy, so it will let us derive experience, which later interacts with the disjunction about preparedness.
<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>The rule states that a healthy character is automatically experienced.</text>
<logic>is_experienced(hero_dakota) :- is_healthy(hero_dakota)</logic>
</step>

The relaxation‑to‑patient rule will become useful once we manage to prove that Dakota is relaxed; it will give us patient status, which later helps us negate knowledgeability.
<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>The rule declares that if a character is relaxed then that character is patient.</text>
<logic>is_patient(hero_dakota) :- is_relaxed(hero_dakota)</logic>
</step>

The preparation‑to‑relaxation rule links the prepared condition directly to being relaxed. Since the disjunction may give us preparedness, this rule will let us move forward to relaxation.
<step n="4" type="premise">
<text>The rule indicates that being prepared guarantees that the character is relaxed.</text>
<logic>is_relaxed(hero_dakota) :- is_prepared(hero_dakota)</logic>
</step>

The knowledgeable‑to‑not‑patient rule creates a conflict: if Dakota were knowledgeable, he could not be patient. By later showing Dakota is patient, we will be forced to conclude he cannot be knowledgeable.
<step n="5" type="premise">
<text>The rule asserts that a knowledgeable character cannot be patient.</text>
<logic>~is_patient(hero_dakota) :- is_knowledgeable(hero_dakota)</logic>
</step>

The disjunction tells us that at least one of two statements holds for Dakota: either he is not experienced or he is prepared. This will be pivotal once we have a concrete fact about his experience.
<step n="6" type="premise">
<text>The knowledge base includes the disjunction that Hero Dakota is either not experienced or prepared.</text>
<logic>~is_experienced(hero_dakota) v is_prepared(hero_dakota)</logic>
</step>

The fact that NPC Kai is healthy is included for completeness, though it does not directly affect Dakota's confidence chain. It demonstrates the broader health context in the game world.
<step n="7" type="premise">
<text>It is a fact that NPC Kai is healthy.</text>
<logic>is_healthy(npc_kai)</logic>
</step>

The fact that NPC Kai is relaxed similarly provides background detail but does not play a role in the derivation for Dakota.
<step n="8" type="premise">
<text>It is a fact that NPC Kai is relaxed.</text>
<logic>is_relaxed(npc_kai)</logic>
</step>

The fact that Hero Dakota is healthy is the key starting point for deriving experience via PREMISE_2, which then activates the disjunction in PREMISE_6.
<step n="9" type="premise">
<text>It is a fact that Hero Dakota is healthy.</text>
<logic>is_healthy(hero_dakota)</logic>
</step>

Because we have the fact that Dakota is healthy and the rule that health implies experience, we can directly infer that Dakota is experienced. This step gives us a concrete truth to plug into the disjunction.
<step n="10" type="derived" from="9,2">
<text>From Hero Dakota being healthy and the rule linking health to experience, we infer that Hero Dakota is experienced.</text>
<logic>is_experienced(hero_dakota)</logic>
</step>

The disjunction says Dakota is either not experienced or prepared. Since we have just established that Dakota is experienced, the 'not experienced' option is false, so the only way for the disjunction to hold is that Dakota must be prepared. Thus we conclude preparedness.
<step n="11" type="derived" from="10,6">
<text>Using the disjunction that Hero Dakota is not experienced or prepared together with the fact that Hero Dakota is experienced, we resolve the disjunction in favor of the second option, concluding that Hero Dakota is prepared.</text>
<logic>is_prepared(hero_dakota)</logic>
</step>

Now that we know Dakota is prepared, we can use the rule that preparation guarantees relaxation. This lets us derive that Dakota is relaxed, moving us toward the patient status needed later.
<step n="12" type="derived" from="11,4">
<text>Because Hero Dakota is prepared and the rule that preparation leads to relaxation, we deduce that Hero Dakota is relaxed.</text>
<logic>is_relaxed(hero_dakota)</logic>
</step>

With Dakota relaxed, the rule linking relaxation to patience applies, giving us that Dakota is patient. This patient status will later clash with the rule about knowledgeable characters, allowing us to negate knowledgeability.
<step n="13" type="derived" from="12,3">
<text>Given Hero Dakota is relaxed and the rule that relaxation yields patience, we conclude that Hero Dakota is patient.</text>
<logic>is_patient(hero_dakota)</logic>
</step>

We now have Dakota as patient, but PREMISE_5 tells us that any knowledgeable character cannot be patient. Since Dakota is patient, the only consistent conclusion is that he cannot be knowledgeable; therefore, he is not knowledgeable.
<step n="14" type="derived" from="13,5">
<text>Since Hero Dakota is patient and the rule that knowledgeable characters are not patient, we infer that Hero Dakota cannot be knowledgeable; therefore, Hero Dakota is not knowledgeable.</text>
<logic>~is_knowledgeable(hero_dakota)</logic>
</step>

Having established that Dakota is not knowledgeable, we can finally apply the confidence rule from PREMISE_1, which states that non‑knowledgeable characters become confident. Consequently, Dakota is confident.
<step n="15" type="derived" from="14,1">
<text>Finally, because Hero Dakota is not knowledgeable and the rule that non‑knowledgeable characters become confident, we derive that Hero Dakota is confident.</text>
<logic>is_confident(hero_dakota)</logic>
</step>

Therefore Hero_dakota is is confident, so the conclusion is True.
</think>
Answer: True

examples.context: In the airline's pilot roster, two pilots are mentioned: Rowan and Seth. Both Rowan and Seth are noted to be physically fit for the demanding flights. The airline has a policy concerning Seth's abilities: if Seth happens to be proficient, then the airline considers him disciplined. Moreover, whenever Seth is disciplined, the airline regards him as generous. In turn, any generous pilot is deemed knowledgeable by the training department. Additionally, the airline states that any pilot who is fit is automatically proficient.
examples.question: Based on the given information, is Seth knowledgeable?
examples.completion:
<think>
We are asked to determine whether Seth possesses the property of being knowledgeable. To answer, we will follow the chain of conditions provided in the context, starting from the facts we know about Seth's fitness.

The first premise establishes a direct conditional link between proficiency and discipline for Seth. Recognizing this connection will be essential once we can show Seth is proficient.
<step n="1" type="premise">
<text>The rule says that if Seth is proficient, then Seth is disciplined.</text>
<logic>disciplined(seth) :- proficient(seth)</logic>
</step>

The second premise gives us another conditional: discipline leads to generosity for Seth. This will become useful after we have derived discipline.
<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>The rule says that if Seth is disciplined, then Seth is generous.</text>
<logic>generous(seth) :- disciplined(seth)</logic>
</step>

The third premise ties generosity to knowledge, stating that any generous Seth is knowledgeable. This is the final link we need to reach our goal.
<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>The rule says that if Seth is generous, then Seth is knowledgeable.</text>
<logic>knowledgeable(seth) :- generous(seth)</logic>
</step>

The fourth premise is a general rule that fitness implies proficiency. Since it applies to any pilot, it will allow us to move from the fact that Seth is fit to the fact that he is proficient.
<step n="4" type="premise">
<text>The rule says that if someone is fit, then that person is proficient; applied to Seth this means fit(seth) implies proficient(seth).</text>
<logic>proficient(seth) :- fit(seth)</logic>
</step>

The fact that Rowan is fit is recorded, but it does not directly affect Seth's knowledge chain. It is included for completeness of the scenario.
<step n="5" type="premise">
<text>Rowan is fit.</text>
<logic>fit(rowan)</logic>
</step>

The fact that Seth is fit is crucial because it activates the fitness‑to‑proficiency rule, giving us the starting point for the chain of deductions.
<step n="6" type="premise">
<text>Seth is fit.</text>
<logic>fit(seth)</logic>
</step>

Using the fact that Seth is fit together with the rule that fitness yields proficiency, we deduce that Seth must be proficient. This conversion from a concrete fact to a derived property is the first step toward discipline.
<step n="7" type="derived" from="6,4">
<text>Since Seth is fit, we can infer that Seth is proficient.</text>
<logic>proficient(seth)</logic>
</step>

Now that we know Seth is proficient, we can apply the rule linking proficiency to discipline. By modus ponens, Seth's proficiency guarantees his discipline, moving the proof forward.
<step n="8" type="derived" from="7,1">
<text>Having established that Seth is proficient, the earlier rule lets us conclude that Seth is disciplined.</text>
<logic>disciplined(seth)</logic>
</step>

With Seth established as disciplined, we invoke the rule that discipline leads to generosity. Again by modus ponens, Seth's disciplined status forces him to be generous.
<step n="9" type="derived" from="8,2">
<text>Because Seth is disciplined, the rule about discipline leads us to conclude that Seth is generous.</text>
<logic>generous(seth)</logic>
</step>

Finally, having shown Seth is generous, we apply the last rule that generosity implies knowledge. This deduction confirms that Seth is knowledgeable, completing the logical chain.
<step n="10" type="derived" from="9,3">
<text>Given Seth's generosity, the final rule allows us to infer that Seth is knowledgeable.</text>
<logic>knowledgeable(seth)</logic>
</step>

Therefore Seth is knowledgeable, so the conclusion is True.
</think>
Answer: True

examples.context: The NPC Casey is rational. The player Anna is courteous. The NPC Casey is clever. The boss Tess is clever. Whenever someone is clever, they are satisfied, so the boss being clever makes her satisfied. If a character is satisfied, then they become robust, which means the boss being satisfied makes her robust. Any rational character is detail_oriented, so rationality guarantees detail‑orientation. If the player is courteous, then she becomes inquisitive. Whenever the player is energetic, she takes initiative. Either the player is not inquisitive, or she is energetic. Either the boss is not robust, or she is rational.
examples.question: Is the boss character detail_oriented?
examples.completion:
<think>
We are given several facts about the characters and a handful of conditional rules that link properties together. Our goal is to show that the boss character, Tess, possesses the property detail_oriented, which corresponds to being meticulous in the original terminology. To do this we will chain the rules and use the disjunction to obtain the necessary rationality, and then apply the rule that translates rationality into detail orientation.

The rule that clever leads to satisfied is crucial because we already know Tess is clever. By establishing satisfaction, we set up the next rule that turns satisfaction into robustness, moving us closer to the disjunction we will need later.
<step n="1" type="premise">
<text>If the boss is clever, then she is satisfied.</text>
<logic>satisfied(boss_tess) :- clever(boss_tess)</logic>
</step>

This rule connects satisfaction to robustness, allowing us to move from a property we will derive (satisfied) to a property (robust) that appears in the disjunction. Obtaining robustness is a prerequisite for using the disjunction that will give us rationality.
<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>If the boss is satisfied, then she is robust.</text>
<logic>robust(boss_tess) :- satisfied(boss_tess)</logic>
</step>

The third rule tells us that any rational character is automatically detail_oriented. This is the final bridge we need: once we can prove Tess is rational, this rule will immediately give us the target property of being detail_oriented.
<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>If someone is rational, then they are detail_oriented.</text>
<logic>detail_oriented(boss_tess) :- rational(boss_tess)</logic>
</step>

The disjunction states that either Tess is not robust or she is rational. After we have shown she is robust, the first alternative becomes false, forcing the second alternative to be true. This logical step is essential for extracting rationality from the information we will have gathered.
<step n="4" type="premise">
<text>Either the boss is not robust, or she is rational.</text>
<logic>~robust(boss_tess) v rational(boss_tess)</logic>
</step>

Knowing that NPC Casey is rational provides an example of the rational property in the world, reinforcing the meaning of rationality. Although this fact is not directly used in the proof, it helps to illustrate that rational characters exist in the scenario.
<step n="5" type="premise">
<text>The NPC Casey is rational.</text>
<logic>rational(npc_casey)</logic>
</step>

Anna being courteous is another factual detail about the characters. While it does not play a direct role in proving Tess's meticulousness, it demonstrates the completeness of the background information provided.
<step n="6" type="premise">
<text>The player Anna is courteous.</text>
<logic>courteous(player_anna)</logic>
</step>

Casey being clever mirrors Tess's cleverness and shows that the clever → satisfied rule can apply to multiple characters. This reinforces the reliability of the rule but is not needed for the specific derivation concerning Tess.
<step n="7" type="premise">
<text>The NPC Casey is clever.</text>
<logic>clever(npc_casey)</logic>
</step>

The fact that the boss Tess is clever is the starting point for our chain of reasoning. It activates the clever → satisfied rule, allowing us to begin constructing the proof that leads to her being detail_oriented.
<step n="8" type="premise">
<text>The boss Tess is clever.</text>
<logic>clever(boss_tess)</logic>
</step>

We combine the fact that Tess is clever (PREMISE_8) with the rule that clever implies satisfied (PREMISE_1). By applying this conditional, we conclude that Tess must be satisfied. This derived fact will be used in the next step to obtain robustness.
<step n="9" type="derived" from="8,1">
<text>Since the boss is clever and clever implies satisfied, we infer that the boss is satisfied.</text>
<logic>satisfied(boss_tess)</logic>
</step>

Having established that Tess is satisfied, we now invoke the rule satisfied → robust (PREMISE_2). Using modus ponens, the satisfaction of Tess directly yields that she is robust. This robustness is the key premise needed for the disjunction in the following step.
<step n="10" type="derived" from="9,2">
<text>Because the boss is satisfied and satisfied implies robust, we conclude that the boss is robust.</text>
<logic>robust(boss_tess)</logic>
</step>

We now have Tess as robust (STEP_10) and the disjunction that either she is not robust or she is rational (PREMISE_4). Since the first option ('not robust') is contradicted by our knowledge that she is robust, the only remaining viable alternative is that Tess is rational. This disjunctive syllogism gives us the rational property we need for the final inference.
<step n="11" type="derived" from="10,4">
<text>Given that the boss is robust and the disjunction that either she is not robust or rational, we can discard the first alternative and infer that she is rational.</text>
<logic>rational(boss_tess)</logic>
</step>

With Tess established as rational, we apply the rule rational → detail_oriented (PREMISE_3). By straightforward implication, her rationality leads directly to her being detail_oriented, which corresponds to the meticulous property we set out to prove.
<step n="12" type="derived" from="11,3">
<text>Since the boss is rational and rational implies detail_oriented, we deduce that the boss is detail_oriented.</text>
<logic>detail_oriented(boss_tess)</logic>
</step>

Therefore Boss_tess is detail oriented, so the conclusion is True.
</think>
Answer: True

examples.context: In the kitchen, the rosemary is nutritious. The saffron is creative. The basil is logical. Whenever rosemary is versatile, it becomes satisfied. Whenever something is logical, the basil becomes firm. If something is not sharp, the saffron becomes instinctive. When rosemary is nutritious, it is also bright. If the saffron is comprehensive, then it is exact. If the basil is diligent, then it is ready. The saffron becomes robust whenever it is not glossy. If the saffron is glossy, then it is not flavorful. If the saffron is sharp, then it is not exact. Either the basil is not firm or the basil is diligent. Either the saffron is not instinctive or the saffron is flavorful. Either the saffron is not creative or the saffron is comprehensive. Either the rosemary is not bright or the rosemary is versatile. Either the saffron is not robust or the saffron is steady.

The fact that rosemary is nutritious is a concrete piece of information we can use immediately with premise 4 to infer that rosemary is bright. It serves as a starting point for deriving further properties of rosemary, though those do not directly affect saffron.

Saffron being creative is a factual statement about the entity we care about. While creativity does not appear in any of the conditional rules, it confirms that saffron possesses at least one positive attribute, setting the stage for any potential connections that might emerge.

examples.context: In a busy photography studio, three photographers work together: Kate, Simon, and Stella. Simon is content. Stella is determined. Kate is knowledgeable. However, the query concerns Kate, so we keep this rule in mind should any chain connect Simon's capability to Kate's cautiousness.

<logic>skillful(stella) :- determined(stella)</logic>

This rule links Kate's knowledge to talent: a knowledgeable Kate becomes talented. Because we have a fact that Kate is knowledgeable, we can later infer that she is talented, unless something else blocks it. Talent is still unrelated to cautious, but it will be useful when we consider the rule that negates talent under a different condition.
<step n="4" type="premise">
<text>If Kate is knowledgeable, then Kate is talented.</text>
<logic>talented(kate) :- knowledgeable(kate)</logic>
</step>

If Stella is decisive, then she is vigorous. This creates a possible pathway from decisiveness to vigor for Stella. The rule may become active if we can establish decisiveness for Stella from other premises or disjunctions.
<step n="5" type="premise">
<text>If Stella is decisive, then Stella is vigorous.</text>
<logic>vigorous(stella) :- decisive(stella)</logic>
</step>

The rule says that Simon's wit follows from his contentment. Since we have Simon content as a fact, we can infer that Simon is witty. This will later be relevant to the disjunction ~witty(simon) v capable(simon).
<step n="6" type="premise">
<text>If Simon is content, then Simon is witty.</text>
<logic>witty(simon) :- content(simon)</logic>
</step>

This conditional asserts that if Kate is courageous, then she is not talented. It provides a way to negate talent, which we already can derive from premise 4. The existence of a disjunction that gives Kate either courageous or agile will make this rule potentially applicable.
<step n="7" type="premise">
<text>If Kate is courageous, then Kate is not talented.</text>
<logic>~talented(kate) :- courageous(kate)</logic>
</step>

The fact that Simon is content is the premise needed for rule 6, allowing us to deduce that Simon is witty. It also stands alone as a piece of information about Simon's emotional state, but it does not mention Kate directly.
<step n="8" type="premise">
<text>Simon is content.</text>
<logic>content(simon)</logic>
</step>

Stella being determined is the fact that activates rule 3, giving us that Stella is skillful. This derived skillfulness will be examined against the disjunction ~skillful(stella) v decisive(stella).
<step n="9" type="premise">
<text>Stella is determined.</text>
<logic>determined(stella)</logic>
</step>

Kate's knowledge is the fact that triggers rule 4, allowing us to conclude that Kate is talented. This derived talent will later be contrasted with the possible negation from rule 7 if Kate turns out to be courageous.
<step n="10" type="premise">
<text>Kate is knowledgeable.</text>
<logic>knowledgeable(kate)</logic>
</step>

<step n="11" type="derived">
<text>Searching for rules that conclude Kate is cautious: no rule or disjunction has "cautious" as a consequent.</text>
<logic>unreachable(cautious(kate))</logic>
</step>

<step n="12" type="derived">
<text>Similarly, no rule concludes Kate is not cautious.</text>
<logic>unreachable(~cautious(kate))</logic>
</step>

<step n="13" type="derived">
<text>Neither Kate is cautious nor Kate is not cautious can be derived from the given premises. The answer is Unknown.</text>
<logic>unknown(cautious(kate))</logic>
</step>

</think>
Answer: Unknown

examples.context: Bree is a steady player and she is quick-witted. Whenever a player is goal-driven, that player is also focused. If a player is quick-witted and there is no abnormality preventing her from being goal-driven, then she is goal-driven. If a player is focused and there is no abnormality preventing talent, then that player is skillful. If a player is steady and there is no abnormality preventing the negation, then the player is not goal-driven.
examples.question: Is Bree skillful?
examples.completion:
<think>
We are asked to decide whether Bree possesses the quality of being skillful. To answer this we must follow the chain of conditions that link her known attributes to talent, keeping in mind the default rules that can be overridden by abnormalities.

The first premise tells us that goal-driven status guarantees focus. This connection will be useful later once we establish that Bree is goal-driven, because it will allow us to move from goal orientation to the focused property needed for the talent rule.
<step n="1" type="premise">
<text>The rule says that any goal-driven athlete is also focused.</text>
<logic>focused(bree) :- goal_driven(bree)</logic>
</step>

The second premise gives a default way to infer goal-driven from quick-wittedness, provided no abnormality blocks it. Since we already know Bree is quick-witted, this rule becomes a key bridge toward establishing her goal-driven status.
<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>The default rule states that if Bree is quick-witted and there is no abnormality preventing her from being goal-driven, then she is goal-driven.</text>
<logic>goal_driven(bree) :- quick_witted(bree), not ab_goal_oriented_a(bree)</logic>
</step>

The third premise links focus to skillfulness, again assuming no abnormality stops talent. Once we have focus, this rule will let us directly infer that Bree is skillful, which is exactly the query we need to answer.
<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>The default rule says that if an athlete is focused and there is no abnormality preventing talent, then the athlete is skillful.</text>
<logic>skillful(bree) :- focused(bree), not ab_skillful(bree)</logic>
</step>

The fourth premise offers a competing default that would make Bree not goal-driven if she is steady and no abnormality blocks the negation. We must consider this conflict when deciding which default takes precedence.
<step n="4" type="premise">
<text>The default rule indicates that if Bree is steady and there is no abnormality preventing the negation, then she is not goal-driven.</text>
<logic>~goal_driven(bree) :- steady(bree), not ab_goal_oriented_b(bree)</logic>
</step>

We are told outright that Bree is steady, giving us the factual basis for the competing default about not being goal-driven.
<step n="5" type="premise">
<text>Fact: Bree is steady.</text>
<logic>steady(bree)</logic>
</step>

We also know directly that Bree is quick-witted, which activates the default that can make her goal-driven, assuming no contrary abnormality.
<step n="6" type="premise">
<text>Fact: Bree is quick-witted.</text>
<logic>quick_witted(bree)</logic>
</step>

Because Bree is quick-witted and there is no information about an abnormality that would prevent her from being goal-driven, the default rule from Premise 2 fires. Although Premise 4 could produce a negation, the priority ordering in the proof favours the positive default, so we conclude that Bree is indeed goal-driven.
<step n="7" type="derived" from="6,2,4">
<text>From Bree’s quick-wittedness and the default rule, together with the contrary default that would make her not goal-driven, we infer that Bree is goal-driven.</text>
<logic>goal_driven(bree)</logic>
</step>

Having established that Bree is goal-driven, we can now apply the rule from Premise 1. That rule tells us that any goal-driven athlete must also be focused, so we infer that Bree is focused.
<step n="8" type="derived" from="7,1">
<text>Since Bree is goal-driven, the rule that goal-driven implies focused lets us conclude Bree is focused.</text>
<logic>focused(bree)</logic>
</step>

With Bree now known to be focused, the talent default from Premise 3 becomes applicable. Since there is no mention of an abnormality blocking talent, the condition is satisfied, allowing us to derive that Bree is skillful.
<step n="9" type="derived" from="8,3">
<text>Given Bree is focused and no abnormality blocks talent, the talent default rule yields that Bree is skillful.</text>
<logic>skillful(bree)</logic>
</step>

Therefore Bree is skillful, so the conclusion is True.
</think>
Answer: True

examples.context: Pilot Shay is highly trained. Whenever a pilot is a team player, they are articulate. Any pilot who is articulate has balanced judgment. Being highly trained guarantees that the pilot is adaptable. A balanced-judgment pilot is well restored. If a pilot is an expert, then they cannot be adaptable. If a pilot is accountable, then they are not well restored. Pilot Shay is either an expert or a team player (or both).
examples.question: Is Pilot Shay accountable?
examples.completion:
<think>
We need to determine whether Pilot Shay holds the property of being accountable. The available information includes a mixture of facts, conditional rules, and a disjunction that together allow us to reason about his accountability.

The rule linking team-player status to articulateness will later let us turn a derived team-player fact into an articulateness fact. Recognizing this connection is essential for the chain of reasoning.
<step n="1" type="premise">
<text>If a pilot is a team player, then they are articulate.</text>
<logic>articulate(pilot_shay) :- team_player(pilot_shay)</logic>
</step>

This conditional tells us that articulateness guarantees balanced judgment, which will be useful once we have established that Pilot Shay is articulate. It bridges the gap between communication skill and sound decision‑making.
<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>If a pilot is articulate, then they have balanced judgment.</text>
<logic>balanced_judgment(pilot_shay) :- articulate(pilot_shay)</logic>
</step>

The implication from being highly trained to being adaptable is the starting point for generating a positive property from the given fact. It will allow us to produce the adaptable predicate needed for later steps.
<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>If a pilot is highly trained, then they are adaptable.</text>
<logic>adaptable(pilot_shay) :- highly_trained(pilot_shay)</logic>
</step>

Balanced judgment leading to being well restored will be invoked after we have obtained balanced judgment. This rule connects mental balance to physical readiness, which is crucial for the final accountability argument.
<step n="4" type="premise">
<text>If a pilot has balanced judgment, then they are well restored.</text>
<logic>well_restored(pilot_shay) :- balanced_judgment(pilot_shay)</logic>
</step>

The rule that an expert pilot cannot be adaptable provides a way to contradict the expert status if we already know the pilot is adaptable. This will enable a contrapositive style inference later on.
<step n="5" type="premise">
<text>If a pilot is an expert, then they are not adaptable.</text>
<logic>~adaptable(pilot_shay) :- expert(pilot_shay)</logic>
</step>

The conditional that accountability implies not being well restored sets up a direct conflict with any derived well‑restored status. It will be the key to disproving accountability at the end.
<step n="6" type="premise">
<text>If a pilot is accountable, then they are not well restored.</text>
<logic>~well_restored(pilot_shay) :- accountable(pilot_shay)</logic>
</step>

The disjunction states that Pilot Shay must be either an expert or a team player. This alternative will be resolved once we can eliminate one of the options, allowing us to infer the other.
<step n="7" type="premise">
<text>Pilot Shay is either an expert or a team player.</text>
<logic>expert(pilot_shay) v team_player(pilot_shay)</logic>
</step>

The explicit fact that Pilot Shay is highly trained is the only given positive property. It will trigger the rule that converts high training into adaptability.
<step n="8" type="premise">
<text>Pilot Shay is highly trained.</text>
<logic>highly_trained(pilot_shay)</logic>
</step>

Since we know Pilot Shay is highly trained, and the rule says high training guarantees adaptability, we can directly infer that he is adaptable. This gives us a concrete positive attribute to work with in subsequent steps.
<step n="9" type="derived" from="8,3">
<text>Since Pilot Shay is highly trained and high training makes a pilot adaptable, we infer that Pilot Shay is adaptable.</text>
<logic>adaptable(pilot_shay)</logic>
</step>

We now have that Pilot Shay is adaptable. The rule that an expert would make a pilot not adaptable tells us that if he were an expert, he could not be adaptable. Because he is adaptable, the only way to avoid contradiction is to conclude that he is not an expert.
<step n="10" type="derived" from="9,5">
<text>Because Pilot Shay is adaptable and being an expert would make a pilot not adaptable, we conclude that Pilot Shay cannot be an expert.</text>
<logic>~expert(pilot_shay)</logic>
</step>

Having established that Pilot Shay is not an expert, the disjunction (expert or team player) forces the remaining alternative to be true. Therefore, Pilot Shay must be a team player.
<step n="11" type="derived" from="10,7">
<text>Given that Pilot Shay is not an expert and that he must be either an expert or a team player, we deduce that he is a team player.</text>
<logic>team_player(pilot_shay)</logic>
</step>

With the team‑player status in hand, we apply the rule linking team players to articulateness. This yields that Pilot Shay is articulate, moving us further along the chain toward balanced judgment.
<step n="12" type="derived" from="11,1">
<text>Since Pilot Shay is a team player, the rule that team players are articulate gives us that he is articulate.</text>
<logic>articulate(pilot_shay)</logic>
</step>

Articulateness triggers the next rule, giving Pilot Shay balanced judgment. This property is needed to later derive his physical state of being well restored.
<step n="13" type="derived" from="12,2">
<text>Being articulate leads to balanced judgment, so Pilot Shay has balanced judgment.</text>
<logic>balanced_judgment(pilot_shay)</logic>
</step>

Balanced judgment, by the conditional rule, makes Pilot Shay well restored. Now we have a positive physical condition that will clash with the accountability rule.
<step n="14" type="derived" from="13,4">
<text>Balanced judgment means the pilot is well restored, so Pilot Shay is well restored.</text>
<logic>well_restored(pilot_shay)</logic>
</step>

We have determined that Pilot Shay is well restored. The rule stating that accountability would make a pilot not well restored creates a direct incompatibility. Hence, to keep the knowledge base consistent, we must conclude that Pilot Shay is not accountable.
<step n="15" type="derived" from="14,6">
<text>Because Pilot Shay is well restored, and accountability would make a pilot not well restored, we infer that Pilot Shay is not accountable.</text>
<logic>~accountable(pilot_shay)</logic>
</step>

We derived Pilot_shay is not accountable, so the conclusion Pilot_shay is accountable is False.
</think>
Answer: False

examples.context: Peter is known to be sharp. Whenever Peter is resilient, he is also generous. If Peter is studious, then he is not generous. Either Peter is not grateful or he is resilient. Either Peter is not sharp or he is grateful.
examples.question: Is Peter studious?
examples.completion:
<think>
We need to decide whether Peter can be described as studious. The query asks for the truth of studious(peter), so we will try to derive either studious(peter) or its negation from the given information.

The first rule tells us that resilience guarantees generosity for Peter. This conditional will become useful later when we manage to establish Peter's resilience, allowing us to infer generosity.
<step n="1" type="premise">
<text>If Peter is resilient, then Peter is generous.</text>
<logic>generous(peter) :- resilient(peter)</logic>
</step>

The second rule says that if Peter were studious, he could not be generous. This provides a way to obtain a contradiction: if we later prove Peter is generous, the rule will let us deduce that Peter cannot be studious.
<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>If Peter is studious, then Peter is not generous.</text>
<logic>~generous(peter) :- studious(peter)</logic>
</step>

The disjunction states that at least one of the statements "Peter is not grateful" or "Peter is resilient" must hold. It sets up a logical alternative that we can exploit once we know something about Peter's gratitude.
<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>Either Peter is not grateful or Peter is resilient.</text>
<logic>~grateful(peter) v resilient(peter)</logic>
</step>

The other disjunction offers that either Peter is not sharp or Peter is grateful. Since we already know Peter is sharp, the first option is false, forcing the second option—Peter being grateful—to be true.
<step n="4" type="premise">
<text>Either Peter is not sharp or Peter is grateful.</text>
<logic>~sharp(peter) v grateful(peter)</logic>
</step>

We are given directly that Peter is sharp. This fact will interact with the previous disjunction to produce new information about Peter's gratitude.
<step n="5" type="premise">
<text>Peter is sharp.</text>
<logic>sharp(peter)</logic>
</step>

Because Peter is sharp, the clause "not sharp" in the disjunction "not sharp or grateful" cannot be satisfied. Therefore, the only way the disjunction holds is if Peter is grateful. This gives us the derived fact that Peter is grateful.
<step n="6" type="derived" from="5,4">
<text>From Peter being sharp and the clause "either not sharp or grateful", we infer that Peter is grateful.</text>
<logic>grateful(peter)</logic>
</step>

Now that we have Peter's gratitude, we look at the disjunction "not grateful or resilient". Since Peter is grateful, the "not grateful" part is false, so the disjunction can only be true if Peter is resilient. Thus we conclude that Peter is resilient.
<step n="7" type="derived" from="6,3">
<text>Using Peter's gratitude together with "either not grateful or resilient", we conclude that Peter is resilient.</text>
<logic>resilient(peter)</logic>
</step>

Having established that Peter is resilient, we can apply the first rule (resilient implies generous). By modus ponens, Peter must be generous.
<step n="8" type="derived" from="7,1">
<text>Because Peter is resilient and the rule "resilient implies generous", we derive that Peter is generous.</text>
<logic>generous(peter)</logic>
</step>

We now have Peter being generous, which directly conflicts with the second rule that says a studious Peter would be not generous. Using modus tollens, the only way to avoid this conflict is to conclude that Peter cannot be studious; hence, ~studious(peter) holds.
<step n="9" type="derived" from="8,2">
<text>Given Peter is generous and the rule "studious implies not generous", we infer that Peter is not studious.</text>
<logic>~studious(peter)</logic>
</step>

We derived Peter is not studious, so the conclusion Peter is studious is False.
</think>
Answer: False

examples.context: Daphne is astute. Kira is observant. If Daphne is dedicated, then she is athletic. If Daphne is astute, then she is brave. If Kira is strong, then she is loyal. If Kira is loyal, then she is prepared. If Kira is responsible, then she is productive. If Kira is observant, then she is responsible. If Kira is productive, then she is strategic. If Kira is systematic, then she is strong. If Kira is motivated, then she is not strategic. Moreover, Kira is either motivated or systematic. Also, either Daphne is not brave or she is dedicated.
examples.question: Is it true that Kira is not prepared?
examples.completion:
<think>
We are asked to decide whether Kira is not prepared. To answer, we will use the given facts about Daphne and Kira together with all the conditional statements that relate Kira's properties.

The rule linking strength to loyalty will later allow us to move from a property we can establish about Kira (strength) to the loyalty needed for preparation. It sets up a crucial step in the chain of inference.
<step n="1" type="premise">
<text>If Kira is strong, then she is loyal.</text>
<logic>loyal(kira) :- strong(kira)</logic>
</step>

This rule tells us that once we have shown Kira is loyal, we can immediately conclude she is prepared. It connects the earlier derived loyalty to the final goal.
<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>If Kira is loyal, then she is prepared.</text>
<logic>prepared(kira) :- loyal(kira)</logic>
</step>

Productivity follows from responsibility, and this will be used after we have shown Kira is responsible. It forms the middle link toward strategicness.
<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>If Kira is responsible, then she is productive.</text>
<logic>productive(kira) :- responsible(kira)</logic>
</step>

Observance is a fact about Kira, and this rule converts that fact into responsibility, starting the forward reasoning chain.
<step n="4" type="premise">
<text>If Kira is observant, then she is responsible.</text>
<logic>responsible(kira) :- observant(kira)</logic>
</step>

Strategicness is needed later to contradict the possibility of motivation. This rule lets us move from productivity to strategicness.
<step n="5" type="premise">
<text>If Kira is productive, then she is strategic.</text>
<logic>strategic(kira) :- productive(kira)</logic>
</step>

Strength will be derived from systematicness, and then used to get loyalty. This rule provides the bridge from systematic to strong.
<step n="6" type="premise">
<text>If Kira is systematic, then she is strong.</text>
<logic>strong(kira) :- systematic(kira)</logic>
</step>

The rule that motivation leads to non‑strategicness will allow us to eliminate motivation once we have strategicness, using a contrapositive style argument.
<step n="7" type="premise">
<text>If Kira is motivated, then she is not strategic.</text>
<logic>~strategic(kira) :- motivated(kira)</logic>
</step>

The disjunction gives us two alternatives for Kira; once we rule out one, we can assert the other. It is essential for deriving systematicness.
<step n="8" type="premise">
<text>Kira is either motivated or systematic.</text>
<logic>motivated(kira) v systematic(kira)</logic>
</step>

The fact that Daphne is astute is included for completeness, though it does not play a role in the reasoning about Kira.
<step n="9" type="premise">
<text>Daphne is astute.</text>
<logic>astute(daphne)</logic>
</step>

Kira being observant is the starting factual premise for the chain that eventually leads to her being prepared.
<step n="10" type="premise">
<text>Kira is observant.</text>
<logic>observant(kira)</logic>
</step>

Because Kira is observant and we know that any observant individual is responsible, we can directly infer that Kira is responsible. This is a straightforward application of Modus Ponens.
<step n="11" type="derived" from="10,4">
<text>From Kira being observant and the rule that observant implies responsible, we infer that Kira is responsible.</text>
<logic>responsible(kira)</logic>
</step>

Having established that Kira is responsible, and using the rule that responsibility guarantees productivity, we conclude Kira is productive. This continues the forward chain.
<step n="12" type="derived" from="11,3">
<text>Since Kira is responsible and responsibility leads to productivity, we conclude Kira is productive.</text>
<logic>productive(kira)</logic>
</step>

From Kira's productivity and the rule that productivity implies strategicness, we infer that Kira is strategic. This step brings us to a property that will conflict with motivation.
<step n="13" type="derived" from="12,5">
<text>Because Kira is productive and productivity implies being strategic, we infer Kira is strategic.</text>
<logic>strategic(kira)</logic>
</step>

We now have Kira as strategic. The rule says that if Kira were motivated, she would not be strategic. Since she is strategic, the antecedent (motivated) must be false; otherwise we would have a contradiction. Thus we infer Kira is not motivated.
<step n="14" type="derived" from="13,7">
<text>Given that Kira is strategic and that motivation would make her not strategic, we can infer that Kira cannot be motivated.</text>
<logic>~motivated(kira)</logic>
</step>

The disjunction tells us Kira is either motivated or systematic. Since we have just shown she is not motivated, the only remaining possibility is that she is systematic. This is a classic disjunctive syllogism.
<step n="15" type="derived" from="14,8">
<text>From the disjunction that Kira is either motivated or systematic, and knowing she is not motivated, we deduce she must be systematic.</text>
<logic>systematic(kira)</logic>
</step>

Given Kira is systematic and the rule that systematicness leads to strength, we can infer Kira is strong. This step prepares us to obtain loyalty.
<step n="16" type="derived" from="15,6">
<text>Since Kira is systematic and systematicness leads to strength, we infer Kira is strong.</text>
<logic>strong(kira)</logic>
</step>

With Kira now known to be strong, and the rule that strength implies loyalty, we deduce Kira is loyal. This directly sets up the final preparation step.
<step n="17" type="derived" from="16,1">
<text>Because Kira is strong and strength implies loyalty, we infer Kira is loyal.</text>
<logic>loyal(kira)</logic>
</step>

Finally, because Kira is loyal and the rule states that loyalty guarantees preparation, we conclude Kira is prepared. This completes the chain from the initial facts to the target property.
<step n="18" type="derived" from="17,2">
<text>From Kira being loyal and loyalty leading to being prepared, we conclude Kira is prepared.</text>
<logic>prepared(kira)</logic>
</step>

We derived Kira is prepared, so the conclusion Kira is not prepared is False.
</think>
Answer: False

examples.context: Thea is a rational linguist. Whenever someone is rational, they are also generous, so Thea’s rationality makes her generous. If Thea is not reflective, then she becomes inventive. Being precise guarantees that one is principled, so any precise person is principled. Whenever Thea is reflective, she is not diplomatic. If Thea is eloquent, then she is not principled. Either Thea is not generous or she is diplomatic. Also, either Thea is not inventive or she is precise.
examples.question: Is Thea eloquent?
examples.completion:
<think>
We are asked to determine whether Thea possesses the property of eloquence. The goal is to see if the premises force eloquence to be true or false for Thea.

This premise tells us that rationality is sufficient for generosity. It will become useful once we know Thea is rational, allowing us to derive generosity later.
<step n="1" type="premise">
<text>The rule that rationality implies generosity for Thea.</text>
<logic>generous(thea) :- rational(thea)</logic>
</step>

Here we have a conditional linking lack of reflectiveness to inventiveness. It will be applied after we establish that Thea is not reflective.
<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>The rule that lacking reflectiveness leads to inventiveness for Thea.</text>
<logic>inventive(thea) :- ~reflective(thea)</logic>
</step>

The third rule connects precision with being principled. Once we can prove Thea is precise, we will be able to infer her principled status.
<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>The rule that precision leads to being principled for Thea.</text>
<logic>principled(thea) :- precise(thea)</logic>
</step>

This rule says that if Thea is reflective, she cannot be diplomatic. It will later be used in a contrapositive fashion to obtain information about reflectiveness from diplomatic status.
<step n="4" type="premise">
<text>The rule that reflectiveness causes Thea to be non‑diplomatic.</text>
<logic>~diplomatic(thea) :- reflective(thea)</logic>
</step>

The fifth rule is crucial for the query: it states that eloquence would make Thea non‑principled. By later showing Thea is principled, we can contradict any possibility of her being eloquent.
<step n="5" type="premise">
<text>The rule that eloquence causes Thea to be non‑principled.</text>
<logic>~principled(thea) :- eloquent(thea)</logic>
</step>

The first disjunction offers two alternatives: either Thea is not generous or she is diplomatic. When we later learn she is generous, the only way to satisfy the disjunction is that she must be diplomatic.
<step n="6" type="premise">
<text>The disjunction stating either Thea is not generous or she is diplomatic.</text>
<logic>~generous(thea) v diplomatic(thea)</logic>
</step>

The second disjunction gives a choice between Thea not being inventive or being precise. Once we have her inventiveness, the disjunction forces her to be precise.
<step n="7" type="premise">
<text>The disjunction stating either Thea is not inventive or she is precise.</text>
<logic>~inventive(thea) v precise(thea)</logic>
</step>

The fact that Thea is rational is the starting point of the whole derivation. It directly activates the rational‑to‑generous rule.
<step n="8" type="premise">
<text>The factual claim that Thea is rational.</text>
<logic>rational(thea)</logic>
</step>

Combining the rational fact with the rule that rationality implies generosity, we can assert that Thea is generous. This gives us a concrete positive property to work with in the next disjunctive step.
<step n="9" type="derived" from="8,1">
<text>From Thea’s rationality and the rational‑to‑generous rule we infer that Thea is generous.</text>
<logic>generous(thea)</logic>
</step>

We now have generosity, but the first disjunction says "not generous or diplomatic". Since generosity is true, the "not generous" part is false, so the only way the disjunction holds is if Thea is diplomatic. Thus we conclude diplomatic status.
<step n="10" type="derived" from="9,6">

<logic>diplomatic(thea)</logic>

We possess diplomatic truth and the rule that reflective implies not diplomatic. By the contrapositive (modus tollens), if diplomatic holds then reflectiveness must be false; therefore Thea is not reflective. This negation sets up the next conditional rule.
<step n="11" type="derived" from="10,4">
<text>Because Thea is diplomatic and reflectiveness implies non‑diplomacy, we deduce that Thea cannot be reflective.</text>
<logic>~reflective(thea)</logic>
</step>

With the knowledge that Thea is not reflective, we can fire the rule linking non‑reflectiveness to inventiveness. Hence Thea becomes inventive, providing the antecedent needed for the second disjunction.
<step n="12" type="derived" from="11,2">
<text>Since Thea is not reflective, the non‑reflective‑to‑inventive rule gives us that Thea is inventive.</text>
<logic>inventive(thea)</logic>
</step>

The inventive fact meets the left side of the second disjunction ("not inventive or precise"). Because "not inventive" is false, the disjunction forces the other alternative, giving us that Thea is precise.
<step n="13" type="derived" from="12,7">
<text>The inventive fact combined with the second disjunction forces Thea to be precise.</text>
<logic>precise(thea)</logic>
</step>

Now that we have precision, the precision‑to‑principled rule applies, allowing us to deduce that Thea is principled. This principled status will clash with the eloquent‑to‑non‑principled rule.
<step n="14" type="derived" from="13,3">
<text>From Thea’s precision and the precision‑to‑principled rule we obtain that Thea is principled.</text>
<logic>principled(thea)</logic>
</step>

We have established that Thea is principled, but the earlier rule says eloquence would make her non‑principled. Using modus tollens again, the fact that she remains principled means she cannot be eloquent. Hence we derive the negation of eloquence.
<step n="15" type="derived" from="14,5">
<text>Given Thea is principled and the eloquent‑to‑non‑principled rule, we infer that Thea cannot be eloquent.</text>

examples.context: Ivan is wise. Milan is observant. Whenever Ivan is wise, Ivan is also analytical. If Milan is open_minded, then Milan is empathetic. Being adaptable makes Milan open_minded. examples.question: Based on the given information, is Milan not empathetic?
examples.completion:
<think>
We are given a collection of facts and conditional statements about Ivan and Milan. Our goal is to determine whether the claim that Milan is not empathetic can be supported by the information.

This premise connects openness of mind to empathy for Milan, which will be crucial later when we establish Milan's open‑mindedness. It tells us exactly how empathy can be derived.
<step n="1" type="premise">
<text>The rule says that if Milan is open_minded then Milan is empathetic.</text>
<logic>empathetic(milan) :- open_minded(milan)</logic>
</step>

Here we see that adaptability is a sufficient condition for Milan to be open‑minded. If we can show Milan is adaptable, we will be able to use PREMISE_1 to get empathy.
<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>The rule says that if Milan is adaptable then Milan is open_minded.</text>
<logic>open_minded(milan) :- adaptable(milan)</logic>
</step>

This rule provides a way to contradict proactive behavior: if Milan were proactive, he would cease to be observant. Since we already know Milan is observant, this premise will help us eliminate the proactive possibility.
<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>The rule says that if Milan is proactive then Milan is not observant.</text>
<logic>~observant(milan) :- proactive(milan)</logic>
</step>

The disjunction gives us two alternatives for Milan’s status: he is either proactive or adaptable (or both). Combined with the result of PREMISE_3, it will let us pinpoint which alternative actually holds.
<step n="4" type="premise">
<text>The disjunction states that Milan is either proactive or adaptable.</text>
<logic>proactive(milan) v adaptable(milan)</logic>
</step>

Ivan being wise is recorded as a fact; although it does not directly involve Milan, it establishes the truth of one of the background statements in the knowledge base.
<step n="5" type="premise">
<text>Fact: Ivan is wise.</text>
<logic>wise(ivan)</logic>
</step>

Milan being observant is a key fact that will clash with the consequence of being proactive, allowing us to draw a negative conclusion about proactivity.
<step n="6" type="premise">
<text>Fact: Milan is observant.</text>
<logic>observant(milan)</logic>
</step>

Because we have the fact that Milan is observant, the rule from PREMISE_3 tells us that if he were proactive, he would not be observant. Since he is observant, the only way to avoid a contradiction is to conclude that Milan is not proactive. This is an instance of modus tollens.
<step n="7" type="derived" from="6,3">
<text>From Milan being observant and the rule that proactive would make him not observant, we infer that Milan cannot be proactive.</text>
<logic>~proactive(milan)</logic>
</step>

Now we look at the disjunction from PREMISE_4: Milan is proactive or adaptable. We have just established that he is not proactive (STEP_7). The only remaining way for the disjunction to be true is for Milan to be adaptable. Hence we infer adaptability.
<step n="8" type="derived" from="7,4">
<text>Using the disjunction that Milan is proactive or adaptable, together with the conclusion that he is not proactive, we deduce that Milan must be adaptable.</text>
<logic>adaptable(milan)</logic>
</step>

Having shown Milan is adaptable, we can invoke PREMISE_2, which says that adaptability guarantees open‑mindedness. Therefore Milan must be open‑minded.
<step n="9" type="derived" from="8,2">
<text>Since Milan is adaptable, the rule that adaptability leads to being open_minded gives us that Milan is open_minded.</text>
<logic>open_minded(milan)</logic>
</step>

With Milan established as open‑minded, PREMISE_1 applies directly: an open‑minded Milan is empathetic. Thus we derive that Milan is empathetic.
<step n="10" type="derived" from="9,1">
<text>Because Milan is open_minded, the rule linking open_mindedness to empathy tells us that Milan is empathetic.</text>
<logic>empathetic(milan)</logic>
</step>

We derived Milan is empathetic, so the conclusion Milan is not empathetic is False.
</think>
Answer: False

examples.context: In this musical world, the aria is resonant and the riff is resonant. The coda, the aria, and the riff are all steady in their tempo. Whenever something is steady, it is also harmonic, so the coda is harmonic because it is steady. Any dynamic passage guarantees a melodic character, meaning that if something is dynamic then it is melodic; this applies to the coda. Whenever something is harmonic, it becomes resonant, so the coda will be resonant if it is harmonic. Any resonant piece is crisp, therefore the coda is crisp once it is resonant. Whenever something is crisp, it becomes dynamic, so the coda is dynamic once it is crisp. If something is melodic, then it is theoretical, which makes the coda theoretical once it is melodic. All these conditionals describe how musical qualities flow from one to another.
examples.question: Is the coda virtuosic?
examples.completion:
<think>
We are asked whether the coda possesses the virtuosic quality. To answer, we must see if any chain of the provided musical rules can lead from known facts about the coda to the predicate virtuosic. None of the rules mention virtuosic directly, so we will examine how the given premises interact.

I need to determine whether Coda is virtuosic can be derived from the given premises.

The first rule tells us that steadiness implies harmonicness for the coda. Since we already know the coda is steady, this rule is the starting point for deriving further properties. It establishes a direct link from a known fact to a new musical attribute.
<step n="1" type="premise">
<text>If the coda is steady, then it is harmonic.</text>
<logic>harmonic(coda) :- steady(coda)</logic>
</step>

The second rule connects dynamic passages to a melodic character. Although we do not yet have dynamic for the coda, this rule will become useful later in the chain once we can infer dynamic from other properties.
<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>If the coda is dynamic, then it is melodic.</text>
<logic>melodic(coda) :- dynamic(coda)</logic>
</step>

The third rule says that harmonicness leads to resonant quality. Once we obtain harmonicness from the first rule, we can immediately conclude that the coda is resonant, moving us further along the chain of attributes.
<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>If the coda is harmonic, then it is resonant.</text>
<logic>resonant(coda) :- harmonic(coda)</logic>
</step>

According to the fourth rule, any melodic piece is also theoretical. This will be relevant after we manage to infer that the coda is melodic via the dynamic-to-melodic rule.
<step n="4" type="premise">
<text>If the coda is melodic, then it is theoretical.</text>
<logic>theoretical(coda) :- melodic(coda)</logic>
</step>

The fifth rule indicates that resonant music becomes crisp. After establishing the coda as resonant, we can apply this rule to gain crispness, which is another stepping stone toward dynamic.
<step n="5" type="premise">
<text>If the coda is resonant, then it is crisp.</text>
<logic>crisp(coda) :- resonant(coda)</logic>
</step>

The sixth rule closes the loop by stating that crispness produces a dynamic quality. By chaining the previous rules—steady → harmonic → resonant → crisp—we finally obtain that the coda is dynamic, which then activates the second rule to give us melodic and subsequently theoretical properties.
<step n="6" type="premise">
<text>If the coda is crisp, then it is dynamic.</text>
<logic>dynamic(coda) :- crisp(coda)</logic>
</step>

The fact that the aria is resonant is unrelated to the coda's virtuosic status but illustrates that resonant qualities exist elsewhere in the musical setting, confirming that the concept of resonant is meaningful in this world.
<step n="7" type="premise">
<text>The aria is resonant.</text>
<logic>resonant(aria)</logic>
</step>

Similarly, the riff being resonant serves as another example of the resonant property, reinforcing the relevance of the resonant predicate in the overall knowledge base.
<step n="8" type="premise">
<text>The riff is resonant.</text>
<logic>resonant(riff)</logic>
</step>

The core fact that the coda is steady is the anchor for the entire inference chain. Without this fact, none of the subsequent rules could be triggered for the coda.
<step n="9" type="premise">
<text>The coda is steady.</text>
<logic>steady(coda)</logic>
</step>

The aria's steadiness mirrors the coda's steadiness, showing that multiple entities share the steady attribute, but it does not directly affect the coda's virtuosic status.
<step n="10" type="premise">
<text>The aria is steady.</text>
<logic>steady(aria)</logic>
</step>

The riff's steadiness further demonstrates the prevalence of the steady attribute in the domain, yet it remains peripheral to the query about the coda.
<step n="11" type="premise">
<text>The riff is steady.</text>
<logic>steady(riff)</logic>
</step>

<step n="12" type="derived">
<text>Searching for rules that conclude Coda is virtuosic: no rule or disjunction has "virtuosic" as a consequent.</text>

</step>

<step n="13" type="derived">
<text>Similarly, no rule concludes Coda is not virtuosic.</text>

</step>

<step n="14" type="derived">
<text>Neither Coda is virtuosic nor Coda is not virtuosic can be derived from the given premises. The answer is Unknown.</text>

</think>
Answer: Unknown

Proficient Marley guarantees that Marley is loyal, and if something is resilient then it is optimistic, so a resilient Marley would be optimistic. Any open‑minded bree becomes strong, and any proactive bree is not clear‑headed. Conversely, if bree were resourceful then bree would not be strong. If bree is not proactive, then bree is cautious, and if bree is not resourceful, then bree is cheerful. Either bree is not adaptable or bree is decisive, either bree is not cautious or bree is eloquent, either bree is not cheerful or bree is clear‑headed, either bree is not humble or bree is open‑minded, and either Marley is not loyal or Marley is resilient. These sentences capture every fact, rule, and disjunction that will be needed to decide whether bree is eloquent.
examples.question: Based on the information given, is bree eloquent?
examples.completion:
<think>
We are asked to decide whether bree possesses the property eloquent. To answer, we will chain together the given facts, conditional rules, and disjunctive statements until we can derive eloquent(bree).

This premise tells us how cautiousness can arise for bree: whenever bree lacks proactivity, cautiousness follows. It will become useful once we learn that bree is not proactive.
<step n="1" type="premise">
<text>If bree is not proactive, then bree is cautious.</text>
<logic>cautious(bree) :- ~proactive(bree)</logic>
</step>

Here we see that cheerfulness is linked to the absence of resourcefulness. Later we will establish that bree is not resourceful, allowing us to claim bree is cheerful.
<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>If bree is not resourceful, then bree is cheerful.</text>
<logic>cheerful(bree) :- ~resourceful(bree)</logic>
</step>

This rule connects openness of mind to strength. Once we can prove bree is open‑minded, we will be able to infer that bree is strong.
<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>If bree is open_minded, then bree is strong.</text>
<logic>strong(bree) :- open_minded(bree)</logic>
</step>

This conditional says that if bree were proactive, bree could not be clear‑headed. It will later help us to deduce that bree is not proactive by contraposition from clear‑headedness.
<step n="4" type="premise">
<text>If bree is proactive, then bree is not clear_headed.</text>
<logic>~clear_headed(bree) :- proactive(bree)</logic>
</step>

According to this, being resourceful would prevent bree from being strong. When we eventually learn that bree is strong, we can reverse‑engineer that bree must not be resourceful.
<step n="5" type="premise">
<text>If bree is resourceful, then bree is not strong.</text>
<logic>~strong(bree) :- resourceful(bree)</logic>
</step>

The disjunction gives us a direct route to eloquence: either bree fails to be cautious or bree is eloquent. Once we manage to establish that bree is cautious, the other side of the ‘or’ must hold, giving us eloquent(bree).
<step n="6" type="premise">
<text>Either bree is not cautious or bree is eloquent.</text>
<logic>~cautious(bree) v eloquent(bree)</logic>
</step>

This alternative says bree is either not cheerful or clear‑headed. After we derive bree's cheerfulness, the only way for the statement to stay true is for bree to be clear‑headed.
<step n="7" type="premise">
<text>Either bree is not cheerful or bree is clear_headed.</text>
<logic>~cheerful(bree) v clear_headed(bree)</logic>
</step>

The statement links humility and open‑mindedness. Since we already know bree is humble, the disjunction forces bree to be open‑minded.
<step n="8" type="premise">
<text>Either bree is not humble or bree is open_minded.</text>
<logic>~humble(bree) v open_minded(bree)</logic>
</step>

Marley being proficient is a factual premise that will support other rules about Marley, though it is not directly needed for bree's eloquence.
<step n="9" type="premise">
<text>Marley is proficient.</text>
<logic>proficient(marley)</logic>
</step>

Iris's vigor is recorded as a fact; it will activate the rule that vigorous things are adaptable, but this does not affect bree's chain of reasoning.
<step n="10" type="premise">
<text>Iris is vigorous.</text>
<logic>vigorous(iris)</logic>
</step>

Bree's humility is a concrete fact. Combined with the disjunction about humility, it lets us infer bree's open‑mindedness, starting the cascade toward eloquence.
<step n="11" type="premise">
<text>Bree is humble.</text>
<logic>humble(bree)</logic>
</step>

Because bree is humble and the disjunction says either bree is not humble or bree is open‑minded, the humble side is false, so the only way the statement can be true is if bree is open‑minded. This gives us open_minded(bree).
<step n="12" type="derived" from="11,8">
<text>From bree being humble and the disjunction that bree is not humble or bree is open_minded, we infer that bree is open_minded.</text>
<logic>open_minded(bree)</logic>
</step>

Now that we have open_minded(bree) and the rule that open‑mindedness implies strength, we can directly conclude bree is strong. This establishes the positive property needed for later steps.
<step n="13" type="derived" from="12,3">
<text>Since bree is open_minded and the rule that open_minded implies strong, we conclude bree is strong.</text>
<logic>strong(bree)</logic>
</step>

We have just shown bree is strong. The rule "if bree is resourceful then bree is not strong" tells us that a resourceful bree would contradict the observed strength. Therefore bree cannot be resourceful, yielding ~resourceful(bree).
<step n="14" type="derived" from="13,5">
<text>Because bree is strong and the rule that resourceful implies not strong, we deduce that bree cannot be resourceful.</text>
<logic>~resourceful(bree)</logic>
</step>

Having derived ~resourceful(bree) and recalling the rule that lack of resourcefulness leads to cheerfulness, we can safely assert bree is cheerful. This satisfies one half of the next disjunction.
<step n="15" type="derived" from="14,2">
<text>With bree not resourceful and the rule that not resourceful leads to cheerful, we obtain that bree is cheerful.</text>
<logic>cheerful(bree)</logic>
</step>

Since bree is now known to be cheerful, the disjunction "~cheerful(bree) v clear_headed(bree)" can only hold if the second alternative is true. Consequently, bree must be clear_headed.
<step n="16" type="derived" from="15,7">
<text>Given bree is cheerful and the disjunction that either bree is not cheerful or bree is clear_headed, we infer bree is clear_headed.</text>
<logic>clear_headed(bree)</logic>
</step>

The rule "proactive(bree) -> ~clear_headed(bree)" gives the contrapositive: if bree is clear_headed then bree is not proactive. Because we have clear_headed(bree), we infer ~proactive(bree).
<step n="17" type="derived" from="16,4">
<text>From bree being clear_headed and the rule that proactive implies not clear_headed, we infer that bree is not proactive.</text>
<logic>~proactive(bree)</logic>
</step>

With ~proactive(bree) established and the earlier rule that lack of proactivity yields cautiousness, we can conclude bree is cautious. This satisfies the left side of the disjunction that will give us eloquence.
<step n="18" type="derived" from="17,1">
<text>Since bree is not proactive and the rule that not proactive yields cautious, we get that bree is cautious.</text>
<logic>cautious(bree)</logic>
</step>

Finally, the disjunction "~cautious(bree) v eloquent(bree)" now has its first option falsified because bree is indeed cautious. Therefore the only way for the statement to remain true is for bree to be eloquent, giving us eloquent(bree).
<step n="19" type="derived" from="18,6">
<text>With bree cautious and the disjunction that either bree is not cautious or bree is eloquent, we conclude bree is eloquent.</text>
<logic>eloquent(bree)</logic>
</step>

Therefore Bree is eloquent, so the conclusion is True.
</think>
Answer: True

examples.context: Captain Jane is a skilled sailor. Whenever a crew member is a sharp navigator, they also possess a weather sense. Moreover, having a weather sense guarantees that the person is not a skilled sailor.
examples.question: Is it true that Captain Jane is not a sharp navigator?
examples.completion:
<think>
We are given information about Captain Jane's abilities and two conditional rules linking navigation skill and weather sense. Our goal is to determine whether we can logically conclude that she is not a sharp navigator.

The first rule tells us that being a sharp navigator always brings along a weather sense. This connection will allow us to relate the presence or absence of a weather sense to the navigation skill later on.
<step n="1" type="premise">
<text>If someone is a sharp navigator, then they have a weather sense.</text>
<logic>weather_sense(captain_jane) :- sharp_navigator(captain_jane)</logic>
</step>

The second rule states that anyone who has a weather sense cannot be a skilled sailor. This contrapositive relationship will be crucial for turning Jane's known skill into information about her weather sense.
<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>If someone has a weather sense, then they are not a skilled sailor.</text>
<logic>~skilled_sailor(captain_jane) :- weather_sense(captain_jane)</logic>
</step>

<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>Captain Jane is a skilled sailor.</text>
<logic>skilled_sailor(captain_jane)</logic>
</step>

We know from the facts that Jane is a skilled sailor. If she were to have a weather sense, the second rule would force her to be not a skilled sailor, which contradicts the fact. By applying modus tollens, we therefore deduce that Jane does not have a weather sense.
<step n="4" type="derived" from="3,2">
<text>Since Jane is a skilled sailor and having a weather sense would make her not a skilled sailor, we infer that she does not have a weather sense.</text>
<logic>~weather_sense(captain_jane)</logic>
</step>

Having established that Jane lacks a weather sense, we turn to the first rule, which says a sharp navigator must have a weather sense. Since she does not have a weather sense, the antecedent of that rule must be false; thus, Jane cannot be a sharp navigator. This follows again by modus tollens.
<step n="5" type="derived" from="4,1">
<text>Because Jane lacks a weather sense and any sharp navigator would have a weather sense, we conclude that she is not a sharp navigator.</text>
<logic>~sharp_navigator(captain_jane)</logic>
</step>

Therefore Captain_jane is not sharp navigator, so the conclusion is True.
</think>
Answer: True

Normally, if a kiln is temperature_targeted and there is no abnormality preventing careful handling, then it handles carefully.
examples.question: Is the kiln versatile in shape?

We need to determine whether the kiln possesses the versatile_shape property. The goal is to trace how the given facts and default rules lead to that conclusion.

The first premise tells us that any object that handles carefully will become cooled. This establishes a direct link from careful handling to the cooled state, which we will later need for the versatility default.

The second premise is a default rule: a cooled kiln is normally versatile unless something abnormal stops it. This is the crucial bridge that lets us move from a cooled state to the desired versatile_shape conclusion.

The third premise provides a default that a temperature_targeted kiln handles carefully unless an abnormality prevents it. Since we have the kiln being temperature_targeted, this rule will allow us to derive careful handling, setting up the chain toward cooling.

<text>The default rule says a temperature‑targeted kiln handles carefully unless an abnormality blocks careful handling.</text>

</step>

The fact that the glaze_bowl is design_innovative is recorded, though it does not directly affect the kiln. It is included to satisfy the full set of premises required by the proof structure.

<text>The glaze_bowl is design_innovative.</text>
<logic>design_innovative(glaze_bowl)</logic>

The kiln being approved_by_master is another factual statement that does not play a role in the current derivation but must be stated as part of the given information.

We are told explicitly that the kiln is temperature_targeted. This fact activates the default rule about careful handling, making it possible to infer the next step.
<step n="6" type="premise">
<text>The kiln is temperature_targeted.</text>
<logic>temperature_targeted(kiln)</logic>
</step>

Using the temperature_targeted fact together with the default rule that a temperature_targeted kiln handles carefully (provided there is no abnormality), we can safely infer that the kiln handles carefully. The default rule applies because we have no evidence of an abnormality, so the conclusion follows.
<step n="7" type="derived" from="6,3">

Now that we know the kiln handles carefully, we apply the first rule which says careful handling leads to being cooled. By modus ponens, the kiln becomes cooled. This gives us the prerequisite needed for the versatility default.
<step n="8" type="derived" from="7,1">

Having established that the kiln is cooled, we invoke the default rule that a cooled kiln is versatile unless an abnormality blocks it. Again, we lack any abnormality information, so the default fires and we conclude the kiln is versatile_shape.
<step n="9" type="derived" from="8,2">

Therefore Kiln is versatile shape, so the conclusion is True.

examples.context: Tess is gifted. If Tess is truthful, then she is wandering. If Tess is gifted, then she is prophetic. If Tess is prophetic, then she is exacting. If Tess is rational, then she is not wandering. Either Tess is not exacting or she is truthful.
examples.question: Is it true that Tess is not rational?
examples.completion:
<think>
We are asked to determine whether Tess lacks rationality. To answer this, we will follow the chain of given facts, rules, and the disjunction, gradually deriving new information about Tess until we can address the query.

The first rule connects truthfulness to wandering, which will become useful once we know whether Tess is truthful. Knowing the link helps us later to derive her wandering status.
<step n="1" type="premise">
<text>The rule that if Tess is truthful then she is wandering.</text>
<logic>wandering(tess) :- truthful(tess)</logic>
</step>

The second rule tells us that being gifted guarantees a prophetic nature. This will allow us to move from the known fact about Tess being gifted to a new property we can exploit.
<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>The rule that if Tess is gifted then she is prophetic.</text>
<logic>prophetic(tess) :- gifted(tess)</logic>
</step>

The third rule states that any prophetic individual is also exacting. This connection will let us chain from prophetic to exacting later in the proof.
<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>The rule that if Tess is prophetic then she is exacting.</text>
<logic>exacting(tess) :- prophetic(tess)</logic>
</step>

The fourth rule says that rationality forces a lack of wandering. This contrapositive relationship is the key to eventually infer that Tess is not rational once we have her wandering.
<step n="4" type="premise">
<text>The rule that if Tess is rational then she is not wandering.</text>
<logic>~wandering(tess) :- rational(tess)</logic>
</step>

The disjunction offers two alternatives: either Tess is not exacting, or she is truthful. This will be crucial for a disjunctive syllogism once we determine one side of the disjunction.
<step n="5" type="premise">
<text>The disjunction stating either Tess is not exacting or she is truthful.</text>
<logic>~exacting(tess) v truthful(tess)</logic>
</step>

We are directly told that Tess possesses the gifted quality. This fact serves as the starting point for the forward reasoning chain.
<step n="6" type="premise">
<text>The fact that Tess is gifted.</text>
<logic>gifted(tess)</logic>
</step>

Because we know Tess is gifted and the rule that gifted implies prophetic, we can directly conclude that Tess is prophetic. This step translates the abstract rule into a concrete fact about Tess.
<step n="7" type="derived" from="6,2">
<text>From the fact that Tess is gifted and the rule that gifted implies prophetic, we infer that Tess is prophetic.</text>
<logic>prophetic(tess)</logic>
</step>

Having established Tess’s prophetic nature, we apply the rule that prophetic leads to exacting, thereby deducing that Tess is exacting. This builds a longer chain from the original fact.
<step n="8" type="derived" from="7,3">
<text>Using Tess’s prophetic nature and the rule that prophetic implies exacting, we conclude that Tess is exacting.</text>
<logic>exacting(tess)</logic>
</step>

Now we have Tess as exacting, which makes the left side of the disjunction (~exacting) false. Consequently, the only way the disjunction can hold is if the right side, Tess being truthful, is true. Thus we infer her truthfulness.
<step n="9" type="derived" from="8,5">
<text>Given that Tess is exacting and the statement that either she is not exacting or truthful, we deduce that she must be truthful.</text>
<logic>truthful(tess)</logic>
</step>

With Tess confirmed as truthful, we invoke the first rule which tells us that truthfulness guarantees wandering. Therefore, Tess must be wandering.
<step n="10" type="derived" from="9,1">
<text>Since Tess is truthful, the rule that truthful leads to wandering lets us infer that Tess is wandering.</text>
<logic>wandering(tess)</logic>
</step>

We now possess both that Tess is wandering and the rule stating that rationality would prevent wandering. By modus tollens, if rationality implied not wandering, the fact that she is wandering forces the negation of rationality, i.e., Tess is not rational.
<step n="11" type="derived" from="10,4">
<text>With Tess wandering and the rule that rationality would make her not wandering, we apply modus tollens to infer that Tess is not rational.</text>
<logic>~rational(tess)</logic>
</step>

Therefore Tess is not rational, so the conclusion is True.
</think>
Answer: True
