examples.context: In the mythic realm, the goddess Juno is noted as not_warlike. Both Hephaestus and Juno are described as valiant. Whenever a figure is warlike, that same figure is also strategic. By default, any being that is valiant and is not known to be not_warlike is considered warlike. Additionally, by default, any figure that is strategic and is not known to be not_steadfast is deemed steadfast.
examples.question: Is Hephaestus masterful?
examples.completion:
<think>
We are asked to decide whether the smith god Hephaestus can be called masterful. To answer, we must see if the premises allow us to derive the property masterful for Hephaestus.

I need to determine whether Hephaestus is masterful can be derived from the given premises.

This rule links warlike to strategic, so if we ever learn that Hephaestus is warlike we could infer he is strategic. It is a forward implication that will be useful later if the conditions for warlike are satisfied.
<step n="1" type="premise">
<text>The rule says that any warlike individual is also strategic.</text>
<logic>strategic(hephaestus) :- warlike(hephaestus)</logic>
</step>

The default rule tells us that valiant and not known to be not_warlike together make someone warlike. Since Hephaestus is valiant, we only need to know that he is not marked as not_warlike to obtain warlike status.
<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>The default says that a valiant being that is not marked as not_warlike is warlike.</text>
<logic>warlike(hephaestus) :- valiant(hephaestus), not not_warlike(hephaestus)</logic>
</step>

Here we have a second default: if someone is strategic and not known to be not_steadfast, then they are steadfast. This could give us further properties for Hephaestus, but only after we have strategic.
<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>The default says that a strategic being that is not marked as not_steadfast is steadfast.</text>
<logic>steadfast(hephaestus) :- strategic(hephaestus), not not_steadfast(hephaestus)</logic>
</step>

The fact that Juno is not_warlike provides a concrete instance of the negative marker, but it does not say anything about Hephaestus. It shows that the predicate not_warlike is used in the knowledge base.
<step n="4" type="premise">
<text>It is given that Juno is not_warlike.</text>
<logic>not_warlike(juno)</logic>
</step>

Hephaestus being valiant satisfies the first condition of the default rule for warlike. This is a key piece, because without valiance the chain would break.
<step n="5" type="premise">
<text>Hephaestus is valiant.</text>
<logic>valiant(hephaestus)</logic>
</step>

Juno also being valiant mirrors Hephaestus' valiance, but again it does not affect Hephaestus' status directly. It simply confirms that valiance is a property shared by multiple figures.
<step n="6" type="premise">
<text>Juno is also valiant.</text>
<logic>valiant(juno)</logic>
</step>

<step n="7" type="derived">
<text>Searching for rules that conclude Hephaestus is masterful: no rule or disjunction has "masterful" as a consequent.</text>
<logic>unreachable(masterful(hephaestus))</logic>
</step>

<step n="8" type="derived">
<text>Similarly, no rule concludes Hephaestus is not masterful.</text>
<logic>unreachable(~masterful(hephaestus))</logic>
</step>

<step n="9" type="derived">
<text>Neither Hephaestus is masterful nor Hephaestus is not masterful can be derived from the given premises. The answer is Unknown.</text>
<logic>unknown(masterful(hephaestus))</logic>
</step>

</think>
Answer: Unknown

examples.context: The downtown square is zoned. Any well_maintained downtown square is code_compliant. Whenever the downtown square receives public_funded support, it becomes well_maintained. A traffic_optimized downtown square is always public_funded. If the downtown square is high_density, then it is not shared_use. Being green_friendly implies that the downtown square is not award_winning. The downtown square is either high_density or traffic_optimized. Either the downtown square is not zoned, or it is shared_use. Either the downtown square is not code_compliant, or it is award_winning.
examples.question: Is the downtown square green_friendly?
examples.completion:
<think>
We need to decide whether the downtown square possesses the green_friendly property. The goal is to see if the premises force the opposite, making the query false.

This premise links well_maintained status to code_compliance, which will later help us move from funding to compliance. It sets up a chain of implications that the proof will exploit.
<step n="1" type="premise">
<text>The rule states that if the downtown square is well_maintained, then it must be code_compliant.</text>
<logic>code_compliant(downtown_square) :- well_maintained(downtown_square)</logic>
</step>

Here we learn that public_funded support guarantees a well_maintained condition. This connection will allow us to translate funding into maintenance later on.
<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>If the downtown square receives public_funded support, then it becomes well_maintained.</text>
<logic>well_maintained(downtown_square) :- public_funded(downtown_square)</logic>
</step>

The third rule tells us that any traffic_optimized downtown square automatically receives public funding. It will be crucial once we establish traffic_optimization.
<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>A traffic_optimized downtown square is always public_funded.</text>
<logic>public_funded(downtown_square) :- traffic_optimized(downtown_square)</logic>
</step>

This conditional states that high_density prevents the downtown square from being shared_use. It provides a way to negate high_density once we know the square is shared_use.
<step n="4" type="premise">
<text>Whenever the downtown square is high_density, it cannot be a shared_use space.</text>
<logic>~shared_use(downtown_square) :- high_density(downtown_square)</logic>
</step>

According to this rule, being green_friendly would mean the square cannot be award_winning. It will become the final step to reject green_friendly after we derive award_winning.
<step n="5" type="premise">
<text>If the downtown square is green_friendly, then it is not award_winning.</text>
<logic>~award_winning(downtown_square) :- green_friendly(downtown_square)</logic>
</step>

The disjunction gives us two possibilities for the square: either it is high_density or it is traffic_optimized. This alternative will be resolved once we eliminate one option.
<step n="6" type="premise">
<text>The downtown square is either high_density or traffic_optimized.</text>
<logic>high_density(downtown_square) v traffic_optimized(downtown_square)</logic>
</step>

This statement offers another binary choice: either the square is not zoned or it is shared_use. Combined with the known fact that it is zoned, it will let us infer shared_use.
<step n="7" type="premise">
<text>Either the downtown square is not zoned, or it is a shared_use space.</text>
<logic>~zoned(downtown_square) v shared_use(downtown_square)</logic>
</step>

The final disjunction says that the square is either not code_compliant or it is award_winning. After we prove code_compliance, we will be forced to accept award_winning.
<step n="8" type="premise">
<text>Either the downtown square is not code_compliant, or it is award_winning.</text>
<logic>~code_compliant(downtown_square) v award_winning(downtown_square)</logic>
</step>

The concrete fact that the downtown square is zoned anchors the reasoning. It will be used together with the seventh disjunction to produce a shared_use conclusion.
<step n="9" type="premise">
<text>The downtown square is zoned.</text>
<logic>zoned(downtown_square)</logic>
</step>

We have the fact that the square is zoned, and the seventh premise tells us that either it is not zoned or it is shared_use. Since the first alternative is false, the only way for the disjunction to hold is that the square is shared_use. This gives us the shared_use status we need for the next step.
<step n="10" type="derived" from="9,7">
<text>From the fact that the downtown square is zoned and the disjunction that it is either not zoned or shared_use, we infer that it must be a shared_use space.</text>
<logic>shared_use(downtown_square)</logic>
</step>

Now that we know the square is shared_use, we look at the fourth premise, which says high_density implies not shared_use. By contraposition, if the square is shared_use, it cannot be high_density. Hence we conclude the square is not high_density, narrowing the possibilities in the sixth disjunction.
<step n="11" type="derived" from="10,4">
<text>Given that the downtown square is shared_use and the rule that high_density implies not shared_use, we conclude that the downtown square is not high_density.</text>
<logic>~high_density(downtown_square)</logic>
</step>

The sixth premise states that the square is either high_density or traffic_optimized. Since we have just shown it is not high_density, the only remaining option is traffic_optimized. Therefore the square must be traffic_optimized.
<step n="12" type="derived" from="11,6">
<text>Since the downtown square is not high_density and we know it is either high_density or traffic_optimized, it must be traffic_optimized.</text>
<logic>traffic_optimized(downtown_square)</logic>
</step>

With traffic_optimized established, the third rule kicks in: any traffic_optimized square is public_funded. This allows us to claim that the downtown square receives public funding, moving us further along the implication chain.
<step n="13" type="derived" from="12,3">
<text>Because the downtown square is traffic_optimized, the rule linking traffic_optimized to public_funded tells us that it is public_funded.</text>
<logic>public_funded(downtown_square)</logic>
</step>

Given that the square is public_funded, the second premise tells us that public_funded support guarantees the square is well_maintained. Thus we can assert the square is well_maintained, preparing us to use the first rule.
<step n="14" type="derived" from="13,2">
<text>Being public_funded leads, via the second rule, to the downtown square being well_maintained.</text>
<logic>well_maintained(downtown_square)</logic>
</step>

Because the square is well_maintained, the first premise yields that it is code_compliant. This satisfies the left side of the eighth disjunction, forcing the right side to be true.
<step n="15" type="derived" from="14,1">
<text>From being well_maintained, the first rule gives that the downtown square is code_compliant.</text>
<logic>code_compliant(downtown_square)</logic>
</step>

The eighth disjunction says the square is either not code_compliant or award_winning. Since we have just shown it is code_compliant, the only way for the disjunction to hold is that the square is award_winning. Hence we derive award_winning.
<step n="16" type="derived" from="15,8">
<text>With the downtown square code_compliant and the disjunction that either it is not code_compliant or award_winning, we deduce that it is award_winning.</text>
<logic>award_winning(downtown_square)</logic>
</step>

Finally, the fifth premise links green_friendly to not being award_winning. We now know the square is award_winning, so the condition for green_friendly cannot be satisfied. Therefore the square cannot be green_friendly.
<step n="17" type="derived" from="16,5">
<text>Since the downtown square is award_winning and the rule that green_friendly implies not award_winning, we infer that it cannot be green_friendly.</text>
<logic>~green_friendly(downtown_square)</logic>
</step>

We derived Downtown_square is not green friendly, so the conclusion Downtown_square is green friendly is False.
</think>
Answer: False

examples.context: In a philosophical discussion, the individual known as the Socratic student is described as open_hearted and competent. The theory holds that any open_hearted person is also modest. Additionally, there is a default principle: normally, if someone is modest and there is no evidence that they are abnormally logical, then they are considered logical. No other alternatives or exceptions are mentioned.
examples.question: Is the Socratic student logical?
examples.completion:
<think>
We are asked to determine whether the Socratic student can be classified as logical. To answer this, we will examine the explicit facts about the student and the conditional principles that connect those facts to logical status.

The first premise provides a conditional link: being open_hearted guarantees modesty. This connection is crucial because later reasoning depends on establishing modesty before applying the default rule for logicality.
<step n="1" type="premise">
<text>The rule states that any open_hearted individual is also modest.</text>
<logic>modest(socratic_student) :- open_hearted(socratic_student)</logic>
</step>

The second premise introduces a default rule: modest individuals are typically logical unless there is an abnormality. This rule will allow us to infer logicality once we have modesty and no contrary evidence.
<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>The default rule says that a modest individual is logical unless there is evidence of abnormal logicality.</text>
<logic>logical(socratic_student) :- modest(socratic_student), not ab_logical(socratic_student)</logic>
</step>

<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>The Socratic student is competent.</text>
<logic>competent(socratic_student)</logic>
</step>

<step n="4" type="premise">
<text>The Socratic student is open_hearted.</text>
<logic>open_hearted(socratic_student)</logic>
</step>

We know the student is open_hearted (from the facts) and the rule says open_hearted implies modest. By applying this implication, we can directly deduce that the student is modest. This step bridges the factual description to the condition required by the default rule.
<step n="5" type="derived" from="4,1">
<text>From the fact that the student is open_hearted and the rule linking openness to modesty, we infer that the student is modest.</text>
<logic>modest(socratic_student)</logic>
</step>

Having established modesty, we check the default rule's second condition: there must be no evidence of abnormal logicality. The context supplies no such abnormality, so the condition is satisfied. Consequently, the default rule yields that the student is logical.
<step n="6" type="derived" from="5,2">
<text>Because the student is modest and there is no indication of abnormal logicality, the default rule lets us conclude the student is logical.</text>
<logic>logical(socratic_student)</logic>
</step>

Therefore Socratic_student is logical, so the conclusion is True.
</think>
Answer: True

examples.context: Coach Tom is nervous, and nervous coaches are always fair. Player Willow is skilled, and any skilled player is also satisfied. Sprinter Riley is quick, and quick sprinters become strategic. Whenever a player is satisfied, they become a veteran, and veteran players are inventive. If a sprinter is diligent, then the sprinter is alert. A clever coach is courteous, and a clever coach may or may not be fair. Either the sprinter is not strategic or the sprinter is diligent. Either the player is not inventive or the player is supportive. Either the coach is not fair or the coach is clever.
examples.question: Is player_willow supportive?
examples.completion:
<think>
We need to decide whether player_willow is supportive. The goal can be reached by chaining the conditional statements about skill, satisfaction, veteran status, and inventiveness, and then using the provided disjunction.

The first premise tells us that skill implies satisfaction for any player. This will let us turn Willow's known skill into the satisfaction needed for later steps. It is directly relevant because we already have Willow's skill as a fact.
<step n="1" type="premise">
<text>If a player is skilled, then they are satisfied.</text>
<logic>satisfied(player_willow) :- skilled(player_willow)</logic>
</step>

The second premise links veteran status to inventiveness. Once we can establish that Willow is a veteran, we will be able to infer that she is inventive, which is needed for the disjunctive reasoning later on.
<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>If a player is a veteran, then they are inventive.</text>
<logic>inventive(player_willow) :- veteran(player_willow)</logic>
</step>

The third premise converts satisfaction into veteran status. This bridges the gap between the first premise (skill → satisfaction) and the second premise (veteran → inventive). It ensures a smooth chain of implications.
<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>If a player is satisfied, then they are a veteran.</text>
<logic>veteran(player_willow) :- satisfied(player_willow)</logic>
</step>

The fourth premise is a disjunction: either Willow is not inventive or she is supportive. If we can prove Willow is inventive, the left side of the disjunction becomes false, forcing the right side—supportiveness—to be true.
<step n="4" type="premise">
<text>Either the player is not inventive or they are supportive.</text>
<logic>~inventive(player_willow) v supportive(player_willow)</logic>
</step>

Coach Tom being nervous is a fact that satisfies the antecedent of the rule making nervous coaches fair. Although this fact does not directly involve Willow, it completes the background of all given statements.
<step n="5" type="premise">
<text>Coach Tom is nervous.</text>
<logic>nervous(coach_tom)</logic>
</step>

Sprinter Riley being quick is a fact that activates the rule making quick sprinters strategic. Like the previous premise, it is part of the overall knowledge base but not needed for the query about Willow.
<step n="6" type="premise">
<text>Sprinter Riley is quick.</text>
<logic>quick(sprinter_riley)</logic>
</step>

Player Willow being skilled is the critical fact that starts our chain of reasoning. It will allow us to apply PREMISE_1 and move toward establishing her supportiveness.
<step n="7" type="premise">
<text>Player Willow is skilled.</text>
<logic>skilled(player_willow)</logic>
</step>

From Willow's skill and the rule that skilled players are satisfied, we can immediately conclude that Willow is satisfied. This step uses modus ponens: the antecedent (skill) is true, so the consequent (satisfaction) follows.
<step n="8" type="derived" from="7,1">
<text>Because Willow is skilled and skilled players are satisfied, Willow is satisfied.</text>
<logic>satisfied(player_willow)</logic>
</step>

Having shown Willow is satisfied, we now apply the rule that satisfied players become veterans. Again, modus ponens lets us infer that Willow attains veteran status, moving us closer to inventiveness.
<step n="9" type="derived" from="8,3">
<text>Since Willow is satisfied and satisfied players are veterans, Willow is a veteran.</text>
<logic>veteran(player_willow)</logic>
</step>

With Willow established as a veteran, the rule linking veterans to inventiveness yields that Willow is inventive. This deduction completes the left side of the disjunction introduced in PREMISE_4.
<step n="10" type="derived" from="9,2">
<text>Given Willow is a veteran, Willow is inventive.</text>
<logic>inventive(player_willow)</logic>
</step>

We now have Willow as inventive, which makes the statement "Willow is not inventive" false. In the disjunction "not inventive or supportive", the false left side forces the right side to be true, so Willow must be supportive. This is a classic use of disjunctive syllogism.
<step n="11" type="derived" from="10,4">
<text>Because Willow is inventive, the only way for the disjunction to hold is that Willow is supportive.</text>
<logic>supportive(player_willow)</logic>
</step>

Therefore Player_willow is supportive, so the conclusion is True.
</think>
Answer: True

examples.context: Ash is content. Faye is grateful. Iris is strong. Whenever someone is industrious, they are agile. If someone is grateful, then they are analytical. An observant person is always anxious, so any observant individual becomes anxious. If someone is analytical, then they become industrious. Being disciplined guarantees that one is observant. Lack of agility follows from being inventive, so any inventive person is not agile. Either Faye is inventive or Faye is disciplined. Contentness makes a person cautious, therefore ash being content implies ash is cautious. Strongness leads to being rested, so iris being strong means iris is rested. Brave follows from meticulous, thus any meticulous individual is brave; in particular, if iris were meticulous, iris would be brave. Supportiveness follows from being fair‑minded, so a fair‑minded ash would be supportive. Additionally, if someone is cautious, they are not fair‑minded, expressed as "not cautious or fair‑minded" for ash, and "not rested or meticulous" for iris. All these facts and conditional statements constitute the background for the reasoning problem.
examples.question: Is faye anxious?
examples.completion:
<think>
We are given a network of personality‑style properties for three individuals. The goal is to determine whether Faye satisfies the property anxious. To reach this, we will follow the chain of implications supplied in the premises, starting from the facts we know about Faye.

The rule linking industriousness to agility tells us that any person who is industrious must also be agile. This will later allow us to move from Faye's industriousness to her agility once we establish the former.
<step n="1" type="premise">
<text>If someone is industrious, then they are agile; this rule applies to faye.</text>
<logic>agile(faye) :- industrious(faye)</logic>
</step>

The grateful→analytical rule gives us a direct route from the fact that Faye is grateful to the conclusion that she is analytical. This connection is essential because analyticity is the gateway to industriousness in the next steps.
<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>Grateful people are analytical, so faye being grateful makes her analytical.</text>
<logic>analytical(faye) :- grateful(faye)</logic>
</step>

The observant→anxious rule states that observance guarantees anxiety. If we can later prove that Faye is observant, this rule will immediately give us the desired anxious property.
<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>Anyone who is observant becomes anxious; thus observant faye would be anxious.</text>
<logic>anxious(faye) :- observant(faye)</logic>
</step>

Analytical→industrious is another bridge in the chain: once we have shown Faye is analytical, we can deduce she is industrious, which feeds into the agile rule.
<step n="4" type="premise">
<text>Analytical individuals are industrious; therefore faye's analyticity leads to industriousness.</text>
<logic>industrious(faye) :- analytical(faye)</logic>
</step>

The disciplined→observant rule connects discipline to observance. Later, when we establish that Faye is disciplined, this rule will let us infer that she is observant, activating the anxious rule.
<step n="5" type="premise">
<text>If a person is disciplined, they are observant; this connects disciplined faye to observant faye.</text>
<logic>observant(faye) :- disciplined(faye)</logic>
</step>

The inventive→~agile rule provides a way to contradict inventiveness by using agility. If we know Faye is agile, then she cannot be inventive, which will be useful when we consider the disjunction about her being inventive or disciplined.
<step n="6" type="premise">
<text>Being inventive causes a lack of agility; inventive faye would imply not agile faye.</text>
<logic>~agile(faye) :- inventive(faye)</logic>
</step>

The disjunction states that Faye is either inventive or disciplined. This binary choice will be resolved once we eliminate one of the options using other derived information.
<step n="7" type="premise">
<text>Either faye is inventive or faye is disciplined; one of these must hold.</text>
<logic>inventive(faye) v disciplined(faye)</logic>
</step>

The fact that ash is content is included for completeness, though it does not directly affect the anxious query about Faye. It satisfies the requirement to mention all given facts.
<step n="8" type="premise">
<text>Ash is content.</text>
<logic>content(ash)</logic>
</step>

Similarly, iris being strong is recorded as a fact. It does not play a role in the reasoning chain for Faye's anxiety but must be present in the context.
<step n="9" type="premise">
<text>Iris is strong.</text>
<logic>strong(iris)</logic>
</step>

We are explicitly told that Faye is grateful. This fact is the starting point for the analytical inference in STEP_11.
<step n="10" type="premise">
<text>Faye is grateful.</text>
<logic>grateful(faye)</logic>
</step>

Using the grateful→analytical rule together with the fact that Faye is grateful, we can directly assert that Faye is analytical. This is the first concrete transformation from a given fact to a new property.
<step n="11" type="derived" from="10,2">
<text>From faye being grateful and the rule that grateful implies analytical, we infer that faye is analytical.</text>
<logic>analytical(faye)</logic>
</step>

Now that we have established Faye is analytical, the analytical→industrious rule lets us infer that she is industrious. This moves us one step closer to the agile property.
<step n="12" type="derived" from="11,4">
<text>Given faye is analytical and the rule that analytical implies industrious, we deduce that faye is industrious.</text>
<logic>industrious(faye)</logic>
</step>

With Faye confirmed as industrious, the industrious→agile rule yields that she is agile. We now possess the agility fact needed to challenge any possible inventiveness.
<step n="13" type="derived" from="12,1">
<text>Since faye is industrious and industrious leads to agile, we conclude that faye is agile.</text>
<logic>agile(faye)</logic>
</step>

Since we have shown Faye is agile, the inventive→~agile rule tells us that if she were inventive, she would not be agile. Because she is agile, the only consistent conclusion is that she cannot be inventive; thus we derive ~inventive(faye).
<step n="14" type="derived" from="13,6">

The disjunction says Faye is either inventive or disciplined. Having ruled out the inventive alternative, the remaining possibility is that Faye must be disciplined. Therefore we derive disciplined(faye).

Given disciplined(faye) and the disciplined→observant rule, we can now assert that Faye is observant. This satisfies the antecedent of the observant→anxious rule.

Finally, with Faye confirmed as observant, the observant→anxious rule allows us to conclude that Faye is anxious. This completes the chain needed to answer the query.

examples.context: Grace is meticulous. Whenever Grace is not honest, she is strategic. If Grace is honest, then she is not loyal. If Grace is punctual, then she is not strategic. Grace is either not attentive or loyal. Grace is either not meticulous or attentive.
examples.question: Is Grace not punctual?
examples.completion:
<think>
We need to determine whether Grace fails to be punctual. The goal is to show that the statement "Grace is not punctual" follows from the given facts and rules, so we will trace a chain of implications that leads to that conclusion.

The first rule tells us that a lack of honesty guarantees strategic behavior. This will become useful later when we manage to establish that Grace is not honest, allowing us to derive that she is strategic.
<step n="1" type="premise">
<text>If Grace is not honest, then she is strategic.</text>
<logic>strategic(grace) :- ~honest(grace)</logic>
</step>

The second rule links honesty to a lack of loyalty. If we ever find Grace to be loyal, this rule will let us infer that she cannot be honest, which is a key step toward using the first rule.
<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>If Grace is honest, then she is not loyal.</text>
<logic>~loyal(grace) :- honest(grace)</logic>
</step>

The third rule says that punctuality forces Grace to be not strategic. Once we have Grace as strategic, this rule will enable us to conclude that she cannot be punctual, which is exactly what we need.
<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>If Grace is punctual, then she is not strategic.</text>
<logic>~strategic(grace) :- punctual(grace)</logic>
</step>

The disjunction gives us a choice: either Grace is not attentive or she is loyal. This alternative will be resolved once we know something about her attentiveness, helping us move toward loyalty.
<step n="4" type="premise">
<text>Grace is either not attentive or loyal.</text>
<logic>~attentive(grace) v loyal(grace)</logic>
</step>

The second disjunction presents another choice: either Grace is not meticulous or she is attentive. Because we already know she is meticulous, this forces the other option, that she must be attentive.
<step n="5" type="premise">
<text>Grace is either not meticulous or attentive.</text>
<logic>~meticulous(grace) v attentive(grace)</logic>
</step>

The fact states directly that Grace possesses the property of being meticulous. This concrete piece of information will interact with the previous disjunction to produce a new conclusion about attentiveness.
<step n="6" type="premise">
<text>Grace is meticulous.</text>
<logic>meticulous(grace)</logic>
</step>

Since Grace is meticulous, the clause "not meticulous or attentive" cannot be satisfied by the first option; therefore the second option must hold, making Grace attentive. This uses disjunctive syllogism to eliminate the impossible alternative.
<step n="7" type="derived" from="6,5">
<text>From Grace being meticulous and the fact that she is either not meticulous or attentive, we conclude that Grace must be attentive.</text>
<logic>attentive(grace)</logic>
</step>

Now that we have Grace attentive, the earlier disjunction "not attentive or loyal" cannot be satisfied by "not attentive" because she is attentive. Consequently, the remaining alternative must be true, so Grace is loyal. Again, disjunctive syllogism resolves the choice.
<step n="8" type="derived" from="7,4">
<text>Given that Grace is attentive and that she is either not attentive or loyal, we infer that Grace is loyal.</text>
<logic>loyal(grace)</logic>
</step>

We have established that Grace is loyal. The rule "if honest then not loyal" tells us that an honest Grace would contradict her loyalty. Because she is loyal, the antecedent (honest) must be false, leading us to conclude that Grace is not honest. This is a contrapositive use of the rule.
<step n="9" type="derived" from="8,2">
<text>Since Grace is loyal and the rule that if Grace is honest then she is not loyal, we deduce that Grace cannot be honest.</text>
<logic>~honest(grace)</logic>
</step>

Having shown that Grace is not honest, we can now apply the first rule, which says that lack of honesty guarantees strategic behavior. Therefore, Grace must be strategic. This follows directly by modus ponens.
<step n="10" type="derived" from="9,1">
<text>Because Grace is not honest and the rule that not honest implies strategic, we establish that Grace is strategic.</text>
<logic>strategic(grace)</logic>
</step>

We now possess two pieces: Grace is strategic, and the rule that punctuality would make her not strategic. If Grace were punctual, she would have to be not strategic, contradicting the fact that she is strategic. Hence, Grace cannot be punctual. This is a classic modus tollens inference.
<step n="11" type="derived" from="10,3">
<text>With Grace being strategic and the rule that punctualness would make her not strategic, we infer that Grace cannot be punctual.</text>
<logic>~punctual(grace)</logic>
</step>

Therefore Grace is not punctual, so the conclusion is True.
</think>
Answer: True

examples.context: In the philosophical circle, Cameron, Leon, Sofia, and Tanya are being evaluated for various virtues. Cameron is known to be resilient, and Leon shares that resilience. Additionally, any proficient person is courageous, and any capable person is persistent. Two alternatives are asserted: either Tanya is not clear‑headed or she is cheerful, and either Sofia is not persistent or she is proficient. These statements capture all the facts, conditionals, and disjunctions relevant to the analysis.
examples.question: Is Tanya cheerful?

Cameron is explicitly not proactive. This fact will later support the rule that non‑proactive individuals are clear‑headed, though it is not essential for Tanya’s derivation.

The crucial fact for our target is that Tanya herself is resilient. This provides the starting point for the chain of implications that will eventually lead to her being cheerful.

examples.question: Is it true that Nico is not persistent?

<logic>persistent(nico) :- witty(nico)</logic>
</step>

The disjunction gives us a logical choice: either Nico lacks evidence‑driven status or he is witty. It ties the property of being witty to the negation of evidence‑driven, allowing us to use the known fact about evidence‑driven to infer wit.
<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>The disjunction states that either Nico is not evidence-driven or Nico is witty.</text>
<logic>~evidence_driven(nico) v witty(nico)</logic>
</step>

<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>We are told directly that Nico is evidence-driven.</text>
<logic>evidence_driven(nico)</logic>
</step>

We know from the fact that Nico is evidence‑driven, which makes the left side of the disjunction (~evidence_driven) false. For the disjunction to remain true, the right side must be true, so Nico must be witty. This is a classic use of disjunctive syllogism.
<step n="4" type="derived" from="3,2">
<text>Because Nico is evidence-driven, the first part of the disjunction (~evidence_driven) is false, so the only way for the disjunction to hold is that Nico is witty.</text>
<logic>witty(nico)</logic>
</step>

Having obtained that Nico is witty, we can now apply the rule from PREMISE_1: wit leads to persistence. Thus, Nico inherits the persistent property directly from his wit, establishing his persistence.
<step n="5" type="derived" from="4,1">
<text>Since we have established that Nico is witty and the rule links wit to persistence, we can conclude that Nico is persistent.</text>
<logic>persistent(nico)</logic>
</step>

We derived Nico is persistent, so the conclusion Nico is not persistent is False.
</think>
Answer: False

examples.context: In the library system, the grant record is noted as active. The lake entry is described as logical and it has also been verified. Whenever a record is verified, it is automatically accepted, so the lake becomes accepted because it is verified. If something is confident, then it is brave, and any active grant is enthusiastic. Being rested guarantees responsibility, meaning that if the lake were rested it would be responsible. The knowledge base also contains a disjunction: either the grant is not enthusiastic or the grant is confident. By default, if the lake is accepted and there is no abnormality called ab_brilliant, then the lake is considered brilliant. Furthermore, if the lake is brilliant and there is no abnormality called ab_rested_a, then the lake is rested. On the other hand, if the lake is logical and there is no abnormality called ab_rested_b, then the lake is not rested. All these statements together constitute the full set of facts, rules, disjunctions, and default rules for the problem.
examples.question: Based on the given information, is the lake responsible?
examples.completion:
<think>
We are asked to determine whether the lake holds the property of responsibility. The goal is to trace the chain of rules and defaults that could give the lake this attribute.

<step n="1" type="premise">
<text>The rule states that any lake that is verified is also accepted.</text>
<logic>accepted(lake) :- verified(lake)</logic>
</step>

<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>The rule says that if something is rested then it is responsible, so a rested lake would be responsible.</text>
<logic>responsible(lake) :- rested(lake)</logic>
</step>

<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>The default rule asserts that a lake becomes brilliant when it is accepted and there is no abnormality ab_brilliant.</text>
<logic>brilliant(lake) :- accepted(lake), not ab_brilliant(lake)</logic>
</step>

<step n="4" type="premise">
<text>According to another default rule, a lake becomes rested when it is brilliant and there is no abnormality ab_rested_a.</text>
<logic>rested(lake) :- brilliant(lake), not ab_rested_a(lake)</logic>
</step>

<step n="5" type="premise">
<text>A competing default rule claims that a lake is not rested whenever it is logical and there is no abnormality ab_rested_b.</text>
<logic>~rested(lake) :- logical(lake), not ab_rested_b(lake)</logic>
</step>

<step n="6" type="premise">
<text>It is given as a fact that the grant is active.</text>
<logic>active(grant)</logic>
</step>

<step n="7" type="premise">
<text>It is given as a fact that the lake is logical.</text>
<logic>logical(lake)</logic>
</step>

<step n="8" type="premise">
<text>It is given as a fact that the lake is verified.</text>
<logic>verified(lake)</logic>
</step>

The fact that the lake has been verified directly triggers the conditional rule that any verified lake is accepted. By applying this implication, we can safely add acceptance to the lake's profile. This new fact will be useful for the subsequent default reasoning steps.
<step n="9" type="derived" from="8,1">
<text>From the fact that the lake is verified and the rule linking verification to acceptance, we infer that the lake is accepted.</text>
<logic>accepted(lake)</logic>
</step>

Having established that the lake is accepted, we look at the default rule concerning brilliance. The rule requires acceptance and the absence of an abnormality labeled ab_brilliant. Because the problem statement does not mention such an abnormality, we can assume it is not present, allowing us to infer that the lake is brilliant.
<step n="10" type="derived" from="9,3">
<text>Using the default rule, the newly established acceptance of the lake (and assuming no abnormality ab_brilliant) lets us conclude that the lake is brilliant.</text>
<logic>brilliant(lake)</logic>
</step>

Now that the lake is brilliant, the next default rule tells us that a brilliant lake becomes rested, provided the abnormality ab_rested_a does not hold. Again, there is no mention of this abnormality, so we can conclude the lake is rested. At the same time, another default would make the lake not rested because it is logical, but the reasoning framework gives priority to the rested conclusion when both defaults are applicable, so we keep the rested result.
<step n="11" type="derived" from="10,4,5">
<text>Because the lake is now brilliant, the default rule about rested applies; with no abnormality ab_rested_a, we derive that the lake is rested, and this conclusion outweighs the competing default that would make it not rested.</text>
<logic>rested(lake)</logic>
</step>

With the lake confirmed as rested, we can apply the responsibility rule that any rested entity is responsible. This straightforward implication yields that the lake is indeed responsible, fulfilling the chain of inference we needed.
<step n="12" type="derived" from="11,2">
<text>Since the lake is rested, the responsibility rule tells us that the lake is responsible.</text>
<logic>responsible(lake)</logic>
</step>

Therefore Lake is responsible, so the conclusion is True.
</think>
Answer: True

examples.context: Archaeologists have determined that the bronze lance is well preserved. In this study, any artifact that is well preserved is considered to have an innovative design. Moreover, whenever an artifact possesses an innovative design, it is regarded as tactically shaped for use in battle. These statements together provide the logical background for the analysis.
examples.question: Based on the information, can we say that the bronze lance is not tactically shaped?
examples.completion:
<think>
We are asked to evaluate whether the bronze lance can be said to be not tactically shaped. To answer this, we need to trace the logical consequences of the given facts and rules and see if the negation can hold.

The first rule connects the property of being well preserved to having an innovative design. Recognizing this link is essential because it allows us to move from the known fact about preservation to a new property that will be used later in the chain.
<step n="1" type="premise">
<text>The rule states that if the bronze lance is well preserved, then it has an innovative design.</text>
<logic>innovative_design(bronze_lance) :- well_preserved(bronze_lance)</logic>
</step>

The second rule links an innovative design to being tactically shaped. This conditional is crucial because once we establish the lance has an innovative design, we can immediately derive that it is tactically shaped, directly addressing the query.
<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>The rule states that if the bronze lance has an innovative design, then it is tactically shaped.</text>
<logic>tactically_shaped(bronze_lance) :- innovative_design(bronze_lance)</logic>
</step>

The fact that the bronze lance is well preserved gives us a concrete starting point. Without this factual anchor, the conditional rules would have no premise to activate, leaving the argument stalled.
<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>It is a fact that the bronze lance is well preserved.</text>
<logic>well_preserved(bronze_lance)</logic>
</step>

Applying modus ponens, we combine the fact that the lance is well preserved with the first rule, which tells us that any well‑preserved artifact is innovatively designed. Hence we can confidently assert that the lance possesses an innovative design.
<step n="4" type="derived" from="3,1">
<text>From the fact that the lance is well preserved and the first rule, we infer that the lance has an innovative design.</text>
<logic>innovative_design(bronze_lance)</logic>
</step>

Now that we have the innovative design property, we again use modus ponens with the second rule. This rule guarantees that any artifact with an innovative design is tactically shaped, so we conclude that the lance is indeed tactically shaped.
<step n="5" type="derived" from="4,2">
<text>Using the newly derived innovative design and the second rule, we conclude that the lance is tactically shaped.</text>
<logic>tactically_shaped(bronze_lance)</logic>
</step>

We derived Bronze_lance is tactically shaped, so the conclusion Bronze_lance is not tactically shaped is False.
</think>
Answer: False

examples.context: In the workshop, George, Seth, Walter and Yuri are all described as industrious. Specifically, George is industrious, Seth is industrious, Walter is industrious, and Yuri is industrious. The engineering guidelines state that whenever Yuri is industrious, he is also prepared. Moreover, the guidelines add that any time Yuri is prepared, he becomes motivated. Finally, the safety protocol says that if Yuri is attentive, then he cannot be motivated. No other facts or defaults are given.
examples.question: Is Yuri attentive?
examples.completion:
<think>
We are asked to decide whether Yuri possesses the property of attentiveness. Our knowledge base contains several facts about who is industrious and three conditional rules linking industriousness, preparation, motivation, and attentiveness for Yuri. The goal is to see if the information forces Yuri to be attentive or its opposite.

The first rule tells us that preparation leads to motivation for Yuri. This connection will let us move from a state of being prepared to a state of being motivated later in the proof. It is essential because motivation is the predicate that appears in the third rule.
<step n="1" type="premise">
<text>The rule says that if Yuri is prepared then Yuri is motivated.</text>
<logic>motivated(yuri) :- prepared(yuri)</logic>
</step>

The second rule links Yuri's industriousness to his preparedness. Since we already know Yuri is industrious, this rule will allow us to derive that he is prepared, which is a stepping stone toward establishing motivation.
<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>The rule says that if Yuri is industrious then Yuri is prepared.</text>
<logic>prepared(yuri) :- industrious(yuri)</logic>
</step>

The third rule provides a contrapositive relationship: if Yuri were attentive, he could not be motivated. This will be crucial for applying modus tollens once we have established that Yuri is indeed motivated.
<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>The rule says that if Yuri is attentive then Yuri is not motivated.</text>
<logic>~motivated(yuri) :- attentive(yuri)</logic>
</step>

George being industrious is recorded, but it does not directly affect Yuri's status. It is part of the broader fact list that the context mentions.
<step n="4" type="premise">
<text>George is industrious.</text>
<logic>industrious(george)</logic>
</step>

Seth being industrious is also noted among the facts. Like George's fact, it adds completeness to the description of the workshop team.
<step n="5" type="premise">
<text>Seth is industrious.</text>
<logic>industrious(seth)</logic>
</step>

Walter is industrious, completing the enumeration of all four individuals as industrious. These facts collectively illustrate the environment but are not used in the later derivations concerning Yuri.
<step n="6" type="premise">
<text>Walter is industrious.</text>
<logic>industrious(walter)</logic>
</step>

Yuri is industrious, which is the key fact that will trigger the rule about preparation. This fact anchors the chain of reasoning that leads to conclusions about his motivation and attentiveness.
<step n="7" type="premise">
<text>Yuri is industrious.</text>
<logic>industrious(yuri)</logic>
</step>

Given Yuri's industriousness and the rule that industriousness implies preparedness for Yuri, we can infer that Yuri is prepared. This step uses a straightforward application of the conditional rule, moving from the known antecedent to the consequent.
<step n="8" type="derived" from="7,2">
<text>From Yuri being industrious and the rule that industrious implies prepared, we infer that Yuri is prepared.</text>
<logic>prepared(yuri)</logic>
</step>

Now that we have established Yuri is prepared, we invoke the first rule, which says prepared implies motivated. By applying modus ponens, we deduce that Yuri is motivated. This new fact will be pivotal for the next step involving attentiveness.
<step n="9" type="derived" from="8,1">
<text>Since Yuri is prepared and the rule says prepared implies motivated, we conclude that Yuri is motivated.</text>
<logic>motivated(yuri)</logic>
</step>

We have Yuri motivated, and the third rule tells us that if Yuri were attentive, he would not be motivated. Since we have the opposite—Yuri is motivated—the only way to avoid a contradiction is to conclude that Yuri cannot be attentive. This is an application of modus tollens, deriving the negation of the antecedent.
<step n="10" type="derived" from="9,3">
<text>Because Yuri is motivated and the rule states that attentiveness would make him not motivated, we deduce that Yuri cannot be attentive.</text>
<logic>~attentive(yuri)</logic>
</step>

We derived Yuri is not attentive, so the conclusion Yuri is attentive is False.
</think>
Answer: False

examples.context: Plato is empathetic. Socrates is encouraging. Aristotle is coherent. If Aristotle is resolute, then he is satisfied. If Aristotle is devoted, then he is resolute. If Aristotle is coherent, then he is receptive. If Aristotle is satisfied, then he is tolerant. If Aristotle is tolerant, then he is calm. If Socrates is encouraging, then Socrates is structured. If Plato is empathetic, then Plato is sound. If Plato is tenacious, then Plato is systematic. Either Aristotle is not receptive or he is devoted. Either Plato is not sound or Plato is tenacious. Either Socrates is not structured or Socrates is thoughtful.
examples.question: Is Aristotle not calm?
examples.completion:
<think>
We are asked whether Aristotle is not calm. To answer this, we must trace the chain of philosophical traits that lead from the facts we know about Aristotle to his state of calmness.

The rule linking resoluteness to satisfaction is crucial because it allows us to move from a stronger trait (being resolute) to a more observable condition (being satisfied). This connection will later help us reach calmness.
<step n="1" type="premise">
<text>If Aristotle is resolute, then he is satisfied.</text>
<logic>satisfied(aristotle) :- resolute(aristotle)</logic>
</step>

Knowing that devotion entails resoluteness gives us a way to derive resoluteness once we have established devotion. It sets up a forward step in the reasoning chain.
<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>If Aristotle is devoted, then he is resolute.</text>
<logic>resolute(aristotle) :- devoted(aristotle)</logic>
</step>

The implication from coherence to receptiveness connects the factual statement that Aristotle is coherent to a new property, receptiveness, which will be needed to activate the disjunctive premise.
<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>If Aristotle is coherent, then he is receptive.</text>
<logic>receptive(aristotle) :- coherent(aristotle)</logic>
</step>

The link from satisfaction to tolerance is another link in the chain that will eventually bring us to calmness. It shows how emotional contentment translates into patience.
<step n="4" type="premise">
<text>If Aristotle is satisfied, then he is tolerant.</text>
<logic>tolerant(aristotle) :- satisfied(aristotle)</logic>
</step>

Finally, tolerance leading to calmness closes the chain: once we have tolerance, we can directly infer calmness, which is the property under question.
<step n="5" type="premise">
<text>If Aristotle is tolerant, then he is calm.</text>
<logic>calm(aristotle) :- tolerant(aristotle)</logic>
</step>

The disjunction offers two alternatives: either Aristotle lacks receptiveness or he is devoted. This will allow us to use the fact that he is receptive to force the second alternative, giving us devotion.
<step n="6" type="premise">
<text>Either Aristotle is not receptive or he is devoted.</text>
<logic>~receptive(aristotle) v devoted(aristotle)</logic>
</step>

Plato's empathy is listed for completeness, though it does not directly affect Aristotle's calmness. It demonstrates the broader network of traits in the scenario.
<step n="7" type="premise">
<text>Plato is empathetic.</text>
<logic>empathetic(plato)</logic>
</step>

Socrates' encouragement similarly provides background information about another character, but it does not play a role in the derivation concerning Aristotle.
<step n="8" type="premise">
<text>Socrates is encouraging.</text>
<logic>encouraging(socrates)</logic>
</step>

The fact that Aristotle is coherent is essential because it activates the rule that makes him receptive, which then interacts with the disjunction to produce devotion.
<step n="9" type="premise">
<text>Aristotle is coherent.</text>
<logic>coherent(aristotle)</logic>
</step>

We combine the fact that Aristotle is coherent (Premise 9) with the rule that coherence implies receptiveness (Premise 3). By applying this conditional, we obtain that Aristotle is receptive, a necessary step for the next inference.
<step n="10" type="derived" from="9,3">
<text>Since Aristotle is coherent and coherence implies receptiveness, we infer that Aristotle is receptive.</text>
<logic>receptive(aristotle)</logic>
</step>

Now that we have established Aristotle is receptive, the first part of the disjunction (~receptive) is false. Therefore, to satisfy the disjunction, the second alternative must hold, giving us that Aristotle is devoted. This is a classic use of disjunctive reasoning.
<step n="11" type="derived" from="10,6">
<text>Given that Aristotle is receptive, the alternative 'Aristotle is not receptive' is false, so from the statement that either he is not receptive or devoted, we conclude he is devoted.</text>
<logic>devoted(aristotle)</logic>
</step>

Having derived that Aristotle is devoted, we can invoke the rule that devotion leads to resoluteness (Premise 2). This yields that Aristotle is resolute, moving us further along the chain toward calmness.
<step n="12" type="derived" from="11,2">
<text>Because Aristotle is devoted, and devotion leads to resoluteness, we infer he is resolute.</text>
<logic>resolute(aristotle)</logic>
</step>

With resoluteness in hand, the rule linking resoluteness to satisfaction (Premise 1) lets us infer that Aristotle is satisfied. This bridges the gap between internal resolve and a more outward emotional state.
<step n="13" type="derived" from="12,1">
<text>Having established Aristotle is resolute, and resoluteness leads to satisfaction, we infer he is satisfied.</text>
<logic>satisfied(aristotle)</logic>
</step>

Since Aristotle is now known to be satisfied, we apply the rule that satisfaction implies tolerance (Premise 4). This gives us that Aristotle is tolerant, bringing us one step closer to calmness.
<step n="14" type="derived" from="13,4">
<text>Since Aristotle is satisfied, and satisfaction implies tolerance, we infer he is tolerant.</text>
<logic>tolerant(aristotle)</logic>
</step>

Finally, tolerance triggers calmness via the last rule (Premise 5). Thus we conclude that Aristotle is calm, directly addressing the query about his lack of calmness.
<step n="15" type="derived" from="14,5">
<text>Because Aristotle is tolerant, and tolerance leads to calmness, we infer he is calm.</text>
<logic>calm(aristotle)</logic>
</step>

We derived Aristotle is calm, so the conclusion Aristotle is not calm is False.
</think>
Answer: False

No other alternatives or disjunctions are mentioned.
examples.question: Is Ralph compliant?
examples.completion:
<think>
We need to decide whether Ralph satisfies the property of being compliant. The problem provides several facts, rules, and default rules that interact, so we must follow the logical chain to reach a conclusion.

This premise links cheerfulness to bravery, which will be essential later because compliance depends on bravery. Recognising this connection early helps us see how establishing cheerfulness can eventually give us compliance.

Compliance is derived from bravery via another default rule, provided no abnormality (ab_compliant) blocks it. This premise sets the final step we need once we have bravery.
<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>Typically, if Ralph is brave and there is no abnormality preventing compliance, then Ralph is compliant.</text>
<logic>compliant(ralph) :- brave(ralph), not ab_compliant(ralph)</logic>
</step>

This default produces a potential conflict: athleticness can make Ralph not cheerful unless an abnormality (ab_cheerful_b) interferes. We must compare this with the cheerful default to see which one prevails.
<step n="4" type="premise">
<text>Usually, if Ralph is athletic and there is no abnormality b, then Ralph is not cheerful.</text>

The fact that Ralph is athletic activates the default that could make him not cheerful. It is a crucial piece because it introduces the competing conclusion of ~cheerful.
<step n="5" type="premise">

</step>

The fact that Ralph is meticulous activates the cheerful default. Together with the athletic fact, we now have two competing defaults that need resolution.
<step n="6" type="premise">
<text>Ralph is meticulous.</text>
<logic>meticulous(ralph)</logic>
</step>

Both defaults are applicable: the meticulous fact tries to make Ralph cheerful, while the athletic fact tries to make him not cheerful. In the given framework, the cheerful default has priority over the negating default, so we adopt cheerful(Ralph) and discard ~cheerful(Ralph). This gives us a concrete instance of cheerfulness for Ralph.
<step n="7" type="derived" from="6,2,4">
<text>Because Ralph is meticulous and no abnormality a blocks cheerfulness, the default makes him cheerful, and this overrides the competing default that would make him not cheerful due to his athleticism.</text>
<logic>cheerful(ralph)</logic>
</step>

Having established that Ralph is cheerful, we can now use the rule from PREMISE_1 that cheerfulness implies bravery. By applying this rule, we conclude that Ralph is brave, which is the needed antecedent for the compliance default.
<step n="8" type="derived" from="7,1">
<text>Since Ralph is cheerful, the rule that cheerfulness implies bravery lets us infer that Ralph is brave.</text>
<logic>brave(ralph)</logic>

Now that Ralph is brave, the default rule for compliance (PREMISE_3) applies, assuming no abnormality blocks it. Since no abnormality for compliance is mentioned, we can safely infer that Ralph is compliant.
<step n="9" type="derived" from="8,3">
<text>Given Ralph's bravery and no abnormality preventing compliance, the default rule yields that Ralph is compliant.</text>
<logic>compliant(ralph)</logic>
</step>

Therefore Ralph is compliant, so the conclusion is True.
