examples.context: The test_vehicle is reliable. Any engineered test_vehicle is well_balanced. Any safe test_vehicle is robust. Whenever a test_vehicle is timely, it is not well_balanced. The test_vehicle is either timely or accurate. Either the test_vehicle is not reliable or it is safe. Either the test_vehicle is not robust or it is engineered.
examples.question: Is the test_vehicle not accurate?
examples.completion:
<think>
We are asked to decide whether the test_vehicle fails to be accurate. To answer this, we must trace the logical consequences of the given facts, rules, and disjunctions until we can determine the truth value of the predicate accurate for the test_vehicle.

The rule linking engineered to well_balanced tells us that any vehicle built with engineering quality automatically satisfies the well_balanced property. This will become useful once we establish that the test_vehicle is engineered later in the proof.
<step n="1" type="premise">
<text>If the test_vehicle is engineered, then it is well_balanced.</text>
<logic>well_balanced(test_vehicle) :- engineered(test_vehicle)</logic>
</step>

The rule that safety leads to robustness provides a direct way to obtain robustness once we know the vehicle is safe. Since safety appears in one of the disjunctions, this rule will help us move from safety to a concrete property.
<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>If the test_vehicle is safe, then it is robust.</text>
<logic>robust(test_vehicle) :- safe(test_vehicle)</logic>
</step>

The rule connecting timeliness to the negation of well_balanced indicates that if the vehicle were timely, it could not be well_balanced. This contrapositive relationship will allow us to eliminate timeliness once we have well_balanced.
<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>If the test_vehicle is timely, then it is not well_balanced.</text>
<logic>~well_balanced(test_vehicle) :- timely(test_vehicle)</logic>
</step>

The disjunction stating the vehicle is either timely or accurate gives us a binary choice: at least one of these two properties holds. When we later rule out timeliness, we will be forced to accept accuracy.
<step n="4" type="premise">
<text>The test_vehicle is either timely or accurate.</text>
<logic>timely(test_vehicle) v accurate(test_vehicle)</logic>
</step>

The disjunction that the vehicle is either not reliable or safe links the known reliability fact to safety. By combining this with the fact that the vehicle is reliable, we can derive safety through disjunctive syllogism.
<step n="5" type="premise">
<text>Either the test_vehicle is not reliable or it is safe.</text>
<logic>~reliable(test_vehicle) v safe(test_vehicle)</logic>
</step>

The disjunction that the vehicle is either not robust or engineered connects robustness to engineering. Once we have robustness, we can apply this disjunction to obtain engineering via disjunctive syllogism.
<step n="6" type="premise">
<text>Either the test_vehicle is not robust or it is engineered.</text>
<logic>~robust(test_vehicle) v engineered(test_vehicle)</logic>
</step>

The explicit fact that the test_vehicle is reliable serves as a cornerstone for the first disjunctive inference. Knowing the vehicle is reliable allows us to eliminate the negated alternative in the relevant disjunction.
<step n="7" type="premise">
<text>The test_vehicle is reliable.</text>
<logic>reliable(test_vehicle)</logic>
</step>

We start with the reliable fact and the disjunction that the vehicle is either not reliable or safe. Since the vehicle is reliable, the 'not reliable' option is impossible, leaving safety as the only viable alternative. Thus, we conclude the vehicle is safe.
<step n="8" type="derived" from="7,5">
<text>From reliability and the disjunction that the vehicle is either not reliable or safe, we infer that the vehicle must be safe.</text>
<logic>safe(test_vehicle)</logic>
</step>

Having established safety, we invoke the rule that safety guarantees robustness. This direct implication lets us infer that the vehicle is robust without any further assumptions.
<step n="9" type="derived" from="8,2">
<text>Because the vehicle is safe and safety guarantees robustness, we conclude the vehicle is robust.</text>
<logic>robust(test_vehicle)</logic>
</step>

Now that robustness is known, we turn to the disjunction that the vehicle is either not robust or engineered. Because the vehicle is robust, the 'not robust' side cannot hold, so the engineered alternative must be true. Hence, the vehicle is engineered.
<step n="10" type="derived" from="9,6">
<text>Given the vehicle is robust and the disjunction that it is either not robust or engineered, we deduce it is engineered.</text>
<logic>engineered(test_vehicle)</logic>
</step>

With engineering confirmed, we apply the earlier rule that engineering leads to being well_balanced. This yields the well_balanced property for the vehicle, bridging the gap between engineering and the later timeliness rule.
<step n="11" type="derived" from="10,1">
<text>Since the vehicle is engineered and engineered implies well_balanced, we obtain that it is well_balanced.</text>
<logic>well_balanced(test_vehicle)</logic>
</step>

We possess the well_balanced status and also the rule that timeliness would cause a vehicle to lose the well_balanced property. Since the vehicle is indeed well_balanced, it cannot be timely; otherwise a contradiction would arise. Therefore, we deduce the vehicle is not timely.
<step n="12" type="derived" from="11,3">
<text>From the well_balanced status and the rule that timeliness would make it not well_balanced, we infer that the vehicle cannot be timely.</text>
<logic>~timely(test_vehicle)</logic>
</step>

Finally, we revisit the disjunction that the vehicle is either timely or accurate. Because we have just shown the vehicle is not timely, the only remaining possibility is that the vehicle is accurate. This gives us the desired accuracy conclusion.
<step n="13" type="derived" from="12,4">
<text>With the knowledge that the vehicle is not timely and the earlier alternative that it is either timely or accurate, we determine it must be accurate.</text>
<logic>accurate(test_vehicle)</logic>
</step>

We derived Test_vehicle is accurate, so the conclusion Test_vehicle is not accurate is False.
</think>
Answer: False

No other default assumptions are made.

The fact that Hollis is loyal is a concrete piece of information. examples.context: Maria is creative, and Rex is dedicated. Whenever someone is creative, that person is also skillful, so Maria being creative makes her skillful. If someone is energetic, then that person is talented, which gives a rule linking energetic(Maria) to talented(Maria). In the garden club, defaults operate as follows: Normally, if Rex is normal and there is no abnormal reason (ab_diplomatic), then Rex is diplomatic. Also, normally, if Rex is diplomatic and there is no abnormal reason (ab_enrolled), then Rex is enrolled. Additionally, normally, if Rex is dedicated and there is no abnormal reason (ab_normal), then Rex is normal. Finally, normally, if Rex is enrolled and there is no abnormal reason (ab_recognized), then Rex is recognized. No abnormality facts are mentioned, so the defaults can be applied freely.
examples.question: Is Rex recognized in the garden club?

The diplomatic default connects normality to diplomacy, which will be useful later because we can obtain normality from Rex's dedication. The normality default is crucial because it turns the concrete fact dedicated(Rex) into normal(Rex). Without this bridge, we could not activate the diplomatic default. This rule therefore starts the chain that will eventually produce recognized(Rex).
<step n="3" type="premise">

The recognition default tells us that once Rex is enrolled, the desired conclusion follows, provided no abnormality blocks it. This is the last link in the chain, so keeping it in mind clarifies how the earlier steps will culminate in the goal.

The fact that Maria is creative is unrelated to Rex's recognition, but the context requires us to acknowledge it. It also participates in other rules about skillfulness, showing that the knowledge base is consistent.

<text>A basic fact tells us that Maria is creative.</text>
<logic>creative(maria)</logic>
</step>

The fact dedicated(Rex) is the seed of the reasoning process for Rex. It directly triggers the normality default, allowing us to move from a concrete property to a series of defaults that will eventually yield recognition.
<step n="6" type="premise">

Starting with dedicated(Rex) and applying the normality default (since no abnormality is present), we infer normal(Rex). The structured‑to‑not‑courteous rule will be the final piece that lets us turn a courteous conclusion into a negated structured claim. It is the crucial step for reaching the query.

The disjunction "not hopeful or appreciative" gives us a logical alternative involving Sam's hopefulness and appreciation. Once we have hopefulness, this clause will let us infer appreciation.

The disjunction "not initiative or effective" provides a way to move from initiative to effectiveness. When we later secure initiative, this statement will force the conclusion of effectiveness.

Sam's cleverness is the starting point for the chain of deductions. Knowing Sam is clever allows us to activate the optimistic (hopeful) rule immediately.

From Sam's cleverness we apply the rule that clever implies hopeful, giving us hopeful(sam). This new fact is required for the upcoming disjunctive reasoning step.

Now that we have appreciative(sam), we look at the rule that skilled implies not appreciative. Since appreciative is true, the antecedent (skilled) cannot hold, allowing us to conclude not skilled(sam) by modus tollens.

With not skilled(sam) established, we invoke the rule that not skilled leads to initiative. This gives us initiative(sam), moving us forward toward effectiveness.

The disjunction "not initiative or effective" together with initiative(sam) eliminates the first option, forcing the second. Thus we obtain effective(sam).

Finally, we have courteous(sam) and the rule that structured implies not courteous. By contraposition (modus tollens), the presence of courteous forces the negation of structured, giving us not structured(sam).

examples.context: In the cosmic survey, the red dwarf named kurt shines brightly, so kurt is radiant. The planet called nadia also shines brightly, making nadia radiant. The comet sofia travels along a regular path, so sofia is orbiting. Whenever something is radiant, it is also massive for the planet nadia: any radiant planet nadia is massive. Whenever something is orbiting, it is also spectral for the planet nadia: any orbiting planet nadia is spectral. Whenever something is massive, it becomes orbiting for the planet nadia: any massive planet nadia is orbiting.
examples.question: Is planet_nadia spectral?
examples.completion:
<think>
We are asked to determine whether the planet named nadia possesses the spectral property. Our goal is to trace a chain of astronomical attributes that leads from known facts to the spectral status of nadia.

The first rule tells us that any radiant entity, specifically the planet nadia, must also be massive. This connection will allow us to move from radiance to mass, which is a crucial step in the chain.
<step n="1" type="premise">
<text>Any object that is radiant is also massive for the planet nadia.</text>
<logic>massive(planet_nadia) :- radiant(planet_nadia)</logic>
</step>

The second rule establishes that any object that is orbiting automatically gains the spectral attribute. This will be the final link we need once we can show that nadia is orbiting.
<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>Any object that is orbiting is also spectral for the planet nadia.</text>
<logic>spectral(planet_nadia) :- orbiting(planet_nadia)</logic>
</step>

The third rule bridges mass and orbit: anything that is massive becomes orbiting. Consequently, if we can prove nadia is massive, we can immediately infer that she is orbiting.
<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>Any object that is massive becomes orbiting for the planet nadia.</text>
<logic>orbiting(planet_nadia) :- massive(planet_nadia)</logic>
</step>

The survey notes that the red dwarf kurt is radiant, giving us a concrete example of a radiant celestial body. While this fact does not directly involve nadia, it demonstrates the presence of radiant objects in our universe.
<step n="4" type="premise">
<text>The red dwarf kurt is radiant.</text>
<logic>radiant(red_dwarf_kurt)</logic>
</step>

Similarly, the comet sofia is reported as radiant, reinforcing the prevalence of radiance among the objects we are considering. These facts help confirm the consistency of the described properties.
<step n="5" type="premise">
<text>The comet sofia is radiant.</text>
<logic>radiant(comet_sofia)</logic>
</step>

The red dwarf kurt follows a stable orbit, so kurt is orbiting. This fact shows that orbiting objects exist, supporting the relevance of the orbiting‑to‑spectral rule.
<step n="6" type="premise">
<text>The red dwarf kurt is orbiting.</text>
<logic>orbiting(red_dwarf_kurt)</logic>
</step>

The comet sofia also travels along a regular path, making sofia orbiting. Together with the previous fact, this confirms that orbiting is a recognized attribute in our context.
<step n="7" type="premise">
<text>The comet sofia is orbiting.</text>
<logic>orbiting(comet_sofia)</logic>
</step>

Crucially, the planet nadia is explicitly described as radiant. This is the starting point for applying the rule that radiant implies massive.
<step n="8" type="premise">
<text>The planet nadia is radiant.</text>
<logic>radiant(planet_nadia)</logic>
</step>

Given that nadia is radiant and the rule that any radiant thing is massive, we can conclude that nadia is massive. This step uses a straightforward implication: radiance leads directly to mass for nadia.
<step n="9" type="derived" from="8,1">
<text>From nadia being radiant and the rule that radiant implies massive, we infer that nadia is massive.</text>
<logic>massive(planet_nadia)</logic>
</step>

Having established nadia's massiveness, we invoke the rule that massive objects become orbiting. Therefore, nadia must be orbiting. This bridges the gap between mass and the orbital property.
<step n="10" type="derived" from="9,3">
<text>Since nadia is massive and the rule that massive implies orbiting, we conclude that nadia is orbiting.</text>
<logic>orbiting(planet_nadia)</logic>
</step>

Now that we know nadia is orbiting, we apply the rule that any orbiting entity is spectral. Consequently, nadia acquires the spectral attribute, fulfilling the condition we set out to prove.
<step n="11" type="derived" from="10,2">
<text>Because nadia is orbiting and the rule that orbiting implies spectral, we deduce that nadia is spectral.</text>

Therefore Planet_nadia is spectral, so the conclusion is True.
</think>

examples.context: Zara is dedicated, Justice is meticulous, and Ash is reflective. Whenever something is meticulous, it is also active, so Justice being meticulous makes her active. If something is clever, then it is alert, and if something is resourceful, it is clever; thus resourcefulness guarantees cleverness and alertness. Consistency follows from reflectiveness, so Ash being reflective means Ash is consistent. Persistence leads to energetic, but that rule is not needed here. Alertness guarantees reliability, so any alert entity is reliable. Thoroughness implies studiousness for Ash, though that fact does not play a role later. Productive things are not curious, and vigorous things are not evidence-driven. Either Zara is productive or Zara is resourceful. Either Zara is not dedicated or Zara is curious. Either Zara is not reliable or Zara is evidence-driven. These are all the explicit facts, conditionals, and alternatives that the reasoning will use.
examples.question: Is Zara vigorous?
examples.completion:
<think>
We are asked to decide whether Zara possesses the property vigorous given a collection of facts, conditionals, and alternatives. The goal is to see if the premise "vigorous(zara)" can be supported or contradicted by the supplied information.

This rule links cleverness to alertness for Zara, which will become crucial once we establish her cleverness later in the proof. It tells us that proving Zara is clever automatically gives us her alert status.
<step n="1" type="premise">
<text>If Zara is clever, then she is alert.</text>
<logic>alert(zara) :- clever(zara)</logic>
</step>

Here we have the bridge from resourcefulness to cleverness. If we can show Zara is resourceful, this rule will let us conclude she is clever, moving us forward toward alertness and reliability.
<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>If Zara is resourceful, then she is clever.</text>
<logic>clever(zara) :- resourceful(zara)</logic>
</step>

The rule states that alertness guarantees reliability. Once we have alertness for Zara, we can immediately infer she is reliable, which will later interact with a disjunction about reliability and evidence-drivenness.
<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>If Zara is alert, then she is reliable.</text>
<logic>reliable(zara) :- alert(zara)</logic>
</step>

This conditional tells us that productivity forces the negation of curiosity. It will be used in a contrapositive fashion: if we discover Zara is curious, we can deny her productivity.
<step n="4" type="premise">
<text>If Zara is productive, then she is not curious.</text>
<logic>~curious(zara) :- productive(zara)</logic>
</step>

This is the only rule that directly mentions vigor: it says that if Zara were vigorous, she could not be evidence-driven. Later we will obtain evidence-drivenness, allowing us to refute vigor via contrapositive reasoning.
<step n="5" type="premise">
<text>If Zara is vigorous, then she is not evidence-driven.</text>
<logic>~evidence_driven(zara) :- vigorous(zara)</logic>
</step>

The disjunction offers two possibilities for Zara: she is either productive or resourceful. By eliminating one option, we can force the other, which is essential for obtaining resourcefulness and then cleverness.
<step n="6" type="premise">
<text>Either Zara is productive or Zara is resourceful.</text>
<logic>productive(zara) v resourceful(zara)</logic>
</step>

This alternative gives a way to derive curiosity from Zara's dedication. Since we know she is dedicated, the only way for the disjunction to hold is for Zara to be curious, which will later help us rule out productivity.
<step n="7" type="premise">
<text>Either Zara is not dedicated or Zara is curious.</text>
<logic>~dedicated(zara) v curious(zara)</logic>
</step>

This disjunction connects reliability and evidence-drivenness. Once we have reliability, the only way for the statement to be true is for Zara to be evidence-driven, setting up a conflict with the vigor rule later.
<step n="8" type="premise">
<text>Either Zara is not reliable or Zara is evidence-driven.</text>
<logic>~reliable(zara) v evidence_driven(zara)</logic>
</step>

Justice being meticulous is a factual statement that will later allow us to infer her activity, though that chain is not needed for the final conclusion about Zara. It demonstrates how facts can trigger conditionals.
<step n="9" type="premise">
<text>Justice is meticulous.</text>
<logic>meticulous(justice)</logic>
</step>

Ash's reflectiveness is recorded as a fact; it would make Ash consistent via another rule, but this does not impact Zara's vigor assessment. It is included to satisfy the requirement of stating all facts.
<step n="10" type="premise">
<text>Ash is reflective.</text>
<logic>reflective(ash)</logic>
</step>

Zara's dedication is a core fact. Combined with the disjunction in PREMISE_7, it enables us to deduce her curiosity, starting the cascade of inferences toward disproving vigor.
<step n="11" type="premise">
<text>Zara is dedicated.</text>
<logic>dedicated(zara)</logic>
</step>

We have Zara dedicated and the disjunction that she is either not dedicated or curious. Since the first alternative contradicts a known fact, the only viable option is that Zara is curious. This is a direct application of disjunctive syllogism.
<step n="12" type="derived" from="11,7">
<text>From Zara being dedicated and the disjunction that she is either not dedicated or curious, we infer that Zara is curious.</text>
<logic>curious(zara)</logic>
</step>

Now that Zara is curious, we look at the rule that productive implies not curious. If Zara were productive, she would have to be not curious, which clashes with the established curiosity. Therefore, she cannot be productive, giving us ~productive(zara).
<step n="13" type="derived" from="12,4">
<text>Since Zara is curious and the rule that productive implies not curious, we conclude that Zara cannot be productive.</text>
<logic>~productive(zara)</logic>
</step>

The disjunction from PREMISE_6 states Zara is productive or resourceful. Because we have just ruled out productivity, the only remaining way for the disjunction to hold is for Zara to be resourceful. Hence we infer resourceful(zara).
<step n="14" type="derived" from="13,6">
<text>Given that Zara is not productive and the disjunction that she is either productive or resourceful, we deduce that Zara must be resourceful.</text>
<logic>resourceful(zara)</logic>
</step>

Having established resourcefulness, we invoke the rule that resourceful entails clever. This straightforward modus ponens yields clever(zara), moving us closer to alertness and reliability.
<step n="15" type="derived" from="14,2">
<text>Because Zara is resourceful, the rule that resourceful implies clever lets us infer that Zara is clever.</text>
<logic>clever(zara)</logic>
</step>

With cleverness in hand, we apply the rule linking clever to alert. By modus ponens again, we derive alert(zara), which is a prerequisite for establishing reliability.
<step n="16" type="derived" from="15,1">
<text>With Zara clever, the rule that clever leads to alert gives us that Zara is alert.</text>
<logic>alert(zara)</logic>
</step>

Alertness triggers the rule that any alert entity is reliable. Using modus ponens, we conclude reliable(zara), setting up the final conflict with the disjunction about reliability and evidence-drivenness.
<step n="17" type="derived" from="16,3">
<text>Alertness of Zara triggers the rule that alert implies reliable, so Zara is reliable.</text>
<logic>reliable(zara)</logic>
</step>

We now have reliable(zara) together with the disjunction that either Zara is not reliable or she is evidence-driven. Since the first alternative is false, the second must be true, so we infer evidence_driven(zara).
<step n="18" type="derived" from="17,8">
<text>Reliability together with the disjunction that either Zara is not reliable or evidence-driven forces the conclusion that Zara is evidence-driven.</text>
<logic>evidence_driven(zara)</logic>
</step>

We possess evidence_driven(zara) and the rule that vigor would make Zara not evidence-driven. By contrapositive reasoning (modus tollens), the presence of evidence-drivenness forces the negation of vigor, giving us ~vigorous(zara).
<step n="19" type="derived" from="18,5">
<text>Evidence-driven and the rule that vigor would make her not evidence-driven yields that Zara cannot be vigorous.</text>
<logic>~vigorous(zara)</logic>
</step>

We derived Zara is not vigorous, so the conclusion Zara is vigorous is False.
</think>
Answer: False

examples.question: Is Blake clear-headed?
examples.completion:
<think>
We are given several relationships about Blake’s attributes and a disjunction concerning his skillfulness or clarity of mind. The goal is to determine whether Blake can be concluded to be clear-headed.

The first rule tells us that vigor guarantees enthusiasm for Blake. This is important because we already know Blake is vigorous, so the rule can be activated later in the proof.

</step>

The second rule links strength to fitness. Once we manage to establish that Blake is strong, this rule will allow us to deduce his fitness, moving us toward a contradiction with another rule.
<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>The rule that strong Blake implies fit Blake.</text>
<logic>fit(blake) :- strong(blake)</logic>
</step>

The third rule says that enthusiasm leads to strength. Together with the first rule, it creates a chain from vigor to strength, which is needed for later steps.
<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>The rule that enthusiastic Blake implies strong Blake.</text>
<logic>strong(blake) :- enthusiastic(blake)</logic>
</step>

The fourth rule states that if Blake were skillful, he could not be fit. This contrapositive relationship will be used to eliminate the possibility that Blake is skillful once we know he is fit.
<step n="4" type="premise">
<text>The rule that skillful Blake implies not fit Blake.</text>
<logic>~fit(blake) :- skillful(blake)</logic>
</step>

The disjunction provides two exclusive possibilities: Blake is either skillful or clear-headed. By ruling out one side, we can directly infer the other.
<step n="5" type="premise">
<text>The disjunction stating Blake is either skillful or clear-headed.</text>
<logic>skillful(blake) v clear_headed(blake)</logic>
</step>

The fact that Blake is vigorous gives us a concrete starting point. It triggers the first rule, allowing us to begin the forward reasoning chain.
<step n="6" type="premise">
<text>The fact that Blake is vigorous.</text>
<logic>vigorous(blake)</logic>
</step>

Applying the first rule to the fact that Blake is vigorous, we conclude that Blake is enthusiastic. This follows directly because the rule says vigorous implies enthusiastic.
<step n="7" type="derived" from="6,1">
<text>From Blake being vigorous we infer that Blake is enthusiastic.</text>
<logic>enthusiastic(blake)</logic>
</step>

With Blake now known to be enthusiastic, we use the third rule, which says enthusiasm guarantees strength, to infer that Blake is strong. This step bridges enthusiasm to the next attribute needed for fitness.
<step n="8" type="derived" from="7,3">
<text>From Blake being enthusiastic we infer that Blake is strong.</text>
<logic>strong(blake)</logic>
</step>

Having established Blake’s strength, we invoke the second rule, which converts strength into fitness. Thus Blake is fit, setting up the condition needed for the next contradiction.
<step n="9" type="derived" from="8,2">
<text>From Blake being strong we infer that Blake is fit.</text>
<logic>fit(blake)</logic>
</step>

We know Blake is fit, and the fourth rule tells us that if Blake were skillful, he would not be fit. Since the consequent (not fit) is false, modus tollens lets us infer that Blake cannot be skillful; therefore, ~skillful Blake holds.
<step n="10" type="derived" from="9,4">
<text>Since Blake is fit and skillful would make him not fit, we conclude Blake is not skillful.</text>
<logic>~skillful(blake)</logic>
</step>

The disjunction from premise five tells us Blake is either skillful or clear-headed. Because we have just shown Blake is not skillful, the only remaining viable alternative is that Blake is clear-headed. This is a classic disjunctive syllogism.
<step n="11" type="derived" from="10,5">
<text>Having ruled out that Blake is skillful, the remaining alternative in the disjunction gives that Blake is clear-headed.</text>
<logic>clear_headed(blake)</logic>
</step>

Therefore Blake is clear headed, so the conclusion is True.
</think>
Answer: True

examples.context: Faye is compassionate, and Jack is also compassionate. Emma shows enthusiasm, while Lena is focused. Whenever someone is resourceful, they are diplomatic, so if Jack were resourceful then Jack would be diplomatic. Conversely, if Jack is not resourceful then he becomes judicious. If a person lacks brilliance, that person is precise. A relaxed individual is proficient, and any judicious person is relaxed. Should someone be brilliant, they would not be compassionate. If a person is diplomatic, then that person cannot be precise. Additionally, either Jack is not evidence-driven or Lena is honest, and either Emma is not prepared or Emma is respectful. All these statements together form the knowledge base for the problem.
examples.question: Is Jack proficient?
examples.completion:
<think>
We are asked to determine whether Jack is proficient given a collection of facts and conditional statements. The goal is to chain the rules together until we can derive the predicate proficient(jack).

This rule tells us that resourcefulness is sufficient for diplomacy in Jack, which will become useful if we ever establish Jack as resourceful. It sets up a forward direction we may need to block later.
<step n="1" type="premise">
<text>If Jack is resourceful, then Jack is diplomatic.</text>
<logic>diplomatic(jack) :- resourceful(jack)</logic>
</step>

Here we have the contrapositive counterpart: lacking resourcefulness guarantees judiciousness. This will allow us to infer judiciousness once we can show Jack is not resourceful.
<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>If Jack is not resourceful, then Jack is judicious.</text>
<logic>judicious(jack) :- ~resourceful(jack)</logic>
</step>

The rule links the absence of brilliance to precision. If we can prove Jack is not brilliant, we will be able to claim he is precise, a key step toward negating diplomacy.
<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>If Jack is not brilliant, then Jack is precise.</text>
<logic>precise(jack) :- ~brilliant(jack)</logic>
</step>

This conditional connects the relaxed state directly to proficiency. Once we establish Jack is relaxed, we can immediately conclude he is proficient.
<step n="4" type="premise">
<text>If Jack is relaxed, then Jack is proficient.</text>
<logic>proficient(jack) :- relaxed(jack)</logic>
</step>

Judiciousness leads to a relaxed condition. This bridge will let us move from the judicious status we obtain later to the relaxed status needed for proficiency.
<step n="5" type="premise">
<text>If Jack is judicious, then Jack is relaxed.</text>
<logic>relaxed(jack) :- judicious(jack)</logic>
</step>

The rule states that brilliance would make Jack not compassionate. Since we already know Jack is compassionate, this rule will help us infer that Jack cannot be brilliant.
<step n="6" type="premise">
<text>If Jack is brilliant, then Jack is not compassionate.</text>
<logic>~compassionate(jack) :- brilliant(jack)</logic>
</step>

If Jack were diplomatic, he would not be precise. This provides a way to derive the negation of diplomacy once we have precision, which we will obtain from the lack of brilliance.
<step n="7" type="premise">
<text>If Jack is diplomatic, then Jack is not precise.</text>
<logic>~precise(jack) :- diplomatic(jack)</logic>
</step>

Faye's compassion is a factual statement but does not directly affect Jack; it simply fulfills the requirement that all facts be mentioned in the context.
<step n="8" type="premise">
<text>Faye is compassionate.</text>
<logic>compassionate(faye)</logic>
</step>

Emma's enthusiasm is recorded as a fact; it is part of the background knowledge and does not play a role in the current derivation.
<step n="9" type="premise">
<text>Emma is enthusiastic.</text>
<logic>enthusiastic(emma)</logic>
</step>

Lena being focused is another factual piece that completes the set of given facts; it is not used later in the proof of Jack's proficiency.
<step n="10" type="premise">
<text>Lena is focused.</text>
<logic>focused(lena)</logic>
</step>

Jack's compassion is crucial because it allows us to apply the rule about brilliance, setting up a chain that will eventually give us a non‑brilliant status for Jack.
<step n="11" type="premise">
<text>Jack is compassionate.</text>
<logic>compassionate(jack)</logic>
</step>

Since Jack is compassionate and the rule says brilliance would make him not compassionate, the only way for him to remain compassionate is to not be brilliant. This is a classic use of modus tollens, giving us ~brilliant(jack).
<step n="12" type="derived" from="11,6">
<text>Because Jack is compassionate and brilliance would make him not compassionate, we conclude that Jack is not brilliant.</text>
<logic>~brilliant(jack)</logic>
</step>

Having established that Jack is not brilliant, the rule that non‑brilliance yields precision applies directly, allowing us to infer precise(jack). The existence of some brilliant individual is just a technical condition to activate the rule, which we satisfy.
<step n="13" type="derived" from="12,3">
<text>Since Jack is not brilliant and there exists someone who is brilliant, the rule that non‑brilliance leads to precision gives us that Jack is precise.</text>
<logic>precise(jack)</logic>
</step>

Now that Jack is precise, the rule stating that diplomacy would prevent precision forces us to reject diplomacy for Jack. If Jack were diplomatic, he would be not precise, contradicting what we just derived, so we conclude ~diplomatic(jack).
<step n="14" type="derived" from="13,7">
<text>Jack is precise, and if he were diplomatic he would not be precise; therefore Jack cannot be diplomatic.</text>
<logic>~diplomatic(jack)</logic>
</step>

With ~diplomatic(jack) in hand, we look back at the rule linking resourcefulness to diplomacy. Since resourcefulness would make Jack diplomatic, the denial of diplomacy implies Jack cannot be resourceful, giving us ~resourceful(jack).
<step n="15" type="derived" from="14,1">
<text>Jack is not diplomatic, and resourcefulness would cause diplomacy, so Jack must not be resourceful.</text>
<logic>~resourceful(jack)</logic>
</step>

The rule that a lack of resourcefulness leads to judiciousness now fires: because Jack is not resourceful, we can directly infer judicious(jack). This is a straightforward modus ponens step.
<step n="16" type="derived" from="15,2">
<text>Jack is not resourceful, and the rule says a lack of resourcefulness makes one judicious; thus Jack is judicious.</text>
<logic>judicious(jack)</logic>

Judiciousness triggers the relaxed condition according to the next rule, so from judicious(jack) we obtain relaxed(jack). This moves us one step closer to proficiency.

Finally, the rule that a relaxed individual is proficient lets us conclude proficient(jack) from relaxed(jack). This completes the chain of reasoning needed to answer the query.

Therefore Jack is proficient, so the conclusion is True.

examples.context: Marine biologist Thea is known for her efficiency while cataloguing reef species. Thea could be either talented or brilliant; this is explicitly stated as a disjunction: she is talented or brilliant. Whenever someone is brilliant, they become anxious, so if Thea were brilliant she would be anxious. Anxiety leads to decisiveness, therefore any anxious individual is decisive. Decisiveness, in turn, brings about reflectiveness, so any decisive person is reflective. Moreover, if someone is talented, they cannot be consistent; talent implies not consistent. No other default rules apply.

<step n="10" type="derived" from="9,1">
<text>From Thea’s efficiency and the rule linking efficiency to analytical, we infer that Thea is analytical.</text>
<logic>analytical(thea)</logic>
</step>

Now that we have Thea as analytical, we can use the rule stating that any analytical person is generous. By substituting Thea into this rule, we infer her generosity. This moves us further along the chain toward consistency.
<step n="11" type="derived" from="10,5">

<step n="12" type="derived" from="11,3">

<logic>consistent(thea)</logic>
</step>

The rule about talent says that if someone is talented, they cannot be consistent. Since we have already shown Thea is consistent, the contrapositive tells us she cannot be talented. Thus we derive that Thea is not talented, preparing us to use the disjunction.
<step n="13" type="derived" from="12,7">

The context includes a disjunction: Thea is either talented or brilliant. Because we have just shown she is not talented, the only remaining possibility is that she is brilliant. This elimination step gives us Thea’s brilliance.
<step n="14" type="derived" from="13,8">

Having established that Thea is brilliant, we can apply the rule that brilliance leads to anxiety. Therefore Thea must be anxious. This new fact will help us reach decisiveness.
<step n="15" type="derived" from="14,2">

From Thea’s anxiety and the rule that anxious individuals become decisive, we infer that Thea is decisive. This is the penultimate link before reflectiveness.

<logic>decisive(thea)</logic>

Finally, the rule that decisiveness implies reflectiveness lets us conclude that Thea is reflective. This directly satisfies the query we set out to answer.

If an entity is determined then it is consistent, which makes August consistent. Consistency guarantees resilience, so August is resilient. Brave individuals cannot be resilient; that is, if someone is brave then they are not resilient. Additionally, either Sofia is not organized or Sofia is grateful. Respectful people are those who are grateful, and a respectful Sofia would require her to be grateful, but that does not affect the current chain. All these statements together form the knowledge base.
examples.question: Is August brave?
examples.completion:

We are asked to decide whether August possesses the property of bravery. To answer this, we will trace the logical consequences of the facts and rules provided in the context, aiming to see if bravery can be supported or contradicted.

This rule tells us that determination leads to consistency for August. It will become useful later when we have established that August is determined.

</step>

Here we learn that persistence guarantees determination for August. This connects the chain that starts with thoroughness and moves upward.
<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>If August is persistent, then he is determined.</text>

</step>

The statement links August's thoroughness to persistence. Since we know August is thorough, this rule is the first step in building the inference chain.
<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>If August is thorough, then he is persistent.</text>

This rule says that if August is consistent, then he must also be resilient. It will allow us to derive resilience once we have consistency.

According to this conditional, bravery would force August to be not resilient. This creates a direct conflict with any derived resilience, enabling a reductio argument.

<step n="7" type="premise">

</step>

August being thorough is the crucial factual starting point for the chain of implications that will lead to conclusions about his resilience and bravery.
<step n="8" type="premise">

Using the rule that thoroughness implies persistence, and the fact that August is thorough, we can safely infer that August is persistent. This moves us one step up the implication ladder.

With August determined, the rule connecting determination to consistency allows us to conclude that August is consistent. This is a pivotal intermediate result for later reasoning.

<logic>resilient(august)</logic>

We now have August resilient, while the rule says a brave August would be not resilient. By modus tollens, the only way to avoid contradiction is to assert that August is not brave. This directly answers the query.

examples.context: In the bustling kitchen of the Harvest Bistro, the chefs Dean, Harley, and Riley are all noted for being confident when they create new dishes. The kitchen handbook states that any chef who is confident also works diligently, so confidence guarantees diligence. It further declares that every diligent chef necessarily pays close attention to every ingredient, which means diligence leads to meticulousness. The head chef adds that a meticulous chef tends to be generous, sharing extra portions with the team, so meticulousness implies generosity. Finally, the handbook says that generosity is taken as a sign of honesty among the staff, meaning a generous chef is honest. These statements together capture all the facts and conditional rules that govern the behavior of the three chefs.
examples.question: Based on the given information, can we conclude that Riley is not honest?
examples.completion:

We are asked to decide whether the claim "Riley is not honest" can be supported by the kitchen’s rules and facts. To answer, we must follow the chain of conditions that start from the known facts about confidence and end at honesty. By tracing each implication step by step, we will see what conclusion the premises force us to accept.

The first rule tells us that confidence always brings diligence. This is crucial because it establishes a direct link from the fact that Riley is confident to a property we can later use. Recognizing this link allows us to move from confidence to the next stage in the chain.

<text>Any chef who is confident is also diligent.</text>

The second rule connects meticulousness to generosity. It will become important after we have shown Riley is meticulous, because it will let us infer generosity, a step needed before we can talk about honesty. Keeping this conditional in mind prepares us for the later deduction.

<logic>generous(riley) :- meticulous(riley)</logic>

The third rule says that generosity implies honesty. This is the final bridge in the logical chain: once we have generosity, we can immediately conclude honesty. It explains why establishing generosity is sufficient for our goal.

<text>If a chef is generous, then the chef is honest.</text>
<logic>honest(riley) :- generous(riley)</logic>
</step>

The fourth rule links diligence to meticulousness. After we obtain diligence from confidence, this rule will let us deduce meticulousness, which then triggers the generosity rule. Understanding this intermediate step is essential for the forward reasoning process.
<step n="4" type="premise">
<text>Being diligent guarantees that the chef is meticulous.</text>
<logic>meticulous(riley) :- diligent(riley)</logic>

Dean being confident is a fact that illustrates the rule about confidence, but it is not directly needed for Riley's case. Mentioning it shows that the rule applies uniformly to all chefs, reinforcing the reliability of the rule we will use for Riley.

<text>Dean is confident.</text>
<logic>confident(dean)</logic>
</step>

Harley being confident serves the same purpose as Dean's fact: it demonstrates the consistency of the confidence‑diligence rule across individuals. While not needed for Riley, it confirms the generality of the premise.
<step n="6" type="premise">
<text>Harley is confident.</text>
<logic>confident(harley)</logic>
</step>

Riley's confidence is the starting point for the specific chain we are tracing. This fact activates the first rule, allowing us to begin deriving the subsequent properties that lead to honesty.
<step n="7" type="premise">
<text>Riley is confident.</text>
<logic>confident(riley)</logic>
</step>

Using Riley's confidence together with the confidence‑to‑diligence rule, we infer that Riley must be diligent. This step translates a general conditional into a concrete property for Riley, setting up the next deduction. It shows how the initial fact triggers the chain of reasoning.
<step n="8" type="derived" from="7,1">
<text>From Riley's confidence we infer that Riley is diligent.</text>
<logic>diligent(riley)</logic>
</step>

Because we now know Riley is diligent, the diligence‑to‑meticulousness rule gives us that Riley is meticulous. This moves us one link further along the chain, converting diligence into the property required for generosity. The inference follows directly from the fourth premise.
<step n="9" type="derived" from="8,4">
<text>Since Riley is diligent, we can infer that Riley is meticulous.</text>
<logic>meticulous(riley)</logic>
</step>

Having established that Riley is meticulous, we apply the meticulous‑to‑generous rule to conclude Riley is generous. This step is essential because generosity is the direct antecedent of honesty in the third rule. The reasoning is a straightforward application of the second premise.
<step n="10" type="derived" from="9,2">
<text>Because Riley is meticulous, we conclude that Riley is generous.</text>
<logic>generous(riley)</logic>
</step>

With Riley's generosity confirmed, the generosity‑to‑honesty rule allows us to deduce that Riley is honest. This is the final link in the logical sequence, turning the earlier derived properties into the desired conclusion about honesty. It completes the chain that started with confidence.
<step n="11" type="derived" from="10,3">
<text>Given Riley's generosity, we deduce that Riley is honest.</text>

We derived Riley is honest, so the conclusion Riley is not honest is False.

examples.question: Based on the information given, is Alex persistent?
examples.completion:
<think>
We are asked to determine whether Alex possesses the property of persistence. The knowledge base supplies a mixture of facts, conditional rules, and exclusive alternatives that we can chain together to reach a conclusion about Alex's persistence.

The rule linking non‑open‑mindedness to capability will become useful once we can establish that Alex is not open‑minded, because it will then give us the capability needed for later steps.
<step n="1" type="premise">
<text>If Alex is not open‑minded, then Alex is capable.</text>
<logic>capable(alex) :- ~open_minded(alex)</logic>
</step>

Knowing that a lack of enthusiasm guarantees politeness is crucial because the final disjunction involves politeness versus persistence; proving Alex is polite will allow us to activate that disjunction.
<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>If Alex is not enthusiastic, then Alex is polite.</text>
<logic>polite(alex) :- ~enthusiastic(alex)</logic>
</step>

The scholarly‑to‑studious rule provides a bridge from any scholarly information we might derive about Alex to the studious property, which later helps us infer non‑open‑mindedness.
<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>If Alex is scholarly, then Alex is studious.</text>
<logic>studious(alex) :- scholarly(alex)</logic>
</step>

The rule that enthusiasm leads to not‑intuition will be instrumental after we obtain intuition about Alex, enabling us to reverse‑engineer Alex's enthusiasm status.
<step n="4" type="premise">
<text>If Alex is enthusiastic, then Alex is not intuitive.</text>
<logic>~intuitive(alex) :- enthusiastic(alex)</logic>
</step>

This rule gives a way to turn an open‑minded assumption into a contradiction with studiousness, which we will later exploit via contrapositive reasoning.
<step n="5" type="premise">
<text>If Alex is open‑minded, then Alex is not studious.</text>
<logic>~studious(alex) :- open_minded(alex)</logic>
</step>

The disjunction presents two alternatives for Alex; because we already know Alex is articulate, the only way for the disjunction to hold is for Alex to be scholarly. This will start our chain of deductions.
<step n="6" type="premise">
<text>Either Alex is not articulate or Alex is scholarly.</text>
<logic>~articulate(alex) v scholarly(alex)</logic>
</step>

This alternative will help us move from capability to intuition, once we have established Alex's capability, thereby setting up the next inference about enthusiasm.
<step n="7" type="premise">
<text>Either Alex is not capable or Alex is intuitive.</text>
<logic>~capable(alex) v intuitive(alex)</logic>
</step>

The final disjunction directly connects politeness with persistence. Once we can show Alex is polite, the disjunction forces the conclusion that Alex must be persistent.
<step n="8" type="premise">
<text>Either Alex is not polite or Alex is persistent.</text>
<logic>~polite(alex) v persistent(alex)</logic>
</step>

Ralph's proficiency is a background fact that does not directly affect Alex's persistence, but it is part of the complete knowledge base.
<step n="9" type="premise">

<logic>proficient(ralph)</logic>
</step>

Zion's supportiveness is another background fact included for completeness; it does not play a role in the current proof chain.

<logic>supportive(zion)</logic>
</step>

Alex being articulate is a factual anchor that will interact with the first disjunction to produce a scholarly conclusion.
<step n="11" type="premise">
<text>Alex is articulate.</text>
<logic>articulate(alex)</logic>
</step>

Because we have the fact that Alex is articulate, the disjunction "Alex is not articulate or Alex is scholarly" cannot be satisfied by the first option. Therefore, the only way for the statement to be true is that Alex is scholarly. This gives us a new positive property for Alex.
<step n="12" type="derived" from="11,6">
<text>From Alex being articulate and the disjunction that Alex is not articulate or scholarly, we infer that Alex must be scholarly.</text>
<logic>scholarly(alex)</logic>
</step>

Having established that Alex is scholarly, we can invoke the rule that any scholarly individual is also studious. This straightforward implication yields that Alex is studious.
<step n="13" type="derived" from="12,3">
<text>Since Alex is scholarly, the rule that scholarly implies studious lets us conclude Alex is studious.</text>
<logic>studious(alex)</logic>
</step>

We now have Alex as studious, but the rule "If Alex is open‑minded then Alex is not studious" tells us that open‑mindedness would contradict the studious fact. By contraposition, since Alex is indeed studious, Alex cannot be open‑minded. Hence we deduce Alex is not open‑minded.
<step n="14" type="derived" from="13,5">
<text>Using the fact that Alex is studious together with the rule that open‑mindedness leads to not studious, we apply contrapositive reasoning to obtain that Alex is not open‑minded.</text>
<logic>~open_minded(alex)</logic>

With Alex confirmed as not open‑minded, the first rule directly applies: a non‑open‑minded person is capable. Consequently, Alex becomes capable.

<logic>capable(alex)</logic>

Answer: True

examples.context: The main library branch, haven, is known to be proficient. The assistant, olga, is also proficient, and it is explicitly stated that olga is not determined. Whenever something is proficient, it is also diplomatic, so haven is diplomatic. Any entity that is rested is generous, establishing a link between rest and generosity. Diplomatic entities are well prepared, meaning haven becomes well prepared as soon as it is diplomatic. If someone is determined, then they cannot be well prepared; conversely, a determined person would make the well‑prepared condition false. An entity that is not determined is resilient, so non‑determined individuals guarantee resilience. Either haven is not resilient or haven is rested; this disjunction must hold. All these facts and rules together form the knowledge base.
examples.question: Is it true that haven is not generous?
examples.completion:
<think>
We are given a collection of facts about two library personnel, haven and olga, together with several conditional rules linking properties such as proficiency, diplomacy, and generosity. Our goal is to determine whether the statement that haven is not generous can be supported by the information provided.

The first rule connects proficiency to diplomacy, stating that any entity that is proficient automatically qualifies as diplomatic. This will allow us to move from haven's proficiency to a diplomatic status later in the proof.
<step n="1" type="premise">
<text>The rule says that any proficient entity is diplomatic.</text>
<logic>diplomatic(haven) :- proficient(haven)</logic>
</step>

The second rule links the state of being rested to generosity, indicating that once we can establish that haven is rested we will be able to claim generosity. It sets up a pathway toward the target property.
<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>The rule says that any rested entity is generous.</text>
<logic>generous(haven) :- rested(haven)</logic>
</step>

The third rule tells us that diplomatic entities are well prepared. This creates a bridge from diplomatic status (which we will obtain from proficiency) to the well‑prepared condition, which is useful for later deductions.
<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>The rule says that any diplomatic entity is well prepared.</text>
<logic>well_prepared(haven) :- diplomatic(haven)</logic>
</step>

The fourth rule says that if an entity is determined, then it cannot be well prepared. This contrapositive will later let us infer non‑determination from the presence of well preparation.
<step n="4" type="premise">
<text>The rule says that if someone is determined then they are not well prepared.</text>
<logic>~well_prepared(haven) :- determined(haven)</logic>
</step>

The disjunction provides two alternatives concerning haven: either it is not resilient or it is rested. Once we know one of these alternatives holds, we can infer the other by eliminating the false option.
<step n="5" type="premise">
<text>Either haven is not resilient or haven is rested.</text>
<logic>~resilient(haven) v rested(haven)</logic>
</step>

Olga's proficiency is recorded as a fact, giving us an example of a proficient individual besides haven. Although not directly used for the final conclusion, it mirrors the structure of the knowledge base.
<step n="6" type="premise">
<text>Olga is proficient.</text>
<logic>proficient(olga)</logic>
</step>

Olga is also noted as not determined, reinforcing the pattern that proficiency does not imply determination in this domain. This fact helps illustrate the independence of the properties.
<step n="7" type="premise">
<text>Olga is not determined.</text>
<logic>~determined(olga)</logic>
</step>

The explicit fact that haven is proficient is crucial because it activates the first rule, allowing us to derive diplomatic status for haven.
<step n="8" type="premise">
<text>Haven is proficient.</text>
<logic>proficient(haven)</logic>
</step>

Using the fact that haven is proficient together with the rule that proficiency implies diplomacy, we can conclude haven is diplomatic. This step follows a straightforward forward‑chaining from a known attribute to a derived one.
<step n="9" type="derived" from="8,1">
<text>From haven being proficient and the rule that any proficient entity is diplomatic, we infer that haven is diplomatic.</text>
<logic>diplomatic(haven)</logic>
</step>

Having established that haven is diplomatic, we apply the rule that diplomatic entities are well prepared. Consequently, haven inherits the well‑prepared property, moving us closer to the non‑determination inference.
<step n="10" type="derived" from="9,3">
<text>Since haven is diplomatic and diplomatic entities are well prepared, we conclude that haven is well prepared.</text>
<logic>well_prepared(haven)</logic>
</step>

Now that haven is well prepared, we invoke the contrapositive of the rule linking determination to lack of preparation: if someone were determined they would not be well prepared. Since haven is indeed well prepared, it cannot be determined, so we infer ~determined(haven).
<step n="11" type="derived" from="10,4">
<text>Because haven is well prepared and if someone were determined they would not be well prepared, we infer that haven is not determined.</text>
<logic>~determined(haven)</logic>
</step>

With ~determined(haven) in hand, we turn to the rule stating that any non‑determined entity is resilient. This yields resilient(haven), giving us a concrete truth about haven's resilience.
<step n="12" type="derived" from="11">
<text>Given that haven is not determined and the rule that any non‑determined entity is resilient, we deduce that haven is resilient.</text>
<logic>resilient(haven)</logic>
</step>

The disjunction says that either haven is not resilient or haven is rested. Because we have just shown that haven is resilient, the first alternative is false, forcing the second alternative to be true; therefore, haven must be rested.
<step n="13" type="derived" from="12,5">
<text>Since we know haven is resilient, the disjunction that either haven is not resilient or rested must be satisfied by the latter, so haven is rested.</text>
<logic>rested(haven)</logic>
</step>

Finally, using the rule that rested entities are generous, the rested status of haven lets us infer generous(haven). This directly contradicts the query that haven is not generous.
<step n="14" type="derived" from="13,2">
<text>Because haven is rested and rested entities are generous, we infer that haven is generous.</text>
<logic>generous(haven)</logic>
</step>

We derived Haven is generous, so the conclusion Haven is not generous is False.
</think>
Answer: False

examples.context: In the pottery studio the clay artisan is known for using an abundant glaze. Any artisan who uses an abundant glaze has a steady vision. If the artisan applies steady pressure, then they handle the clay carefully. If the artisan honors tradition, they apply steady pressure. Being material sensitive leads the artisan to honor tradition. The artisan is either logically designed or material sensitive.
examples.question: Can we conclude that the clay artisan is not careful in handling?
examples.completion:
<think>
We need to decide whether the clay artisan can be said to be not careful in handling the clay. The goal is to see if the premises force the artisan to be careful, which would make the negation false.

The rule connecting steady pressure to careful handling is important because it links a later derived property (steady pressure) to the target property (careful handling). Recognizing this conditional will allow us to propagate forward once we have steady pressure.

Safety compliance follows from steady vision, completing the chain that starts with abundant glaze. This rule will let us move from vision to the disciplined domain.

The conditional that a logical design implies not being methodical sets up a contradiction later, because we will derive methodical behavior. This rule enables a modus tollens step to deny the rational property.

Stating that the artisan is either logically designed or material sensitive gives us a disjunction that will be resolved once we eliminate one of the options. It creates the opportunity for a disjunctive syllogism later.

The fact that the artisan uses an abundant glaze is the starting point of the forward reasoning. It directly triggers the rule that yields a steady vision.

With safety compliance established, the rule that safety compliance implies methodical work allows us to infer that the artisan works methodically. This step is necessary for the later contradiction with the rational rule.
<step n="12" type="derived" from="11,3">

We now have that the artisan is methodical, but the rule says that if the artisan is logically designed, then they are not methodical. examples.context: In the linguistics department, Keith and Morgan are known for being very attentive. Grace, on the other hand, is thoughtful. The department holds that any attentive scholar is impartial, and any impartial scholar is proficient. Moreover, if a scholar is thoughtful, then that scholar is bold. For Grace, being fluent guarantees eloquence. Additionally, Grace is either not bold or she is fluent. These statements together capture all the given facts, rules, and the disjunctive condition.
examples.question: Is Keith proficient?
examples.completion:
<think>
We are asked to determine whether Keith possesses the property of proficiency. To answer this, we need to trace how the given facts and departmental rules connect Keith's attentiveness to proficiency. The proof proceeds by applying the conditional rules step by step.

The first premise tells us that attentiveness guarantees impartiality. Because the department’s policy links these two attributes, any scholar who is attentive automatically qualifies as impartial. This premise will allow us to move from Keith's attentiveness to impartiality later in the proof.
<step n="1" type="premise">
<text>The rule states that any attentive individual, such as Keith, is impartial.</text>
<logic>impartial(keith) :- attentive(keith)</logic>
</step>

The second premise establishes that impartiality, in turn, guarantees proficiency. This creates a chain: if we can show Keith is impartial, we can immediately conclude he is proficient. This premise is essential for the final inference.
<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>The rule says that any impartial person, like Keith if he is impartial, is proficient.</text>
<logic>proficient(keith) :- impartial(keith)</logic>
</step>

The third premise simply records that Morgan is attentive. While this fact does not directly involve Keith, it illustrates that the rule about attentiveness applies in the department and confirms the context of the rule.
<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>Morgan is attentive.</text>
<logic>attentive(morgan)</logic>
</step>

The fourth premise notes that Grace is thoughtful. This supports the rule linking thoughtfulness to boldness, but it is not needed for the query about Keith. It is included to satisfy the requirement that every fact be mentioned in the context.
<step n="4" type="premise">
<text>Grace is thoughtful.</text>
<logic>thoughtful(grace)</logic>
</step>

The fifth premise states explicitly that Keith is attentive. This is the critical factual anchor for our reasoning, as it enables us to activate the rule from Premise 1 concerning attentiveness and impartiality.
<step n="5" type="premise">
<text>Keith is attentive.</text>
<logic>attentive(keith)</logic>
</step>

Combining Keith's attentiveness (Premise 5) with the rule that attentiveness implies impartiality (Premise 1), we apply modus ponens to derive that Keith is impartial. This step bridges the factual basis to the first conditional link in our chain.
<step n="6" type="derived" from="5,1">
<text>From Keith being attentive and the rule that attentiveness implies impartiality, we conclude that Keith is impartial.</text>
<logic>impartial(keith)</logic>
</step>

Now that we have established Keith's impartiality, we use the second rule (Premise 2) which says impartiality leads to proficiency. By modus ponens again, we infer that Keith is proficient. This step completes the logical progression from the original fact to the desired property.
<step n="7" type="derived" from="6,2">
<text>Since Keith is now known to be impartial and impartiality leads to proficiency, we infer that Keith is proficient.</text>
<logic>proficient(keith)</logic>
</step>

Therefore Keith is proficient, so the conclusion is True.
</think>
Answer: True

Having established Sutton's reflectiveness, we apply the reflective‑to‑knowledgeable rule from premise 2. By modus ponens, we deduce that Sutton is knowledgeable, which is the key piece needed for the disjunctive reasoning that follows.
<step n="8" type="derived" from="7,2">

Now we face the disjunction that Sutton is either not knowledgeable or wise. Since we have just proved that Sutton is knowledgeable, the first alternative of the disjunction is false, leaving the only viable option that Sutton is wise. This is a classic use of disjunctive syllogism.
<step n="9" type="derived" from="8,4">

We derived Sutton is wise, so the conclusion Sutton is not wise is False.

examples.context: Gail is known to be inventive. Whenever someone is inventive, that person is also ambitious. If a person is wise, then the person is courageous. examples.question: Can we conclude that Gail is not courageous?
examples.completion:
<think>
We are asked to determine whether Gail lacks courage. The target statement is the negation of courageous(gail), so we must see if the available facts and rules force courageous(gail) to be false. Our strategy is to follow the forward chain of implications from the known fact that Gail is inventive, and also to use the exclusive disjunction about cautiousness and evidence‑driven status.

The rule linking inventiveness to ambition is crucial because it starts the cascade of properties that eventually affect courage. Recognizing this conditional lets us later turn the fact that Gail is inventive into a concrete claim about her ambition.
<step n="1" type="premise">
<text>Any inventive person is also ambitious.</text>
<logic>ambitious(gail) :- inventive(gail)</logic>
</step>

Courage depends directly on wisdom, according to this rule. Keeping this in mind tells us that if we can establish wise(gail), we will automatically obtain courageous(gail), which is the opposite of what we want to prove.
<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>Being wise guarantees that a person is courageous.</text>
<logic>courageous(gail) :- wise(gail)</logic>
</step>

The connection between evidence‑driven behavior and being fair‑minded provides a bridge from the disjunction later on to the wisdom chain. If we manage to show that Gail is evidence‑driven, this rule will give us fair‑minded, and subsequently wise.
<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>If someone is evidence‑driven, then they are fair‑minded.</text>
<logic>fair_minded(gail) :- evidence_driven(gail)</logic>
</step>

Ambition leading to humility is another link in the chain. Once we have ambition, we can progress toward humility, then intuition, and eventually use the cautiousness rule to eliminate a possibility.
<step n="4" type="premise">
<text>Ambitious people become humble.</text>
<logic>humble(gail) :- ambitious(gail)</logic>
</step>

Humility guaranteeing intuition allows us to move from a moral quality to a mental attribute. This step will become important when we compare intuition with the consequence of cautiousness.
<step n="5" type="premise">
<text>Humility leads to being intuitive.</text>
<logic>intuitive(gail) :- humble(gail)</logic>
</step>

Fair‑mindedness implying wisdom closes the loop from evidence‑driven status back to the courage rule. It shows how the disjunction can ultimately affect courage through a series of implications.
<step n="6" type="premise">
<text>Fair‑minded individuals are wise.</text>
<logic>wise(gail) :- fair_minded(gail)</logic>
</step>

The rule that cautiousness forces not‑intuition creates a potential contradiction with the intuition we will derive later. Exploiting this contradiction will let us deny cautiousness, which in turn will activate the disjunction.
<step n="7" type="premise">
<text>Cautiousness implies not being intuitive.</text>
<logic>~intuitive(gail) :- cautious(gail)</logic>
</step>

The exclusive disjunction states that at least one of cautious(gail) or evidence_driven(gail) holds. By later showing that cautious(gail) is impossible, we will be forced to accept evidence_driven(gail).
<step n="8" type="premise">
<text>Either Gail is cautious or she is evidence‑driven.</text>
<logic>cautious(gail) v evidence_driven(gail)</logic>
</step>

The sole factual premise gives us a solid starting point: Gail is inventive. All subsequent reasoning will build on this concrete fact.
<step n="9" type="premise">
<text>Gail is inventive.</text>
<logic>inventive(gail)</logic>
</step>

Using the fact that Gail is inventive together with the rule that inventive implies ambitious, we can directly infer that Gail is ambitious. This is a straightforward application of Modus Ponens, turning a condition into a concrete property.
<step n="10" type="derived" from="9,1">
<text>From Gail being inventive and the rule that inventive implies ambitious, we infer that Gail is ambitious.</text>
<logic>ambitious(gail)</logic>
</step>

Now that we know Gail is ambitious, we apply the ambition‑to‑humility rule. Modus Ponens again lets us conclude that Gail is humble, moving us further along the chain toward intuition.
<step n="11" type="derived" from="10,4">
<text>Because Gail is ambitious and ambition leads to humility, we conclude that Gail is humble.</text>
<logic>humble(gail)</logic>
</step>

With humility established, the humility‑to‑intuition rule gives us that Gail is intuitive. This step is essential because it sets up the later conflict with the cautiousness rule.
<step n="12" type="derived" from="11,5">
<text>Given Gail's humility and the rule that humble people are intuitive, we derive that Gail is intuitive.</text>
<logic>intuitive(gail)</logic>
</step>

We have intuition, but the cautiousness rule says that if Gail were cautious, she would be not intuitive. Since we already have intuition, the antecedent (cautious) cannot be true; otherwise we would have a contradiction. Therefore, by Modus Tollens we infer that Gail is not cautious.
<step n="13" type="derived" from="12,7">
<text>Since intuitive and the rule that cautiousness leads to not intuitive conflict, we obtain that Gail is not cautious.</text>
<logic>~cautious(gail)</logic>
</step>

The disjunction tells us that Gail is either cautious or evidence‑driven. Because we have just shown she cannot be cautious, the only remaining option is that she must be evidence‑driven. This is a classic use of Disjunctive Syllogism.
<step n="14" type="derived" from="13,8">
<text>With the information that Gail is not cautious and the disjunction that she is either cautious or evidence‑driven, we deduce that she must be evidence‑driven.</text>
<logic>evidence_driven(gail)</logic>
</step>

Having established evidence‑driven status, we invoke the rule that evidence‑driven implies fair‑minded. Modus Ponens gives us that Gail is fair‑minded, advancing us toward wisdom.
<step n="15" type="derived" from="14,3">
<text>From Gail being evidence‑driven and the rule that evidence‑driven implies fair‑minded, we infer that Gail is fair‑minded.</text>
<logic>fair_minded(gail)</logic>
</step>

From fair‑mindedness we apply the rule that fair‑minded leads to wisdom. Again, Modus Ponens lets us infer that Gail is wise, which is the direct antecedent needed for courage.
<step n="16" type="derived" from="15,6">
<text>Because Gail is fair‑minded and fair‑minded implies wise, we conclude that Gail is wise.</text>
<logic>wise(gail)</logic>
</step>

Finally, the wisdom‑to‑courage rule tells us that a wise person is courageous. Since we have just proven Gail is wise, we conclude that she is courageous. This completes the forward chain of implications.
<step n="17" type="derived" from="16,2">
<text>Finally, wise persons are courageous, so we infer that Gail is courageous.</text>
<logic>courageous(gail)</logic>
</step>

We derived Gail is courageous, so the conclusion Gail is not courageous is False.
</think>
Answer: False

examples.question: Is it true that Nico is not healthy?
examples.completion:
<think>
We are asked to determine whether Nico is not healthy. The goal is to use the given facts, rules, and the disjunction about Nico to derive a statement about his health status. The reasoning will proceed by chaining implications and applying contraposition and disjunctive syllogism.

<step n="1" type="premise">
<text>Whenever Nico is productive, he is ambitious.</text>
<logic>ambitious(nico) :- productive(nico)</logic>
</step>

<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>Whenever Nico is analytical, he is productive.</text>
<logic>productive(nico) :- analytical(nico)</logic>
</step>

<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>Whenever Nico is ambitious, he is resourceful.</text>
<logic>resourceful(nico) :- ambitious(nico)</logic>
</step>

<step n="4" type="premise">
<text>Any capable individual is wise.</text>
<logic>wise(nico) :- capable(nico)</logic>
</step>

<step n="5" type="premise">
<text>If someone is healthy, then they are not resourceful.</text>
<logic>~resourceful(nico) :- healthy(nico)</logic>
</step>

<step n="6" type="premise">
<text>If someone is patient, then they are not wise.</text>
<logic>~wise(nico) :- patient(nico)</logic>
</step>

<step n="7" type="premise">
<text>Either Nico is patient or Nico is analytical.</text>
<logic>patient(nico) v analytical(nico)</logic>
</step>

<step n="8" type="premise">
<text>Steve is determined.</text>
<logic>determined(steve)</logic>
</step>

<step n="9" type="premise">
<text>Nico is capable.</text>
<logic>capable(nico)</logic>
</step>

We start with the explicit fact that Nico is capable. The rule that every capable individual is wise allows us to directly infer that Nico is wise. This step establishes the truth of the predicate wise(nico) which will be crucial for later contraposition.
<step n="10" type="derived" from="9,4">
<text>From Nico being capable and the rule that every capable being is wise, we infer that Nico is wise.</text>
<logic>wise(nico)</logic>
</step>

Having obtained wise(nico), we look at the rule linking patient to not wise: if someone is patient then they are not wise. Taking the contrapositive of this rule, the presence of wisdom means the antecedent (patient) must be false. Hence we can safely conclude that Nico is not patient.
<step n="11" type="derived" from="10,6">
<text>Since Nico is wise and the rule says that being patient would make someone not wise, we conclude that Nico cannot be patient.</text>
<logic>~patient(nico)</logic>
</step>

The problem also tells us that either Nico is patient or Nico is analytical. Since we have just shown that Nico cannot be patient, the only remaining option in this disjunction is that Nico is analytical. Thus, analytical(nico) follows by disjunctive syllogism.
<step n="12" type="derived" from="11,7">
<text>Given that Nico is not patient and that either Nico is patient or analytical, we deduce that Nico must be analytical.</text>
<logic>analytical(nico)</logic>
</step>

Now that we know Nico is analytical, we can apply the rule that analytical implies productive. By modus ponens, this yields productive(nico). This new fact moves us forward toward establishing ambition and resourcefulness.
<step n="13" type="derived" from="12,2">
<text>Because Nico is analytical and the rule that analytical implies productive, we obtain that Nico is productive.</text>
<logic>productive(nico)</logic>
</step>

With productive(nico) in hand, we use the rule that productivity leads to ambition. Again by modus ponens, we derive ambitious(nico). This step is necessary because ambition is the premise for the next rule concerning resourcefulness.
<step n="14" type="derived" from="13,1">
<text>With Nico being productive and the rule that productivity leads to ambition, we derive that Nico is ambitious.</text>
<logic>ambitious(nico)</logic>
</step>

Given ambitious(nico) and the rule that ambition results in resourcefulness, we infer resourceful(nico). At this point we have a positive statement about Nico's resourcefulness, setting up a clash with the rule that health would cause non‑resourcefulness.
<step n="15" type="derived" from="14,3">
<text>From Nico's ambition and the rule that ambition yields resourcefulness, we conclude that Nico is resourceful.</text>
<logic>resourceful(nico)</logic>
</step>

Finally, we consider the rule that if someone is healthy then they are not resourceful. Applying modus tollens with the established fact that Nico is indeed resourceful, we must conclude that the antecedent (healthy) cannot hold. Therefore, we deduce ~healthy(nico).
<step n="16" type="derived" from="15,5">
<text>Since Nico is resourceful and the rule that health would cause non‑resourcefulness, we infer that Nico cannot be healthy.</text>
<logic>~healthy(nico)</logic>
</step>

Therefore Nico is not healthy, so the conclusion is True.
</think>
Answer: True

We are asked to determine whether the sea turtle can be considered balanced. To answer, we will examine the given facts and conditional statements and see how they combine to support that property.

We are directly told that the sea turtle possesses school fidelity. Because the disjunction states that either the turtle lacks school fidelity or it is bold, and we know the turtle does have school fidelity, the only way for the disjunction to hold is that the turtle must be bold.

</step>

</step>

We already know Kira is compassionate from the facts, and the first rule says compassion implies determination for her. By applying that rule, we can confidently assert that Kira is determined. This step converts the initial fact into a new property needed for the next inference.
