examples.context: In the dojo, Stella is known to be confident, and Avery is recognized as polite. Whenever someone is principled, they become agile, so if Stella is principled then she is agile. If Avery is polite, then Avery is cautious. If Stella is grateful, then Stella is principled. If Avery is humble, then Avery becomes resourceful. Being brave forces Stella to lack intuition, meaning that if Stella is brave then she is not intuitive. Strength guarantees a lack of motivation, so if Stella is strong then she is not motivated. Regarding alternatives, Stella is either brave or grateful (or possibly both). Either Stella is not agile or she is motivated. Either Avery is not cautious or Avery is humble. Finally, either Stella is not confident or Stella is intuitive.
examples.question: Can we conclude that Stella is not strong?
examples.completion:
<think>
We are asked to determine whether Stella lacks the property of being strong. To answer this, we will trace the logical consequences of the given facts and rules until we can either assert or deny Stella's strength.

The rule linking principled status to agility is essential because it lets us move from a moral quality to a physical one. Recognizing this connection will later allow us to derive agility once we have established principledness.
<step n="1" type="premise">
<text>If Stella is principled, then she is agile.</text>
<logic>agile(stella) :- principled(stella)</logic>
</step>

Gratitude serving as a gateway to being principled gives us a path from an emotional state to a moral attribute. This chain is useful because we will later obtain gratitude for Stella, which then yields principledness.
<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>If Stella is grateful, then she is principled.</text>
<logic>principled(stella) :- grateful(stella)</logic>
</step>

The conditional that bravery eliminates intuition is a negative implication that will be used in a contrapositive style later. Knowing this helps us later to reject bravery when intuition is present.
<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>If Stella is brave, then she is not intuitive.</text>
<logic>~intuitive(stella) :- brave(stella)</logic>
</step>

The rule stating that strength prevents motivation creates a direct link between the property we ultimately care about and motivation. This will allow us to infer non‑strength once we have motivation.
<step n="4" type="premise">
<text>If Stella is strong, then she is not motivated.</text>
<logic>~motivated(stella) :- strong(stella)</logic>
</step>

The disjunction that Stella is either brave or grateful gives us a fallback: if we can eliminate one option, the other must hold. This will become crucial after we rule out bravery.
<step n="5" type="premise">
<text>Stella is either brave or grateful.</text>
<logic>brave(stella) v grateful(stella)</logic>
</step>

The statement that either Stella is not agile or she is motivated provides a way to infer motivation once we know she is agile. It sets up a classic disjunctive syllogism scenario.
<step n="6" type="premise">
<text>Either Stella is not agile or she is motivated.</text>
<logic>~agile(stella) v motivated(stella)</logic>
</step>

The alternative that Stella is either not confident or intuitive lets us combine her known confidence with this disjunction to produce intuition. This is the first step toward eliminating bravery.
<step n="7" type="premise">
<text>Either Stella is not confident or she is intuitive.</text>
<logic>~confident(stella) v intuitive(stella)</logic>
</step>

Avery being polite is a simple fact that will later enable us to infer caution, though that path is not needed for the final goal. It is included to satisfy the requirement of stating all facts.
<step n="8" type="premise">
<text>Avery is polite.</text>
<logic>polite(avery)</logic>
</step>

Stella's confidence is another explicit fact that will interact with the confidence‑intuition disjunction. It is the premise that triggers the first derivation of intuition.
<step n="9" type="premise">
<text>Stella is confident.</text>
<logic>confident(stella)</logic>
</step>

We have Stella's confidence and the disjunction that she is either not confident or intuitive. Since the first alternative is false (she is confident), the only way the disjunction can be true is if she is intuitive. Thus we conclude Stella is intuitive.
<step n="10" type="derived" from="9,7">
<text>From Stella being confident and the fact that either she is not confident or intuitive, we infer that Stella must be intuitive.</text>
<logic>intuitive(stella)</logic>
</step>

Now that Stella is intuitive, we look at the rule stating that bravery would make her not intuitive. If she were brave, she could not be intuitive, which contradicts our derived intuition. Therefore, by rejecting the antecedent, we infer that Stella is not brave.
<step n="11" type="derived" from="10,3">
<text>Since Stella is intuitive and bravery would make her not intuitive, we conclude that Stella cannot be brave.</text>
<logic>~brave(stella)</logic>
</step>

With the knowledge that Stella is not brave and the disjunction that she is either brave or grateful, the only remaining way for the disjunction to hold is for her to be grateful. Hence we conclude Stella is grateful.
<step n="12" type="derived" from="11,5">
<text>Given that Stella is not brave and she is either brave or grateful, the only way the disjunction holds is that she is grateful.</text>
<logic>grateful(stella)</logic>
</step>

Since we have established that Stella is grateful, and the rule says gratitude leads to being principled, we can directly infer that Stella is principled.
<step n="13" type="derived" from="12,2">
<text>Because Stella is grateful and gratitude leads to being principled, we deduce that Stella is principled.</text>
<logic>principled(stella)</logic>
</step>

Having shown Stella is principled, we can apply the earlier rule that principled individuals are agile. This gives us the fact that Stella is agile.
<step n="14" type="derived" from="13,1">
<text>With Stella being principled and the rule that principled implies agile, we obtain that Stella is agile.</text>
<logic>agile(stella)</logic>
</step>

We now possess both the fact that Stella is agile and the disjunction that either she is not agile or motivated. Because the first part of the disjunction is false, the second part must be true, so we conclude Stella is motivated.
<step n="15" type="derived" from="14,6">
<text>Stella is agile, and the statement that either she is not agile or motivated forces her to be motivated.</text>
<logic>motivated(stella)</logic>
</step>

Finally, we use the rule that if Stella were strong, she would not be motivated. Since we have just derived that she is motivated, the antecedent (being strong) cannot hold. Therefore, Stella is not strong.
<step n="16" type="derived" from="15,4">
<text>Motivation contradicts the implication that strength would remove motivation, so Stella cannot be strong.</text>
<logic>~strong(stella)</logic>
</step>

Therefore Stella is not strong, so the conclusion is True.
</think>
Answer: True

examples.context: In the laboratory, the compound called molecule_faye is known to be reactive. The theoretical framework supplies two conditional statements: whenever a molecule is not electron_rich, it is catalytic, and whenever a molecule is electron_rich, it cannot be reactive. No other facts or defaults are provided.
examples.question: Is molecule_faye not catalytic?
examples.completion:
<think>
We are asked whether the molecule named molecule_faye can be said to be not catalytic. To answer, we must use the given facts about its reactivity and the two conditional chemical rules linking electron richness, reactivity, and catalytic ability.

The first conditional provides a direct link from the absence of electron richness to catalytic behavior. Recognizing this connection will be crucial once we know whether molecule_faye lacks electron richness.
<step n="1" type="premise">
<text>The rule states that if a molecule is not electron_rich, then it must be catalytic.</text>
<logic>catalytic(molecule_faye) :- ~electron_rich(molecule_faye)</logic>
</step>

The second conditional tells us that electron richness precludes reactivity. Since we already know molecule_faye is reactive, this rule will allow us to reason about the presence or absence of electron richness.
<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>The rule states that if a molecule is electron_rich, then it cannot be reactive.</text>
<logic>~reactive(molecule_faye) :- electron_rich(molecule_faye)</logic>
</step>

The fact that molecule_faye is reactive gives us a solid starting point for the deduction. Reactivity is a concrete property we can combine with the second rule.
<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>The molecule molecule_faye is reactive.</text>
<logic>reactive(molecule_faye)</logic>
</step>

Given that electron_rich would make a molecule non‑reactive, but we have observed molecule_faye to be reactive, the only consistent conclusion is that molecule_faye cannot be electron_rich. This is an application of modus tollens: the antecedent of the rule must be false because its consequent is false.
<step n="4" type="derived" from="3,2">
<text>Because molecule_faye is reactive and electron_rich would prevent reactivity, we infer that molecule_faye is not electron_rich.</text>
<logic>~electron_rich(molecule_faye)</logic>
</step>

Now that we have established molecule_faye is not electron_rich, we can invoke the first rule, which says any molecule lacking electron richness is catalytic. By directly applying this implication (modus ponens), we conclude that molecule_faye is catalytic.
<step n="5" type="derived" from="4,1">
<text>Having established that molecule_faye is not electron_rich, the first rule tells us that molecule_faye is catalytic.</text>
<logic>catalytic(molecule_faye)</logic>
</step>

We derived Molecule_faye is catalytic, so the conclusion Molecule_faye is not catalytic is False.
</think>
Answer: False

examples.context: The market analyst is dynamic. The consumer confidence is insightful. The policy maker is accountable. If someone is ethical, then they are resilient, so an ethical consumer confidence is resilient. If someone is rigorous, then they are scholarly, so a rigorous policy maker is scholarly. If someone is optimistic, then they are not stable, so an optimistic policy maker would be not stable.
examples.question: Is the policy maker thorough?
examples.completion:
<think>
We need to determine whether the policy maker possesses the thorough attribute. The available facts, rules, and disjunctions will be used to derive this conclusion.

The rule that a structured policy maker is stable will later allow us to obtain stability once we can show the policy maker is structured. Stability is also needed to eliminate the possibility of optimism later in the proof.
<step n="1" type="premise">
<text>If a policy maker is structured, then they are stable.</text>

Knowing that a rigorous policy maker is scholarly gives us a way to move from rigor to scholarship. This connection will be crucial after we derive rigor from the accountability disjunction.
<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>If a policy maker is rigorous, then they are scholarly.</text>
<logic>scholarly(policy_maker) :- rigorous(policy_maker)</logic>
</step>

The conditional stating that optimism leads to non‑stability lets us use a contrapositive style argument: if we can prove the policy maker is stable, we can infer they cannot be optimistic. This will be essential for the final disjunction.
<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>If a policy maker is optimistic, then they are not stable.</text>
<logic>~stable(policy_maker) :- optimistic(policy_maker)</logic>
</step>

The disjunction that the policy maker is either optimistic or thorough directly ties the target attribute to optimism. Once we rule out optimism, this clause will force the policy maker to be thorough.
<step n="4" type="premise">
<text>Either the policy maker is optimistic or they are thorough.</text>
<logic>optimistic(policy_maker) v thorough(policy_maker)</logic>
</step>

The disjunction offering either not accountable or rigorous provides a bridge from the known fact of accountability to the needed property of rigor. By eliminating the first alternative, we can secure rigor.
<step n="5" type="premise">
<text>Either the policy maker is not accountable or they are rigorous.</text>
<logic>~accountable(policy_maker) v rigorous(policy_maker)</logic>
</step>

The disjunction that either not scholarly or structured lets us turn a derived scholarly fact into a structured fact. Structured status is required for the stability rule in PREMISE_1.
<step n="6" type="premise">
<text>Either the policy maker is not scholarly or they are structured.</text>
<logic>~scholarly(policy_maker) v structured(policy_maker)</logic>
</step>

The fact that the market analyst is dynamic is part of the background knowledge but does not directly influence the chain concerning the policy maker. It is included to reflect the full set of premises.
<step n="7" type="premise">
<text>The market analyst is dynamic.</text>
<logic>dynamic(market_analyst)</logic>
</step>

The fact that consumer confidence is insightful similarly belongs to the broader context and is not used in the immediate derivation of thoroughness for the policy maker.
<step n="8" type="premise">
<text>The consumer confidence is insightful.</text>
<logic>insightful(consumer_confidence)</logic>
</step>

The policy maker being accountable is the starting point for applying the disjunction in PREMISE_5. It gives us a concrete fact to work with in the first derivation step.
<step n="9" type="premise">
<text>The policy maker is accountable.</text>
<logic>accountable(policy_maker)</logic>
</step>

We have the fact that the policy maker is accountable, and PREMISE_5 tells us that either the policy maker is not accountable or they are rigorous. Since the first option is false, the only way for the disjunction to hold is that the policy maker must be rigorous. This yields the rigorous property.
<step n="10" type="derived" from="9,5">
<text>From the fact that the policy maker is accountable and the disjunction that either the policy maker is not accountable or they are rigorous, we infer that the policy maker is rigorous.</text>
<logic>rigorous(policy_maker)</logic>
</step>

Having established that the policy maker is rigorous, we can invoke PREMISE_2, which states that any rigorous policy maker is scholarly. Therefore, the policy maker inherits the scholarly attribute.
<step n="11" type="derived" from="10,2">
<text>Since the policy maker is rigorous and a rigorous policy maker is scholarly, we conclude the policy maker is scholarly.</text>
<logic>scholarly(policy_maker)</logic>
</step>

Now that the policy maker is scholarly, we look at PREMISE_6, which offers a choice between not scholarly or being structured. The scholarly condition makes the first alternative impossible, so the policy maker must be structured to satisfy the disjunction.
<step n="12" type="derived" from="11,6">
<text>Given the policy maker is scholarly and the disjunction that either the policy maker is not scholarly or they are structured, we deduce the policy maker is structured.</text>
<logic>structured(policy_maker)</logic>
</step>

With the policy maker confirmed as structured, PREMISE_1 applies: a structured policy maker is stable. Consequently, we can assert that the policy maker is stable.
<step n="13" type="derived" from="12,1">
<text>Because the policy maker is structured and a structured policy maker is stable, we infer the policy maker is stable.</text>
<logic>stable(policy_maker)</logic>
</step>

We have just shown the policy maker is stable. PREMISE_3 tells us that if the policy maker were optimistic, they would be not stable. Since stability holds, the antecedent of that conditional cannot be true; therefore, the policy maker cannot be optimistic. This gives us the negation of optimism.
<step n="14" type="derived" from="13,3">

The disjunction in PREMISE_4 says the policy maker is either optimistic or thorough. From STEP_14 we know the policy maker is not optimistic, so the only remaining way for the disjunction to be true is that the policy maker must be thorough. Hence, we derive thoroughness.
<step n="15" type="derived" from="14,4">

Therefore Policy_maker is thorough, so the conclusion is True.

<step n="1" type="premise">
<text>If the shuttle is tensioned, then it is balanced.</text>
<logic>balanced(shuttle) :- tensioned(shuttle)</logic>
</step>

The second rule states that a woven shuttle becomes tensioned. This connects the known fact about the shuttle being woven to the condition needed for balance. It gives us a pathway from a concrete fact to the desired property.
<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>If the shuttle is woven, then it is tensioned.</text>
<logic>tensioned(shuttle) :- woven(shuttle)</logic>
</step>

The fact that the bobbin is dyed is recorded, though it does not directly affect the shuttle's balance. Including it satisfies the requirement to mention every fact from the premises.
<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>The bobbin is dyed.</text>
<logic>dyed(bobbin)</logic>
</step>

The fact that the loom is patterned is also noted, even though it is unrelated to the shuttle's balance. It fulfills the completeness condition for the context.
<step n="4" type="premise">
<text>The loom is patterned.</text>
<logic>patterned(loom)</logic>
</step>

We are explicitly told that the shuttle is woven. This fact will trigger the rule that makes the shuttle tensioned, moving us toward the balanced conclusion.
<step n="5" type="premise">
<text>The shuttle is woven.</text>
<logic>woven(shuttle)</logic>
</step>

From the fact that the shuttle is woven and the rule that a woven shuttle is tensioned, we can infer that the shuttle is tensioned. This is a direct application of modus ponens, turning a known condition into a new fact.
<step n="6" type="derived" from="5,2">
<text>Therefore, the shuttle is tensioned.</text>
<logic>tensioned(shuttle)</logic>
</step>

Having established that the shuttle is tensioned, we now apply the rule that a tensioned shuttle is balanced. By modus ponens again, we deduce that the shuttle is indeed balanced.
<step n="7" type="derived" from="6,1">
<text>Consequently, the shuttle is balanced.</text>
<logic>balanced(shuttle)</logic>
</step>

Therefore Shuttle is balanced, so the conclusion is True.
</think>
Answer: True

examples.context: In the library, the collection named book_cole is fully stocked. The librarian named librarian_phoenix is well_maintained, and the shelf called shelf_river is also well_maintained. Any item that is stocked is automatically searchable, so because book_cole is stocked it is searchable. Whenever something is logical, it is also thorough; thus any logical entity becomes thorough. Any entity that is well_maintained is logical, so a well_maintained librarian is logical. If something is precise, then it cannot be thorough; that is, being precise guarantees not being thorough. A dedicated status follows from being responsive, so any responsive item is dedicated. Either book_cole is not searchable or it is responsive.
examples.question: Is it true that librarian_phoenix is not precise?
examples.completion:
<think>
We need to determine whether the librarian identified as librarian_phoenix lacks the property of being precise. The goal is to show that the negation of precise holds for this individual, using the facts and rules provided in the context.

This premise gives us a conditional link between being logical and being thorough for any entity. Recognizing this connection will let us later turn a logical status into a thorough one for the librarian.
<step n="1" type="premise">
<text>The rule states that any logical librarian becomes thorough.</text>
<logic>thorough(librarian_phoenix) :- logical(librarian_phoenix)</logic>
</step>

Here we have a rule that upgrades any well_maintained status to a logical one. Since we already know the librarian is well_maintained, this rule is crucial for moving forward in the chain of inference.
<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>The rule asserts that any well_maintained entity is logical.</text>
<logic>logical(librarian_phoenix) :- well_maintained(librarian_phoenix)</logic>
</step>

The third rule tells us that being precise forces the opposite of thoroughness. This will be the key to deriving the negation of precise from a known thorough status.
<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>The rule declares that if something is precise, then it is not thorough.</text>
<logic>~thorough(librarian_phoenix) :- precise(librarian_phoenix)</logic>
</step>

The fact that book_cole is stocked is part of the background information, establishing that the collection satisfies the stocked condition mentioned in the context. Although not directly used in the later derivation, it fulfills the requirement to state every fact.
<step n="4" type="premise">
<text>It is given that book_cole is stocked.</text>
<logic>stocked(book_cole)</logic>
</step>

The statement that shelf_river is well_maintained provides another concrete example of the well_maintained predicate, again satisfying the completeness of the context.
<step n="5" type="premise">
<text>It is given that shelf_river is well_maintained.</text>
<logic>well_maintained(shelf_river)</logic>
</step>

The explicit fact that librarian_phoenix is well_maintained is the starting point for our reasoning about the librarian's logical and thorough properties.
<step n="6" type="premise">
<text>It is given that librarian_phoenix is well_maintained.</text>
<logic>well_maintained(librarian_phoenix)</logic>
</step>

From PREMISE_6 we know librarian_phoenix is well_maintained, and by applying PREMISE_2 (well_maintained implies logical) we can conclude that librarian_phoenix is logical. This step bridges the factual status of the librarian to the logical predicate required for the next inference.
<step n="7" type="derived" from="6,2">
<text>From the fact that librarian_phoenix is well_maintained and the rule that well_maintained implies logical, we infer that librarian_phoenix is logical.</text>
<logic>logical(librarian_phoenix)</logic>
</step>

Having established that librarian_phoenix is logical, we now use PREMISE_1, which says logical leads to thorough. By modus ponens, we infer that librarian_phoenix is thorough. This gives us a concrete positive property that will later clash with the consequence of being precise.
<step n="8" type="derived" from="7,1">
<text>Using the fact that librarian_phoenix is logical together with the rule that logical implies thorough, we infer that librarian_phoenix is thorough.</text>
<logic>thorough(librarian_phoenix)</logic>
</step>

We now have librarian_phoenix as thorough (from STEP_8). PREMISE_3 tells us that if something were precise, it could not be thorough. Since we already have thoroughness, the only way to avoid contradiction is to assert that librarian_phoenix is not precise. This uses the contrapositive reasoning inherent in the rule, yielding the desired negation.
<step n="9" type="derived" from="8,3">
<text>Since librarian_phoenix is thorough and the rule that precise implies not thorough, we conclude that librarian_phoenix cannot be precise.</text>
<logic>~precise(librarian_phoenix)</logic>
</step>

Therefore Librarian_phoenix is not precise, so the conclusion is True.
</think>
Answer: True

examples.context: Henry is ambitious. Elena is productive. Whenever someone is ambitious, they are cautious, so Henry being ambitious means Henry is cautious. Any cautious person is compassionate, therefore Henry's caution makes him compassionate. Being compassionate guarantees confidence, so Henry is confident. If someone is dedicated, they are curious. If someone is energetic, they are dedicated. Any curious individual is punctual, so Henry would be punctual if he were curious. If a person is fit, then they are not confident. Either Henry is fit or Henry is energetic. Either Elena is not patient or Elena is methodical. Whenever someone is methodical, Elena becomes friendly. Since Elena is productive, she is patient.
examples.question: Is it true that Henry is not punctual?
examples.completion:
<think>
We are asked to evaluate whether Henry fails to be punctual. The knowledge base provides a chain of properties linking ambition to punctuality, as well as information about fitness that can block confidence. Our goal is to see if the premises force Henry to be punctual, thereby contradicting the query.

This premise gives us the first conditional link in the chain: ambition guarantees caution. It will let us move from the known fact of Henry's ambition toward later properties.
<step n="1" type="premise">
<text>The rule states that any ambitious person is also cautious, so Henry being ambitious implies Henry is cautious.</text>
<logic>cautious(henry) :- ambitious(henry)</logic>
</step>

Here we obtain the second link, turning caution into compassion. It is needed because later steps will rely on compassion to reach confidence.
<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>The rule says that any cautious person is compassionate, so Henry's caution leads to compassion.</text>
<logic>compassionate(henry) :- cautious(henry)</logic>
</step>

The third rule converts compassion into confidence. Confidence will later be important when we consider the effect of fitness on confidence.
<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>The rule declares that any compassionate person is confident, so Henry's compassion makes him confident.</text>
<logic>confident(henry) :- compassionate(henry)</logic>
</step>

This rule connects dedication to curiosity, which is essential for eventually deriving punctuality, since curiosity implies punctuality.
<step n="4" type="premise">
<text>The rule indicates that any dedicated person is curious, establishing a link from dedication to curiosity for Henry.</text>
<logic>curious(henry) :- dedicated(henry)</logic>
</step>

The fifth rule tells us that energetic individuals are dedicated. It will become useful after we establish that Henry is energetic.
<step n="5" type="premise">
<text>The rule tells us that any energetic person is dedicated, so if Henry were energetic he would be dedicated.</text>
<logic>dedicated(henry) :- energetic(henry)</logic>
</step>

The sixth rule provides the final bridge from curiosity to punctuality, allowing us to conclude punctuality once we have curiosity.
<step n="6" type="premise">
<text>The rule asserts that any curious person is punctual, so curiosity would give Henry punctuality.</text>
<logic>punctual(henry) :- curious(henry)</logic>
</step>

The seventh rule offers a way to infer the negation of fitness from confidence: if Henry were fit, he would not be confident. This will let us eliminate the fit alternative later.
<step n="7" type="premise">
<text>The rule says that if someone is fit then they are not confident, providing a way to deny confidence through fitness.</text>
<logic>~confident(henry) :- fit(henry)</logic>
</step>

The disjunction states that Henry must be either fit or energetic. By ruling out one option we can force the other, which is key to unlocking the dedication‑curiosity chain.
<step n="8" type="premise">
<text>The disjunction states that Henry is either fit or energetic.</text>
<logic>fit(henry) v energetic(henry)</logic>
</step>

The fact that Elena is productive is unrelated to Henry's punctuality but is part of the full knowledge base, ensuring the context is complete.
<step n="9" type="premise">
<text>The fact records that Elena is productive.</text>
<logic>productive(elena)</logic>
</step>

The fact that Henry is ambitious is the starting point for the forward reasoning chain that leads to punctuality.
<step n="10" type="premise">
<text>The fact records that Henry is ambitious.</text>
<logic>ambitious(henry)</logic>
</step>

Using the fact that Henry is ambitious together with Premise 1, we apply modus ponens to obtain that Henry is cautious. This moves us one step forward along the chain.
<step n="11" type="derived" from="10,1">
<text>From Henry's ambition and the rule that ambition leads to caution, we infer that Henry is cautious.</text>
<logic>cautious(henry)</logic>
</step>

Now that we have Henry's caution, Premise 2 lets us infer compassion via modus ponens. Compassion is the next link toward confidence.
<step n="12" type="derived" from="11,2">
<text>Given Henry is cautious and the rule that cautiousness entails compassion, we conclude Henry is compassionate.</text>
<logic>compassionate(henry)</logic>
</step>

With compassion established, Premise 3 gives us confidence. At this point we have a property (confidence) that will help us address the fitness alternative.
<step n="13" type="derived" from="12,3">
<text>Since Henry is compassionate and compassion guarantees confidence, Henry is confident.</text>
<logic>confident(henry)</logic>
</step>

Since we know Henry is confident, Premise 7 tells us that if he were fit he would not be confident. By contraposition (modus tollens) we conclude that Henry cannot be fit.
<step n="14" type="derived" from="13,7">
<text>Because Henry is confident and the rule that being fit would make one not confident, we deduce that Henry cannot be fit.</text>
<logic>~fit(henry)</logic>
</step>

Having eliminated the fit possibility and knowing from Premise 8 that Henry must be either fit or energetic, we must conclude that Henry is energetic. This is a classic disjunctive syllogism.
<step n="15" type="derived" from="14,8">
<text>With the knowledge that Henry is not fit and the disjunction that Henry is either fit or energetic, we infer that Henry must be energetic.</text>
<logic>energetic(henry)</logic>
</step>

From Henry's energetic status, Premise 5 allows us to deduce that he is dedicated, because energetic individuals are dedicated.
<step n="16" type="derived" from="15,5">
<text>From Henry being energetic and the rule that energetic people are dedicated, we derive that Henry is dedicated.</text>
<logic>dedicated(henry)</logic>
</step>

With dedication in hand, Premise 4 yields curiosity, as dedication leads to curiosity.
<step n="17" type="derived" from="16,4">
<text>Given Henry is dedicated and the rule that dedication leads to curiosity, we obtain that Henry is curious.</text>
<logic>curious(henry)</logic>
</step>

Finally, Premise 6 turns curiosity into punctuality, so we derive that Henry is punctual.
<step n="18" type="derived" from="17,6">
<text>Since Henry is curious and curiosity leads to punctuality, we determine that Henry is punctual.</text>
<logic>punctual(henry)</logic>
</step>

We derived Henry is punctual, so the conclusion Henry is not punctual is False.
</think>
Answer: False

examples.question: Is model_rhea not creative?
examples.completion:

We are given a collection of fashion‑related facts and conditional statements about designer_sarah and model_rhea. The goal is to determine whether the statement that model_rhea is not creative can be logically derived from these premises.

This rule connects determination with avant‑garde style, so if we ever establish that model_rhea is determined, we can immediately infer she is avant‑garde. It will be useful later when we need to link determination to timeliness.

Here we see that chicness guarantees determination. Proving that model_rhea is chic will therefore give us a path to her being determined, and subsequently avant‑garde.

</step>

The rule tells us that avant‑garde status forces timeliness. Once we have avant‑garde, we can obtain timeliness, which will be crucial for applying the rule about creativity.

Trend‑sensitivity directly yields methodical behavior. Since we already have the fact that model_rhea is trend‑sensitive, this rule will let us derive that she is methodical.

This conditional says that if model_rhea were creative, she could not be timely. Later, when we obtain timeliness, we will be able to use modus tollens to conclude non‑creativity.
<step n="5" type="premise">
<text>The rule asserts that if model_rhea is creative, then she is not timely.</text>
<logic>~timely(model_rhea) :- creative(model_rhea)</logic>
</step>

The rule links sustainability to a lack of methodical behavior. By showing model_rhea is methodical, we will be able to infer that she cannot be sustainable, which will feed into the disjunction.
<step n="6" type="premise">
<text>The rule claims that if model_rhea is sustainable, then she is not methodical.</text>
<logic>~methodical(model_rhea) :- sustainable(model_rhea)</logic>
</step>

The disjunction offers two possibilities for model_rhea: either she is sustainable or she is chic. If we can eliminate one option, the other must hold, giving us a route to chicness.
<step n="7" type="premise">
<text>The disjunction tells us that model_rhea is either sustainable or chic.</text>
<logic>sustainable(model_rhea) v chic(model_rhea)</logic>
</step>

The fact that designer_sarah is on the runway establishes her as active, which later allows us to infer she is detail‑oriented, though that detail is not directly needed for the final query.
<step n="8" type="premise">
<text>Fact: designer_sarah is on the runway.</text>

The fact that model_rhea is trend‑sensitive is the starting point for deriving her methodical nature via Premise 4.

</step>

Combining the fact that model_rhea is trend‑sensitive with the rule that trend‑sensitivity leads to methodical behavior, we can directly conclude she is methodical. This gives us a concrete property that will interact with the sustainability rule.
<step n="10" type="derived" from="9,4">

We now have that model_rhea is methodical, while Premise 6 tells us that sustainability would make her not methodical. Applying modus tollens, the only way the conditional can hold is if she is not sustainable. This eliminates one side of the disjunction.
<step n="11" type="derived" from="10,6">

</step>

Since we have shown that model_rhea is not sustainable, the disjunction from Premise 7 forces the remaining alternative: she must be chic. This uses disjunctive syllogism to obtain chicness, which is a key step toward determination.
<step n="12" type="derived" from="11,7">
<text>Given that model_rhea is not sustainable and the disjunction that she is either sustainable or chic, we deduce via disjunctive syllogism that she must be chic.</text>
<logic>chic(model_rhea)</logic>

With chicness established, Premise 2 gives us that chicness guarantees determination. Therefore, we can infer that model_rhea is determined, moving us closer to avant‑garde status.
<step n="13" type="derived" from="12,2">
<text>Since model_rhea is chic and the rule that chic leads to being determined, we infer that she is determined.</text>

We have timeliness, and Premise 5 says that if she were creative she would not be timely. Using modus tollens, the presence of timeliness forces the negation of creativity, so we conclude she is not creative.

examples.question: Is Consumer Jane not brand-loyal?

<step n="4" type="premise">

<logic>price_anxious(consumer_jane)</logic>
</step>

Applying modus ponens, Jane's price-anxiety together with the first rule yields that she must be detail-precise. This derived fact bridges the gap between her anxiety and the next conditional about budgeting.
<step n="5" type="derived" from="4,1">

<logic>detail_precise(consumer_jane)</logic>
</step>

Now that we know Jane is detail-precise, we again use modus ponens with the second rule to conclude that she is budget-prepared. This step gives us the concrete property that will clash with the loyalty rule.
<step n="6" type="derived" from="5,2">
<text>Given her detail-precision and the second rule, we infer that she is budget-prepared.</text>
<logic>budget_prepared(consumer_jane)</logic>
</step>

We have Jane being budget-prepared, but the loyalty rule says any brand-loyal consumer would be not budget-prepared. By modus tollens, the presence of budget preparedness forces the denial of brand loyalty, so Jane cannot be brand-loyal.
<step n="7" type="derived" from="6,3">

<logic>~brand_loyal(consumer_jane)</logic>
</step>

Therefore Consumer_jane is not brand loyal, so the conclusion is True.
</think>
Answer: True

examples.context: Neil is detail_oriented. Whenever something is self_assessing, it is steady, so if Neil is self_assessing then Neil is steady. Either Neil is not detail_oriented, or Neil is robust.
examples.question: Is Neil data_focused?
examples.completion:
<think>
We need to decide whether Neil is data_focused. The proof will use the given facts, rules, and disjunctions to see if the statement can be supported or contradicted.

The rule linking self_assessment to steadiness will later allow us to move from a self_assessing status to a steady one. It is relevant because we will eventually establish Neil's self_assessment.
<step n="1" type="premise">
<text>If Neil is self_assessing, then Neil is steady.</text>

</step>

This rule tells us that resilience to change gives us self_assessment. It will be used after we know Neil is resilient, which we will derive from the disjunctions.
<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>If Neil is resilient_to_change, then Neil is self_assessing.</text>
<logic>self_assessing(neil) :- resilient_to_change(neil)</logic>
</step>

This conditional connects being data_focused with not being satisfied. It will be crucial for the final step where we compare Neil's satisfaction status with the possibility of being data_focused.
<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>If Neil is data_focused, then Neil is not satisfied.</text>
<logic>~satisfied(neil) :- data_focused(neil)</logic>
</step>

The rule states that being fully_equipped prevents robustness. It will help us eliminate the fully_equipped option later on.
<step n="4" type="premise">
<text>If Neil is fully_equipped, then Neil is not robust.</text>
<logic>~robust(neil) :- fully_equipped(neil)</logic>
</step>

The disjunction offers two alternatives: Neil is either fully_equipped or resilient. This will become a key point when we rule out one of the options.
<step n="5" type="premise">
<text>Neil is either fully_equipped or resilient_to_change.</text>
<logic>fully_equipped(neil) v resilient_to_change(neil)</logic>
</step>

This disjunction gives us a way to link steadiness with satisfaction. Once we have steadiness, we can infer satisfaction using this premise.
<step n="6" type="premise">
<text>Either Neil is not steady, or Neil is satisfied.</text>
<logic>~steady(neil) v satisfied(neil)</logic>
</step>

Here we have a choice between not being detail_oriented or being robust. Combined with the fact that Neil is detail_oriented, it forces us to conclude robustness.
<step n="7" type="premise">
<text>Either Neil is not detail_oriented, or Neil is robust.</text>
<logic>~detail_oriented(neil) v robust(neil)</logic>
</step>

The fact that Neil is detail_oriented is the starting point for the first derivation. It interacts directly with the previous disjunction to produce a new property.
<step n="8" type="premise">
<text>Neil is detail_oriented.</text>
<logic>detail_oriented(neil)</logic>
</step>

Because Neil is detail_oriented and the disjunction says either he is not detail_oriented or he is robust, the only way to satisfy the disjunction is to accept that Neil is robust. This is a classic use of disjunctive syllogism.
<step n="9" type="derived" from="8,7">
<text>From Neil being detail_oriented and the disjunction that either he is not detail_oriented or he is robust, we infer that Neil is robust.</text>
<logic>robust(neil)</logic>
</step>

We now know Neil is robust. The rule about fully_equipped says that if Neil were fully_equipped, he could not be robust. Since he is robust, the only consistent conclusion is that Neil is not fully_equipped. This is a modus tollens step.
<step n="10" type="derived" from="9,4">
<text>Since Neil is robust and if Neil were fully_equipped he would not be robust, we conclude that Neil is not fully_equipped.</text>
<logic>~fully_equipped(neil)</logic>
</step>

With the knowledge that Neil is not fully_equipped, the earlier disjunction (fully_equipped or resilient) leaves resilient as the only viable alternative. Therefore, Neil must be resilient_to_change.
<step n="11" type="derived" from="10,5">
<text>Given that Neil is not fully_equipped and that he is either fully_equipped or resilient_to_change, we deduce that Neil is resilient_to_change.</text>
<logic>resilient_to_change(neil)</logic>
</step>

Having established that Neil is resilient_to_change, we can apply the rule that resilience implies self_assessment. This gives us that Neil is self_assessing.
<step n="12" type="derived" from="11,2">
<text>Because Neil is resilient_to_change and the rule that resilience implies self_assessment, we obtain that Neil is self_assessing.</text>
<logic>self_assessing(neil)</logic>
</step>

Now that Neil is self_assessing, the rule linking self_assessment to steadiness lets us infer that Neil is steady.
<step n="13" type="derived" from="12,1">
<text>With Neil self_assessing and the rule that self-assessment leads to steadiness, we get that Neil is steady.</text>
<logic>steady(neil)</logic>
</step>

Neil's steadiness activates the disjunction that either he is not steady or he is satisfied. Since he is steady, the only way for the disjunction to hold is that Neil is satisfied.
<step n="14" type="derived" from="13,6">
<text>Neil is steady and from the disjunction either not steady or satisfied, we infer that Neil is satisfied.</text>
<logic>satisfied(neil)</logic>
</step>

We have derived that Neil is satisfied. The rule about data_focused says that if Neil were data_focused, he would not be satisfied. Because he is satisfied, we must conclude that Neil is not data_focused, using modus tollens.
<step n="15" type="derived" from="14,3">
<text>Neil is satisfied and the rule that data_focused would make him not satisfied, so we infer that Neil is not data_focused.</text>
<logic>~data_focused(neil)</logic>
</step>

We derived Neil is not data focused, so the conclusion Neil is data focused is False.
</think>
Answer: False

We have Daphne as cautious, while the rule says that if someone were experienced they would be not cautious. Using the contrapositive (modus tollens), the fact that Daphne is cautious forces the conclusion that she cannot be experienced.
<step n="19" type="derived" from="18,8">

The resourceful‑to‑organized rule indicates that any resourceful individual becomes organized. Since we have a disjunction that Max is either diligent or resourceful, this rule could allow us to infer organized(max) if the resourceful alternative holds.

<logic>prepared(emma) :- curious(emma)</logic>
</step>

The rule about diligence producing a negated analytical property is important because it creates a conflict: if Max were diligent, we would have both analytical(max) (from respect) and not analytical(max). This tension will affect whether we can consistently assign the diligent alternative.
<step n="7" type="premise">

<logic>~analytical(max) :- diligent(max)</logic>
</step>

Similarly, the strategic‑to‑not‑organized rule tells us that a strategic Max would contradict any organized(max) we might obtain from resourcefulness. This will be relevant when we examine the strategic versus open‑minded disjunction.
<step n="8" type="premise">
<text>If Max is strategic, then Max is not organized.</text>
<logic>~organized(max) :- strategic(max)</logic>
</step>

The explicit fact that Max is respectful activates Premise 1, allowing us to derive analytical(max) directly. This concrete piece of information is a cornerstone for the subsequent reasoning about Max's other attributes.
<step n="9" type="premise">

<logic>respectful(max)</logic>

The fact that Emma is vigorous triggers Premise 4, giving us fit(emma). This derived fact will interact with the disjunction concerning Emma's fitness and curiosity.

<logic>vigorous(emma)</logic>

Answer: Unknown

examples.context: Wade is rational, and he is also resilient. Whenever Wade is registered, he becomes resourceful. Normally, if Wade is resourceful and there is no abnormal reason to doubt his ambition, then Wade is ambitious. In the usual case, if Wade is resilient and there is no abnormal reason to doubt his registration, then Wade is registered. There are no other alternatives or exceptions mentioned.
examples.question: Is Wade ambitious?
examples.completion:

We need to determine whether Wade can be considered ambitious based on the information provided. The goal is to trace how the given facts and default rules lead to that conclusion.

The first premise gives us a direct implication: registration guarantees resourcefulness for Wade. This will become useful once we can establish Wade's registration status.

<logic>resourceful(wade) :- registered(wade)</logic>
</step>

The second premise is a default rule that links resourcefulness to ambition, provided there is no abnormality preventing ambition. This rule will allow us to derive ambition once we have resourcefulness.
<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>A default rule says that if Wade is resourceful and there is no abnormality concerning ambition, then Wade is ambitious.</text>
<logic>ambitious(wade) :- resourceful(wade), not ab_ambitious(wade)</logic>
</step>

The third premise is another default rule that turns resilience into registration, again assuming no abnormality blocks registration. Since we already know Wade is resilient, this premise is the key to obtaining registration.
<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>Another default rule says that if Wade is resilient and there is no abnormality concerning registration, then Wade is registered.</text>
<logic>registered(wade) :- resilient(wade), not ab_registered(wade)</logic>
</step>

<step n="4" type="premise">
<text>It is given that Wade is rational.</text>
<logic>rational(wade)</logic>
</step>

<step n="5" type="premise">

</step>

Using Wade's known resilience and the default rule that resilience (without abnormality) yields registration, we can safely infer that Wade is registered. The lack of any abnormality condition in the context lets us apply the rule straightforwardly.
<step n="6" type="derived" from="5,3">
<text>From Wade's resilience and the default registration rule we infer that Wade is registered.</text>
<logic>registered(wade)</logic>
</step>

Having established Wade's registration, we can now apply the first rule which says registration implies resourcefulness. This logical step follows directly from the rule, giving us that Wade is resourceful.
<step n="7" type="derived" from="6,1">
<text>Since Wade is now known to be registered, the registration‑to‑resourceful rule lets us conclude that Wade is resourceful.</text>
<logic>resourceful(wade)</logic>

With Wade confirmed as resourceful and no abnormality noted for ambition, the default ambition rule activates, allowing us to conclude that Wade is ambitious. The chain of reasoning is now complete.
<step n="8" type="derived" from="7,2">

Therefore Wade is ambitious, so the conclusion is True.

examples.context: In the forensic lab, analyst Petra is methodical. Detective Quinn is also methodical, and technician Rio is methodical as well. The lab’s policy states that if analyst Petra is not evidence‑focused, then she is considered conclusive. Conversely, whenever analyst Petra is evidence‑focused, the policy says she is not methodical. These are the only rules governing Petra’s traits, and there are no other disjunctions or defaults to consider.
examples.question: Is analyst Petra conclusive?
examples.completion:
<think>
We are asked to determine whether analyst Petra can be considered conclusive according to the lab’s policy. The goal is to use the given facts and rules to see if the condition for her being conclusive is satisfied.

The first rule tells us the exact condition under which Petra would be labeled conclusive: she must not be evidence‑focused. Keeping this in mind will allow us to link any information about her evidence‑focus status to the desired conclusion.
<step n="1" type="premise">
<text>If analyst Petra is not evidence‑focused, then she is conclusive.</text>
<logic>conclusive(analyst_petra) :- ~evidence_focused(analyst_petra)</logic>
</step>

The second rule provides a way to reason about Petra’s evidence‑focus by connecting it to her methodical trait. If we can show she is methodical, this rule will help us rule out the possibility that she is evidence‑focused.
<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>If analyst Petra is evidence‑focused, then she is not methodical.</text>
<logic>~methodical(analyst_petra) :- evidence_focused(analyst_petra)</logic>
</step>

The fact that detective Quinn is methodical serves to populate the laboratory’s roster with other methodical individuals, but it does not directly affect Petra’s status. It is included to satisfy the requirement that all given facts be noted.
<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>Detective Quinn is methodical.</text>
<logic>methodical(detective_quinn)</logic>
</step>

Similarly, the fact that technician Rio is methodical adds another methodical team member to the context. While not directly used later, it ensures the context is complete.
<step n="4" type="premise">
<text>Technician Rio is methodical.</text>
<logic>methodical(technician_rio)</logic>
</step>

We are explicitly told that analyst Petra herself is methodical. This fact is crucial because it will interact with the second rule to eliminate the alternative that she might be evidence‑focused.
<step n="5" type="premise">
<text>Analyst Petra is methodical.</text>
<logic>methodical(analyst_petra)</logic>
</step>

Because Petra is methodical, the second rule indicates that if she were evidence‑focused, she would have to be not methodical, which contradicts the fact we just noted. By applying modus tollens, we conclude that Petra cannot be evidence‑focused, giving us the statement that she is not evidence‑focused.
<step n="6" type="derived" from="5,2">
<text>Since analyst Petra is methodical and the rule says that being evidence‑focused would make her not methodical, we infer that she cannot be evidence‑focused; therefore, she is not evidence‑focused.</text>
<logic>~evidence_focused(analyst_petra)</logic>
</step>

Now that we have established Petra is not evidence‑focused, the first rule becomes applicable: the absence of evidence‑focus directly implies that she is conclusive. Thus we can deduce her conclusive status without further information.
<step n="7" type="derived" from="6,1">
<text>Having established that analyst Petra is not evidence‑focused, the earlier rule directly yields that she must be conclusive.</text>
<logic>conclusive(analyst_petra)</logic>
</step>

Therefore Analyst_petra is conclusive, so the conclusion is True.
</think>
Answer: True

examples.context: In this musical setting, the maestro is rhythmic. Whenever the maestro plays in a staccato manner, his performance is melodic. If the maestro ever sounds off_key, his melody ceases, so he is not melodic. Moreover, either the maestro is not rhythmic or he plays staccato. No other default assumptions are made.
examples.question: Can we conclude that the maestro is not off_key?
examples.completion:
<think>
We are asked to decide whether the maestro can be said to be not off_key. To answer this, we will follow the chain of musical properties given in the context, moving from what is known about rhythm to conclusions about melody and keyness.

The first rule tells us that staccato playing guarantees a melodic outcome. Recognizing this relationship will let us turn a staccato fact into a melodic one later in the proof.
<step n="1" type="premise">
<text>If the maestro is staccato, then he is melodic.</text>
<logic>melodic(maestro) :- staccato(maestro)</logic>
</step>

The second rule links being off_key to the absence of melody, stating that an off_key maestro cannot be melodic. This contrapositive connection will be crucial for eliminating the possibility of off_key once we have established melody.
<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>If the maestro is off_key, then he is not melodic.</text>
<logic>~melodic(maestro) :- off_key(maestro)</logic>
</step>

The disjunction offers two alternatives: either the maestro lacks rhythm, or he plays staccato. Since we already know the maestro is rhythmic, this statement will help us isolate the staccato alternative.
<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>Either the maestro is not rhythmic or he is staccato.</text>
<logic>~rhythmic(maestro) v staccato(maestro)</logic>
</step>

The fact that the maestro is rhythmic gives us a concrete starting point. Combined with the disjunction, it allows us to discard the 'not rhythmic' option and focus on the other side of the disjunction.
<step n="4" type="premise">
<text>The maestro is rhythmic.</text>
<logic>rhythmic(maestro)</logic>
</step>

Because the maestro is rhythmic, the 'not rhythmic' part of the disjunction is false, leaving the only viable option that the maestro is staccato. This reasoning follows the standard disjunctive syllogism pattern, converting the disjunction into a definitive staccato fact.
<step n="5" type="derived" from="4,3">
<text>From the fact that the maestro is rhythmic and the disjunction, we infer that the maestro is staccato.</text>
<logic>staccato(maestro)</logic>
</step>

Having established that the maestro is staccato, we can now apply the first rule: staccato implies melodic. Thus, the maestro must be melodic, linking his rhythmic style to a melodic quality.
<step n="6" type="derived" from="5,1">
<text>Because the maestro is staccato and the rule that staccato implies melodic, we conclude the maestro is melodic.</text>
<logic>melodic(maestro)</logic>
</step>

We now have the maestro as melodic, but the second rule says an off_key maestro would be not melodic. Since the maestro is indeed melodic, the condition that would make him not melodic must be false, meaning he cannot be off_key. This is a classic use of modus tollens.
<step n="7" type="derived" from="6,2">
<text>Since the maestro is melodic and the rule that off_key would make him not melodic, we deduce that the maestro cannot be off_key.</text>
<logic>~off_key(maestro)</logic>
</step>

Therefore Maestro is not off key, so the conclusion is True.
</think>
Answer: True
