<logic>supportive(casey) :- trustworthy(casey)</logic>
</step>

Supportiveness implying systematic means that any supportive Casey will also be systematic. This will be needed to later apply the contrapositive of the astute rule.
<step n="4" type="premise">
<text>If Casey is supportive, then Casey is systematic.</text>
<logic>systematic(casey) :- supportive(casey)</logic>
</step>

The rule that cautiousness guarantees trustworthiness directly turns the given fact into a new property. This is the first step in the forward reasoning.
<step n="5" type="premise">
<text>If Casey is cautious, then Casey is trustworthy.</text>
<logic>trustworthy(casey) :- cautious(casey)</logic>
</step>

Athletic implying vigorous lets us convert athletic status into vigor, which then feeds into honesty via another rule.
<step n="6" type="premise">
<text>If Casey is athletic, then Casey is vigorous.</text>
<logic>vigorous(casey) :- athletic(casey)</logic>
</step>

The astute rule states that if Casey were astute, systematic would be false. By later showing systematic is true, we can use modus tollens to deny astuteness.
<step n="7" type="premise">
<text>If Casey is astute, then Casey is not systematic.</text>
<logic>~systematic(casey) :- astute(casey)</logic>
</step>

The disjunction offers two possibilities: Casey is either astute or determined. Once we eliminate the astute option, the other must hold.
<step n="8" type="premise">
<text>Either Casey is astute or Casey is determined.</text>
<logic>astute(casey) v determined(casey)</logic>
</step>

The second disjunction says that either Casey lacks honesty or possesses wisdom. After we establish honesty, the only remaining option is wisdom.
<step n="9" type="premise">
<text>Either Casey is not honest or Casey is wise.</text>
<logic>~honest(casey) v wise(casey)</logic>
</step>

The fact that Casey is cautious is our starting point; it triggers the trustworthiness rule and starts the chain of deductions.
<step n="10" type="premise">
<text>Casey is cautious.</text>
<logic>cautious(casey)</logic>
</step>

Applying the rule from premise 5 to the fact in premise 10, we infer that Casey must be trustworthy. This conversion is straightforward modus ponens, turning cautiousness into trust.
<step n="11" type="derived" from="10,5">
<text>From Casey being cautious and the rule that cautious implies trustworthy, we infer that Casey is trustworthy.</text>
<logic>trustworthy(casey)</logic>
</step>

Now that we have trustworthiness, premise 3 tells us that a trustworthy Casey is supportive. Using modus ponens again, we add supportiveness to Casey's profile.
<step n="12" type="derived" from="11,3">
<text>Using the fact that Casey is trustworthy together with the rule that trustworthy implies supportive, we deduce that Casey is supportive.</text>
<logic>supportive(casey)</logic>
</step>

With supportiveness established, premise 4 indicates that supportive individuals are systematic. Therefore, Casey becomes systematic, extending the chain toward the astute rule.
<step n="13" type="derived" from="12,4">
<text>Given that Casey is supportive and the rule that supportive leads to systematic, we conclude that Casey is systematic.</text>
<logic>systematic(casey)</logic>
</step>

We have systematic as true, while premise 7 says that an astute Casey would make systematic false. Since systematic holds, the only way for the implication to stay valid is for the antecedent (astute) to be false. Hence, Casey is not astute by modus tollens.
<step n="14" type="derived" from="13,7">
<text>Since Casey is systematic and the rule that an astute Casey would be not systematic, we apply modus tollens to infer that Casey is not astute.</text>
<logic>~astute(casey)</logic>
</step>

The disjunction in premise 8 leaves two options: astute or determined. Since we have just shown Casey is not astute, the remaining alternative must be true, giving us that Casey is determined.
<step n="15" type="derived" from="14,8">
<text>With the knowledge that Casey is not astute and the disjunction that Casey is either astute or determined, we resolve that Casey must be determined.</text>
<logic>determined(casey)</logic>
</step>

Having determined that Casey is determined, premise 1 allows us to conclude athletic status. This step continues the forward propagation of properties.
<step n="16" type="derived" from="15,1">
<text>Because Casey is determined and the rule that determined implies athletic, we obtain that Casey is athletic.</text>
<logic>athletic(casey)</logic>
</step>

Athletic leads to vigorous via premise 6, so Casey's newly acquired athleticism yields vigor. This prepares us to reach honesty.
<step n="17" type="derived" from="16,6">
<text>From Casey being athletic and the rule that athletic implies vigorous, we get that Casey is vigorous.</text>
<logic>vigorous(casey)</logic>
</step>

Premise 2 converts vigor into honesty, so Casey is now known to be honest. This fact will be crucial for the final disjunctive reasoning.
<step n="18" type="derived" from="17,2">
<text>Using Casey's vigor together with the rule that vigor implies honesty, we infer that Casey is honest.</text>
<logic>honest(casey)</logic>
</step>

We now possess honesty, and premise 9 offers a disjunction: either not honest or wise. Since honesty is true, the first disjunct is false, forcing the second disjunct to be true. Thus, Casey is wise.
<step n="19" type="derived" from="18,9">
<text>Given that Casey is honest and the disjunction that either Casey is not honest or wise, we apply disjunctive syllogism to conclude that Casey is wise.</text>
<logic>wise(casey)</logic>
</step>

We derived Casey is wise, so the conclusion Casey is not wise is False.
</think>
Answer: False

This fact tells us that Presley is clever, which activates the clever‑to‑knowledgeable rule and initiates the inference chain we will follow.

examples.context: The white knight is tactful. Whenever the white knight is energetic, it is aggressive. If the white knight is tactful, then it is bold. Any strategic white knight is also daring. If the white knight is bold, then it is strategic. A reliable white knight is always timely. Aggressive white knights are reliable. If the white knight is steady, then it is not daring. The white knight is either steady or energetic (or possibly both).
examples.question: Is the white knight timely?
examples.completion:
<think>
We are given a collection of properties about the white knight and several conditional relationships among them. Our aim is to determine whether the white knight is timely. To do this, we will chain the implications and use the disjunctive information to eventually reach the desired property.

<step n="1" type="premise">
<text>If the white knight is energetic, then it is aggressive.</text>
<logic>aggressive(white_knight) :- energetic(white_knight)</logic>
</step>

<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>If the white knight is tactful, then it is bold.</text>
<logic>bold(white_knight) :- tactful(white_knight)</logic>
</step>

<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>If the white knight is strategic, then it is daring.</text>
<logic>daring(white_knight) :- strategic(white_knight)</logic>
</step>

<step n="4" type="premise">
<text>If the white knight is bold, then it is strategic.</text>
<logic>strategic(white_knight) :- bold(white_knight)</logic>
</step>

<step n="5" type="premise">
<text>If the white knight is reliable, then it is timely.</text>
<logic>timely(white_knight) :- reliable(white_knight)</logic>
</step>

<step n="6" type="premise">
<text>If the white knight is aggressive, then it is reliable.</text>
<logic>reliable(white_knight) :- aggressive(white_knight)</logic>
</step>

<step n="7" type="premise">
<text>If the white knight is steady, then it is not daring.</text>
<logic>~daring(white_knight) :- steady(white_knight)</logic>
</step>

<step n="8" type="premise">
<text>The white knight is either steady or energetic.</text>
<logic>steady(white_knight) v energetic(white_knight)</logic>
</step>

<step n="9" type="premise">
<text>The white knight is tactful.</text>
<logic>tactful(white_knight)</logic>
</step>

Starting with the fact that the white knight is tactful, we look at the rule that any tactful piece is bold. By applying this implication, we can directly infer that the white knight possesses boldness. This establishes the first link in our chain toward the target property.
<step n="10" type="derived" from="9,2">
<text>Since the white knight is tactful and tactful implies bold, we conclude the white knight is bold.</text>
<logic>bold(white_knight)</logic>
</step>

Having established boldness, we turn to the rule that bold pieces are strategic. The implication allows us to conclude that the white knight is strategic. This new strategic attribute will be useful for the next steps.
<step n="11" type="derived" from="10,4">
<text>Because the white knight is bold and bold implies strategic, the white knight is strategic.</text>
<logic>strategic(white_knight)</logic>
</step>

The strategic nature of the white knight triggers the rule that strategic pieces are daring. By invoking this, we determine that the white knight is daring. This fact will later help us eliminate the alternative of being steady.
<step n="12" type="derived" from="11,3">
<text>Given the white knight is strategic and strategic implies daring, we infer the white knight is daring.</text>
<logic>daring(white_knight)</logic>
</step>

We also have a rule stating that a steady piece cannot be daring. Since we have just shown the knight is daring, the only way to avoid a contradiction is to reject the possibility that the knight is steady. Hence we infer that the knight is not steady.
<step n="13" type="derived" from="12,7">
<text>The white knight is daring, but if the knight were steady it would not be daring; therefore the knight cannot be steady, i.e., it is not steady.</text>
<logic>~steady(white_knight)</logic>
</step>

The disjunction tells us the knight is either steady or energetic. Because we have just ruled out steadiness, the only remaining option is that the knight is energetic. Therefore we conclude the knight is energetic.
<step n="14" type="derived" from="13,8">
<text>With the knowledge that the knight is not steady and that the knight is either steady or energetic, we deduce the knight must be energetic.</text>
<logic>energetic(white_knight)</logic>
</step>

The energetic property activates the rule that energetic pieces are aggressive. Applying this, we deduce that the white knight is aggressive.
<step n="15" type="derived" from="14,1">
<text>Since the knight is energetic and energetic implies aggressive, the knight is aggressive.</text>
<logic>aggressive(white_knight)</logic>
</step>

Aggressiveness leads to reliability via the corresponding rule. Consequently, the white knight is reliable.
<step n="16" type="derived" from="15,6">
<text>Because the knight is aggressive and aggressive implies reliable, the knight is reliable.</text>
<logic>reliable(white_knight)</logic>
</step>

Reliability directly yields timeliness according to the final rule. Thus we finally derive that the white knight is timely.
<step n="17" type="derived" from="16,5">
<text>From the knight being reliable and reliable implying timely, we obtain that the knight is timely.</text>
<logic>timely(white_knight)</logic>
</step>

Therefore White_knight is timely, so the conclusion is True.
</think>
Answer: True

We are asked to determine whether investor_beta lacks the property of being well_informed. To answer, we will trace the given economic traits through the chain of conditional statements and disjunctions.

The rule linking aggression to being well_capitalized is needed because later we will establish that investor_beta is aggressive, allowing us to infer a financial strength property. This connection will feed into subsequent steps toward experience.

The implication from neutrality to risk_tolerance will be used after we obtain neutrality for investor_beta. It helps us move from a neutral stance to a more daring financial attitude.

Cooperation follows from being risk_tolerant, so once we have risk_tolerance we can claim investor_beta is cooperative. This fact will later interact with the ethical‑non‑cooperation rule.
<step n="3" type="premise">

<logic>cooperative(investor_beta) :- risk_tolerant(investor_beta)</logic>

Innovative implies neutral, which is the first bridge from the given fact that investor_beta is innovative to the chain of properties. Establishing neutrality is essential for the next implication.
<step n="4" type="premise">
<text>If a person is innovative, they are neutral.</text>

Well_capitalized leads to experience, so after we prove investor_beta is well_capitalized we can claim they are experienced. Experience is required to obtain quick_wittedness.

Experience yields quick_wittedness, a property that participates in the final disjunction about being well_informed. This step will allow us to resolve that disjunction.
<step n="6" type="premise">

<logic>quick_witted(investor_beta) :- experienced(investor_beta)</logic>

<step n="7" type="premise">

<step n="16" type="derived" from="15,8">

<step n="17" type="derived" from="16,1">

<step n="18" type="derived" from="17,5">

Motivation implies open‑mindedness. After we get motivation for Lyric, this rule will give us open‑mindedness, which participates in a key disjunction about evidence‑drivenness.

Neil’s talent similarly validates the talent‑to‑motivation rule.

Whenever something is timely_reported, it is research_funded. Whenever something is research_funded, it is structurally_intact. Whenever something is carefully_excavated, it is not structurally_intact. If the ancient vase is well_preserved, then it is meticulously_documented. If the ancient vase is artistically_exquisite, then it is expertly_restored. Either the ancient vase is not meticulously_documented or it is artistically_exquisite.
examples.question: Is the buried tomb not carefully_excavated?
examples.completion:
<think>
We are asked to determine whether the buried tomb is not carefully_excavated. To answer this, we need to trace the chain of conditions that connect the tomb's known properties to the possibility of it being carefully excavated.

The first rule tells us that structural sturdiness guarantees historical significance. This will let us turn the tomb's sturdiness into a historically significant status, which is an early link in the reasoning chain.
<step n="1" type="premise">
<text>If something is structurally_sturdy, then it is historically_significant.</text>
<logic>historically_significant(buried_tomb) :- structurally_sturdy(buried_tomb)</logic>
</step>

The second rule links research funding to structural integrity. Later we will obtain that the tomb is research_funded, and this rule will then give us structural integrity, a key fact for the final contradiction.
<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>If something is research_funded, then it is structurally_intact.</text>
<logic>structurally_intact(buried_tomb) :- research_funded(buried_tomb)</logic>
</step>

The third rule says that a timely report implies research funding. Once we know the tomb is timely_reported, we can derive that it is research_funded, moving us forward toward structural integrity.
<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>If something is timely_reported, then it is research_funded.</text>
<logic>research_funded(buried_tomb) :- timely_reported(buried_tomb)</logic>
</step>

The fourth rule converts historical significance into a timely report. After we establish the tomb is historically_significant, this rule will let us claim it is timely_reported, feeding into the previous rule.
<step n="4" type="premise">
<text>If something is historically_significant, then it is timely_reported.</text>
<logic>timely_reported(buried_tomb) :- historically_significant(buried_tomb)</logic>
</step>

The fifth rule provides a negative connection: if something is carefully_excavated, then it cannot be structurally_intact. This will be crucial for a contrapositive step that shows the tomb cannot be carefully_excavated once we have structural integrity.
<step n="5" type="premise">
<text>If something is carefully_excavated, then it is not structurally_intact.</text>
<logic>~structurally_intact(buried_tomb) :- carefully_excavated(buried_tomb)</logic>
</step>

The fact that the ancient vase is well_preserved is recorded, though it does not directly affect the tomb. It is included to satisfy the requirement that all given facts appear in the context.
<step n="6" type="premise">
<text>The ancient vase is well_preserved.</text>
<logic>well_preserved(ancient_vase)</logic>
</step>

The lost city being structurally_sturdy is another factual statement that rounds out the background information, again not directly used for the tomb's reasoning.
<step n="7" type="premise">
<text>The lost city is structurally_sturdy.</text>
<logic>structurally_sturdy(lost_city)</logic>
</step>

The buried tomb is structurally_sturdy, giving us the starting point for the forward chain of implications that will eventually lead to a contradiction with careful excavation.
<step n="8" type="premise">
<text>The buried tomb is structurally_sturdy.</text>
<logic>structurally_sturdy(buried_tomb)</logic>
</step>

Applying the rule from PREMISE_1 to the fact in PREMISE_8, we conclude the buried tomb is historically_significant. This step transforms the raw sturdiness into a property that can trigger further rules.
<step n="9" type="derived" from="8,1">
<text>From the fact that the buried tomb is structurally_sturdy and the rule linking structural sturdiness to historical significance, we infer that the buried tomb is historically_significant.</text>
<logic>historically_significant(buried_tomb)</logic>
</step>

Using the newly derived historically_significant status together with the rule from PREMISE_4, we infer that the tomb is timely_reported. This moves us one link further along the chain toward research funding.
<step n="10" type="derived" from="9,4">
<text>Because the buried tomb is historically_significant, the rule about historical significance leading to timely reporting gives us that the buried tomb is timely_reported.</text>
<logic>timely_reported(buried_tomb)</logic>
</step>

With the tomb now known to be timely_reported, the rule from PREMISE_3 allows us to deduce that it is research_funded. This is a direct application of the implication connecting reports to funding.
<step n="11" type="derived" from="10,3">
<text>With the buried tomb being timely_reported, the rule that timely reports imply research funding lets us conclude the buried tomb is research_funded.</text>
<logic>research_funded(buried_tomb)</logic>
</step>

Having established that the tomb is research_funded, we invoke PREMISE_2 to obtain that the tomb is structurally_intact. This gives us a concrete positive property that will clash with the conditional in PREMISE_5.
<step n="12" type="derived" from="11,2">
<text>Since the buried tomb is research_funded, the rule that research funding ensures structural integrity yields that the buried tomb is structurally_intact.</text>
<logic>structurally_intact(buried_tomb)</logic>
</step>

We now have the tomb being structurally_intact, while PREMISE_5 tells us that any carefully_excavated object would be not structurally_intact. By contraposition, if the tomb is structurally_intact, it cannot be carefully_excavated, leading to the conclusion that the tomb is not carefully_excavated.
<step n="13" type="derived" from="12,5">
<text>We have that the buried tomb is structurally_intact, but the rule stating that careful excavation would make something not structurally_intact allows us, via contrapositive reasoning, to deduce that the buried tomb cannot be carefully_excavated.</text>
<logic>~carefully_excavated(buried_tomb)</logic>
</step>

Therefore Buried_tomb is not carefully excavated, so the conclusion is True.
</think>
Answer: True

examples.question: Is the downtown block walkable?
examples.completion:
<think>
We are given several properties of a downtown block and a set of conditional relationships among them. Our goal is to determine whether the downtown block possesses the walkable characteristic.

The rule that a lack of green_view guarantees sustainability will be useful later when we learn the block is not green_view. It lets us turn a negative green_view fact into a positive sustainable fact.
<step n="1" type="premise">
<text>If the downtown block is not green_view, then it is sustainable.</text>
<logic>sustainable(downtown_block) :- ~green_view(downtown_block)</logic>
</step>

The conditional linking green_view to the absence of timely_maintenance connects the visual quality of the block to its upkeep schedule. When we later establish green_view, this rule will let us infer something about timely_maintenance.

The fact that the downtown block is well-lit provides the concrete positive information needed to activate the first disjunction and start the chain of deductions.

examples.context: Grace is disciplined and she is also resilient. Whenever someone is generous, that person is capable. If a person is resilient, then that person is fit. By default, if a person is fit and there is no abnormality called ab_enthusiastic, the person is enthusiastic. By default, if a person is enthusiastic and there is no abnormality called ab_generous_a, the person is generous. By default, if a person is disciplined and there is no abnormality called ab_generous_b, the person is not generous. All of these conditionals hold for Grace.
examples.question: Is Grace capable?
examples.completion:
<think>
We are asked to determine whether Grace possesses the capability property. The knowledge base supplies several facts about Grace and a network of conditional and default rules that connect generosity, fitness, enthusiasm, and capability. Our goal is to chain these pieces of information together to reach a conclusion about Grace's capability.

The rule that generous implies capable is crucial because it provides the final link we need: if we can show Grace is generous, we immediately obtain her capability. We keep this rule in mind as we work toward establishing generosity.
<step n="1" type="premise">
<text>Any generous individual is also capable, so the rule states that generous implies capable for Grace.</text>
<logic>capable(grace) :- generous(grace)</logic>
</step>

The implication from resilience to fitness is important because we already know Grace is resilient. By converting resilience into fitness, we open the path to apply the default rule that generates enthusiasm, which later leads to generosity.
<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>Being resilient guarantees that one is fit; thus resilient implies fit for Grace.</text>
<logic>fit(grace) :- resilient(grace)</logic>
</step>

This default rule tells us that fitness, together with the absence of an abnormality, yields enthusiasm. Since we will soon have Grace as fit, this rule will allow us to infer enthusiasm, a stepping stone toward generosity.
<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>If someone is fit and there is no abnormality ab_enthusiastic, then they are enthusiastic; this is a default rule.</text>
<logic>enthusiastic(grace) :- fit(grace), not ab_enthusiastic(grace)</logic>
</step>

Here we see that enthusiasm, again assuming no abnormality, produces generosity. This rule directly connects the enthusiasm we plan to derive to the generous status we need for the final capability conclusion.
<step n="4" type="premise">
<text>If someone is enthusiastic and there is no abnormality ab_generous_a, then they are generous; another default rule.</text>
<logic>generous(grace) :- enthusiastic(grace), not ab_generous_a(grace)</logic>
</step>

The competing default states that a disciplined person, absent a different abnormality, would be not generous. Because Grace is disciplined, this rule could block our attempt to prove generosity, so we must consider how the system resolves the conflict between the two defaults.
<step n="5" type="premise">
<text>If someone is disciplined and there is no abnormality ab_generous_b, then they are not generous; this default produces a negated conclusion.</text>
<logic>~generous(grace) :- disciplined(grace), not ab_generous_b(grace)</logic>
</step>

Grace being disciplined is a given fact and will activate the default that could make her not generous. This fact is therefore relevant when we later evaluate which default takes precedence.
<step n="6" type="premise">
<text>Grace is disciplined, as stated directly.</text>
<logic>disciplined(grace)</logic>
</step>

Grace's resilience is also a given fact and will trigger the rule that makes her fit. This fact is the starting point for our forward reasoning chain.
<step n="7" type="premise">
<text>Grace is resilient, as stated directly.</text>
<logic>resilient(grace)</logic>
</step>

Using the fact that Grace is resilient and the rule that resilience implies fitness, we can directly infer that Grace is fit. This follows the classic modus ponens pattern: the antecedent holds, so the consequent must hold as well.
<step n="8" type="derived" from="7,2">
<text>From Grace's resilience and the rule that resilience implies fit, we infer that Grace is fit.</text>
<logic>fit(grace)</logic>
</step>

Now that we have established Grace is fit, we apply the default rule that a fit individual becomes enthusiastic provided there is no abnormality ab_enthusiastic. Since the knowledge base does not mention any such abnormality, we can safely assume its absence and thus conclude Grace is enthusiastic.
<step n="9" type="derived" from="8,3">
<text>Because Grace is fit and there is no abnormality ab_enthusiastic, the default rule gives us that Grace is enthusiastic.</text>
<logic>enthusiastic(grace)</logic>
</step>

With Grace enthusiastic, the default rule for generosity becomes applicable, yielding Grace as generous as long as ab_generous_a is absent, which it is. At the same time, the earlier default could make her not generous because she is disciplined, but the system's priority mechanism prefers the generous conclusion when both defaults are triggered. Consequently, we adopt the generous outcome and set aside the not‑generous alternative.
<step n="10" type="derived" from="9,4,5">
<text>Grace is enthusiastic and there is no abnormality ab_generous_a, so the generous default fires; although a conflicting default could make her not generous, the system gives priority to the generous conclusion, so we conclude Grace is generous.</text>
<logic>generous(grace)</logic>
</step>

Having obtained Grace's generosity, we can finally invoke the rule that generous implies capable. By modus ponens, Grace's generosity guarantees that she is capable, completing the chain of reasoning required for the query.
<step n="11" type="derived" from="10,1">
<text>Since Grace is generous, the earlier rule that generous implies capable lets us conclude Grace is capable.</text>
<logic>capable(grace)</logic>
</step>

Therefore Grace is capable, so the conclusion is True.
</think>
Answer: True

examples.context: In the apiary, any gentle bee is diligent. Every adaptable bee is modest. Whenever a bee is scent_intuitive, it becomes hive_supportive. examples.question: Is the worker bee not hive_supportive?
examples.completion:
<think>
We are asked whether the worker bee lacks the hive_supportive quality. To answer, we will follow the chain of rules and facts provided in the context, deriving the bee's properties step by step.

The first rule tells us that gentleness guarantees diligence. This will allow us to turn the known gentle nature of the worker bee into a statement about its diligence later on.
<step n="1" type="premise">
<text>If a bee is gentle, then it is diligent.</text>
<logic>diligent(worker_bee) :- gentle(worker_bee)</logic>
</step>

The second rule links adaptability to modesty. It will become useful once we establish that the worker bee is adaptable, letting us infer modesty.
<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>If a bee is adaptable, then it is modest.</text>
<logic>modest(worker_bee) :- adaptable(worker_bee)</logic>
</step>

According to the third rule, being scent_intuitive forces a bee to be hive_supportive. This connection is crucial for establishing the final supportive status of the worker bee.
<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>If a bee is scent_intuitive, then it is hive_supportive.</text>
<logic>hive_supportive(worker_bee) :- scent_intuitive(worker_bee)</logic>
</step>

The fourth rule states that experienced bees are not diligent. This contrapositive relationship will help us eliminate the experienced possibility once we know the bee is diligent.
<step n="4" type="premise">
<text>If a bee is experienced, then it is not diligent.</text>
<logic>~diligent(worker_bee) :- experienced(worker_bee)</logic>
</step>

The context explicitly says the worker bee is either experienced or adaptable. This disjunction will be resolved once we rule out one of the options.
<step n="5" type="premise">
<text>The worker bee is experienced or adaptable.</text>
<logic>experienced(worker_bee) v adaptable(worker_bee)</logic>
</step>

We also know that the worker bee is either not modest or scent_intuitive. This alternative will let us obtain scent_intuitive once we have modestness.
<step n="6" type="premise">
<text>The worker bee is either not modest or scent_intuitive.</text>
<logic>~modest(worker_bee) v scent_intuitive(worker_bee)</logic>
</step>

The fact that the worker bee is gentle gives us a concrete starting point for applying the first rule and deriving diligence.
<step n="7" type="premise">
<text>The worker bee is gentle.</text>
<logic>gentle(worker_bee)</logic>
</step>

Combining the fact that the bee is gentle (PREMISE_7) with the rule that gentle implies diligent (PREMISE_1), we conclude the bee is diligent. This new fact sets up a clash with the experienced rule later.
<step n="8" type="derived" from="7,1">
<text>Because the worker bee is gentle and gentle bees are diligent, the worker bee is diligent.</text>
<logic>diligent(worker_bee)</logic>
</step>

We have the bee being diligent (STEP_8) and the rule that an experienced bee would be not diligent (PREMISE_4). By modus tollens, if the bee were experienced it would not be diligent, but it is diligent, so the bee cannot be experienced. This eliminates one side of the earlier disjunction.
<step n="9" type="derived" from="8,4">
<text>Since the worker bee is diligent and experienced bees would be not diligent, the worker bee cannot be experienced.</text>
<logic>~experienced(worker_bee)</logic>
</step>

Now that we know the bee is not experienced, the disjunction that it is either experienced or adaptable (PREMISE_5) forces the alternative: the bee must be adaptable. This gives us a new property to work with.
<step n="10" type="derived" from="9,5">
<text>Given that the worker bee is not experienced and that it must be either experienced or adaptable, we conclude it is adaptable.</text>
<logic>adaptable(worker_bee)</logic>
</step>

Using the adaptability just obtained (STEP_10) together with the rule that adaptable implies modest (PREMISE_2), we infer that the bee is modest. This will be needed to activate the next disjunction.
<step n="11" type="derived" from="10,2">
<text>Because the worker bee is adaptable and adaptable bees are modest, the worker bee is modest.</text>
<logic>modest(worker_bee)</logic>
</step>

We have modesty (STEP_11) and the statement that the bee is either not modest or scent_intuitive (PREMISE_6). Since the bee is modest, the first option is false, leaving scent_intuitive as the only way to satisfy the disjunction. Hence the bee is scent_intuitive.
<step n="12" type="derived" from="11,6">
<text>Since the worker bee is modest and we know that either it is not modest or it is scent_intuitive, we deduce it is scent_intuitive.</text>
<logic>scent_intuitive(worker_bee)</logic>
</step>

Finally, with the bee being scent_intuitive (STEP_12) and the rule that scent_intuitive leads to hive_supportive (PREMISE_3), we conclude the bee is hive_supportive. This directly contradicts the query's negation.
<step n="13" type="derived" from="12,3">
<text>Because the worker bee is scent_intuitive and scent_intuitive bees are hive_supportive, the worker bee is hive_supportive.</text>
<logic>hive_supportive(worker_bee)</logic>
</step>

We derived Worker_bee is hive supportive, so the conclusion Worker_bee is not hive supportive is False.
</think>
Answer: False

examples.context: In this forensic laboratory, Stella is known to be curious. Rhea is described as fair‑minded. Ari, Cole, Jack, and Lennon are all punctual. Whenever someone is fair‑minded, they are active; thus Rhea is active. Evidence‑driven behavior follows from punctuality, so any punctual person is also evidence‑driven; consequently Cole, being punctual, is evidence‑driven. If a person is evidence‑driven, they are confident; therefore Cole is confident. Confidence leads to insight, so any confident individual is insightful, making Cole insightful. Insightfulness implies vigor, so Cole is vigorous. Conversely, if someone is prepared, they cannot be vigorous; that is, any prepared individual is not vigorous. For Cole, either he is prepared or he is fit. Either Rhea is not active or Rhea is calm. Either Stella is not open‑minded or Stella is content. Curiosity makes one open‑minded, so Stella’s curiosity gives her open‑mindedness. Contentment leads to being well‑prepared, so any content individual is well‑prepared; if Stella were content, she would be well‑prepared. Calmness yields persistence, so any calm person is persistent; thus calm Rhea would be persistent. These statements constitute all the facts, rules, and alternatives governing the situation.
examples.question: Based on the given information, can we conclude that Cole is not fit?
examples.completion:
<think>
We are asked to determine whether the statement that Cole is not fit follows from the forensic laboratory facts and rules. The goal is to see if we can derive ~fit(cole) or its negation using the given premises.

<step n="1" type="premise">
<text>Any evidence‑driven individual is confident; this rule links evidence‑driven status to confidence.</text>
<logic>confident(cole) :- evidence_driven(cole)</logic>
</step>

<step n="2" type="premise">

<logic>evidence_driven(cole) :- punctual(cole)</logic>
</step>

<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>Confidence guarantees insightfulness: any confident person is insightful.</text>
<logic>insightful(cole) :- confident(cole)</logic>
</step>

<step n="4" type="premise">
<text>Insightfulness guarantees vigor: any insightful person is vigorous.</text>
<logic>vigorous(cole) :- insightful(cole)</logic>
</step>

<step n="5" type="premise">
<text>Preparedness prevents vigor: if someone is prepared, they are not vigorous.</text>
<logic>~vigorous(cole) :- prepared(cole)</logic>
</step>

<step n="6" type="premise">
<text>For Cole, either he is prepared or he is fit; this is a disjunctive fact about his state.</text>
<logic>prepared(cole) v fit(cole)</logic>
</step>

<step n="7" type="premise">
<text>Stella is curious.</text>
<logic>curious(stella)</logic>
</step>

<step n="8" type="premise">
<text>Rhea is fair‑minded.</text>
<logic>fair_minded(rhea)</logic>
</step>

<step n="9" type="premise">
<text>Ari is punctual.</text>
<logic>punctual(ari)</logic>
</step>

<step n="10" type="premise">
<text>Jack is punctual.</text>
<logic>punctual(jack)</logic>
</step>

<step n="11" type="premise">
<text>Lennon is punctual.</text>
<logic>punctual(lennon)</logic>
</step>

<step n="12" type="premise">
<text>Cole is punctual.</text>
<logic>punctual(cole)</logic>
</step>

Because Cole is listed as punctual, we can activate the rule that punctuality leads to being evidence‑driven. This gives us the intermediate fact evidence_driven(cole), which will be useful for later steps. The reasoning is straightforward application of the conditional premise to a known fact.
<step n="13" type="derived" from="12,2">
<text>From Cole being punctual and the rule that punctuality implies evidence‑driven, we infer that Cole is evidence‑driven.</text>
<logic>evidence_driven(cole)</logic>
</step>

Now that we have evidence_driven(cole), we can use the rule linking evidence‑driven status to confidence. By modus ponens, the presence of evidence_driven(cole) allows us to conclude confident(cole). This moves us one step closer to the chain that eventually reaches vigor.
<step n="14" type="derived" from="13,1">
<text>Using the rule that evidence‑driven implies confident, we conclude that Cole is confident.</text>
<logic>confident(cole)</logic>
</step>

With confident(cole) established, we invoke the rule that confidence yields insightfulness. Applying modus ponens again, we infer insightful(cole). This derived property is essential for the next implication concerning vigor.
<step n="15" type="derived" from="14,3">
<text>From confidence and the rule that confidence implies insightfulness, we deduce that Cole is insightful.</text>
<logic>insightful(cole)</logic>
</step>

Having insightful(cole), we turn to the rule that insightfulness guarantees vigor. Using modus ponens, we deduce vigorous(cole). At this point we possess a positive statement about Cole's vigor, which will later clash with a rule about preparedness.
<step n="16" type="derived" from="15,4">
<text>Given insightfulness and the rule that insightful people are vigorous, we infer that Cole is vigorous.</text>
<logic>vigorous(cole)</logic>
</step>

The rule ~vigorous(cole) :- prepared(cole) tells us that if Cole were prepared, he could not be vigorous. Since we have vigorous(cole), the contrapositive (modus tollens) lets us conclude that Cole cannot be prepared. Thus we obtain ~prepared(cole). This negation sets up the use of the disjunction involving prepared and fit.
<step n="17" type="derived" from="16,5">
<text>Since Cole is vigorous and the rule that prepared individuals are not vigorous, we apply modus tollens to obtain that Cole is not prepared.</text>
<logic>~prepared(cole)</logic>
</step>

We know from the disjunction prepared(cole) v fit(cole) that at least one of these holds. Combined with the newly derived ~prepared(cole), the only remaining option is fit(cole). By disjunctive syllogism, we therefore conclude that Cole is fit. This directly contradicts the query's claim.
<step n="18" type="derived" from="17,6">
<text>From the disjunction that Cole is prepared or fit, together with the knowledge that he is not prepared, we infer via disjunctive syllogism that Cole is fit.</text>
<logic>fit(cole)</logic>
</step>

We derived Cole is fit, so the conclusion Cole is not fit is False.
</think>
Answer: False

examples.context: In the fashion studio, the designer is expressive, the tailor is structured, and the runway model is durable. Whenever something is expressive, it is also trend_aware, and any trend_aware item is orderly. Being orderly guarantees that the item is collaborative. If an item is quick_change, then it cannot be collaborative. Either the designer is quick_change or the designer is vibrant. Whenever something is durable, it becomes practical, and any practical thing is also comprehensive. If something is comprehensive, then it is meticulous. Whenever something is tailored, it is ready, and any structured item is also stylish. Either the designer is not practical or the runway model is comprehensive, and either the tailor is not stylish or the tailor is tailored.
examples.question: Is it true that the designer is not vibrant?

<step n="2" type="premise">

examples.question: Can we conclude that the coral reef is not balanced?

The disjunction gives us a choice: either the reef is not nutrient_rich or it is thriving. When we later learn the reef is nutrient_rich, the first alternative will be ruled out, forcing the conclusion that the reef must be thriving.

The kelp forest being vibrant is a supporting fact for the overall environment, though it does not directly involve the reef. It shows that the property of vibrancy exists in the domain.

The kelp forest's thriving status illustrates that thriving can occur in this setting, which will be mirrored when we prove the reef is thriving.

examples.context: The engine block is stable. The transmission is stable. The fuel injector is maintained. The brake system is idle. Whenever the brake system is idle, it becomes overheating. If the brake system is high_performance, then it is balanced. If an engine block is not efficient, then it is optimized. Any fuel injector that is maintained is also monitored. If a fuel injector is operational, then it is cooled. If an engine block is efficient, then it is not stable. Either the brake system is not overheating or it is high_performance. Either the fuel injector is not monitored or it is operational. No other default rules apply.
examples.question: Is the engine block not optimized?
examples.completion:
<think>
We need to decide whether the engine block fails to be optimized. The goal is to evaluate the truth of the statement that the engine block is not optimized.

This premise introduces a conditional link between lack of efficiency and optimization for the engine block. It will allow us to derive optimization later once we establish non‑efficiency.
<step n="1" type="premise">
<text>The rule states that an engine block becomes optimized whenever it is not efficient.</text>
<logic>optimized(engine_block) :- ~efficient(engine_block)</logic>
</step>

Here we have a conditional that connects efficiency with the opposite of stability for the engine block. This relationship will be useful for applying a contrapositive argument.
<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>The rule says that if an engine block is efficient, then it cannot be stable.</text>
<logic>~stable(engine_block) :- efficient(engine_block)</logic>
</step>

The fact that the transmission is stable is recorded, but it does not directly affect the engine block. It is included to ensure the context mentions all given facts.
<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>The transmission is reported to be stable.</text>
<logic>stable(transmission)</logic>
</step>

The fuel injector being maintained is another factual statement. Like the transmission fact, it does not play a role in the immediate derivation but fulfills the requirement to list all premises.
<step n="4" type="premise">
<text>The fuel injector is reported to be maintained.</text>
<logic>maintained(fuel_injector)</logic>
</step>

The brake system is idle, which triggers the rule that idle components become overheating. This fact will be relevant for the disjunctions, though not for the core query.
<step n="5" type="premise">
<text>The brake system is reported to be idle.</text>
<logic>idle(brake_system)</logic>
</step>

The engine block is explicitly stated to be stable. This concrete fact is essential because it will be combined with the efficiency‑implies‑not‑stable rule to infer non‑efficiency.
<step n="6" type="premise">
<text>The engine block is reported to be stable.</text>
<logic>stable(engine_block)</logic>
</step>

Since we know the engine block is stable, and the rule tells us that an efficient engine block would be not stable, we can apply modus tollens. The only way for the engine block to remain stable is for it not to be efficient, so we conclude it is not efficient.
<step n="7" type="derived" from="6,2">
<text>Because the engine block is stable and efficiency would make it not stable, we infer that the engine block is not efficient.</text>
<logic>~efficient(engine_block)</logic>
</step>

Now that we have established the engine block is not efficient, we can use the first rule which says that lack of efficiency guarantees optimization. Consequently, the engine block must be optimized.
<step n="8" type="derived" from="7,1">
<text>Given that the engine block is not efficient, the earlier rule forces us to conclude that the engine block is optimized.</text>
<logic>optimized(engine_block)</logic>
</step>

We derived Engine_block is optimized, so the conclusion Engine_block is not optimized is False.
</think>
Answer: False

examples.context: Alice is scholarly, and Grace is also scholarly. Whenever Grace is organized, she becomes relaxed. Moreover, if Grace ever finds herself relaxed, then she can no longer be scholarly. These are the only facts and rules we know about the two people.
examples.question: Based on the given information, can we conclude that Grace is not organized?
examples.completion:
<think>
We are asked to determine whether Grace lacks the quality of being organized. The goal is to see if the premises force a contradiction with Grace being organized, allowing us to assert the opposite. To do this we will trace the implications that involve Grace's scholarly and relaxed states. By following the logical chain, we can reach a definitive answer.

The first rule tells us that organization leads to relaxation for Grace. This conditional will be useful later when we know something about Grace's relaxation status. It sets up a direct link between the two properties we care about.
<step n="1" type="premise">
<text>If Grace is organized, then she is relaxed.</text>
<logic>relaxed(grace) :- organized(grace)</logic>
</step>

The second rule states that relaxation would eliminate Grace's scholarly status. This gives us a way to connect Grace's known scholarly fact with her possible relaxation. If we ever find Grace relaxed, we would have to conclude she is not scholarly.
<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>If Grace is relaxed, then she is not scholarly.</text>
<logic>~scholarly(grace) :- relaxed(grace)</logic>
</step>

Alice being scholarly is a factual statement, but it does not directly affect Grace. It is included for completeness and to demonstrate that the scholarly predicate can apply to multiple individuals. It shows the predicate is not exclusive to Grace.
<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>Alice has the property of being scholarly.</text>
<logic>scholarly(alice)</logic>
</step>

Grace being scholarly is a crucial fact because it provides the positive side of the conditional in the second rule. Knowing she is scholarly will allow us to test the contrapositive of that rule. This fact is the starting point for the modus tollens argument.
<step n="4" type="premise">
<text>Grace has the property of being scholarly.</text>
<logic>scholarly(grace)</logic>
</step>

From the fact that Grace is scholarly and the rule that relaxation would make her not scholarly, we apply modus tollens: if relaxation implied not scholarly, and we observe scholarly, then relaxation cannot hold. Hence we derive that Grace is not relaxed. This step flips the implication by using the observed truth of the consequent's negation.
<step n="5" type="derived" from="4,2">
<text>Since Grace is scholarly and the rule says that being relaxed would make her not scholarly, we infer that Grace cannot be relaxed.</text>
<logic>~relaxed(grace)</logic>
</step>

Now we have established that Grace is not relaxed. Combining this with the first rule (organized → relaxed), we again use modus tollens: if being organized would guarantee relaxation, but relaxation is false, then Grace cannot be organized. This gives us the desired negation of the organized property.
<step n="6" type="derived" from="5,1">
<text>Given that Grace is not relaxed and the rule that organized implies relaxed, we deduce that Grace is not organized.</text>
<logic>~organized(grace)</logic>
</step>

Therefore Grace is not organized, so the conclusion is True.
</think>
Answer: True

examples.context: Cumulus_jules is intense. Whenever something is intense, it is stable. Whenever an entity is coherent, it is balanced. If something is balanced, then it is forecastable. If something is moisture_rich, then it is coherent. If something is stable, then it is structured. If something is structured, then it is pressure_respecting. If something is windy, then it is not pressure_respecting. Either Cumulus_jules is windy or it is moisture_rich.
examples.question: Is Cumulus_jules not forecastable?
examples.completion:
<think>
We are asked whether Cumulus_jules fails to be forecastable. To answer this, we need to trace the chain of properties that stem from the known fact that Cumulus_jules is intense and see if forecastability can be established. If we can derive forecastability, the negated query will be false.

<step n="1" type="premise">
<text>The rule that any intense entity is stable.</text>
<logic>stable(cumulus_jules) :- intense(cumulus_jules)</logic>
</step>

<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>The rule that any coherent entity is balanced.</text>
<logic>balanced(cumulus_jules) :- coherent(cumulus_jules)</logic>
</step>

<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>The rule that any balanced entity is forecastable.</text>
<logic>forecastable(cumulus_jules) :- balanced(cumulus_jules)</logic>
</step>

<step n="4" type="premise">
<text>The rule that any moisture_rich entity is coherent.</text>
<logic>coherent(cumulus_jules) :- moisture_rich(cumulus_jules)</logic>
</step>

<step n="5" type="premise">

<logic>structured(cumulus_jules) :- stable(cumulus_jules)</logic>
</step>

<step n="6" type="premise">
<text>The rule that any structured entity is pressure_respecting.</text>
<logic>pressure_respecting(cumulus_jules) :- structured(cumulus_jules)</logic>
</step>

<step n="7" type="premise">
<text>The rule that any windy entity is not pressure_respecting.</text>
<logic>~pressure_respecting(cumulus_jules) :- windy(cumulus_jules)</logic>
</step>

<step n="8" type="premise">
<text>The disjunction stating that Cumulus_jules is either windy or moisture_rich.</text>
<logic>windy(cumulus_jules) v moisture_rich(cumulus_jules)</logic>
</step>

<step n="9" type="premise">
<text>The fact that Cumulus_jules is intense.</text>
<logic>intense(cumulus_jules)</logic>
</step>

Starting with the fact that Cumulus_jules is intense, we apply the rule that intensity guarantees stability. This gives us a concrete new property—stability—for Cumulus_jules, which will be useful for later steps that depend on stable entities.
<step n="10" type="derived" from="9,1">
<text>From Cumulus_jules being intense and the rule that intensity implies stability, we infer that Cumulus_jules is stable.</text>
<logic>stable(cumulus_jules)</logic>
</step>

Having established that Cumulus_jules is stable, we invoke the rule linking stability to being structured. This conversion is straightforward: every stable thing is structured, so Cumulus_jules inherits the structured property.
<step n="11" type="derived" from="10,5">
<text>Because Cumulus_jules is stable and stability implies being structured, we conclude that Cumulus_jules is structured.</text>
<logic>structured(cumulus_jules)</logic>
</step>

With Cumulus_jules now known to be structured, we use the rule that structured entities are pressure_respecting. This yields the fact that Cumulus_jules respects pressure, a property that will later help us eliminate the windy alternative.
<step n="12" type="derived" from="11,6">
<text>Given that Cumulus_jules is structured and structured things are pressure_respecting, we obtain that Cumulus_jules is pressure_respecting.</text>
<logic>pressure_respecting(cumulus_jules)</logic>
</step>

The rule about windy entities states that if something is windy, it cannot be pressure_respecting. Since we already have Cumulus_jules as pressure_respecting, the only way to avoid a contradiction is to conclude that Cumulus_jules is not windy. This negative information will be crucial for resolving the earlier disjunction.
<step n="13" type="derived" from="12,7">
<text>Since Cumulus_jules is pressure_respecting and a windy entity cannot be pressure_respecting, we deduce that Cumulus_jules cannot be windy.</text>
<logic>~windy(cumulus_jules)</logic>
</step>

The original disjunction told us that Cumulus_jules is either windy or moisture_rich. Because we have just shown that Cumulus_jules cannot be windy, the disjunction forces the other option to be true: Cumulus_jules must be moisture_rich. This step narrows down the possible characteristics of Cumulus_jules dramatically.
<step n="14" type="derived" from="13,8">
<text>From the earlier knowledge that Cumulus_jules is either windy or moisture_rich, and now knowing it is not windy, we must have that Cumulus_jules is moisture_rich.</text>
<logic>moisture_rich(cumulus_jules)</logic>
</step>

Now that Cumulus_jules is moisture_rich, we apply the rule that moisture richness leads to coherence. This gives us a new attribute—coherence—for Cumulus_jules, moving us closer to the target property of forecastability.
<step n="15" type="derived" from="14,4">
<text>Because Cumulus_jules is moisture_rich and moisture_richness leads to coherence, we infer that Cumulus_jules is coherent.</text>
<logic>coherent(cumulus_jules)</logic>
</step>

Coherence, according to the next rule, guarantees that an entity is balanced. By chaining the previous result, we can assert that Cumulus_jules is balanced, setting up the final implication toward forecastability.
<step n="16" type="derived" from="15,2">
<text>From Cumulus_jules being coherent and coherence implying balance, we derive that Cumulus_jules is balanced.</text>
<logic>balanced(cumulus_jules)</logic>
</step>

The last rule in the chain states that any balanced entity is forecastable. Since we have just derived that Cumulus_jules is balanced, we can directly infer that Cumulus_jules is forecastable. This completes the forward reasoning needed to answer the query.
<step n="17" type="derived" from="16,3">
<text>Finally, since Cumulus_jules is balanced and balance guarantees forecastability, we conclude that Cumulus_jules is forecastable.</text>
<logic>forecastable(cumulus_jules)</logic>
</step>

We derived Cumulus_jules is forecastable, so the conclusion Cumulus_jules is not forecastable is False.
</think>
Answer: False

examples.context: On the chessboard, the bishop is principled, the queen is powerful, and the pawn is prepared. Any bishop that is principled is also impartial. Whenever a bishop is aggressive, it becomes daring. If a queen is powerful, then it is decisive. Whenever a queen is calculating, it is purposeful. If a pawn is resilient, then it is systematic. Whenever a pawn is cooperative, it is vigilant. If a pawn is competent, then it is not vigilant. Moreover, the pawn is either competent or flexible. The queen is either not decisive or calculating. The bishop is either not impartial or aggressive. The pawn is either not systematic or cooperative. Finally, the pawn is either not prepared or resilient.
examples.question: Can we conclude that the pawn is not flexible?
examples.completion:
<think>
We are asked to decide whether the pawn lacks flexibility. The premises give us information about the pawn's preparedness, its possible competence or flexibility, and several conditional relationships linking cooperation, vigilance, resilience, and systematic behavior. Our goal is to see if we can derive the pawn being flexible, which would make the negation false.

The first rule connects cooperation to vigilance, telling us that any pawn that is cooperative must also be vigilant. This will become useful once we know whether the pawn is cooperative.
<step n="1" type="premise">
<text>If the pawn is cooperative, then it is vigilant.</text>
<logic>vigilant(pawn) :- cooperative(pawn)</logic>
</step>

The second rule states that resilience guarantees systematic behavior. If we can prove the pawn is resilient, we will obtain systematic as a stepping stone toward cooperation.
<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>If the pawn is resilient, then it is systematic.</text>
<logic>systematic(pawn) :- resilient(pawn)</logic>
</step>

The third rule provides a contrapositive style link: a competent pawn cannot be vigilant. This will allow us to eliminate competence once we have vigilance.
<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>If the pawn is competent, then it is not vigilant.</text>
<logic>~vigilant(pawn) :- competent(pawn)</logic>
</step>

The disjunction says the pawn is either competent or flexible. This is a key choice point: if we can rule out competence, flexibility must hold.
<step n="4" type="premise">
<text>The pawn is either competent or flexible.</text>
<logic>competent(pawn) v flexible(pawn)</logic>
</step>

The fifth disjunction offers another alternative: either the pawn is not systematic or it is cooperative. Once we have systematic, this forces cooperation.
<step n="5" type="premise">
<text>Either the pawn is not systematic or it is cooperative.</text>
<logic>~systematic(pawn) v cooperative(pawn)</logic>
</step>

The sixth disjunction links preparedness to resilience, asserting that either the pawn is not prepared or it is resilient. Since we know the pawn is prepared, we can infer resilience.
<step n="6" type="premise">
<text>Either the pawn is not prepared or it is resilient.</text>
<logic>~prepared(pawn) v resilient(pawn)</logic>
</step>

The fact that the bishop is principled is recorded, though it does not directly affect the pawn, it satisfies the overall knowledge base.
<step n="7" type="premise">
<text>The bishop is principled.</text>
<logic>principled(bishop)</logic>
</step>

The fact that the queen is powerful is similarly recorded for completeness of the scenario.
<step n="8" type="premise">
<text>The queen is powerful.</text>
<logic>powerful(queen)</logic>
</step>

The pawn being prepared is a given fact that will activate the disjunction about preparedness and resilience.
<step n="9" type="premise">
<text>The pawn is prepared.</text>
<logic>prepared(pawn)</logic>
</step>

Because the pawn is prepared and the disjunction "not prepared or resilient" must be true, the only way for the disjunction to hold is that the pawn is resilient. Thus we derive resilience for the pawn.
<step n="10" type="derived" from="9,6">
<text>From the pawn being prepared and the disjunction that the pawn is not prepared or resilient, we infer that the pawn is resilient.</text>
<logic>resilient(pawn)</logic>
</step>

Now that we have resilience, we apply the rule that resilience implies systematic behavior. This gives us that the pawn is systematic, moving us closer to cooperation.
<step n="11" type="derived" from="10,2">
<text>Since the pawn is resilient and resilience implies systematic, the pawn is systematic.</text>
<logic>systematic(pawn)</logic>
</step>

With systematic established, we look at the disjunction "not systematic or cooperative". Since systematic is true, the first alternative is false, so the second alternative—cooperation—must be true. Hence the pawn is cooperative.
<step n="12" type="derived" from="11,5">
<text>Because the pawn is systematic and we have the disjunction that the pawn is not systematic or cooperative, we deduce that the pawn is cooperative.</text>
<logic>cooperative(pawn)</logic>
</step>

Having cooperation, we invoke the rule that cooperation leads to vigilance. Therefore the pawn is vigilant, a fact needed to confront the competence rule.
<step n="13" type="derived" from="12,1">
<text>Given the pawn is cooperative and the rule that cooperation yields vigilance, the pawn is vigilant.</text>
<logic>vigilant(pawn)</logic>
</step>

The rule "if competent then not vigilant" together with the derived vigilance tells us that the pawn cannot be competent; otherwise it would contradict vigilance. Consequently we infer that the pawn is not competent.
<step n="14" type="derived" from="13,3">
<text>The pawn is vigilant and the rule that competence leads to non‑vigilance together give that the pawn cannot be competent; thus it is not competent.</text>
<logic>~competent(pawn)</logic>
</step>

Finally, the disjunction "competent or flexible" combined with the knowledge that the pawn is not competent forces the remaining option: the pawn is flexible. This directly contradicts the query that the pawn is not flexible.
<step n="15" type="derived" from="14,4">
<text>With the pawn not being competent and the disjunction that the pawn is either competent or flexible, we conclude that the pawn is flexible.</text>
<logic>flexible(pawn)</logic>
</step>

We derived Pawn is flexible, so the conclusion Pawn is not flexible is False.
</think>
Answer: False

examples.context: David is known to be perceptive. Gail, Luca, Maya and Ross each show a supportive attitude toward their fellow researchers. In addition, Gail, Luca and Ross are all thorough in their data collection. Maya is also supportive. Whenever something is diligent, it is also astute, and any diligent Maya guarantees that Maya is astute. If Maya is inventive, then Maya is brilliant, and conversely any brilliant Maya makes her inventive. Consistency follows from support, so a supportive Maya is consistent. Moreover, a brilliant Maya ensures she is diligent, and a diligent Maya leads back to her being astute. An inventive Maya arises whenever Maya is thorough, and a thorough Maya appears whenever Maya is not respectful. If something is not respectful, then Maya cannot be consistent, because a respectful entity would make Maya not consistent. Rationality stems from astuteness, so any astute Maya is rational. Respectfulness blocks consistency: whenever Maya is respectful, she is not consistent. For David, logicality brings discipline, so any logical David is disciplined, and being perceptive makes David enthusiastic. Finally, regarding David, either he is not enthusiastic or he is logical. No other default rules apply.
examples.question: Is it true that Maya is not rational?
examples.completion:
<think>
We are asked to decide whether Maya lacks rationality. The knowledge base provides a chain of conditions linking Maya's supportiveness to consistency, then to respectfulness, thoroughness, inventiveness, brilliance, diligence, astuteness, and finally rationality. Our goal is to see if we can establish Maya's rationality, which would refute the claim that she is not rational. We will follow the given rules step by step.

This premise tells us that diligence implies astuteness for Maya. It will become useful later when we manage to prove Maya is diligent, allowing us to move forward to astuteness.
<step n="1" type="premise">
<text>If Maya is diligent, then Maya is astute.</text>
<logic>astute(maya) :- diligent(maya)</logic>
</step>

examples.context: In this reef ecosystem, the kelp forest is settled. The sea turtle is resilient. The clownfish is fertile. Any sea turtle that is resilient is luminescent. Whenever a kelp forest is robust, it is vibrant. If a sea turtle is a keen swimmer, then it is migratory. Being settled guarantees that the kelp forest is a reef builder. Any clownfish that is alert is also adept. Any clownfish that is fertile becomes alert. If a clownfish is nurturing, then it is not adept. Either the sea turtle is not luminescent or it is a keen swimmer. Either the kelp forest is not a reef builder or it is robust.
examples.question: Is the clownfish nurturing?
examples.completion:
<think>
We are asked to determine whether the clownfish possesses the nurturing quality. To answer this, we must trace the implications that connect the known facts about the clownfish to the possibility of it being nurturing.

The rule stating that any clownfish that is alert is also adept will allow us to infer adeptness once we have alertness. Since our goal involves checking nurturing, which negates adeptness, this rule is essential.
<step n="1" type="premise">
<text>If a clownfish is alert, then it is adept.</text>
<logic>adept(clownfish) :- alert(clownfish)</logic>
</step>

The second rule tells us that fertility guarantees alertness for a clownfish. Because we already know the clownfish is fertile, this rule will let us derive alertness, a stepping stone toward adeptness.
<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>If a clownfish is fertile, then it is alert.</text>
<logic>alert(clownfish) :- fertile(clownfish)</logic>
</step>

The third rule asserts that if a clownfish is nurturing then it cannot be adept. This provides a direct link between the property we are testing and the adeptness we will later establish, enabling a contrapositive argument.
<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>If a clownfish is nurturing, then it is not adept.</text>
<logic>~adept(clownfish) :- nurturing(clownfish)</logic>
</step>

<step n="4" type="premise">
<text>The kelp forest is settled.</text>
<logic>settled(kelp_forest)</logic>
</step>

<step n="5" type="premise">
<text>The sea turtle is resilient.</text>
<logic>resilient(sea_turtle)</logic>
</step>

<step n="6" type="premise">
<text>The clownfish is fertile.</text>
<logic>fertile(clownfish)</logic>
</step>

From the fact that the clownfish is fertile and the rule that fertility implies alertness, we conclude that the clownfish is alert. This follows by applying the conditional directly to the known fertile status.
<step n="7" type="derived" from="6,2">
<text>From the clownfish being fertile we infer that the clownfish is alert.</text>
<logic>alert(clownfish)</logic>
</step>

Having established that the clownfish is alert, we use the rule that alertness leads to adeptness to infer that the clownfish is adept. This chain connects the original fact to the property that appears in the negation rule.
<step n="8" type="derived" from="7,1">
<text>From the clownfish being alert we infer that the clownfish is adept.</text>
<logic>adept(clownfish)</logic>
</step>

We now have that the clownfish is adept, while the rule says nurturing would make it not adept. Using modus tollens, if nurturing implied not adept, the presence of adept means the clownfish cannot be nurturing; therefore we derive that it is not nurturing.
<step n="9" type="derived" from="8,3">
<text>Since the clownfish is adept and nurturing would make it not adept, we conclude the clownfish is not nurturing.</text>
<logic>~nurturing(clownfish)</logic>
</step>

We derived Clownfish is not nurturing, so the conclusion Clownfish is nurturing is False.
</think>
Answer: False

The second rule states that self‑checking implies adaptability. Knowing this will let us move from a self‑checking status to an adaptive one when the appropriate premise is derived.
<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>If Robo Alpha is self‑checking, then it is adaptive.</text>
<logic>adaptive(robo_alpha) :- self_checking(robo_alpha)</logic>
</step>

The third rule connects sensor‑richness with self‑checking. Once we can show Robo Alpha is sensor‑rich, we will be able to infer it is self‑checking.
<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>If Robo Alpha is sensor‑rich, then it is self‑checking.</text>
<logic>self_checking(robo_alpha) :- sensor_rich(robo_alpha)</logic>
</step>

The fourth rule is a conditional about flexibility: if Robo Alpha were flexible, it could not be knowledgeable. This relationship will be crucial for the final contradiction step.
<step n="4" type="premise">
<text>If Robo Alpha is flexible, then it is not knowledgeable.</text>
<logic>~knowledgeable(robo_alpha) :- flexible(robo_alpha)</logic>
</step>

The fifth rule says that steadiness prevents a robot from being in learning mode. This will allow us to use a contrapositive argument once we know Robo Alpha is in learning mode.
<step n="5" type="premise">
<text>If Robo Alpha is steady, then it is not in learning mode.</text>
<logic>~learning_mode(robo_alpha) :- steady(robo_alpha)</logic>
</step>

The disjunction tells us Robo Alpha must be either steady or sensor‑rich. This provides an alternative that we can eliminate to pinpoint the true property.
<step n="6" type="premise">
<text>Robo Alpha is either steady or sensor‑rich.</text>
<logic>steady(robo_alpha) v sensor_rich(robo_alpha)</logic>
</step>

The second disjunction offers that Robo Alpha is either not energy efficient or in learning mode. Combined with the known fact of energy efficiency, it will force the learning mode conclusion.
<step n="7" type="premise">
<text>Robo Alpha is either not energy efficient or in learning mode.</text>
<logic>~energy_efficient(robo_alpha) v learning_mode(robo_alpha)</logic>
</step>

The third disjunction says Robo Alpha is either not structurally sound or knowledgeable. When we later prove structural soundness, the other side of the disjunction will follow.
<step n="8" type="premise">
<text>Robo Alpha is either not structurally sound or knowledgeable.</text>
<logic>~structurally_sound(robo_alpha) v knowledgeable(robo_alpha)</logic>
</step>

We are given directly that Robo Alpha is energy efficient, which will serve as a starting point for the first disjunctive inference.
<step n="9" type="premise">
<text>Robo Alpha is energy efficient.</text>
<logic>energy_efficient(robo_alpha)</logic>
</step>

Because we know Robo Alpha is energy efficient, the alternative "not energy efficient" in the disjunction cannot hold, so the only remaining option is that Robo Alpha is in learning mode. This is a classic use of disjunctive syllogism.
<step n="10" type="derived" from="9,7">
<text>From the fact that Robo Alpha is energy efficient and the disjunction that it is either not energy efficient or in learning mode, we conclude that Robo Alpha is in learning mode.</text>
<logic>learning_mode(robo_alpha)</logic>
</step>

We have established Robo Alpha is in learning mode, but the rule says a steady robot cannot be in learning mode. Applying modus tollens, the presence of learning mode forces us to reject the possibility that Robo Alpha is steady.
<step n="11" type="derived" from="10,5">
<text>Since Robo Alpha is in learning mode and the rule that a steady robot cannot be in learning mode, we infer that Robo Alpha is not steady.</text>
<logic>~steady(robo_alpha)</logic>
</step>

Now that we know Robo Alpha is not steady, the disjunction "steady or sensor‑rich" leaves sensor‑rich as the only viable alternative. Hence Robo Alpha must be sensor‑rich.
<step n="12" type="derived" from="11,6">
<text>Given that Robo Alpha is not steady and the disjunction that it is either steady or sensor‑rich, we deduce that Robo Alpha is sensor‑rich.</text>
<logic>sensor_rich(robo_alpha)</logic>
</step>

Having shown Robo Alpha is sensor‑rich, we can invoke the rule that sensor‑rich robots are self‑checking, giving us Robo Alpha's self‑checking status directly by modus ponens.
<step n="13" type="derived" from="12,3">
<text>Because Robo Alpha is sensor‑rich and sensor‑rich robots are self‑checking, we obtain that Robo Alpha is self‑checking.</text>
<logic>self_checking(robo_alpha)</logic>
</step>

Since Robo Alpha is self‑checking, the rule linking self‑checking to adaptability allows us to infer that Robo Alpha is adaptive.
<step n="14" type="derived" from="13,2">
<text>From Robo Alpha being self‑checking and the rule that self‑checking robots are adaptive, we get that Robo Alpha is adaptive.</text>
<logic>adaptive(robo_alpha)</logic>
</step>

With Robo Alpha adaptive, the conditional that adaptive robots are structurally sound lets us conclude that Robo Alpha is structurally sound.
<step n="15" type="derived" from="14,1">
<text>With Robo Alpha adaptive and the rule that adaptive robots are structurally sound, we infer that Robo Alpha is structurally sound.</text>
<logic>structurally_sound(robo_alpha)</logic>
</step>

We now have structural soundness, and the disjunction "not structurally sound or knowledgeable" forces the knowledgeable side to be true, because the first alternative is false.
<step n="16" type="derived" from="15,8">
<text>Since Robo Alpha is structurally sound and the disjunction that it is either not structurally sound or knowledgeable, we conclude that Robo Alpha is knowledgeable.</text>
<logic>knowledgeable(robo_alpha)</logic>
</step>

Given that Robo Alpha is knowledgeable, the conditional that a flexible robot cannot be knowledgeable lets us apply modus tollens: if Robo Alpha were flexible, it would not be knowledgeable, but it is. Therefore Robo Alpha cannot be flexible, i.e., it is not flexible.
<step n="17" type="derived" from="16,4">
<text>Because Robo Alpha is knowledgeable and the rule that a flexible robot is not knowledgeable, we deduce that Robo Alpha is not flexible.</text>
<logic>~flexible(robo_alpha)</logic>
</step>

We derived Robo_alpha is not flexible, so the conclusion Robo_alpha is flexible is False.
</think>
Answer: False
