The next rule connects punctuality with strength: anyone who is punctual becomes strong. Since we have just derived that Avery is punctual, this rule lets us conclude that Avery is indeed strong. This establishes the positive side of the strength‑systematic relationship.
<step n="17" type="derived" from="16,4">
<text>From Avery's punctuality we derive that Avery is strong.</text>
<logic>strong(avery)</logic>
</step>

We also have a rule stating that if someone is systematic, then they are not strong. By contraposition, if a person is strong, they cannot be systematic. Applying this to Avery, whose strength we just derived, we infer that Avery is not systematic. This negative systematicity will be useful for the diligence rule.
<step n="18" type="derived" from="17,5">
<text>Given Avery's strength and the rule that systematicity implies not‑strong, we infer that Avery cannot be systematic.</text>
<logic>~systematic(avery)</logic>
</step>

There is a specific rule that a non‑systematic individual is diligent. Since we have just shown that Avery is not systematic, the rule fires, and we can assert that Avery is diligent. This fact is crucial for resolving the disjunction concerning alertness.
<step n="19" type="derived" from="18">
<text>Knowing that Avery is not systematic, the rule linking non‑systematicity to diligence lets us assert that Avery is diligent.</text>
<logic>diligent(avery)</logic>
</step>

The disjunction provides two alternatives: either Avery is not diligent or Avery is alert. We have just proved that Avery is diligent, which eliminates the first alternative. With the first option ruled out, the only way for the disjunction to hold is for the second option to be true, so we must conclude that Avery is alert.
<step n="20" type="derived" from="19,6">
<text>Using the disjunction that either Avery is not diligent or Avery is alert, together with the fact that Avery is diligent, we must conclude that Avery is alert.</text>
<logic>alert(avery)</logic>
</step>

We derived Avery is alert, so the conclusion Avery is not alert is False.
</think>
Answer: False

examples.context: Master Lee is honorable. If Master Lee is wise, then Master Lee is skillful. If Master Lee is courteous, then Master Lee is respectful. If Master Lee is honorable, then Master Lee is swift. If Master Lee is respectful, then Master Lee is fair. If Master Lee is swift, then Master Lee is wise. If Master Lee is fair, then Master Lee is enduring. If Master Lee is steady, then Master Lee is not skillful. Master Lee is either steady or courteous. Either Master Lee is not enduring or Master Lee is adept.
examples.question: Is it true that Master Lee is not adept?
examples.completion:
<think>
We are given a collection of facts and conditional statements about Master Lee's qualities. Our goal is to determine whether the claim that Master Lee is not adept can be supported by the information provided.

This premise links wisdom to skillfulness, which will later let us move from a derived wise state to a skillful one. It is essential for building the chain that starts with honor and ends with skillfulness.
<step n="1" type="premise">
<text>The rule that if Master Lee is wise, then he is skillful.</text>
<logic>skillful(master_lee) :- wise(master_lee)</logic>
</step>

Here we see that courtesy guarantees respectfulness. Later we will need respectfulness to obtain fairness, so this rule will become useful after we establish that Master Lee is courteous.
<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>The rule that if Master Lee is courteous, then he is respectful.</text>
<logic>respectful(master_lee) :- courteous(master_lee)</logic>
</step>

The connection between being honorable and being swift is the first step in the forward reasoning chain. It lets us turn the explicit fact of honor into a dynamic attribute, swift.
<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>The rule that if Master Lee is honorable, then he is swift.</text>
<logic>swift(master_lee) :- honorable(master_lee)</logic>
</step>

This rule tells us that respect leads to fairness. Once we have respect from courtesy, we can use this to reach fairness, which is needed for endurance.
<step n="4" type="premise">
<text>The rule that if Master Lee is respectful, then he is fair.</text>
<logic>fair(master_lee) :- respectful(master_lee)</logic>
</step>

Swiftness implying wisdom provides the bridge from the swift state we get from honor to the wise state required for skillfulness. It is a crucial middle link in the chain.
<step n="5" type="premise">
<text>The rule that if Master Lee is swift, then he is wise.</text>
<logic>wise(master_lee) :- swift(master_lee)</logic>
</step>

Fairness leading to endurance will allow us to move from the fairness we obtain later to the endurance needed for the final disjunction. It completes the path toward the adept conclusion.
<step n="6" type="premise">
<text>The rule that if Master Lee is fair, then he is enduring.</text>
<logic>enduring(master_lee) :- fair(master_lee)</logic>
</step>

The rule that steadiness would make Master Lee not skillful sets up a contradiction that we will exploit. By showing Master Lee is skillful, we can infer that he cannot be steady.
<step n="7" type="premise">
<text>The rule that if Master Lee is steady, then he is not skillful.</text>
<logic>~skillful(master_lee) :- steady(master_lee)</logic>
</step>

This disjunction gives us two possible ways to describe Master Lee: either steady or courteous. Once we rule out steadiness, we will be forced to accept courtesy.
<step n="8" type="premise">
<text>The disjunction that Master Lee is either steady or courteous.</text>
<logic>steady(master_lee) v courteous(master_lee)</logic>
</step>

The final disjunction states that either Master Lee is not enduring or he is adept. When we later establish endurance, this will push us to accept that he must be adept.
<step n="9" type="premise">
<text>The disjunction that either Master Lee is not enduring or he is adept.</text>
<logic>~enduring(master_lee) v adept(master_lee)</logic>
</step>

The explicit fact that Master Lee is honorable serves as the foundation for the entire derivation. It activates the rule that honor leads to swiftness.
<step n="10" type="premise">
<text>The fact that Master Lee is honorable.</text>
<logic>honorable(master_lee)</logic>
</step>

Combining the fact of honor with the rule that honor implies swift, we can directly conclude that Master Lee is swift. This is a straightforward application of modus ponens.
<step n="11" type="derived" from="10,3">
<text>From Master Lee being honorable and the rule that honorable implies swift, we infer that Master Lee is swift.</text>
<logic>swift(master_lee)</logic>
</step>

Now that we know Master Lee is swift, we apply the rule that swift leads to wisdom. This yields the conclusion that Master Lee is wise, moving us further along the chain.
<step n="12" type="derived" from="11,5">
<text>From Master Lee being swift and the rule that swift implies wise, we infer that Master Lee is wise.</text>
<logic>wise(master_lee)</logic>
</step>

Having established wisdom, we use the rule linking wisdom to skillfulness. Consequently, Master Lee is skillful, which is a pivotal fact for the next contradiction step.
<step n="13" type="derived" from="12,1">
<text>From Master Lee being wise and the rule that wise implies skillful, we infer that Master Lee is skillful.</text>
<logic>skillful(master_lee)</logic>
</step>

Since we have shown Master Lee is skillful, the rule that steadiness would make him not skillful cannot hold if he were steady. Therefore, we infer that Master Lee cannot be steady, i.e., he is not steady.
<step n="14" type="derived" from="13,7">
<text>Since Master Lee is skillful, the rule that steady would make him not skillful forces the conclusion that Master Lee is not steady.</text>
<logic>~steady(master_lee)</logic>
</step>

With the knowledge that Master Lee is not steady and the earlier disjunction that he is either steady or courteous, the only remaining possibility is that he is courteous. This follows by disjunctive syllogism.
<step n="15" type="derived" from="14,8">
<text>Given that Master Lee is not steady and the disjunction that he is either steady or courteous, we deduce that he must be courteous.</text>
<logic>courteous(master_lee)</logic>
</step>

Given Master Lee's courtesy, the rule that courtesy implies respectfulness allows us to deduce that he is respectful. This step activates the next rule in the chain.
<step n="16" type="derived" from="15,2">
<text>From Master Lee being courteous and the rule that courteous implies respectful, we infer that he is respectful.</text>
<logic>respectful(master_lee)</logic>
</step>

From respectfulness we apply the rule that respect leads to fairness, concluding that Master Lee is fair. Fairness is needed to obtain endurance in the following step.
<step n="17" type="derived" from="16,4">
<text>From Master Lee being respectful and the rule that respectful implies fair, we infer that he is fair.</text>
<logic>fair(master_lee)</logic>
</step>

Using the fairness we have just derived, the rule that fairness implies endurance gives us that Master Lee is enduring. This satisfies the antecedent of the final disjunction.
<step n="18" type="derived" from="17,6">
<text>From Master Lee being fair and the rule that fair implies enduring, we infer that he is enduring.</text>
<logic>enduring(master_lee)</logic>
</step>

Now that Master Lee is enduring, the disjunction stating "either not enduring or adept" forces the conclusion that he must be adept, because the first alternative is false. Thus we derive that Master Lee is adept.
<step n="19" type="derived" from="18,9">
<text>Since Master Lee is enduring, the disjunction that either he is not enduring or he is adept lets us conclude that he is adept.</text>
<logic>adept(master_lee)</logic>
</step>

We derived Master_lee is adept, so the conclusion Master_lee is not adept is False.
</think>
Answer: False

examples.context: In the library, the patrons Faith, Helen, Rory, and Wren each have several attributes. Helen, Rory, and Wren are diligent. examples.context: In the realm of the gods, Ingrid, Ivan, Nora, and Phoebe are all known for their quick humor; each of them is witty. A special rule governs Nora: if she happens to be proficient, then she cannot be reliable. Moreover, there is a standing statement about Nora that presents two possibilities: either Nora is not witty, or Nora is reliable. No other defaults or hidden clauses affect these characters.
examples.question: Is it true that Nora is not proficient?
examples.completion:
<think>
We are asked to determine whether Nora lacks the property of being proficient. To answer this, we must examine the facts about Nora’s wittiness and the conditional rule linking proficiency to reliability.

The conditional rule is crucial because it tells us that proficiency would preclude reliability for Nora. Knowing this allows us to test any derived reliability claim against possible proficiency.
<step n="1" type="premise">
<text>The rule states that if Nora is proficient, then she is not reliable.</text>
<logic>~reliable(nora) :- proficient(nora)</logic>
</step>

The disjunction gives us a direct link between Nora’s wittiness and reliability. It tells us that at least one of the statements "Nora is not witty" or "Nora is reliable" must hold, setting up a choice for later inference.
<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>Either Nora is not witty, or Nora is reliable.</text>
<logic>~witty(nora) v reliable(nora)</logic>
</step>

<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>Ingrid possesses the quality of being witty.</text>
<logic>witty(ingrid)</logic>
</step>

<step n="4" type="premise">
<text>Ivan is also witty.</text>
<logic>witty(ivan)</logic>
</step>

<step n="5" type="premise">
<text>Phoebe is witty as well.</text>
<logic>witty(phoebe)</logic>
</step>

<step n="6" type="premise">
<text>Nora herself is witty.</text>
<logic>witty(nora)</logic>
</step>

We already have the fact that Nora is witty, which directly contradicts the "not witty" side of the disjunction. Since that side cannot be true, the only way for the disjunction to remain true is for the other side—"Nora is reliable"—to be true. Hence we conclude Nora is reliable.
<step n="7" type="derived" from="6,2">
<text>Because Nora is witty, the alternative that she is not witty must be false, so the disjunction forces that Nora is reliable.</text>
<logic>reliable(nora)</logic>
</step>

Now we combine the reliability we just derived with the earlier conditional rule. The rule says that if Nora were proficient, she would not be reliable. But we have just shown she is reliable, so the antecedent (Nora being proficient) must be false. Therefore, Nora is not proficient.
<step n="8" type="derived" from="7,1">

<logic>~proficient(nora)</logic>
</step>

Therefore Nora is not proficient, so the conclusion is True.
</think>
Answer: True

examples.context: The first mate is skillful. Any skillful crew member is also flexible. Being brave guarantees being strong. If someone is flexible, then they are brave. Modest people are negotiators. If someone is keen, then they are modest. Negotiators are reliable. If someone is thoughtful, then they are not strong. The first mate is either thoughtful or keen.
examples.question: Can we conclude that the first mate is not reliable?
examples.completion:
<think>
We are given a collection of sailing‑related qualities for the first mate and a set of logical relationships among those qualities. The goal is to see whether the statement that the first mate is not reliable can be supported by the information.

<step n="1" type="premise">
<text>The rule states that any skillful person is also flexible.</text>

</step>

<step n="2" type="premise">

</step>

<step n="3" type="premise">

</step>

</step>

<step n="7" type="premise">
<text>The rule specifies that thoughtful people are not strong.</text>
<logic>~strong(first_mate) :- thoughtful(first_mate)</logic>
</step>

<step n="8" type="premise">
<text>It is given that the first mate is either thoughtful or keen.</text>
<logic>thoughtful(first_mate) v keen(first_mate)</logic>
</step>

<step n="9" type="premise">
<text>It is a fact that the first mate is skillful.</text>
<logic>skillful(first_mate)</logic>
</step>

We start with the explicit fact that the first mate is skillful. The first rule tells us that any skillful person is flexible, so we can directly apply this conditional to obtain flexibility for the first mate. This gives us a new property to work with in later steps.
<step n="10" type="derived" from="9,1">
<text>From the fact that the first mate is skillful and the rule that skillful implies flexible, we infer that the first mate is flexible.</text>
<logic>flexible(first_mate)</logic>
</step>

Having established that the first mate is flexible, we turn to the rule linking flexibility to bravery. The rule says that anyone who is flexible is also brave, so by substituting the first mate's flexibility we infer that the first mate is brave. This bridges the gap between the initial fact and the later strength rule.
<step n="11" type="derived" from="10,3">
<text>Using the newly obtained flexibility and the rule that flexibility leads to bravery, we conclude that the first mate is brave.</text>
<logic>brave(first_mate)</logic>
</step>

Now that we know the first mate is brave, we can use the rule that bravery guarantees strength. Applying this conditional to the first mate's bravery yields that the first mate is strong. This step is crucial because it will later allow us to reason about the negation of thoughtfulness.
<step n="12" type="derived" from="11,2">
<text>With bravery established, the rule that bravery leads to strength lets us infer that the first mate is strong.</text>
<logic>strong(first_mate)</logic>
</step>

We have derived that the first mate is strong, while another rule tells us that if someone is thoughtful, then they are not strong. By contraposition (modus tollens), the presence of strength means the antecedent of that rule—thoughtfulness—must be false. Hence the first mate cannot be thoughtful.
<step n="13" type="derived" from="12,7">
<text>Since we have derived that the first mate is strong, and the rule that thoughtful people are not strong, we can apply modus tollens to deduce that the first mate is not thoughtful.</text>
<logic>~thoughtful(first_mate)</logic>
</step>

The disjunction tells us the first mate is either thoughtful or keen. Since we have just shown they are not thoughtful, the only remaining possibility is that they are keen. This is a classic application of disjunctive syllogism, eliminating one option to confirm the other.
<step n="14" type="derived" from="13,8">
<text>Given that the first mate is not thoughtful and that they must be either thoughtful or keen, we infer by disjunctive syllogism that the first mate is keen.</text>
<logic>keen(first_mate)</logic>
</step>

With keenness established, we invoke the rule that keen individuals are modest. Substituting the first mate's keenness gives us modesty, adding another attribute that will feed into the negotiator and reliability chain.
<step n="15" type="derived" from="14,5">
<text>From the keenness and the rule that keen implies modest, we infer that the first mate is modest.</text>
<logic>modest(first_mate)</logic>
</step>

The modesty we just derived triggers the rule that modest people are negotiators. By applying this rule, we obtain that the first mate is a negotiator, moving us one step closer to reliability.
<step n="16" type="derived" from="15,4">
<text>With modesty and the rule that modest individuals are negotiators, we infer that the first mate is a negotiator.</text>
<logic>negotiator(first_mate)</logic>
</step>

Finally, the rule linking negotiators to reliability allows us to conclude that the first mate is reliable. This is the opposite of the query we were asked to evaluate, showing that the available information actually supports reliability rather than its negation.
<step n="17" type="derived" from="16,6">
<text>Finally, from negotiator status and the rule that negotiators are reliable, we conclude that the first mate is reliable.</text>
<logic>reliable(first_mate)</logic>
</step>

We derived First_mate is reliable, so the conclusion First_mate is not reliable is False.

Answer: False

<logic>principled(rory)</logic>
</step>

<step n="10" type="premise">
<text>Willow is principled.</text>
<logic>principled(willow)</logic>
</step>

<step n="11" type="premise">
<text>Milan is studious.</text>
<logic>studious(milan)</logic>
</step>

<step n="12" type="premise">
<text>Blake is principled.</text>
<logic>principled(blake)</logic>
</step>

Blake is known to be principled. The rule linking principled individuals to strength tells us that any principled person is strong. By applying this rule, we can safely assert that Blake possesses strength. This is the first link in the chain leading toward agility.
<step n="13" type="derived" from="12,7">
<text>From Blake being principled and the rule that principled implies strong, we infer Blake is strong.</text>
<logic>strong(blake)</logic>
</step>

Having established Blake's strength, we turn to the rule that strength guarantees caution. Since Blake is strong, the conditional guarantees that Blake must also be cautious. This builds the next rung on the ladder toward fitness and ultimately agility.
<step n="14" type="derived" from="13,2">
<text>Using Blake's newly established strength and the rule that strength implies caution, we conclude Blake is cautious.</text>
<logic>cautious(blake)</logic>
</step>

The cautiousness of Blake activates the rule that a cautious person is fit. Because Blake is cautious, we infer his fitness. Fitness is crucial because it unlocks the next implication toward determination.
<step n="15" type="derived" from="14,5">
<text>Because Blake is cautious and the rule that caution leads to fitness, we derive that Blake is fit.</text>
<logic>fit(blake)</logic>
</step>

With Blake now known to be fit, we employ the rule that fitness implies determination. Therefore Blake is determined. This determination will allow us to move forward to experience.
<step n="16" type="derived" from="15,3">
<text>Given Blake's fitness and the rule that fitness yields determination, we obtain that Blake is determined.</text>
<logic>determined(blake)</logic>
</step>

The rule stating that determined individuals are experienced now applies. Since Blake is determined, we can conclude that he is experienced. Experience is the penultimate step before reaching patient status.
<step n="17" type="derived" from="16,4">
<text>With Blake determined and the rule that determination brings experience, we see that Blake is experienced.</text>
<logic>experienced(blake)</logic>
</step>

Experience leads directly to patienthood via the corresponding rule. Blake's experience therefore gives us that Blake is patient. Patient status is the final condition needed for agility.
<step n="18" type="derived" from="17,6">
<text>Since Blake is experienced and the rule states experience makes one patient, we get that Blake is patient.</text>
<logic>patient(blake)</logic>
</step>

The rule linking patient individuals to agility now comes into play. Because Blake is patient, we deduce that Blake is agile. This completes the forward chain from principled to agile.
<step n="19" type="derived" from="18,1">
<text>Finally, Blake being patient together with the rule that patient implies agile gives us Blake is agile.</text>
<logic>agile(blake)</logic>
</step>

We derived Blake is agile, so the conclusion Blake is not agile is False.
</think>
Answer: False

examples.context: Winter's wine is balanced. Kate's vintage is well aged. Clara is a vine expert. Whenever a wine is not acidic, it is structured. If a wine is acidic, then it is not refined. If a wine is ripe, then it is not structured. Either Clara is not a vine expert, or Clara's wine is consistent. Either Clara's wine is not consistent, or it is refined. Whenever a wine is terroir_sensitive, it is bold. Whenever a wine is aromatic, Winter's wine is full_body. If a wine is well_aged, then it has proper_acidity. If a wine is balanced, then it is sustainable. If a wine is acidic, then it is not refined. If a wine is ripe, then it is not structured.
examples.question: Can we conclude that Clara's wine is not ripe?
examples.completion:
<think>
We are asked to determine whether Clara's wine can be shown to be not ripe. The goal is to derive the negation of the 'ripe' property using the information provided about Clara and the logical connections among the wine attributes.

The first rule links the absence of acidity to a structured character. This connection will become useful once we have established that Clara's wine is not acidic, allowing us to move toward the target property.
<step n="1" type="premise">
<text>The rule states that if Clara's wine is not acidic, then it is structured.</text>
<logic>structured(clara) :- ~acidic(clara)</logic>
</step>

The second rule tells us that acidity prevents a wine from being refined. By later showing that Clara's wine is refined, we will be able to infer that it cannot be acidic, which is a crucial intermediate step.
<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>The rule states that if Clara's wine is acidic, then it is not refined.</text>
<logic>~refined(clara) :- acidic(clara)</logic>
</step>

The third rule says that a ripe wine cannot be structured. This contrapositive relationship will let us turn a derived structured status into the desired conclusion that the wine is not ripe.
<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>The rule states that if Clara's wine is ripe, then it is not structured.</text>
<logic>~structured(clara) :- ripe(clara)</logic>
</step>

The disjunction provides two alternatives: either Clara lacks expertise as a vine expert, or her wine is consistent. Since we already know she is a vine expert, this clause will force the consistency of her wine.
<step n="4" type="premise">
<text>Either Clara is not a vine expert, or her wine is consistent.</text>
<logic>~vine_expert(clara) v consistent(clara)</logic>
</step>

The second disjunction offers another pair: either the wine is not consistent, or it is refined. Once we have consistency, this statement will compel the wine to be refined.
<step n="5" type="premise">
<text>Either Clara's wine is not consistent, or it is refined.</text>
<logic>~consistent(clara) v refined(clara)</logic>
</step>

Winter's wine being balanced is a factual statement that supports other rules about sustainability, though it does not directly affect Clara's case. It is included to satisfy the requirement that all facts be mentioned.
<step n="6" type="premise">
<text>Winter's wine is balanced.</text>
<logic>balanced(winter)</logic>
</step>

Kate's vintage being well aged is another factual premise that underpins rules about proper acidity and boldness, but it is not directly used in the chain toward Clara's ripeness.
<step n="7" type="premise">
<text>Kate's vintage is well aged.</text>
<logic>well_aged(kate)</logic>
</step>

Clara being a vine expert is a key fact that interacts with the first disjunction, enabling us to deduce consistency for her wine.
<step n="8" type="premise">
<text>Clara is a vine expert.</text>
<logic>vine_expert(clara)</logic>
</step>

Because Clara is a vine expert, the left side of the first disjunction is false, so the right side must hold; thus her wine is consistent. This application of disjunctive syllogism gives us a concrete property to work with in the next step.
<step n="9" type="derived" from="8,4">
<text>From Clara being a vine expert and the disjunction that either she is not a vine expert or her wine is consistent, we infer that her wine is consistent.</text>
<logic>consistent(clara)</logic>
</step>

Now that we know Clara's wine is consistent, the second disjunction forces the alternative: the wine must be refined. Again, disjunctive syllogism turns the established consistency into refinement, moving us closer to the acidity argument.
<step n="10" type="derived" from="9,5">
<text>Using the consistency of Clara's wine and the disjunction that either it is not consistent or it is refined, we conclude that her wine is refined.</text>
<logic>refined(clara)</logic>
</step>

Having established that the wine is refined, we recall the rule that an acidic wine would block refinement. By modus tollens, if the wine were acidic, it could not be refined; since it is refined, the wine cannot be acidic. This yields the crucial ~acidic fact.
<step n="11" type="derived" from="10,2">
<text>Since Clara's wine is refined and the rule that an acidic wine would prevent refinement, we deduce that her wine is not acidic.</text>
<logic>~acidic(clara)</logic>
</step>

With the non‑acidic status in hand, we invoke the rule linking non‑acidic wines to a structured character. Modus ponens lets us conclude that Clara's wine is structured, providing the premise needed for the final inference about ripeness.
<step n="12" type="derived" from="11,1">
<text>Given that the wine is not acidic and the rule that a non‑acidic wine makes it structured, we obtain that Clara's wine is structured.</text>
<logic>structured(clara)</logic>
</step>

Finally, we apply the rule that a ripe wine cannot be structured. Since we have shown the wine is structured, the contrapositive gives us that the wine is not ripe. This directly answers the query.
<step n="13" type="derived" from="12,3">
<text>With Clara's wine being structured and the rule that a structured wine cannot be ripe, we infer that the wine is not ripe.</text>
<logic>~ripe(clara)</logic>
</step>

Therefore Clara is not ripe, so the conclusion is True.
</think>
Answer: True

examples.context: Cora is adaptable. Whenever Cora is not diligent, she is creative. If Cora is diligent, then she is not consistent. Being wise guarantees that Cora is not creative. Cora is either not adaptable or dedicated. Either Cora is not dedicated or she is consistent.
examples.question: Is Cora not wise?
examples.completion:
<think>
We are asked to decide whether Cora lacks wisdom. The goal is to show that the statement "Cora is not wise" follows from the given facts and rules. To do this we will chain together several implications and disjunctions provided in the premises.

The first rule tells us that the absence of diligence guarantees creativity. This will become useful later when we establish that Cora is indeed not diligent, allowing us to derive her creativity. Keeping this conditional in mind sets up a link between a negative property and a positive one.
<step n="1" type="premise">
<text>If Cora is not diligent, then she is creative.</text>
<logic>creative(cora) :- ~diligent(cora)</logic>
</step>

The second rule links diligence to the negation of consistency. In other words, if Cora were diligent she could not be consistent. This contrapositive relationship will later enable us to infer that Cora cannot be diligent once we have proven she is consistent.
<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>If Cora is diligent, then she is not consistent.</text>
<logic>~consistent(cora) :- diligent(cora)</logic>
</step>

The third rule states that wisdom forces the opposite of creativity: a wise Cora would not be creative. Recognizing this inverse connection is crucial because once we obtain creativity for Cora, we can flip the implication to conclude that she cannot be wise.
<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>If Cora is wise, then she is not creative.</text>
<logic>~creative(cora) :- wise(cora)</logic>
</step>

The first disjunction gives a choice: either Cora lacks adaptability or she is dedicated. Since we already know she is adaptable, the left side of the disjunction is false, which forces the right side—dedication—to be true. This logical narrowing will start our chain of deductions.
<step n="4" type="premise">
<text>Cora is either not adaptable or dedicated.</text>
<logic>~adaptable(cora) v dedicated(cora)</logic>
</step>

The second disjunction says that either Cora is not dedicated or she is consistent. After we have shown she is dedicated, the first alternative becomes false, leaving consistency as the only viable option. This will let us move forward toward establishing her non‑diligence.
<step n="5" type="premise">
<text>Either Cora is not dedicated or she is consistent.</text>
<logic>~dedicated(cora) v consistent(cora)</logic>
</step>

The fact that Cora is adaptable is the starting point for applying the first disjunction. Knowing this truth lets us eliminate the "not adaptable" possibility and directly obtain dedication, as explained in the next step.
<step n="6" type="premise">
<text>Cora is adaptable.</text>
<logic>adaptable(cora)</logic>
</step>

Because Cora is adaptable, the clause "not adaptable" in the first disjunction cannot hold. Disjunctive syllogism therefore leaves us with the only remaining option: Cora must be dedicated. This step converts a disjunctive statement into a concrete fact about Cora.
<step n="7" type="derived" from="6,4">
<text>From Cora being adaptable and the first disjunction, we infer that Cora is dedicated.</text>
<logic>dedicated(cora)</logic>
</step>

Having established that Cora is dedicated, we turn to the second disjunction. The alternative "not dedicated" is now false, so the disjunction forces the other side—consistency—to be true. Thus we conclude that Cora is consistent, moving us closer to the target conclusion.
<step n="8" type="derived" from="7,5">
<text>Using Cora’s dedication and the second disjunction, we conclude that Cora is consistent.</text>
<logic>consistent(cora)</logic>
</step>

We now have consistency for Cora, and the second rule tells us that diligence would make someone not consistent. By modus tollens, if consistency holds, the antecedent (diligence) must be false. Hence we infer that Cora is not diligent, which sets up the condition needed for the creativity rule.
<step n="9" type="derived" from="8,2">
<text>Because Cora is consistent and diligence would make her not consistent, we deduce that Cora is not diligent.</text>
<logic>~diligent(cora)</logic>
</step>

With the knowledge that Cora is not diligent, we can invoke the first rule, which directly states that non‑diligence implies creativity. Applying modus ponens, we obtain that Cora is creative. This newly derived property will be pivotal for the final inference about wisdom.
<step n="10" type="derived" from="9,1">
<text>Since Cora is not diligent, the rule about non‑diligence gives us that Cora is creative.</text>
<logic>creative(cora)</logic>
</step>

Now that Cora is creative, we look back at the third rule, which says that wisdom would entail non‑creativity. The contrapositive of this rule is that if someone is creative, they cannot be wise. Using modus tollens on this contrapositive, we deduce that Cora is not wise.
<step n="11" type="derived" from="10,3">
<text>Creative Cora together with the rule linking wisdom to non‑creativity lets us infer that Cora is not wise.</text>
<logic>~wise(cora)</logic>
</step>

Therefore Cora is not wise, so the conclusion is True.
</think>
Answer: True

examples.context: In the Shaolin dojo, Jane trains as a martial artist. Jane is punctual, so she always arrives on time for class. The master has taught that any student who is not dedicated will nevertheless become experienced in the art. He also says that any student who is dedicated will end up not punctual, because dedication makes one stay late training. These statements constitute all the facts and conditional rules we know about Jane.
examples.question: Is it true that Jane is not experienced?
examples.completion:
<think>
We are asked to decide whether the statement that Jane is not experienced can be supported by the information given about her training habits. To answer this, we will follow the logical chain from the facts and conditional rules provided in the context.

The first rule tells us that lacking dedication guarantees experience; this will become useful if we can show Jane is not dedicated. Keeping this conditional in mind helps us link a possible lack of dedication to the property we ultimately care about, experience. 
<step n="1" type="premise">
<text>If Jane is not dedicated, then she is experienced.</text>
<logic>experienced(jane) :- ~dedicated(jane)</logic>
</step>

The second rule states that dedication would cause Jane to miss being punctual. This rule is important because it lets us reason about dedication by looking at Jane's punctuality status. If we know Jane is punctual, we can use this rule in reverse to argue about dedication.
<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>If Jane is dedicated, then she is not punctual.</text>
<logic>~punctual(jane) :- dedicated(jane)</logic>
</step>

<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>Jane is punctual.</text>
<logic>punctual(jane)</logic>
</step>

We know from the fact that Jane is punctual. The rule says that a dedicated person would be not punctual, which contradicts the fact. Applying modus tollens, if the consequent (not punctual) is false, the antecedent (dedicated) must also be false. Therefore we conclude that Jane is not dedicated.
<step n="4" type="derived" from="3,2">
<text>Since Jane is punctual, and being dedicated would make her not punctual, we infer that Jane is not dedicated.</text>
<logic>~dedicated(jane)</logic>
</step>

Having established that Jane is not dedicated, we can now activate the first rule: non‑dedicated implies experienced. By modus ponens, the truth of the antecedent (~dedicated) directly yields the truth of the consequent (experienced). Thus Jane is experienced.
<step n="5" type="derived" from="4,1">
<text>Because Jane is not dedicated, the rule that non‑dedicated implies experienced lets us conclude that Jane is experienced.</text>
<logic>experienced(jane)</logic>
</step>

We derived Jane is experienced, so the conclusion Jane is not experienced is False.
</think>
Answer: False

examples.context: In the textile workshop, the loom operator is muscle-bound. Whenever the loom operator is muscle-bound, it guarantees that they are a team player. Whenever the loom operator is a team player, they are also optimistic. If the loom operator is optimistic and there is no evidence that they are unskilled, then they are considered skilled. There is no indication that the loom operator is unskilled.
examples.question: Is the loom operator skilled?
examples.completion:
<think>
We need to decide whether the loom operator possesses the skill required for the workshop. The goal is to show that the property 'skilled' holds for the loom operator based on the available facts and rules.

The first premise tells us that optimism follows from being a team player. This connection will be useful later when we have established that the loom operator is a team player.
<step n="1" type="premise">
<text>The rule that any loom operator who is a team player is also optimistic.</text>
<logic>optimistic(loom_operator) :- team_player(loom_operator)</logic>
</step>

The second premise links the physical attribute of being muscle-bound to the social attribute of being a team player. It gives us a way to move from a concrete fact to a relational property needed for the next steps.
<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>The rule that any muscle-bound loom operator is a team player.</text>
<logic>team_player(loom_operator) :- muscle_bound(loom_operator)</logic>
</step>

The default rule states that optimism leads to being considered skilled as long as there is no contrary evidence. This is the crucial rule that will finally allow us to claim the loom operator is skilled.
<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>The default rule that an optimistic loom operator is considered skilled unless there is evidence they are unskilled.</text>
<logic>skilled(loom_operator) :- optimistic(loom_operator), not ab_skilled(loom_operator)</logic>
</step>

The fact that the loom operator is muscle-bound provides the starting point for the chain of reasoning. It is the only explicit fact we have, and it will trigger the rule about team players.
<step n="4" type="premise">
<text>The fact that the loom operator is muscle-bound.</text>
<logic>muscle_bound(loom_operator)</logic>
</step>

Because we know the loom operator is muscle-bound and we have the rule that muscle-bound implies team player, we can directly infer that the loom operator is a team player. This step bridges the physical fact to the social property needed for optimism.
<step n="5" type="derived" from="4,2">
<text>From the loom operator being muscle-bound and the rule that muscle-bound implies team player, we infer that the loom operator is a team player.</text>
<logic>team_player(loom_operator)</logic>
</step>

Having established that the loom operator is a team player, we apply the rule that any team player is optimistic. This yields the optimism property, which is a prerequisite for the default skill rule.
<step n="6" type="derived" from="5,1">
<text>Using the fact that the loom operator is a team player and the rule that a team player implies optimism, we conclude the loom operator is optimistic.</text>
<logic>optimistic(loom_operator)</logic>
</step>

Now that the loom operator is optimistic and we have explicitly noted there is no evidence of them being unskilled, the default rule activates, allowing us to conclude the loom operator is skilled. The lack of contrary evidence satisfies the 'not ab_talented' condition of the rule.
<step n="7" type="derived" from="6,3">
<text>Given the loom operator is optimistic and there is no evidence that they are unskilled, the default rule lets us conclude the loom operator is skilled.</text>
<logic>skilled(loom_operator)</logic>
</step>

Therefore Loom_operator is skilled, so the conclusion is True.
</think>
Answer: True

examples.context: The Andromeda star is massive. The Vega planet is spectrally_resolved. Whenever something is well_studied, it is luminescent. Whenever something is steady, it is high_energy. If something is spectrally_resolved then it is hydrogen_rich. If something is massive then it is precise. If something is stable_orbit then it is tidally_locked. If something is luminescent then it is steady. If something is gravitationally_bound then it is not high_energy. If something is accreting then it is not precise. Either the Andromeda star is accreting or it is well_studied. Either the Vega planet is not hydrogen_rich or it is stable_orbit.
examples.question: Is the Andromeda star gravitationally_bound?
examples.completion:
<think>
We are asked to decide whether the Andromeda star possesses the property of being gravitationally_bound. To answer, we must trace the chain of given facts and rules that connect the star's known attributes to the possibility of it being gravitationally_bound.

This rule tells us that any well_studied object shines brightly, i.e., becomes luminescent. It will become useful later when we establish that the star is well_studied, allowing us to derive luminescence.
<step n="1" type="premise">
<text>If the Andromeda star is well_studied, then it is luminescent.</text>
<logic>luminescent(andromeda_star) :- well_studied(andromeda_star)</logic>
</step>

Here we learn that steadiness guarantees high energy. Once we can prove the star is steady, we will be able to infer that it is high_energy, a key step toward reaching a contradiction with gravitationally_bound.
<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>If the Andromeda star is steady, then it is high_energy.</text>
<logic>high_energy(andromeda_star) :- steady(andromeda_star)</logic>
</step>

The rule links massive objects to precision. Since we already know the star is massive, this implication will let us obtain the precise property, which later helps eliminate the accreting alternative.
<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>If the Andromeda star is massive, then it is precise.</text>
<logic>precise(andromeda_star) :- massive(andromeda_star)</logic>
</step>

This conditional shows that luminescence leads to steadiness. After we get luminescence from a well_studied status, we will be able to chain to steadiness and then to high_energy.
<step n="4" type="premise">
<text>If the Andromeda star is luminescent, then it is steady.</text>
<logic>steady(andromeda_star) :- luminescent(andromeda_star)</logic>
</step>

The crucial negative rule states that if something is gravitationally_bound, it cannot be high_energy. By later establishing high_energy, we will be able to use this rule in reverse to infer that the star is not gravitationally_bound.
<step n="5" type="premise">
<text>If the Andromeda star is gravitationally_bound, then it is not high_energy.</text>
<logic>~high_energy(andromeda_star) :- gravitationally_bound(andromeda_star)</logic>
</step>

This rule says that accreting objects fail to be precise. It will allow us to use the precise property we obtain to rule out the accreting option via Modus Tollens.
<step n="6" type="premise">
<text>If the Andromeda star is accreting, then it is not precise.</text>
<logic>~precise(andromeda_star) :- accreting(andromeda_star)</logic>
</step>

The disjunction offers two possibilities for the star: either it is accreting or it is well_studied. Once we eliminate the accreting case, we can conclude the star must be well_studied.
<step n="7" type="premise">
<text>Either the Andromeda star is accreting or it is well_studied.</text>
<logic>accreting(andromeda_star) v well_studied(andromeda_star)</logic>
</step>

The fact about the Vega planet being spectrally_resolved is not directly needed for the target query, but it satisfies the requirement to mention all given facts in the context.
<step n="8" type="premise">
<text>The Vega planet is spectrally_resolved.</text>
<logic>spectrally_resolved(vega_planet)</logic>
</step>

The fact that the Andromeda star is massive is the starting point for deriving precision, which then triggers a cascade of deductions toward the final conclusion.
<step n="9" type="premise">
<text>The Andromeda star is massive.</text>
<logic>massive(andromeda_star)</logic>
</step>

We combine the fact that the star is massive with the rule that massive implies precise, giving us the precise property. This step sets up the condition needed to eliminate the accreting alternative in the next step.
<step n="10" type="derived" from="9,3">
<text>From the fact that the Andromeda star is massive and the rule that massive implies precise, we infer that the star is precise.</text>
<logic>precise(andromeda_star)</logic>
</step>

Since we now know the star is precise, and the rule says accreting would make it not precise, we can apply Modus Tollens: the antecedent (accreting) must be false, so the star cannot be accreting. This eliminates one side of the earlier disjunction.
<step n="11" type="derived" from="10,6">
<text>Because the star is precise and accreting would make it not precise, we conclude that the star cannot be accreting.</text>
<logic>~accreting(andromeda_star)</logic>
</step>

Having ruled out accreting, the disjunction "accreting or well_studied" forces the remaining option: the star must be well_studied. This is a classic disjunctive syllogism, moving us closer to luminescence.
<step n="12" type="derived" from="11,7">
<text>Since the star cannot be accreting and we know that either it is accreting or well_studied, we deduce that the star is well_studied.</text>
<logic>well_studied(andromeda_star)</logic>
</step>

With the star established as well_studied, we invoke the rule that well_studied objects are luminescent, thereby granting the star the luminescent property. This bridges the gap to steadiness.
<step n="13" type="derived" from="12,1">
<text>Given that the star is well_studied, the rule that well_studied implies luminescent lets us infer that the star is luminescent.</text>
<logic>luminescent(andromeda_star)</logic>
</step>

Luminescence triggers steadiness via the corresponding rule, so we now have that the star is steady. This prepares us to derive high_energy in the following step.
<step n="14" type="derived" from="13,4">
<text>From luminescence we obtain steadiness, because luminescent things are steady.</text>
<logic>steady(andromeda_star)</logic>
</step>

Because the star is steady, the rule that steady implies high_energy allows us to conclude the star is high_energy. This high_energy status is the opposite of what a gravitationally_bound object would exhibit, setting up the final contradiction.
<step n="15" type="derived" from="14,2">
<text>Steadiness then yields high_energy, as steady objects are high_energy.</text>
<logic>high_energy(andromeda_star)</logic>
</step>

We now have high_energy, and the rule says a gravitationally_bound object cannot be high_energy. Applying Modus Tollens again, we infer that the star is not gravitationally_bound. This directly addresses the original query.
<step n="16" type="derived" from="15,5">
<text>Having high_energy and the rule that a gravitationally_bound object would not be high_energy, we infer that the star is not gravitationally_bound.</text>
<logic>~gravitationally_bound(andromeda_star)</logic>
</step>

We derived Andromeda_star is not gravitationally bound, so the conclusion Andromeda_star is gravitationally bound is False.
</think>
Answer: False

examples.context: Ruth is friendly, and Riley is polite. Whenever someone is fit, they are empathetic, so if Ruth were fit she would be empathetic. If a person is polite, then they are persistent, which means Riley's politeness makes her persistent. Any eloquent individual is reliable, so eloquence would guarantee reliability for Riley. Loyalty guarantees strategic ability, so anyone who is loyal becomes strategic; this applies to Ruth as well. Being enthusiastic forces a person to be not friendly, therefore any enthusiastic Ruth would not be friendly. If someone is relaxed, then they cannot be strategic, so relaxation blocks strategic status for Ruth. Either Ruth is enthusiastic or Ruth is loyal; the information leaves both possibilities open. Additionally, Ruth is either relaxed or fit, giving another binary choice. Finally, either Ruth is not persistent or she is eloquent, providing a disjunction about Riley’s attributes.
examples.question: Based on the given information, is Ruth empathetic?
examples.completion:
<think>

The disjunction provides two exclusive possibilities for Ruth: she is either enthusiastic or loyal. This binary choice will be resolved once we can rule out one of the options.

The second disjunction gives another pair of options: Ruth is either relaxed or fit. Determining which of these holds will be crucial for later steps involving fitness and empathy.

Now that we know Ruth is loyal, we can apply the loyalty‑to‑strategic rule. Loyalty guarantees strategic ability, so we conclude that Ruth is strategic. This new fact will be used to address the relaxed‑to‑strategic rule.

examples.context: In the garden, the rosebush is soil_friendly. The oak_tree is seasoned. Whenever a rosebush is well_pruned, it is also hardy. Whenever an oak_tree is seasoned, it is also balanced. Being soil_friendly guarantees that the rosebush is reflective. Whenever an oak_tree is balanced, it becomes prolific. For the rosebush, either it is not reflective or it is well_pruned. For the oak_tree, either it is not prolific or it is systematic.
examples.question: Is the oak_tree systematic?
examples.completion:
<think>
We are asked whether the oak_tree in the garden exhibits systematic behavior. To answer, we must chain together the given facts and rules about the oak_tree's properties.

The first premise tells us that any oak_tree that is seasoned is also balanced. This conditional will let us move from the known seasoned status of the oak_tree to a new attribute, which is needed for later steps.
<step n="1" type="premise">
<text>The rule that a seasoned oak_tree is balanced.</text>
<logic>balanced(oak_tree) :- seasoned(oak_tree)</logic>
</step>

The second premise states that a balanced oak_tree becomes prolific. Once we have shown the oak_tree is balanced, this rule will allow us to infer prolificness, setting up the use of the disjunction later on.
<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>The rule that a balanced oak_tree is prolific.</text>
<logic>prolific(oak_tree) :- balanced(oak_tree)</logic>
</step>

The third premise offers a choice: either the oak_tree is not prolific or it is systematic. This logical or will become useful once we know whether the oak_tree is prolific, because then the only way for the disjunction to hold is that the oak_tree is systematic.
<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>The disjunction stating that either the oak_tree is not prolific or it is systematic.</text>
<logic>~prolific(oak_tree) v systematic(oak_tree)</logic>
</step>

We are also told that the rosebush is soil_friendly, which is unrelated to the oak_tree's systematic status but is part of the overall garden description. It ensures the context mentions all given facts.
<step n="4" type="premise">
<text>The fact that the rosebush is soil_friendly.</text>
<logic>soil_friendly(rosebush)</logic>
</step>

The fact that the oak_tree is seasoned gives us the starting point for applying the first rule. With this fact, we can trigger the chain of implications that lead to systematic.
<step n="5" type="premise">
<text>The fact that the oak_tree is seasoned.</text>
<logic>seasoned(oak_tree)</logic>
</step>

Combining the seasoned fact with the rule that seasoned implies balanced, we can assert that the oak_tree is balanced. This follows directly by modus ponens: the condition of the rule is satisfied, so its conclusion must hold.
<step n="6" type="derived" from="5,1">
<text>From the fact that the oak_tree is seasoned and the rule linking seasoned to balanced, we infer that the oak_tree is balanced.</text>
<logic>balanced(oak_tree)</logic>
</step>

Now that we have established the oak_tree is balanced, we can use the second rule, which says balanced implies prolific. Again, modus ponens lets us move from the balanced condition to the conclusion that the oak_tree is prolific.
<step n="7" type="derived" from="6,2">
<text>Using the newly derived balanced status and the rule linking balanced to prolific, we conclude that the oak_tree is prolific.</text>
<logic>prolific(oak_tree)</logic>
</step>

We now possess the fact that the oak_tree is prolific. The disjunction tells us that either the oak_tree is not prolific or it is systematic. Since the first option is false (the oak_tree is indeed prolific), the only way for the disjunction to remain true is that the oak_tree must be systematic. This is a classic application of disjunctive syllogism.
<step n="8" type="derived" from="7,3">
<text>Applying the disjunction with the knowledge that the oak_tree is prolific, we deduce that the oak_tree must be systematic.</text>
<logic>systematic(oak_tree)</logic>
</step>

Therefore Oak_tree is systematic, so the conclusion is True.
</think>
Answer: True

examples.context: Hayden is impartial, and Maya is industrious. Whenever something is impartial, it is vigorous, so Hayden is vigorous. For Maya, being trustworthy guarantees that she is diplomatic, and being diplomatic guarantees that she is content. Content in turn guarantees enthusiasm, so any Maya who is diplomatic becomes content and then enthusiastic. Maya’s industriousness guarantees that she is studious. If something is brilliant, then Maya cannot be studious, and conversely, if Maya were logical she would not be enthusiastic. Additionally, Maya is either brilliant or trustworthy. These are all the facts, rules, and disjunctions that govern the situation.
examples.question: Can we conclude that Maya is not logical?
examples.completion:
<think>
We are given a network of properties about Hayden and Maya, and the goal is to determine whether Maya can be logical. The query asks us to show that Maya is not logical, so we must derive a contradiction from assuming she were logical.

<step n="1" type="premise">
<text>If Maya is diplomatic, then Maya is content.</text>
<logic>content(maya) :- diplomatic(maya)</logic>
</step>

<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>If Maya is trustworthy, then Maya is diplomatic.</text>
<logic>diplomatic(maya) :- trustworthy(maya)</logic>
</step>

<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>If Maya is content, then Maya is enthusiastic.</text>
<logic>enthusiastic(maya) :- content(maya)</logic>
</step>

<step n="4" type="premise">
<text>If Maya is industrious, then Maya is studious.</text>
<logic>studious(maya) :- industrious(maya)</logic>
</step>

<step n="5" type="premise">
<text>If Maya is logical, then Maya is not enthusiastic.</text>
<logic>~enthusiastic(maya) :- logical(maya)</logic>
</step>

<step n="6" type="premise">
<text>If Maya is brilliant, then Maya is not studious.</text>
<logic>~studious(maya) :- brilliant(maya)</logic>
</step>

<step n="7" type="premise">
<text>Maya is either brilliant or trustworthy.</text>
<logic>brilliant(maya) v trustworthy(maya)</logic>
</step>

<step n="8" type="premise">
<text>Hayden is impartial.</text>
<logic>impartial(hayden)</logic>
</step>

<step n="9" type="premise">
<text>Maya is industrious.</text>
<logic>industrious(maya)</logic>
</step>

Maya is stated to be industrious, and one of the rules tells us that any industrious individual is also studious. By applying this rule directly to Maya, we can safely add the fact that Maya is studious to our knowledge base.
<step n="10" type="derived" from="9,4">
<text>From Maya being industrious and the rule that industrious implies studious, we infer that Maya is studious.</text>
<logic>studious(maya)</logic>
</step>

We now have Maya as studious. Another rule says that if someone is brilliant, they cannot be studious. Since Maya is already studious, the only way the rule could be violated is if Maya were brilliant, which would force a conflict. To avoid inconsistency, we conclude that Maya cannot be brilliant.
<step n="11" type="derived" from="10,6">
<text>Since Maya is studious and the rule that brilliant implies not studious, we infer that Maya cannot be brilliant.</text>
<logic>~brilliant(maya)</logic>
</step>

The premises include a disjunction that Maya is either brilliant or trustworthy. Because we have just shown that Maya cannot be brilliant, the disjunction can only be satisfied if Maya is trustworthy. Hence, we infer that Maya must be trustworthy.
<step n="12" type="derived" from="11,7">
<text>Given that Maya is not brilliant and the disjunction that Maya is either brilliant or trustworthy, we deduce that Maya must be trustworthy.</text>
<logic>trustworthy(maya)</logic>
</step>

Having established that Maya is trustworthy, we can use the rule linking trustworthiness to diplomacy. The rule states that any trustworthy person is diplomatic, so Maya inherits the diplomatic property from her trustworthiness.
<step n="13" type="derived" from="12,2">
<text>From Maya being trustworthy and the rule that trustworthy implies diplomatic, we obtain that Maya is diplomatic.</text>
<logic>diplomatic(maya)</logic>
</step>

With Maya now known to be diplomatic, we turn to the rule that diplomatic individuals are content. Applying this rule gives us the new fact that Maya is content.
<step n="14" type="derived" from="13,1">
<text>From Maya being diplomatic and the rule that diplomatic implies content, we obtain that Maya is content.</text>
<logic>content(maya)</logic>
</step>

The rule connecting content to enthusiasm tells us that any content individual is enthusiastic. Since Maya is content, we can directly infer that Maya is enthusiastic.
<step n="15" type="derived" from="14,3">
<text>From Maya being content and the rule that content implies enthusiastic, we infer that Maya is enthusiastic.</text>
<logic>enthusiastic(maya)</logic>
</step>

One of the conditional statements says that if someone is logical, then they are not enthusiastic. We have just derived that Maya is enthusiastic, which directly contradicts the consequence of being logical. Therefore, Maya cannot be logical; the assumption of logical leads to a conflict, so we conclude ¬logical(Maya).
<step n="16" type="derived" from="15,5">
<text>Since Maya is enthusiastic and the rule that logical implies not enthusiastic, we conclude that Maya cannot be logical.</text>
<logic>~logical(maya)</logic>
</step>

Therefore Maya is not logical, so the conclusion is True.
</think>
Answer: True

examples.completion:
<think>
We are asked to determine whether Presley can be shown to be not rested. To answer this, we must trace the logical consequences of the facts and conditional statements provided, ending with a claim about his rested status.

The second disjunction connects health with enthusiasm. This bridge will let us move from the health fact we eventually obtain to a statement about enthusiasm, setting up the chain toward rested.

<text>Either Presley lacks health or he is enthusiastic.</text>
<logic>~healthy(presley) v enthusiastic(presley)</logic>
</step>

The explicit fact that Presley is confident provides the starting point for the inference chain. It activates the confidence‑to‑health rule and thus begins the derivation process.
<step n="4" type="premise">
<text>It is given that Presley is confident.</text>
<logic>confident(presley)</logic>
</step>

By applying the confidence‑to‑health rule to the known confidence of Presley, we conclude that he is healthy. This step translates a qualitative attribute (confidence) into a concrete health status, which we will need for the next disjunction.
<step n="5" type="derived" from="4,1">

<logic>healthy(presley)</logic>
</step>

We have the disjunction "not healthy or enthusiastic" and we now know Presley is healthy. Since the "not healthy" option is false, the only way for the disjunction to hold is for Presley to be enthusiastic. This uses disjunctive syllogism to derive enthusiasm from health.
<step n="6" type="derived" from="5,3">
<text>Using the disjunction that either not healthy or enthusiastic, together with the fact that Presley is healthy, we deduce that Presley must be enthusiastic.</text>

With Presley established as enthusiastic, we turn to the earlier disjunction "not enthusiastic or rested". Because the "not enthusiastic" side is ruled out, the disjunction forces the conclusion that Presley must be rested. This step completes the forward chain toward the rested predicate.
<step n="7" type="derived" from="6,2">

We derived Presley is rested, so the conclusion Presley is not rested is False.
</think>
Answer: False

examples.context: In this reef community, the angelfish is hardy, the pufferfish is hardy, and the whale is hardy. The jellyfish is disciplined. The angelfish and the pufferfish are not vibrant. Whenever something is disciplined, it is also alert. Whenever something is not vibrant, it becomes ready. If something is observant, then it is timely. If something is hardy, then it is observant. If something is timely, then it is swift. If something is swift, then it is cunning. If something is vibrant, then it is not cunning. Either the whale is not ready or the whale is placid. Also, either the jellyfish is not alert or the jellyfish is bold. All these statements together describe the marine scene.
examples.question: Can we conclude that the whale is not placid?
examples.completion:
<think>
We are asked whether we can assert that the whale is not placid. To answer, we must follow the chain of properties given in the context and see if placid can be derived.

The first rule connects timeliness to swiftness, telling us that any whale that is timely must also be swift. This will let us move from a temporal property to a speed property later in the proof.

<text>The rule states that any whale that is timely is also swift.</text>
<logic>swift(whale) :- timely(whale)</logic>

The second rule says observance guarantees timeliness. This provides a bridge from the whale's basic hardiness to the temporal domain, which is essential for later steps.
<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>If a whale is observant, then it is timely.</text>
<logic>timely(whale) :- observant(whale)</logic>

The third rule links swiftness to cunning, meaning a swift whale becomes cunning. This will be used to obtain the strategic (cunning) attribute of the whale.
<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>Whenever a whale is swift, it is also cunning.</text>
<logic>cunning(whale) :- swift(whale)</logic>
</step>

The fourth rule tells us that a hardy creature is automatically observant. Since the whale is hardy, this rule will allow us to start the forward chain of inferences.
<step n="4" type="premise">
<text>A whale that is hardy is automatically observant.</text>
<logic>observant(whale) :- hardy(whale)</logic>
</step>

The fifth rule states that if a whale is vibrant, it cannot be cunning. This contrapositive relationship will later help us infer the lack of vibrancy from the presence of cunning.
<step n="5" type="premise">
<text>If a whale is vibrant, then it cannot be cunning.</text>
<logic>~cunning(whale) :- vibrant(whale)</logic>
</step>

The disjunction gives us two alternatives: either the whale is not ready, or it is placid. Once we know the whale is ready, we can eliminate the first alternative and conclude placid.
<step n="6" type="premise">
<text>Either the whale is not ready, or it is placid.</text>
<logic>~ready(whale) v placid(whale)</logic>
</step>

The fact that the angelfish is hardy provides an example of a hardy marine creature, reinforcing the context but not directly used in the whale's derivation.
<step n="7" type="premise">
<text>The angelfish is hardy.</text>
<logic>hardy(angelfish)</logic>
</step>

Similarly, the pufferfish being hardy adds another instance of the hardy property, supporting the general rule about hardness.
<step n="8" type="premise">

<logic>hardy(pufferfish)</logic>
</step>

The jellyfish being disciplined supplies the antecedent for the rule that disciplined implies alert, though this rule is not needed for the whale argument.
<step n="9" type="premise">
<text>The jellyfish is disciplined.</text>
<logic>disciplined(jellyfish)</logic>
</step>

The angelfish not being vibrant aligns with the rule that lack of vibrancy leads to readiness, showing how negative properties can trigger other facts.

<logic>~vibrant(angelfish)</logic>

The pufferfish also lacks vibrancy, mirroring the angelfish's situation and illustrating the pattern of non‑vibrant creatures.
<step n="11" type="premise">

<logic>~vibrant(pufferfish)</logic>

The whale being hardy is the crucial starting point for our chain: it activates the rule that hardy implies observant.
<step n="12" type="premise">
<text>The whale is hardy.</text>
<logic>hardy(whale)</logic>
</step>

Because the whale is hardy and the rule says hardy → observant, we can safely conclude the whale is observant. This moves us from a basic physical trait to a cognitive‑like trait.

<text>From the whale being hardy and the rule that hardy implies observant, we infer the whale is observant.</text>

</step>

Having established that the whale is observant, we apply the rule observant → timely, yielding that the whale is timely. This gives us a temporal property needed for the next step.
<step n="14" type="derived" from="13,2">

From timeliness we invoke the rule timely → swift, allowing us to deduce that the whale is swift. Now the whale possesses a speed attribute that connects to cunning.
<step n="15" type="derived" from="14,1">

The swift property triggers the rule swift → cunning, so the whale becomes cunning. At this point we have derived the strategic (cunning) characteristic of the whale.

We now have the whale being cunning, but the rule "vibrant → not cunning" lets us use modus tollens: if the whale were vibrant, it could not be cunning. Since it is cunning, the antecedent must be false, so the whale is not vibrant.

examples.question: Can we conclude that the celestial_hero is diligent?

<step n="2" type="premise">

The disjunction gives us two alternatives: either the celestial_hero is not neutral, or the celestial_hero is diligent. This statement will become decisive once we know whether the celestial_hero is neutral.
<step n="3" type="premise">

We are explicitly told that the celestial_hero is a negotiator. This fact serves as the starting point for applying the first rule and beginning the chain of deductions.
<step n="4" type="premise">

Applying the first rule to the known negotiator status, we can conclude that the celestial_hero must be battle_ready. This step uses modus ponens: the antecedent holds, so the consequent follows.

Now that we have established battle_ready, the second rule tells us that any battle_ready entity is neutral. Consequently, the celestial_hero is neutral, again by modus ponens.

examples.context: The blueprint is aesthetic. Either the blueprint is not aesthetic, or it is spacious. If a blueprint is spacious, then it is structurally sound. If a blueprint is energy efficient, then it is not structurally sound.
examples.question: Is the blueprint energy efficient?
examples.completion:
<think>
We are asked to decide whether the blueprint possesses the energy efficient property. To answer, we will use the given facts, the conditional relationships, and the logical connections among them.

The rule linking spaciousness to structural soundness is important because it lets us turn a spatial property into a structural one later in the proof. Recognizing this implication will allow us to derive a concrete fact about the blueprint's soundness.
<step n="1" type="premise">
<text>Any blueprint that is spacious is also structurally sound.</text>
<logic>structurally_sound(blueprint) :- spacious(blueprint)</logic>
</step>

The second conditional tells us that being energy efficient forces the blueprint to lack structural soundness. This contrapositive relationship will be crucial when we later have a positive statement about structural soundness.
<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>If a blueprint is energy efficient, then it is not structurally sound.</text>
<logic>~structurally_sound(blueprint) :- energy_efficient(blueprint)</logic>
</step>

The disjunction gives us a choice between the blueprint not being aesthetic or being spacious. Combined with the fact that the blueprint is aesthetic, this will let us eliminate one option and pinpoint the other.
<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>Either the blueprint is not aesthetic, or it is spacious.</text>
<logic>~aesthetic(blueprint) v spacious(blueprint)</logic>
</step>

Stating explicitly that the blueprint is aesthetic provides the concrete fact needed to resolve the disjunction from the previous premise. It tells us the blueprint does possess the aesthetic quality.
<step n="4" type="premise">
<text>The blueprint is aesthetic.</text>
<logic>aesthetic(blueprint)</logic>
</step>

Because we know the blueprint is aesthetic, the first part of the disjunction (~aesthetic) is false, so the only way the statement "either not aesthetic or spacious" can hold is if the blueprint is spacious. Thus we deduce spaciousness directly from the disjunction and the fact.
<step n="5" type="derived" from="4,3">
<text>From the blueprint being aesthetic and the either-or statement, we infer that the blueprint must be spacious.</text>
<logic>spacious(blueprint)</logic>
</step>

Having established that the blueprint is spacious, we can now apply the rule that any spacious blueprint is structurally sound. This yields the conclusion that the blueprint is indeed structurally sound.
<step n="6" type="derived" from="5,1">
<text>Since the blueprint is spacious, the rule that spacious implies structurally sound lets us conclude the blueprint is structurally sound.</text>
<logic>structurally_sound(blueprint)</logic>
</step>

We now have a positive claim that the blueprint is structurally sound. The rule about energy efficiency says that if the blueprint were energy efficient, it would have to be not structurally sound. Since we have the opposite, we infer that the blueprint cannot be energy efficient; otherwise we would have a contradiction.
<step n="7" type="derived" from="6,2">
<text>Given the blueprint is structurally sound, and the rule that energy efficient implies not structurally sound, we can infer that the blueprint cannot be energy efficient.</text>
<logic>~energy_efficient(blueprint)</logic>
</step>

We derived Blueprint is not energy efficient, so the conclusion Blueprint is energy efficient is False.
</think>
Answer: False

examples.question: Is the chassis efficient_route_planning?

We are asked to determine whether the chassis possesses efficient_route_planning. The goal is to chain the given facts and rules to reach that property for the chassis.

The premise that any reliable component is safety_oriented will be useful because we know the chassis is reliable, allowing us to infer safety orientation later in the proof.

Linking service_friendly to robustness gives us a way to move from a service_friendly status of the chassis—once we establish it—to a robust status, which is a step toward efficient_route_planning.
<step n="2" type="premise">

The rule that robust components are efficient_route_planning is the final bridge needed; once we have robustness for the chassis, this rule will let us conclude the desired property.

<text>Any robust component is efficient_route_planning, expressing that robustness guarantees efficient route planning.</text>
<logic>efficient_route_planning(chassis) :- robust(chassis)</logic>

The premise that user_friendly implies not safety_oriented will help us eliminate the user_friendly alternative for the chassis, which is crucial for later disjunctive reasoning.
<step n="4" type="premise">

<logic>~safety_oriented(chassis) :- user_friendly(chassis)</logic>

The disjunction stating the chassis is either user_friendly or service_friendly sets up a choice; by ruling out one side we can force the other, which is essential for deriving service_friendly.
<step n="5" type="premise">
<text>The chassis is either user_friendly or service_friendly, providing a disjunction about the chassis' properties.</text>
<logic>user_friendly(chassis) v service_friendly(chassis)</logic>

The fact that the fuel_injector is service_friendly does not directly involve the chassis, but it illustrates the presence of the service_friendly predicate in the domain.

Knowing the fuel_injector is reliable similarly demonstrates the reliable predicate, reinforcing the pattern that reliability leads to safety orientation.

The adaptiveness of the nav_system is unrelated to the current query but completes the set of given facts.
<step n="8" type="premise">

<logic>adaptive(nav_system)</logic>
</step>

The chassis being reliable is a key fact; combined with the reliable‑to‑safety_oriented rule, it enables us to infer safety orientation for the chassis.
<step n="9" type="premise">
<text>The chassis is reliable.</text>
<logic>reliable(chassis)</logic>
</step>

Applying the rule that reliable implies safety_oriented to the chassis' reliability yields safety_oriented for the chassis. This step establishes a property that will later allow us to reject the user_friendly alternative.
<step n="10" type="derived" from="9,1">
<text>From the chassis being reliable and the rule that reliable implies safety_oriented, we infer that the chassis is safety_oriented.</text>

Since the chassis is safety_oriented, and a user_friendly component would make it not safety_oriented, the only consistent conclusion is that the chassis is not user_friendly. This denial is derived by contraposition of the user_friendly‑to‑not‑safety rule.

With the chassis not being user_friendly and the earlier disjunction that it must be either user_friendly or service_friendly, we are forced to accept that the chassis is service_friendly. This is a classic disjunctive syllogism.
<step n="12" type="derived" from="11,5">

Now that we have established the chassis as service_friendly, we can invoke the rule linking service_friendly to robustness, giving us that the chassis is robust. This moves us one step closer to the target property.
<step n="13" type="derived" from="12,2">
<text>Since the chassis is service_friendly and service_friendly leads to robustness, we derive that the chassis is robust.</text>

Finally, using the robust‑to‑efficient_route_planning rule, the robustness of the chassis directly yields efficient_route_planning for the chassis, completing the chain of reasoning.
<step n="14" type="derived" from="13,3">
