examples.context: Harley is motivated, Hayden is goal oriented, and Scout is wise. Whenever Harley is motivated, Harley becomes cheerful. If Scout is wise, then Scout is confident. When Hayden is goal oriented, Hayden becomes curious. If someone is clever, then Hayden becomes diligent; likewise, if someone is determined, Harley becomes supportive. Being thorough guarantees that Scout is focused, and any focused scout is reflective. A cooperative scout ensures that Scout is thorough. Proficiency of Scout leads to Scout not being confident, while openness of mind makes Scout not reflective. The knowledge base also contains three alternatives: either Scout is proficient or Scout is cooperative; either Harley is not cheerful or Harley is determined; and either Hayden is not curious or Hayden is clever.
examples.question: Is Scout open-minded?
examples.completion:
<think>
We are asked to determine whether Scout possesses the property of being open-minded. The goal is to see if the available facts and rules force open_minded(scout) to be true or false.

This premise connects wisdom to confidence for Scout, which will later allow us to derive confidence from the known fact that Scout is wise. Knowing confidence is essential because another rule talks about the effect of proficiency on confidence.
<step n="1" type="premise">
<text>The rule says that if Scout is wise then Scout is confident.</text>
<logic>confident(scout) :- wise(scout)</logic>
</step>

Here we learn that thoroughness guarantees focus for Scout. This chain will become useful after we establish that Scout is thorough, letting us move toward focus and eventually reflectiveness.
<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>The rule states that if Scout is thorough then Scout is focused.</text>
<logic>focused(scout) :- thorough(scout)</logic>
</step>

The third premise tells us that any focused Scout becomes reflective. It creates a bridge from focus (which we will get from thoroughness) to reflectiveness, a property that later interacts with openness.
<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>The rule declares that if Scout is focused then Scout is reflective.</text>
<logic>reflective(scout) :- focused(scout)</logic>
</step>

This rule says that if Scout is cooperative, then Scout must be thorough. It will allow us to turn a cooperative status into thoroughness, feeding the focus chain.
<step n="4" type="premise">
<text>The rule indicates that if Scout is cooperative then Scout is thorough.</text>
<logic>thorough(scout) :- cooperative(scout)</logic>
</step>

According to this rule, proficiency would make Scout not confident. This negative link will be used in a contrapositive style to eliminate the possibility of proficiency once we have confidence.
<step n="5" type="premise">
<text>The rule asserts that if Scout is proficient then Scout is not confident.</text>
<logic>~confident(scout) :- proficient(scout)</logic>
</step>

The sixth premise states that being open-minded would cause Scout to lose reflectiveness. By later establishing that Scout is reflective, we can use this to argue that Scout cannot be open-minded.
<step n="6" type="premise">
<text>The rule claims that if Scout is open-minded then Scout is not reflective.</text>
<logic>~reflective(scout) :- open_minded(scout)</logic>
</step>

The disjunction gives us two possible ways to satisfy Scout's status: either Scout is proficient or Scout is cooperative. This logical alternative will be crucial when we rule out one option, forcing the other.
<step n="7" type="premise">
<text>The disjunction tells us that either Scout is proficient or Scout is cooperative.</text>
<logic>proficient(scout) v cooperative(scout)</logic>
</step>

The fact that Hayden is goal oriented is part of the broader knowledge base but does not directly affect the open-minded query. It is listed to ensure completeness of the context.
<step n="8" type="premise">
<text>It is a fact that Hayden is goal oriented.</text>
<logic>goal_oriented(hayden)</logic>
</step>

Harley's motivation is another background fact that supports other rules about cheerfulness and supportiveness, though it is not directly needed for our target conclusion.
<step n="9" type="premise">
<text>It is a fact that Harley is motivated.</text>
<logic>motivated(harley)</logic>
</step>

The fact that Scout is wise is the cornerstone for deriving confidence via PREMISE_1. It gives us a concrete starting point for the confidence chain.
<step n="10" type="premise">
<text>It is a fact that Scout is wise.</text>
<logic>wise(scout)</logic>
</step>

Since we know Scout is wise (PREMISE_10) and the rule that wisdom implies confidence (PREMISE_1), we can directly infer that Scout is confident. This step establishes confidence, which will later help us eliminate proficiency.
<step n="11" type="derived" from="10,1">
<text>From the fact that Scout is wise and the rule linking wisdom to confidence, we infer that Scout is confident.</text>
<logic>confident(scout)</logic>
</step>

We now have confidence for Scout, and PREMISE_5 tells us that proficiency would make Scout not confident. Because Scout is already confident, the only way to avoid a contradiction is to conclude that Scout cannot be proficient. This is a classic use of modus tollens.
<step n="12" type="derived" from="11,5">
<text>Because Scout is confident and the rule that proficiency would make Scout not confident, we conclude that Scout cannot be proficient.</text>
<logic>~proficient(scout)</logic>
</step>

Having shown that Scout is not proficient, we return to the disjunction from PREMISE_7, which said Scout is either proficient or cooperative. Since the first option is false, the second must hold, so Scout is cooperative. This follows the principle of disjunctive syllogism.
<step n="13" type="derived" from="12,7">
<text>Given that Scout is not proficient and the disjunction that Scout is either proficient or cooperative, we deduce that Scout must be cooperative.</text>
<logic>cooperative(scout)</logic>
</step>

With Scout identified as cooperative, PREMISE_4 lets us deduce that Scout is thorough. Cooperative status directly triggers thoroughness, moving us further along the chain toward reflectiveness.
<step n="14" type="derived" from="13,4">
<text>Since Scout is cooperative and cooperative implies thoroughness, we obtain that Scout is thorough.</text>
<logic>thorough(scout)</logic>
</step>

Now that Scout is thorough, PREMISE_2 allows us to move from thoroughness to focus. This step is a straightforward application of the rule that thoroughness guarantees focus.
<step n="15" type="derived" from="14,2">
<text>With Scout being thorough and the rule that thoroughness leads to focus, we get that Scout is focused.</text>
<logic>focused(scout)</logic>
</step>

Having obtained focus for Scout, we apply PREMISE_3, which converts focus into reflectiveness. Thus Scout becomes reflective, setting up the final contradiction with openness.
<step n="16" type="derived" from="15,3">
<text>Because Scout is focused and focus leads to reflectiveness, we infer that Scout is reflective.</text>
<logic>reflective(scout)</logic>
</step>

We have established that Scout is reflective, but PREMISE_6 says that if Scout were open-minded, Scout would not be reflective. Since Scout is indeed reflective, the only consistent conclusion is that Scout cannot be open-minded. This uses modus tollens again.
<step n="17" type="derived" from="16,6">
<text>Reflectiveness together with the rule that openness would negate reflectiveness lets us infer that Scout cannot be open-minded.</text>
<logic>~open_minded(scout)</logic>
</step>

We derived Scout is not open minded, so the conclusion Scout is open minded is False.
</think>
Answer: False

examples.context: Captain Beth keeps her ship orderly. Whenever Beth is helpful, she becomes eager. If Beth works diligently, she stays calm. If Beth is steady, then she is not calm. Either Beth is steady or she is skilled. Beth is either not eager or she is diligent. Either Beth is not orderly or she is helpful.
examples.question: Can we conclude that Captain Beth is not skilled?
examples.completion:
<think>
We are asked to determine whether Captain Beth can be said to lack skill. The goal is to establish the negation of the predicate 'skilled' for Beth, using the facts and conditional statements provided about her character and behavior. By chaining the given rules and disjunctions, we will see what conclusions about her steadiness and skill can be drawn.

This premise tells us how helpfulness leads to eagerness for Beth. It will become useful once we know Beth is helpful, allowing us to infer that she is eager.
<step n="1" type="premise">
<text>The rule that if Beth is helpful then she is eager.</text>
<logic>eager(captain_beth) :- helpful(captain_beth)</logic>
</step>

Here we have the link between diligence and calmness. Once we can prove Beth is diligent, we will be able to assert that she is calm, which later interacts with the rule about steadiness.
<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>The rule that if Beth is diligent then she is calm.</text>
<logic>calm(captain_beth) :- diligent(captain_beth)</logic>
</step>

This rule states that steadiness precludes calmness. It will be crucial for a contrapositive style argument: if we find Beth is calm, we can conclude she cannot be steady.
<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>The rule that if Beth is steady then she is not calm.</text>
<logic>~calm(captain_beth) :- steady(captain_beth)</logic>
</step>

The first disjunction offers two possibilities for Beth: either she is steady or she is skilled. This will be the final place where we can place the skill predicate once we eliminate the steady alternative.
<step n="4" type="premise">
<text>The disjunction that Beth is either steady or skilled.</text>
<logic>steady(captain_beth) v skilled(captain_beth)</logic>
</step>

The second disjunction gives a choice between not being eager or being diligent. When we later establish that Beth is indeed eager, this forces the other side of the disjunction—diligence—to hold.
<step n="5" type="premise">
<text>The disjunction that Beth is either not eager or diligent.</text>
<logic>~eager(captain_beth) v diligent(captain_beth)</logic>
</step>

The third disjunction connects orderliness with helpfulness. Because we already have Beth being orderly as a fact, this disjunction will let us deduce that she must be helpful.
<step n="6" type="premise">
<text>The disjunction that Beth is either not orderly or helpful.</text>
<logic>~orderly(captain_beth) v helpful(captain_beth)</logic>
</step>

The explicit fact that Beth is orderly serves as the starting point for applying the disjunction about orderliness and helpfulness.
<step n="7" type="premise">
<text>The fact that Beth is orderly.</text>
<logic>orderly(captain_beth)</logic>
</step>

Combining the fact that Beth is orderly with the disjunction that she is either not orderly or helpful, we see that the 'not orderly' option is impossible. Therefore, the only way for the disjunction to hold is that Beth is helpful. This gives us the helpfulness needed for the next rule.
<step n="8" type="derived" from="7,6">
<text>From Beth being orderly and the disjunction that she is either not orderly or helpful, we infer that Beth is helpful.</text>
<logic>helpful(captain_beth)</logic>
</step>

Now that we know Beth is helpful, we can use the rule linking helpfulness to eagerness. The rule tells us that any helpful person becomes eager, so we conclude Beth is eager.
<step n="9" type="derived" from="8,1">
<text>Using the rule that helpful implies eager, we conclude that Beth is eager.</text>
<logic>eager(captain_beth)</logic>
</step>

We have Beth being eager, and the disjunction says Beth is either not eager or diligent. Since the first alternative contradicts what we just established, the disjunction forces the second alternative: Beth must be diligent.
<step n="10" type="derived" from="9,5">
<text>Applying the disjunction that Beth is either not eager or diligent together with Beth being eager, we deduce that Beth is diligent.</text>
<logic>diligent(captain_beth)</logic>
</step>

Having established Beth's diligence, we apply the rule that diligence leads to calmness. Thus Beth is calm, giving us a positive fact that will interact with the steadiness rule.
<step n="11" type="derived" from="10,2">
<text>From Beth being diligent and the rule that diligent implies calm, we obtain that Beth is calm.</text>
<logic>calm(captain_beth)</logic>
</step>

We know Beth is calm, and the rule states that if someone is steady then they are not calm. The presence of calmness therefore blocks the possibility of steadiness; we infer that Beth cannot be steady, i.e., she is not steady.
<step n="12" type="derived" from="11,3">
<text>Since Beth is calm and the rule that steady implies not calm, we infer that Beth cannot be steady.</text>
<logic>~steady(captain_beth)</logic>
</step>

With the knowledge that Beth is not steady, we return to the original disjunction that she is either steady or skilled. Since the steady option is ruled out, the only remaining way for the disjunction to be true is that Beth is skilled.
<step n="13" type="derived" from="12,4">
<text>With the knowledge that Beth is not steady and the disjunction that she is either steady or skilled, we derive that Beth is skilled.</text>
<logic>skilled(captain_beth)</logic>
</step>

We derived Captain_beth is skilled, so the conclusion Captain_beth is not skilled is False.
</think>
Answer: False

examples.context: Pilot Stella is known to be truthful. In this aviation setting, any pilot who is mission driven is also teamwork oriented. Conversely, if a pilot is cautious in flying, that pilot cannot be truthful. Regarding Stella's disposition, either she is cautious in flying or she is mission driven (or possibly both).
examples.question: Is it the case that Pilot Stella is not teamwork oriented?
examples.completion:
<think>
We need to determine whether Pilot Stella lacks the property of being teamwork oriented. The goal is to use the given facts, rules, and the either‑or statement to see what can be concluded about her teamwork status.

The first rule connects mission driven behavior to teamwork orientation, which will be useful later when we establish whether Stella is mission driven. It tells us exactly how to move from a motivation property to a cooperative one.
<step n="1" type="premise">
<text>The rule says that whenever a pilot is mission driven, the pilot is teamwork oriented.</text>
<logic>teamwork_oriented(pilot_stella) :- mission_driven(pilot_stella)</logic>
</step>

The second rule provides a link between cautious flying and a lack of truthfulness. This conditional will allow us to reason about Stella's cautiousness by looking at her truthfulness status.
<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>The rule states that if a pilot is cautious in flying, then the pilot is not truthful.</text>
<logic>~truthful(pilot_stella) :- cautious_flying(pilot_stella)</logic>
</step>

The disjunction gives us a limited choice: Stella must be either cautious or mission driven (or both). This binary information will become decisive once we can eliminate one of the options.
<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>The information tells us that Pilot Stella is either cautious in flying or mission driven (or possibly both).</text>
<logic>cautious_flying(pilot_stella) v mission_driven(pilot_stella)</logic>
</step>

We are explicitly told that Stella is truthful. This concrete fact will interact with the rule about cautiousness, because that rule says cautiousness would make a pilot untruthful.
<step n="4" type="premise">
<text>We are told directly that Pilot Stella is truthful.</text>
<logic>truthful(pilot_stella)</logic>
</step>

Because Stella is truthful, the possibility that she is cautious (which would imply she is not truthful) cannot hold. Using modus tollens, we invert the conditional: if cautious leads to not truthful, then being truthful forces the negation of cautiousness. Hence we deduce that Stella is not cautious in flying.
<step n="5" type="derived" from="4,2">
<text>From the fact that Stella is truthful and the rule that cautiousness would make her untruthful, we infer that she cannot be cautious in flying.</text>
<logic>~cautious_flying(pilot_stella)</logic>
</step>

With the knowledge that Stella is not cautious, the disjunction "cautious or mission driven" leaves only the mission driven alternative as viable. Applying disjunctive syllogism, we drop the false disjunct and conclude that Stella must be mission driven.
<step n="6" type="derived" from="5,3">
<text>Since Stella is not cautious in flying and we know she must be either cautious or mission driven, we conclude that she is mission driven.</text>
<logic>mission_driven(pilot_stella)</logic>
</step>

Now that we have established Stella is mission driven, we can invoke the first rule again. Modus ponens lets us move from the antecedent (mission driven) to the consequent, giving us that Stella is teamwork oriented.
<step n="7" type="derived" from="6,1">
<text>Given that Stella is mission driven, the earlier rule lets us deduce that she is teamwork oriented.</text>
<logic>teamwork_oriented(pilot_stella)</logic>
</step>

We derived Pilot_stella is teamwork oriented, so the conclusion Pilot_stella is not teamwork oriented is False.
</think>
Answer: False

examples.question: Is the hydro_station not monitoring?

The seventh premise offers that the hydro station is either not eco_friendly or sustainable. Combined with the fact that it is eco_friendly, we can force sustainability.

The hydro station being eco_friendly is a crucial fact that will interact with the seventh disjunction.

From the hydro station being eco_friendly and the alternative that it is either not eco_friendly or sustainable, we infer that the hydro station must be sustainable. <step n="11" type="derived" from="10,7">

Since the hydro station is sustainable and the rule says that if it were not sustainable then it would be compliant, we can contrapositively conclude that it cannot be compliant. Thus we derive that the hydro station is not compliant.

Given that the hydro station is not compliant and the disjunction that it is either compliant or innovative, the only remaining possibility is that it is innovative. Therefore we deduce the innovative property.

examples.question: Is the four_leaf_clover not pruned?
examples.completion:

We are asked to decide whether the four_leaf_clover is not pruned. To answer, we must follow the garden rules that connect watering, thriving, pest resistance, moisture, spacing, and fragrance to pruning. By chaining these relationships we can determine the pruning status of the four_leaf_clover.

The rule linking well_watered to thriving is essential because we know the four_leaf_clover is well_watered. Using this, we can establish that the plant is thriving, setting up the subsequent step toward pest resistance.

The rule that fragrance leads to pruning will be used later once we can show the four_leaf_clover is fragrant. That will directly give us the pruning status we need for the final query.

</step>

The rule stating that moisture prevents pest resistance allows us to infer the negation of moisture from the presence of pest resistance. This contrapositive reasoning will be crucial after we have established pest resistance.
<step n="4" type="premise">

<logic>~pest_resistant(four_leaf_clover) :- moist(four_leaf_clover)</logic>
</step>

The disjunction "not spaced or fragrant" gives us two possible ways to satisfy the condition for the four_leaf_clover. Once we know whether the plant is spaced, we can resolve which side of the disjunction holds, eventually leading to fragrance.

The fact that rosemary is well_watered is included for completeness but does not directly affect the four_leaf_clover's pruning status. It demonstrates the garden's general watering practice.

</step>

Rosemary being not moist is another factual detail about the garden; it exemplifies the rule that non‑moist plants become spaced, though this will not be needed for the four_leaf_clover chain.
<step n="7" type="premise">
<text>Rosemary is not moist.</text>
<logic>~moist(rosemary)</logic>
</step>

The fact that the four_leaf_clover is well_watered is the starting point for our derivation. It enables us to apply the well_watered‑to‑thriving rule immediately.
<step n="8" type="premise">
<text>The four_leaf_clover is well_watered.</text>
<logic>well_watered(four_leaf_clover)</logic>
</step>

Using the well_watered fact for the four_leaf_clover and the rule that well_watered implies thriving, we conclude the plant is thriving. This step transforms a concrete fact into a more useful property for later inference.
<step n="9" type="derived" from="8,2">

From the thriving status just derived and the rule that thriving leads to pest resistance, we infer that the four_leaf_clover is pest_resistant. This establishes a property that will let us reason about the plant's moisture level.
<step n="10" type="derived" from="9,1">

<logic>pest_resistant(four_leaf_clover)</logic>
</step>

Since we now know the four_leaf_clover is pest_resistant, and we have the rule that moisture would make a plant not pest_resistant, we can apply modus tollens to deduce that the plant cannot be moist; therefore it is not moist. This gives us the negated moisture fact we need for the spacing rule.
<step n="11" type="derived" from="10,4">

<step n="4" type="premise">
<text>Either nora_the_narwhal is not healthy or she is adaptable.</text>
<logic>~healthy(nora_the_narwhal) v adaptable(nora_the_narwhal)</logic>

Victor the vole being stressed is recorded, though it does not directly affect Nora's chain. It illustrates that the stress condition can apply to multiple entities in the reserve.
<step n="5" type="premise">

<logic>stressed(victor_the_vole)</logic>
</step>

Nora the narwhal being stressed is the factual anchor for the first rule we will apply. It activates the conditional in PREMISE_3, allowing us to start the inference process.
<step n="6" type="premise">
<text>Nora the narwhal is stressed.</text>
<logic>stressed(nora_the_narwhal)</logic>
</step>

Using the fact that Nora is stressed (PREMISE_6) together with the rule that stress implies migration readiness (PREMISE_3), we can conclude Nora is migration_ready. This establishes the antecedent needed for the next rule.
<step n="7" type="derived" from="6,3">
<text>From Nora being stressed we infer that she is migration_ready.</text>
<logic>migration_ready(nora_the_narwhal)</logic>
</step>

Now that we know Nora is migration_ready (STEP_7), we apply the rule that migration readiness leads to health (PREMISE_2). This yields the conclusion that Nora is healthy, setting up the left side of the disjunction.
<step n="8" type="derived" from="7,2">
<text>Because Nora is migration_ready, we can conclude she is healthy.</text>
<logic>healthy(nora_the_narwhal)</logic>
</step>

We have derived that Nora is healthy (STEP_8). The disjunction (PREMISE_4) says either Nora is not healthy or she is adaptable. Since the first option is false (she is healthy), the only way for the disjunction to hold is that Nora must be adaptable. Thus we infer adaptability.
<step n="9" type="derived" from="8,4">

Having established that Nora is adaptable (STEP_9), we can finally use the rule linking adaptability to activity (PREMISE_1). This gives us the desired conclusion that Nora is active.

Therefore Nora_the_narwhal is active, so the conclusion is True.

Answer: True

examples.context: In the economic landscape, the firm ash_corp is known to be budget_focused. Whenever a firm is prudent, it is also strategic. If a firm is flexible, then it cannot be strategic. Either the firm is not collaborative or it is prudent. Also, either the firm is not budget_focused or it is collaborative.
examples.question: Is ash_corp flexible?
examples.completion:
<think>
We are asked to decide whether the firm ash_corp possesses the property of being flexible. Our goal is to use the supplied facts, rules, and disjunctions to see if flexibility can be justified or must be rejected.

The first premise gives a direct link between prudence and strategic behavior. Recognizing this connection will be essential later when we manage to establish that ash_corp is prudent.
<step n="1" type="premise">
<text>The rule states that if ash_corp is prudent, then it must be strategic.</text>
<logic>strategic(ash_corp) :- prudent(ash_corp)</logic>
</step>

The second premise tells us that flexibility would rule out strategic status. This contrapositive relationship will let us infer non‑flexibility once we know ash_corp is strategic.
<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>The rule states that if ash_corp is flexible, then it cannot be strategic.</text>
<logic>~strategic(ash_corp) :- flexible(ash_corp)</logic>
</step>

The third premise provides a choice: ash_corp is either not collaborative or it is prudent. This will become useful after we determine whether ash_corp is collaborative.
<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>The disjunction says that ash_corp is either not collaborative or it is prudent.</text>
<logic>~collaborative(ash_corp) v prudent(ash_corp)</logic>
</step>

The fourth premise offers another choice: ash_corp is either not budget_focused or it is collaborative. Because we already have a fact about budget_focused, this disjunction will let us deduce collaboration.
<step n="4" type="premise">
<text>The disjunction says that ash_corp is either not budget_focused or it is collaborative.</text>
<logic>~budget_focused(ash_corp) v collaborative(ash_corp)</logic>
</step>

The fact that ash_corp is budget_focused is a solid starting point. It will interact with the fourth disjunction to produce a new piece of information.
<step n="5" type="premise">
<text>It is a fact that ash_corp is budget_focused.</text>
<logic>budget_focused(ash_corp)</logic>
</step>

Since ash_corp is confirmed to be budget_focused, the disjunction "not budget_focused or collaborative" cannot be satisfied by the first alternative. Consequently, the second alternative must hold, giving us that ash_corp is collaborative. This step uses disjunctive syllogism to eliminate the false option.
<step n="6" type="derived" from="5,4">
<text>From the fact that ash_corp is budget_focused and the disjunction that it is either not budget_focused or collaborative, we infer that ash_corp must be collaborative.</text>
<logic>collaborative(ash_corp)</logic>
</step>

Now that we know ash_corp is collaborative, the disjunction "not collaborative or prudent" can no longer be satisfied by "not collaborative". Therefore, the remaining alternative must be true, and ash_corp is prudent. Again, disjunctive syllogism lets us infer the necessary condition.
<step n="7" type="derived" from="6,3">
<text>Given that ash_corp is collaborative and the disjunction that it is either not collaborative or prudent, we conclude that ash_corp is prudent.</text>
<logic>prudent(ash_corp)</logic>
</step>

Having established that ash_corp is prudent, we can apply the rule that prudence implies strategic status. By modus ponens, we derive that ash_corp is strategic. This links our earlier derivations to the strategic property.
<step n="8" type="derived" from="7,1">
<text>Since ash_corp is prudent, the rule that prudence implies strategic allows us to infer that ash_corp is strategic.</text>
<logic>strategic(ash_corp)</logic>
</step>

We now have ash_corp as strategic, while the second premise tells us that flexibility would make a firm not strategic. Using modus tollens, the presence of the strategic property forces the negation of flexibility, so ash_corp cannot be flexible.
<step n="9" type="derived" from="8,2">
<text>Because ash_corp is strategic and the rule that flexibility would prevent strategic status, we apply modus tollens to deduce that ash_corp is not flexible.</text>
<logic>~flexible(ash_corp)</logic>
</step>

We derived Ash_corp is not flexible, so the conclusion Ash_corp is flexible is False.
</think>
Answer: False

examples.context: Ada, Henry, Sage, Selene, and Zara are individuals under consideration. Ada, Henry and Sage are each capable. Ambition rules out competitiveness: if a person is ambitious, they cannot be competitive. For Zara, cleverness leads to organization, and courage would make her not organized. Selene’s dedication gives her talent, while talent and systematic habits are linked by a disjunction. Additionally, the system tells us that either Henry is ambitious or Henry is studious, and either Zara is not organized or Zara is courageous, and either Selene is not talented or Selene is systematic. These statements together form the complete knowledge base for the problem.

examples.context: In the coastal study, the coral reef is known to be temperature_resistant. The sea turtle constantly signals predator_alert behavior. The kelp_forest glows with bioluminescent light. Whenever something is dominant, it is also migratory, so a dominant sea_turtle would be migratory. If a coral_reef is substrate_dependent, then it tends to be slow_swim. Any organism that is symbiotic is also a filter_feeder. A sea_turtle that is predator_alert will exhibit burst_swim activity. A coral_reef that is temperature_resistant displays camouflage. Either the sea_turtle is not burst_swim or it is dominant. Either the coral_reef is not camouflage or it is substrate_dependent. By default, an organism that is bioluminescent and for which there is no abnormality preventing compliance is considered symbiotic. Likewise, an organism that is a filter_feeder and for which there is no abnormality preventing organization is regarded as colony_structured.
examples.question: Is it true that the kelp_forest is not colony_structured?
examples.completion:
<think>
We are asked to decide whether the kelp_forest fails to be colony_structured. To answer this, we must see if the information given allows us to prove that the kelp_forest actually is colony_structured. The proof proceeds by chaining the default and ordinary rules concerning symbiotic and filter_feeder properties.

The rule linking symbiotic to filter_feeder is crucial because it lets us move from a compliance condition to an activity condition for the kelp_forest. Recognizing this connection will later allow us to reach the organization property.

<text>The rule tells us that any symbiotic kelp_forest is also a filter_feeder.</text>
<logic>filter_feeder(kelp_forest) :- symbiotic(kelp_forest)</logic>
</step>

The default rule about symbiotic provides a way to obtain the symbiotic status of the kelp_forest from its bioluminescent fact, assuming no abnormality. This gives us the first stepping stone toward the final goal.
<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>The default rule says that a bioluminescent kelp_forest, assuming no abnormality, is symbiotic.</text>
<logic>symbiotic(kelp_forest) :- bioluminescent(kelp_forest), not ab_symbiotic(kelp_forest)</logic>
</step>

The second default rule connects a filter_feeder status to the colony_structured organization, again assuming no abnormality. This rule is the final bridge we need to reach the target property.
<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>The default rule states that a filter_feeder kelp_forest, assuming no abnormality, is colony_structured.</text>
<logic>colony_structured(kelp_forest) :- filter_feeder(kelp_forest), not ab_colony_structured(kelp_forest)</logic>
</step>

Stating that the coral_reef is temperature_resistant supplies one of the many contextual facts, though it does not directly influence the kelp_forest chain. It nonetheless completes the list of given facts required by the problem statement.
<step n="4" type="premise">
<text>The fact records that the coral_reef is temperature_resistant.</text>
<logic>temperature_resistant(coral_reef)</logic>
</step>

Similarly, noting that the sea_turtle is predator_alert adds another factual element to the scenario. While irrelevant to the kelp_forest reasoning, it satisfies the requirement to mention every fact.
<step n="5" type="premise">
<text>The fact records that the sea_turtle is predator_alert.</text>
<logic>predator_alert(sea_turtle)</logic>
</step>

The fact that the kelp_forest is bioluminescent is the key observation that activates the default rule for compliance. Without this fact, we could not start the inference toward symbiotic status.
<step n="6" type="premise">
<text>The fact records that the kelp_forest is bioluminescent.</text>
<logic>bioluminescent(kelp_forest)</logic>
</step>

Because the kelp_forest is bioluminescent and we have no indication of an abnormality that would block compliance, the default rule lets us conclude that the kelp_forest is symbiotic. This step translates a physical observation into a relational property needed for later steps.
<step n="7" type="derived" from="6,2">
<text>From the bioluminescent nature of the kelp_forest and the default rule, we infer that the kelp_forest is symbiotic.</text>
<logic>symbiotic(kelp_forest)</logic>
</step>

Having established that the kelp_forest is symbiotic, we can now apply the ordinary rule that symbiotic entities are filter_feeders. This logical move upgrades the kelp_forest from a compliance condition to an activity condition, moving us closer to the organization goal.
<step n="8" type="derived" from="7,1">
<text>Using the rule that symbiotic implies filter_feeder, we derive that the kelp_forest is a filter_feeder.</text>
<logic>filter_feeder(kelp_forest)</logic>
</step>

With the kelp_forest now known to be a filter_feeder and still no abnormality preventing organization, the second default rule yields that the kelp_forest is colony_structured. This final derivation directly provides the positive version of the queried predicate.
<step n="9" type="derived" from="8,3">

<logic>colony_structured(kelp_forest)</logic>
</step>

We derived Kelp_forest is colony structured, so the conclusion Kelp_forest is not colony structured is False.

examples.context: Remy is persistent. Whenever Remy is not perceptive, Remy is evidence driven. If Remy is experienced, then Remy is not evidence driven. Whenever Remy is perceptive, Remy is not motivated. Either Remy is not healthy or Remy is motivated. Either Remy is not persistent or Remy is healthy.
examples.question: Based on the information given, can we conclude that Remy is not experienced?
examples.completion:
<think>
We are asked to decide whether Remy can be shown to be not experienced. The premises provide several conditionals and a few basic facts about Remy, so we will chain them together to reach the target conclusion.

The first rule tells us that a lack of perceptiveness guarantees that Remy is evidence driven. This will become useful later when we manage to establish that Remy is not perceptive.
<step n="1" type="premise">
<text>If Remy is not perceptive, then Remy is evidence driven.</text>
<logic>evidence_driven(remy) :- ~perceptive(remy)</logic>
</step>

The second rule states that if Remy were experienced, then Remy could not be evidence driven. This contrapositive relationship will allow us to infer non‑experience once we have evidence driven.
<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>If Remy is experienced, then Remy is not evidence driven.</text>
<logic>~evidence_driven(remy) :- experienced(remy)</logic>
</step>

The third rule links perceptiveness to a lack of motivation: any perceptive Remy would not be motivated. This will let us flip from motivation back to non‑perceptiveness via contrapositive reasoning.
<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>If Remy is perceptive, then Remy is not motivated.</text>
<logic>~motivated(remy) :- perceptive(remy)</logic>
</step>

The disjunction "not healthy or motivated" gives us two alternatives. Once we know whether Remy is healthy, we can resolve which side holds and possibly obtain motivation.
<step n="4" type="premise">
<text>Either Remy is not healthy or Remy is motivated.</text>
<logic>~healthy(remy) v motivated(remy)</logic>
</step>

The disjunction "not persistent or healthy" ties Remy's persistence to health. Since we already have a fact about persistence, this clause will let us derive health directly.
<step n="5" type="premise">
<text>Either Remy is not persistent or Remy is healthy.</text>
<logic>~persistent(remy) v healthy(remy)</logic>
</step>

We are told explicitly that Remy is persistent. This concrete fact will interact with the persistence‑health disjunction to produce a new piece of information.
<step n="6" type="premise">
<text>Remy is persistent.</text>
<logic>persistent(remy)</logic>
</step>

Because Remy is persistent, the clause "either not persistent or healthy" cannot be satisfied by the first option; the only way for the disjunction to hold is that Remy is healthy. Thus we conclude Remy is healthy.
<step n="7" type="derived" from="6,5">
<text>From Remy's persistence and the disjunction about persistence, we infer that Remy is healthy.</text>
<logic>healthy(remy)</logic>
</step>

Now that we have established Remy is healthy, the disjunction "either not healthy or motivated" is forced to be satisfied by the second option, since the first option is false. Consequently, Remy must be motivated.
<step n="8" type="derived" from="7,4">
<text>Using the fact that Remy is healthy together with the health‑or‑motivated disjunction, we infer that Remy is motivated.</text>
<logic>motivated(remy)</logic>
</step>

We have Remy motivated, and we also possess the rule "if perceptive then not motivated". By the contrapositive (modus tollens), if Remy were perceptive he would not be motivated, but he is motivated, so Remy cannot be perceptive. Hence we infer ~perceptive.
<step n="9" type="derived" from="8,3">
<text>Because Remy is motivated and the rule linking perceptiveness to lack of motivation, we conclude that Remy is not perceptive.</text>
<logic>~perceptive(remy)</logic>
</step>

Having derived that Remy is not perceptive, we can invoke the first rule which says non‑perceptive implies evidence driven. Applying modus ponens, we obtain that Remy is evidence driven.
<step n="10" type="derived" from="9,1">
<text>Given that Remy is not perceptive, the conditional that non‑perceptive implies evidence driven lets us assert that Remy is evidence driven.</text>
<logic>evidence_driven(remy)</logic>
</step>

We now know Remy is evidence driven. The second rule tells us that experienced would lead to not evidence driven. Using modus tollens again, the presence of evidence driven forces the denial of the antecedent, so Remy is not experienced.
<step n="11" type="derived" from="10,2">
<text>Since Remy is evidence driven and the rule that experienced implies not evidence driven, we deduce that Remy is not experienced.</text>
<logic>~experienced(remy)</logic>
</step>

Therefore Remy is not experienced, so the conclusion is True.
</think>
Answer: True

examples.question: Is the mountain tiger adaptive?

The fact that the river fox is vocal is recorded for completeness, though it does not directly affect the tiger's adaptive status. It satisfies the background information required by the problem statement.

The seasoned status of the mountain tiger is a core fact we will use with the seasoned‑to‑hardy rule to start the chain of inference.

<step n="6" type="derived" from="5,2">

Now that we have established the tiger is hardy, we can invoke the rule linking hardness to sharp senses. Modus ponens again yields that the tiger possesses sharp senses. This derived property will be crucial for the next step.

We derived Mountain_tiger is not adaptive, so the conclusion Mountain_tiger is adaptive is False.

examples.question: Is the lead mechanic steady?

examples.question: Based on the information given, is Marlowe calm?
examples.completion:
<think>
We are asked to determine whether Marlowe is calm given a collection of facts, rules, and disjunctions about the kitchen staff. The goal is to chain these pieces of information together to either prove or refute the calmness of Marlowe.

Here we have a rule that turns persistence into proficiency for Marlowe. It sets up a path from a property we can derive (persistence) to another property (proficiency) that will later help us obtain reliability.

This rule states that loyalty would prevent Marlowe from being active. It will allow us to use the contrapositive reasoning once we know Marlowe is active, letting us deduce that Marlowe cannot be loyal.

<text>The rule states that if Marlowe is loyal, then Marlowe is not active.</text>
<logic>~active(marlowe) :- loyal(marlowe)</logic>
</step>

The disjunction gives us two exclusive possibilities for Marlowe: either he is loyal or he is calm. Once we eliminate one alternative, the other must hold, which is the crux of the final step.
<step n="4" type="premise">
<text>The disjunction says that either Marlowe is loyal or Marlowe is calm.</text>
<logic>loyal(marlowe) v calm(marlowe)</logic>
</step>

This disjunction connects Marlowe's experience with his persistence. Since we already know he is experienced, the only way for the disjunction to be true is that Marlowe is persistent, which we will extract via disjunctive syllogism.
<step n="5" type="premise">
<text>The disjunction says that either Marlowe is not experienced or Marlowe is persistent.</text>
<logic>~experienced(marlowe) v persistent(marlowe)</logic>
</step>

This disjunction ties Marlowe's proficiency to his reliability. After we prove Marlowe is proficient, the disjunction forces the conclusion that he must be reliable, giving us the link needed for activity.
<step n="6" type="premise">
<text>The disjunction says that either Marlowe is not proficient or Marlowe is reliable.</text>
<logic>~proficient(marlowe) v reliable(marlowe)</logic>
</step>

The fact that Thea is fit is included for completeness of the scenario, though it does not directly participate in the chain leading to Marlowe's calmness.
<step n="7" type="premise">
<text>Fact: Thea is fit.</text>
<logic>fit(thea)</logic>
</step>

The fact that Nina is productive is also part of the overall knowledge base, but it does not affect the reasoning about Marlowe's calmness.
<step n="8" type="premise">
<text>Fact: Nina is productive.</text>
<logic>productive(nina)</logic>
</step>

Marlowe being experienced is a key fact that will interact with the disjunction about experience and persistence, allowing us to derive persistence early in the proof.
<step n="9" type="premise">
<text>Fact: Marlowe is experienced.</text>
<logic>experienced(marlowe)</logic>
</step>

We apply disjunctive syllogism: the disjunction says ‘not experienced or persistent’. Since we have the fact that Marlowe is experienced, the ‘not experienced’ part is false, leaving persistence as the only viable option. Thus we can conclude Marlowe is persistent.
<step n="10" type="derived" from="9,5">
<text>From the fact that Marlowe is experienced and the disjunction that either Marlowe is not experienced or Marlowe is persistent, we infer that Marlowe must be persistent.</text>
<logic>persistent(marlowe)</logic>
</step>

Having established persistence, we now use modus ponens with the rule that persistence leads to proficiency. The rule’s antecedent matches our derived fact, so we can infer that Marlowe is proficient.
<step n="11" type="derived" from="10,2">
<text>Since we now know Marlowe is persistent and we have the rule that persistence implies proficiency, we conclude that Marlowe is proficient.</text>
<logic>proficient(marlowe)</logic>
</step>

Next we combine the newly found proficiency with the disjunction ‘not proficient or reliable’. Because we now know Marlowe is proficient, the ‘not proficient’ clause cannot hold, so the reliable clause must be true. Therefore Marlowe is reliable.
<step n="12" type="derived" from="11,6">
<text>Using Marlowe's newly established proficiency together with the disjunction that either Marlowe is not proficient or Marlowe is reliable, we deduce that Marlowe is reliable.</text>
<logic>reliable(marlowe)</logic>
</step>

With reliability in hand, we invoke modus ponens again on the rule that reliability implies activity. The antecedent matches, so we deduce that Marlowe is active.
<step n="13" type="derived" from="12,1">

<logic>active(marlowe)</logic>
</step>

We now have Marlowe active, which contradicts the consequence of the loyalty rule (loyal ⇒ not active). Using modus tollens, if the consequent is false (Marlowe is not ‘not active’), the antecedent must also be false, giving us ‘not loyal’. Hence Marlowe cannot be loyal.
<step n="14" type="derived" from="13,3">

Finally we return to the original disjunction ‘loyal or calm’. Since we have just shown Marlowe is not loyal, the only remaining way for the disjunction to hold is for Marlowe to be calm. Thus we conclude Marlowe is calm.
<step n="15" type="derived" from="14,4">
<text>With the knowledge that Marlowe is not loyal and the earlier disjunction that Marlowe is either loyal or calm, we must conclude that Marlowe is calm.</text>

Therefore Marlowe is calm, so the conclusion is True.

examples.context: In the theater troupe, Presley is known to be relaxed. Whenever anyone is relaxed, they become open_minded. If someone is not precise, then that person is enthusiastic. Conversely, being precise guarantees that the person is not loyal. Consistency leads to scholarship: any consistent individual is scholarly. The opposite direction also holds for eloquence: if a person is eloquent, they cannot be scholarly. Additionally, at least one of the following must be true about Presley: either Presley is not enthusiastic or Presley is consistent. And also either Presley is not open_minded or Presley is loyal.
examples.question: Is Presley eloquent?
examples.completion:
<think>
We need to decide whether the claim that Presley is eloquent holds. The goal is to see if the premises and logical consequences force Presley to be eloquent or its negation.

This rule tells us how enthusiasm is linked to precision; it will become useful once we know anything about Presley's precision. It sets up a conditional that we can later apply via modus ponens.
<step n="1" type="premise">
<text>If Presley is not precise, then Presley is enthusiastic.</text>
<logic>enthusiastic(presley) :- ~precise(presley)</logic>
</step>

The open_minded rule connects the known fact of being relaxed to a new property. Since we already have relaxed(Presley) as a fact, this rule will let us derive open_minded(Presley).
<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>If Presley is relaxed, then Presley is open_minded.</text>
<logic>open_minded(presley) :- relaxed(presley)</logic>
</step>

This implication will be needed later to turn a consistency conclusion into a scholarly one. It creates a bridge from consistency to scholarship.
<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>If Presley is consistent, then Presley is scholarly.</text>
<logic>scholarly(presley) :- consistent(presley)</logic>
</step>

Here we have the contrapositive link between precision and loyalty. Once we learn about loyalty, we can work backwards to learn about precision using modus tollens.
<step n="4" type="premise">
<text>If Presley is precise, then Presley is not loyal.</text>
<logic>~loyal(presley) :- precise(presley)</logic>
</step>

This rule is crucial for the final step: it says that if Presley were eloquent, then Presley could not be scholarly. By establishing Presley is scholarly, we will be able to deny eloquence.
<step n="5" type="premise">
<text>If Presley is eloquent, then Presley is not scholarly.</text>
<logic>~scholarly(presley) :- eloquent(presley)</logic>
</step>

The disjunction gives us two alternatives about enthusiasm and consistency. When we later find that Presley is enthusiastic, this clause will force the other alternative—consistency—to be true.
<step n="6" type="premise">
<text>Either Presley is not enthusiastic or Presley is consistent.</text>
<logic>~enthusiastic(presley) v consistent(presley)</logic>
</step>

Similarly, this disjunction links open_mindedness with loyalty. After we derive open_minded(Presley), the clause will let us infer loyalty.
<step n="7" type="premise">
<text>Either Presley is not open_minded or Presley is loyal.</text>
<logic>~open_minded(presley) v loyal(presley)</logic>
</step>

The fact that Presley is relaxed is the starting point of the chain. It will trigger the rule in PREMISE_2 to give us open_mindedness.
<step n="8" type="premise">
<text>Presley is relaxed.</text>
<logic>relaxed(presley)</logic>
</step>

Because we know Presley is relaxed (PREMISE_8) and the rule that relaxed implies open_minded (PREMISE_2), we can directly infer open_minded(Presley) via modus ponens. This gives us a new property to work with in the next disjunction.
<step n="9" type="derived" from="8,2">
<text>From Presley being relaxed and the rule that relaxed implies open_minded, we infer that Presley is open_minded.</text>
<logic>open_minded(presley)</logic>
</step>

We have open_minded(Presley) from STEP_9, and the disjunction that either not open_minded or loyal (PREMISE_7). Since the first option is false, the only way for the disjunction to hold is that Presley is loyal. This is an application of disjunctive syllogism.
<step n="10" type="derived" from="9,7">
<text>Using the disjunction that either not open_minded or loyal, and knowing Presley is open_minded, we conclude Presley is loyal.</text>
<logic>loyal(presley)</logic>
</step>

Now that we know Presley is loyal (STEP_10), we look at the rule that precise implies not loyal (PREMISE_4). If Presley were precise, they would have to be not loyal, contradicting the loyalty we just derived. Therefore, Presley cannot be precise; this follows by modus tollens.
<step n="11" type="derived" from="10,4">
<text>Given that Presley is loyal and the rule that precise implies not loyal, we deduce that Presley cannot be precise.</text>
<logic>~precise(presley)</logic>
</step>

Having established that Presley is not precise (STEP_11), we can use the rule that not precise leads to enthusiasm (PREMISE_1). By modus ponens, we obtain enthusiastic(Presley).
<step n="12" type="derived" from="11,1">
<text>Since Presley is not precise and the rule that not precise implies enthusiastic, we infer that Presley is enthusiastic.</text>
<logic>enthusiastic(presley)</logic>
</step>

Enthusiastic(Presley) together with the disjunction that either not enthusiastic or consistent (PREMISE_6) forces the other disjunct to be true, because the first disjunct is false. Hence, Presley must be consistent, derived via disjunctive syllogism.
<step n="13" type="derived" from="12,6">
<text>From Presley being enthusiastic and the disjunction that either not enthusiastic or consistent, we determine that Presley must be consistent.</text>
<logic>consistent(presley)</logic>
</step>

With consistency now known (STEP_13), the rule that consistency yields scholarly (PREMISE_3) lets us infer scholarly(Presley) by modus ponens. This gives us the crucial fact needed for the final contradiction.
<step n="14" type="derived" from="13,3">
<text>Because Presley is consistent and consistency leads to scholarship, we conclude Presley is scholarly.</text>
<logic>scholarly(presley)</logic>
</step>

We have scholarly(Presley) (STEP_14) and the rule that eloquent would make someone not scholarly (PREMISE_5). If Presley were eloquent, they would have to be not scholarly, which clashes with the scholarly status we just proved. Therefore, Presley cannot be eloquent; this is an application of modus tollens.
<step n="15" type="derived" from="14,5">
<text>With Presley scholarly and the rule that eloquent would make someone not scholarly, we infer that Presley cannot be eloquent.</text>
<logic>~eloquent(presley)</logic>
</step>

We derived Presley is not eloquent, so the conclusion Presley is eloquent is False.
</think>
Answer: False

The third premise states that any radiation‑emitting probe cannot be energy‑absorbing. This contrapositive relationship will help us rule out radiation emission once we know the probe is energy‑absorbing.

The fourth premise offers a disjunction: the probe is either radiation emitting or magnetically aligned. This provides two possible ways to satisfy the unknown property, and we will eliminate one option using other information.

<logic>radiation_emitting(andromeda_probe) v magnetically_aligned(andromeda_probe)</logic>
</step>

The fifth premise gives us the concrete fact that the probe has an unstable orbit. This is the starting point for applying the first rule.
<step n="5" type="premise">
<text>The fact that the probe has an unstable orbit.</text>
<logic>unstable_orbit(andromeda_probe)</logic>
</step>

By applying the first rule to the fact of an unstable orbit, we deduce that the probe must be energy‑absorbing. This step converts the observed orbital condition into a property we can later contrast with the radiation‑emitting rule.
<step n="6" type="derived" from="5,1">
<text>From the unstable orbit and the first rule we infer that the probe is energy‑absorbing.</text>
<logic>energy_absorbing(andromeda_probe)</logic>
</step>

Since we now know the probe is energy‑absorbing, the third rule forces the conclusion that it cannot be radiation emitting; otherwise it would contradict the rule that radiation emission prevents energy absorption. Hence we infer the negation of radiation emission.
<step n="7" type="derived" from="6,3">
<text>Since the probe is energy‑absorbing, the third rule tells us that it cannot be radiation emitting, so we conclude it is not radiation emitting.</text>
<logic>~radiation_emitting(andromeda_probe)</logic>

Having established that the probe is not radiation emitting, the disjunction from premise four leaves magnetically aligned as the only viable alternative. Therefore, we conclude the probe must be magnetically aligned.
<step n="8" type="derived" from="7,4">
<text>Given that it is not radiation emitting and the disjunction, we deduce that the probe must be magnetically aligned.</text>
<logic>magnetically_aligned(andromeda_probe)</logic>
</step>

With magnetically aligned established, we invoke the second rule, which says that such a probe gains signal‑processing capability. This gives us the desired property of signal processing.
<step n="9" type="derived" from="8,2">
<text>With the probe being magnetically aligned, the second rule yields that it can perform signal processing.</text>
<logic>signal_processing(andromeda_probe)</logic>
</step>

Therefore Andromeda_probe is signal processing, so the conclusion is True.
</think>
Answer: True

The fourth default rule says that the tiger becomes monitored when it is studied and there is no abnormality stopping monitoring. We already know there are no such abnormalities, so establishing that the tiger is studied will be sufficient to make the tiger monitored.

The fifth default rule provides a path from the tiger’s steadiness to the caretaker role, again assuming no blocking abnormality. Because we have the fact that the tiger is steady and we are told there is no abnormality, we can infer that the tiger is indeed a caretaker.

The fact that the tiger is steady is a cornerstone for applying the caretaker default rule. Without this fact, we could not trigger the chain that leads to the tiger being studied and eventually monitored.

The fact that the elephant is dormant directly activates the rule that makes the elephant experienced. This derived experience will satisfy the right side of the disjunction, making the disjunction true regardless of the alertness status.

examples.question: Can we conclude that Ash is not courageous?
examples.completion:
<think>
We are asked to determine whether Ash lacks courage. The goal is to see if the premises and derived facts force Ash to be not courageous. To reach a conclusion we will follow the chain of rules provided in the context, paying special attention to the disjunction about Ash's diplomatic or anxious status.

The premise that anxious implies courageous is crucial because it lets us turn any proof of Ash being anxious into a proof of Ash being courageous. This conditional will be used later once we establish Ash's anxiety.
<step n="1" type="premise">
<text>The rule says that any anxious individual is also courageous.</text>
<logic>courageous(ash) :- anxious(ash)</logic>
</step>

The premise linking resourcefulness to scholarship lets us upgrade the factual resourceful status of Ash into a scholarly status. This conversion is needed for the next step where we compare scholarly and not scholarly conditions.
<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>The rule says that any resourceful individual is also scholarly.</text>
<logic>scholarly(ash) :- resourceful(ash)</logic>
</step>

The premise that diplomatic leads to not scholarly creates a tension with the scholarly fact we will obtain. If we can show Ash is diplomatic, we would get a contradiction with Ash being scholarly, which will be instrumental in eliminating the diplomatic possibility.
<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>The rule says that any diplomatic individual is not scholarly.</text>
<logic>~scholarly(ash) :- diplomatic(ash)</logic>
</step>

The disjunction gives us a logical fork: either Ash is diplomatic or Ash is anxious. This will allow us to perform a disjunctive syllogism once we rule out one of the options, thereby pinpointing the other.
<step n="4" type="premise">
<text>We are told that either Ash is diplomatic or Ash is anxious.</text>
<logic>diplomatic(ash) v anxious(ash)</logic>
</step>

Nina being patient is mentioned in the context, but it does not directly affect the chain about Ash's courage. It is recorded here for completeness, as the context must include all factual statements.
<step n="5" type="premise">
<text>It is a fact that Nina is patient.</text>
<logic>patient(nina)</logic>
</step>

Ash being resourceful is the factual anchor we need to activate the rule that turns resourcefulness into scholarship. Without this fact we could not derive Ash's scholarly status.
<step n="6" type="premise">
<text>It is a fact that Ash is resourceful.</text>
<logic>resourceful(ash)</logic>
</step>

Since we know Ash is resourceful (Premise 6) and the rule says every resourceful entity is scholarly (Premise 2), we can confidently infer that Ash is scholarly. This new fact will clash with the potential of Ash being diplomatic in the next step.
<step n="7" type="derived" from="6,2">
<text>From Ash being resourceful and the rule linking resourcefulness to scholarship, we infer that Ash is scholarly.</text>
<logic>scholarly(ash)</logic>
</step>

Now we have Ash scholarly, but Premise 3 tells us that if Ash were diplomatic, Ash would not be scholarly. Because we have scholarly Ash, the only way to avoid a contradiction is to conclude that Ash cannot be diplomatic; thus we derive not diplomatic Ash.
<step n="8" type="derived" from="7,3">
<text>Because Ash is scholarly and the rule that diplomatic implies not scholarly, we deduce that Ash cannot be diplomatic.</text>
<logic>~diplomatic(ash)</logic>
</step>

With the earlier disjunction (Premise 4) stating Ash is either diplomatic or anxious, and having just shown Ash is not diplomatic (Step 8), the only remaining possibility is that Ash must be anxious. This is a direct application of disjunctive syllogism.
<step n="9" type="derived" from="8,4">
<text>Since Ash is not diplomatic and we know Ash is either diplomatic or anxious, the only remaining option is that Ash is anxious.</text>
<logic>anxious(ash)</logic>
</step>

Having established that Ash is anxious (Step 9) and using the rule from Premise 1 that anxiety guarantees courage, we infer that Ash is courageous. This is a straightforward modus ponens step.
<step n="10" type="derived" from="9,1">
<text>Given that Ash is anxious and the rule that anxious implies courageous, we conclude that Ash is courageous.</text>
<logic>courageous(ash)</logic>
</step>

We derived Ash is courageous, so the conclusion Ash is not courageous is False.
</think>
Answer: False

examples.context: In the dojo, master_elliot is agile. fighter_henry, student_june, and competitor_oakley are also agile. fighter_henry, student_june, and competitor_oakley each train in a disciplined manner. sensei_hayden is honorable, and trainer_nell is aggressive. The training code states that any agile practitioner is resolute, so if someone is agile they are resolute. Moreover, any disciplined practitioner is centered, meaning that if someone is disciplined they are centered. Additionally, being precise guarantees disciplined training: whenever someone is precise, they become disciplined. A student is deemed adept if they are not insightful. Conversely, if a practitioner is insightful, they cannot be centered. The dojo also knows that either a practitioner is not resolute or they are precise; similarly, either sensei_hayden is not calm or they are vigilant, and either trainer_nell is not reliable or they are strategic.
examples.question: Is master_elliot adept?
examples.completion:
<think>
We are asked to decide whether master_elliot possesses the property adept. To answer this we must chain together the given facts and conditional statements until we can derive adept(master_elliot).

This premise gives us the conditional that agility guarantees resoluteness. It will be needed later because we already know master_elliot is agile, so it can produce a resolute fact for him. Establishing resoluteness is also important for the disjunction that involves resolute.

</step>

Here we have the link from disciplined behavior to being centered. Once we manage to show master_elliot is disciplined, this rule will allow us to obtain a centered status, which later interacts with the rule about insightful people not being centered.
<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>The rule states that any disciplined person is centered, so for master_elliot, disciplined implies centered.</text>
<logic>centered(master_elliot) :- disciplined(master_elliot)</logic>
</step>

This rule tells us that precision forces discipline. It is the bridge that will let us move from a precise status (which we will get from the disjunction) to a disciplined status, and subsequently to centered.
<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>The rule declares that being precise leads to being disciplined; thus if master_elliot is precise, he becomes disciplined.</text>
<logic>disciplined(master_elliot) :- precise(master_elliot)</logic>
</step>

The crucial rule for our goal: if someone is not insightful, they become adept. Our final aim is to prove not insightful for master_elliot, because then we can immediately infer adeptness.

<text>The rule defines that a person is adept when they are not insightful; so not insightful for master_elliot yields adept.</text>
<logic>adept(master_elliot) :- ~insightful(master_elliot)</logic>
</step>

This conditional says that being insightful prevents one from being centered. It will be used in reverse: if we can show master_elliot is centered, then insightfulness must be false, giving us the needed not insightful fact.
<step n="5" type="premise">
<text>The rule asserts that if someone is insightful then they are not centered; this is the contrapositive for master_elliot.</text>
<logic>~centered(master_elliot) :- insightful(master_elliot)</logic>
</step>

The disjunction provides two alternatives: either master_elliot is not resolute or he is precise. Since we will later prove resolute, the only way for the disjunction to hold is that master_elliot is precise, which is exactly what we need to start the chain toward discipline.
<step n="6" type="premise">

A simple fact that fighter_henry is agile; it is listed for completeness but does not directly affect the derivation for master_elliot.

This fact tells us that master_elliot is agile, the starting point for applying the agile‑to‑resolute rule in the next step.

examples.context: Sprinter Luca is strong. Whenever Luca lacks sportsmanship, he is fast. If Luca is strategic, then he is not fast. Being sportsmanship guarantees that Luca is not diligent. Luca is either not confident or he is diligent. Either Luca lacks strength or he is confident.
examples.question: Is it true that Sprinter Luca is not strategic?
examples.completion:
<think>
We are given several properties of Sprinter Luca together with conditional statements that connect those properties. Our objective is to decide whether Luca can be described as strategic, i.e., whether the negated statement ~strategic(Luca) follows from the information provided.

The rule linking a lack of sportsmanship to speed will later allow us to turn a negative sportsmanship conclusion into a positive speed claim. It therefore sets up a forward‑chaining path from ~sportsmanship to fast.
<step n="1" type="premise">
<text>If Luca does not have sportsmanship, then he is fast.</text>
<logic>fast(sprinter_luca) :- ~sportsmanship(sprinter_luca)</logic>
</step>

The implication that strategic behavior forces a lack of speed is essential for later using the contrapositive. If we ever establish that Luca is fast, this rule will let us infer that he cannot be strategic.
<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>If Luca is strategic, then he is not fast.</text>
<logic>~fast(sprinter_luca) :- strategic(sprinter_luca)</logic>
</step>

The statement that sportsmanship entails not being diligent provides a way to connect the diligent property back to sportsmanship. When we learn Luca is diligent, this rule will let us reverse‑engineer the absence of sportsmanship.
<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>If Luca has sportsmanship, then he is not diligent.</text>
<logic>~diligent(sprinter_luca) :- sportsmanship(sprinter_luca)</logic>
</step>

This disjunction gives us an either/or relationship between confidence and diligence. Once we know one side, we can immediately settle the other, which is crucial for the chain of deductions.
<step n="4" type="premise">
<text>Luca is either not confident or he is diligent.</text>
<logic>~confident(sprinter_luca) v diligent(sprinter_luca)</logic>
</step>

The second disjunction offers a link between strength and confidence. It will become useful once we have a fact about strength, allowing us to derive confidence directly.
<step n="5" type="premise">
<text>Either Luca is not strong or he is confident.</text>
<logic>~strong(sprinter_luca) v confident(sprinter_luca)</logic>
</step>

The fact that Luca is strong serves as the starting point for applying the disjunctive syllogism with the previous disjunction. It gives us a concrete truth from which we can eliminate one alternative.
<step n="6" type="premise">
<text>Luca is strong.</text>
<logic>strong(sprinter_luca)</logic>
</step>

We have the fact that Luca is strong. The disjunction states that either Luca is not strong or he is confident. Since the first alternative is false (Luca is strong), the only way for the disjunction to hold is that the second alternative, confidence, must be true. Hence we derive that Luca is confident.
<step n="7" type="derived" from="6,5">
<text>From Luca being strong and the statement that either he is not strong or he is confident, we conclude that Luca is confident.</text>
<logic>confident(sprinter_luca)</logic>
</step>

Now that we know Luca is confident, we examine the other disjunction that says either he is not confident or he is diligent. The first part is false because we have established confidence, so the second part must be true. Therefore Luca is diligent.
<step n="8" type="derived" from="7,4">
<text>Given that Luca is confident and the alternative that either he is not confident or he is diligent, we infer that Luca is diligent.</text>
<logic>diligent(sprinter_luca)</logic>
</step>

The rule tells us that if someone has sportsmanship then they are not diligent. We have just shown Luca is diligent, which contradicts the consequent of that rule. By applying Modus Tollens, we infer that the antecedent—sportsmanship—cannot hold for Luca; thus Luca lacks sportsmanship.
<step n="9" type="derived" from="8,3">
<text>Since Luca is diligent and the rule that sportsmanship implies not diligence, we deduce that Luca does not have sportsmanship.</text>
<logic>~sportsmanship(sprinter_luca)</logic>
</step>

Having concluded that Luca lacks sportsmanship, we can invoke the earlier rule that a lack of sportsmanship guarantees fastness. By directly applying this implication, we obtain that Luca is fast.
<step n="10" type="derived" from="9,1">
<text>With Luca lacking sportsmanship and the rule that lacking sportsmanship leads to being fast, we obtain that Luca is fast.</text>
<logic>fast(sprinter_luca)</logic>
</step>

The rule connecting strategic behavior to the negation of fastness says that if Luca were strategic, he would not be fast. Since we have proven Luca is fast, the contrapositive of that rule lets us infer that Luca cannot be strategic, i.e., he is not strategic.
<step n="11" type="derived" from="10,2">
<text>Because Luca is fast and the rule that being strategic would make him not fast, we infer that Luca cannot be strategic.</text>
<logic>~strategic(sprinter_luca)</logic>
</step>

Therefore Sprinter_luca is not strategic, so the conclusion is True.
</think>
Answer: True

examples.context: Player_one is data_focused. Whenever a player is an explorer, that player is currently_playing. Whenever a player is goal_oriented, that player is a high_score_seeker. Whenever a player is a high_score_seeker, that player is an explorer. Any data_focused player is logical. If a player is reliable, then that player is not currently_playing. If a player is recharged, then that player is not logical. Either player_one is recharged or player_one is goal_oriented.
examples.question: Can we conclude that player_one is not reliable?
examples.completion:
<think>
We are asked to determine whether player_one can be shown to be not reliable. The query requires us to use the given facts, conditional rules, and the disjunction to build a chain of reasoning that ends with the negation of reliability.

The first rule tells us that explorer status guarantees current playing status. This will later let us turn an explorer conclusion into an active one.
<step n="1" type="premise">
<text>If player_one is an explorer, then they are currently playing.</text>
<logic>currently_playing(player_one) :- explorer(player_one)</logic>
</step>

The second rule links goal‑oriented behavior to being a high‑score seeker. It will be useful once we establish that player_one is goal‑oriented.
<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>If player_one is goal‑oriented, then they are a high‑score seeker.</text>
<logic>high_score_seeker(player_one) :- goal_oriented(player_one)</logic>
</step>

The third rule provides the converse: being a high‑score seeker ensures explorer status. This creates a cycle that will let us move from competitiveness to curiosity.
<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>If player_one is a high‑score seeker, then they are an explorer.</text>
<logic>explorer(player_one) :- high_score_seeker(player_one)</logic>
</step>

The fourth rule says any data‑focused player is logical. Since we have a fact that player_one is data‑focused, this rule will let us derive logicalness immediately.
<step n="4" type="premise">
<text>If player_one is data‑focused, then they are logical.</text>
<logic>logical(player_one) :- data_focused(player_one)</logic>
</step>

The fifth rule states that reliability would prevent a player from being currently playing. This contrapositive relationship will be the final step to obtain the desired negation.
<step n="5" type="premise">
<text>If player_one is reliable, then they are not currently playing.</text>
<logic>~currently_playing(player_one) :- reliable(player_one)</logic>
</step>

The sixth rule informs us that being recharged implies not logical. This will enable a Modus Tollens move once we have logicalness.
<step n="6" type="premise">
<text>If player_one is recharged, then they are not logical.</text>
<logic>~logical(player_one) :- recharged(player_one)</logic>
</step>

The disjunction asserts that player_one is either recharged or goal‑oriented. When we later rule out one alternative, the other must hold.
<step n="7" type="premise">
<text>Player_one is either recharged or goal‑oriented.</text>
<logic>recharged(player_one) v goal_oriented(player_one)</logic>
</step>

We are explicitly told that player_one is data‑focused, which is the starting factual anchor for the chain of deductions.
<step n="8" type="premise">
<text>Player_one is data‑focused.</text>
<logic>data_focused(player_one)</logic>
</step>

From the fact that player_one is data‑focused and the rule that data‑focused implies logical, we directly infer that player_one is logical. This gives us the positive property needed for the next Modus Tollens step.
<step n="9" type="derived" from="8,4">
<text>Since player_one is data‑focused and any data‑focused player is logical, we infer that player_one is logical.</text>
<logic>logical(player_one)</logic>
</step>

The rule "if recharged then not logical" tells us that a recharged player cannot be logical. Since we have just shown player_one is logical, the only way to avoid contradiction is to conclude that player_one is not recharged. This is a classic Modus Tollens application.
<step n="10" type="derived" from="9,6">
<text>Because a recharged player would not be logical, but we have established that player_one is logical, we conclude that player_one is not recharged.</text>
<logic>~recharged(player_one)</logic>
</step>

Having established that player_one is not recharged, we turn to the disjunction "recharged or goal‑oriented". Because the recharged alternative is false, the disjunction forces the remaining option to be true, so player_one must be goal‑oriented.
<step n="11" type="derived" from="10,7">
<text>Given that player_one is not recharged and that player_one must be either recharged or goal‑oriented, we deduce that player_one is goal‑oriented.</text>
<logic>goal_oriented(player_one)</logic>
</step>

Now that we know player_one is goal‑oriented, we apply the second rule, which says goal‑oriented players are high‑score seekers. Hence player_one becomes a high‑score seeker.
<step n="12" type="derived" from="11,2">
<text>From player_one being goal‑oriented and the rule that goal‑oriented players are high‑score seekers, we derive that player_one is a high‑score seeker.</text>
<logic>high_score_seeker(player_one)</logic>
</step>

With player_one identified as a high‑score seeker, we can use the third rule that any high‑score seeker is an explorer. This yields that player_one is an explorer.
<step n="13" type="derived" from="12,3">
<text>Since player_one is a high‑score seeker and any high‑score seeker is an explorer, we infer that player_one is an explorer.</text>
<logic>explorer(player_one)</logic>
</step>

Since player_one is an explorer, the first rule lets us infer that they are currently playing. This gives us the active status needed for the final Modus Tollens.
<step n="14" type="derived" from="13,1">
<text>Being an explorer implies that player_one is currently playing, so we conclude player_one is currently playing.</text>
<logic>currently_playing(player_one)</logic>
</step>

The fifth rule tells us that a reliable player would not be currently playing. We have just shown player_one is currently playing, so by Modus Tollens we must conclude that player_one cannot be reliable; therefore they are not reliable.
<step n="15" type="derived" from="14,5">
<text>If player_one were reliable they would not be currently playing, yet we have shown they are currently playing; therefore player_one is not reliable.</text>
<logic>~reliable(player_one)</logic>
</step>

Therefore Player_one is not reliable, so the conclusion is True.
</think>
Answer: True

examples.question: Is Hope alert?
examples.completion:
<think>
We are asked to decide whether Hope possesses the property alert given the collection of facts, rules, and alternatives. The goal is to see if alert(hope) can be supported or if its negation follows from the information provided.

The rule linking skillfulness to sharpness is important because it creates a bridge from a potential skillful status of Hope to a concrete attribute, sharp. Knowing whether Hope is sharp will later allow us to test the alert‑related rule that forbids sharpness.

The second rule tells us that alertness would immediately make Hope not sharp. This contrapositive relationship will be useful once we have established that Hope is indeed sharp, allowing us to derive the negation of alert.

<logic>~sharp(hope) :- alert(hope)</logic>

The disjunction about Hope’s relaxed or skillful condition offers a way to obtain skillfulness if we can eliminate the possibility that Hope is not relaxed. It sets up a logical fork that we will resolve later.
<step n="3" type="premise">

The alternative concerning Hope’s rested or relaxed state gives us a path from the known fact that Hope is rested to the conclusion that Hope must be relaxed. This will be the first step in the chain toward skillfulness.

Stating that Riley is clear headed provides background for other rules, but it does not directly affect Hope’s alert status. It is included to satisfy the requirement that every premise be mentioned.

Scout being talented is another background fact that supports the rule linking talent to vigor, yet it does not influence the alert question for Hope. It remains part of the complete knowledge base.

<logic>talented(scout)</logic>
</step>

Hope being rested is a concrete fact that we can combine with the disjunction in PREMISE_4. This combination will let us infer a new property of Hope, namely that she is relaxed.
<step n="7" type="premise">
<text>Hope is rested.</text>

</step>

We have the fact that Hope is rested and the disjunction that either Hope is not rested or Hope is relaxed. Since the first option is false (Hope is rested), the disjunction forces the second option to be true, so Hope must be relaxed. This is a classic application of disjunctive syllogism.
<step n="8" type="derived" from="7,4">
<text>From Hope being rested and the disjunction that Hope is not rested or Hope is relaxed, we infer that Hope is relaxed.</text>
<logic>relaxed(hope)</logic>

Now that we know Hope is relaxed, we turn to the other disjunction stating either Hope is not relaxed or Hope is skillful. The relaxed condition makes the first alternative false, leaving Skillful as the only viable choice. Hence Hope is skillful, again via disjunctive syllogism.
<step n="9" type="derived" from="8,3">

Having established that Hope is skillful, we can apply the rule from PREMISE_1 (skillful implies sharp). This direct implication yields that Hope is sharp, using modus ponens on the derived skillful fact.

<logic>sharp(hope)</logic>

With Hope sharp in hand, we invoke the rule from PREMISE_2, which says alertness would make Hope not sharp. Since Hope is already sharp, the only way to keep the rule consistent is to conclude that Hope cannot be alert; thus we derive the negation of alert. This is a modus tollens step.
<step n="11" type="derived" from="10,2">

We derived Hope is not alert, so the conclusion Hope is alert is False.
