<logic>ordered(wizard_azul) :- initiating(wizard_azul)</logic>
</step>

The fifth premise asserts that steadiness prevents strategic behavior. This negative relationship will help us eliminate the possibility that wizard azul is steady once we know he is strategic.
<step n="5" type="premise">
<text>If someone is steady, then they are not strategic.</text>
<logic>~strategic(wizard_azul) :- steady(wizard_azul)</logic>
</step>

The sixth premise gives a disjunction: wizard azul is either steady or initiating. This binary choice will allow us to infer which of the two holds once we rule one out.
<step n="6" type="premise">
<text>Wizard azul is either steady or initiating.</text>
<logic>steady(wizard_azul) v initiating(wizard_azul)</logic>
</step>

The seventh premise records that warrior brann is nervous, a fact that does not directly affect wizard azul but completes the set of given facts for completeness.
<step n="7" type="premise">
<text>Warrior brann is nervous.</text>
<logic>nervous(warrior_brann)</logic>
</step>

The eighth premise records wizard azul's compassion, the starting point for the forward chain of implications we will follow.
<step n="8" type="premise">
<text>Wizard azul is compassionate.</text>
<logic>compassionate(wizard_azul)</logic>
</step>

Because wizard azul is compassionate (Premise 8) and compassion implies charisma (Premise 1), we can conclude wizard azul is charismatic. This is a straightforward application of the conditional rule.
<step n="9" type="derived" from="8,1">
<text>From wizard azul being compassionate and the rule that compassion implies charisma, we infer that wizard azul is charismatic.</text>
<logic>charismatic(wizard_azul)</logic>
</step>

Having established wizard azul's charisma, we apply Premise 2, which tells us that charisma leads to resoluteness, so wizard azul becomes resolute. This moves us one step further along the implication chain.
<step n="10" type="derived" from="9,2">
<text>Since wizard azul is charismatic and charisma leads to resoluteness, wizard azul is resolute.</text>
<logic>resolute(wizard_azul)</logic>
</step>

Now that wizard azul is resolute, Premise 3 allows us to infer that he is strategic. The rule directly converts resoluteness into strategic behavior, giving us a new property to work with.
<step n="11" type="derived" from="10,3">
<text>Because wizard azul is resolute and resoluteness yields strategic behavior, wizard azul becomes strategic.</text>
<logic>strategic(wizard_azul)</logic>
</step>

We notice that strategic behavior conflicts with steadiness according to Premise 5 (steady ⇒ not strategic). Since wizard azul is strategic, the only way to avoid a contradiction is to conclude that he cannot be steady, i.e., ~steady holds.
<step n="12" type="derived" from="11,5">
<text>Given wizard azul is strategic and the rule that steadiness would forbid strategic behavior, we conclude wizard azul cannot be steady.</text>
<logic>~steady(wizard_azul)</logic>
</step>

The disjunction in Premise 6 tells us wizard azul must be either steady or initiating. Having just ruled out steadiness (Step 12), the remaining alternative is that wizard azul is initiating.
<step n="13" type="derived" from="12,6">
<text>With the knowledge that wizard azul is not steady and that he must be either steady or initiating, we deduce that wizard azul is initiating.</text>
<logic>initiating(wizard_azul)</logic>
</step>

Finally, Premise 4 says that initiating guarantees ordered behavior. Because we have just deduced wizard azul is initiating, we can conclude he is ordered.
<step n="14" type="derived" from="13,4">
<text>Because wizard azul is initiating and initiation guarantees ordered behavior, wizard azul is ordered.</text>
<logic>ordered(wizard_azul)</logic>
</step>

We derived Wizard_azul is ordered, so the conclusion Wizard_azul is not ordered is False.
</think>
Answer: False

examples.context: The clay vase is intricate, and it has been cooled after firing. The glazed mug displays a smooth_glaze, indicating a refined surface. The clay vase is also well_finished, showing that its form is carefully completed. The kiln itself is well_finished, meaning its construction follows precise standards. Whenever something is intricate, it becomes high_fired. Whenever something is high_fired, it becomes well_balanced. Whenever something is crisp_edges, it becomes energy_efficient. Whenever something is well_finished, it becomes technique_refined. Whenever something is cooled, it becomes crisp_edges. Whenever something is high_output, it causes the kiln not to be technique_refined. Either the kiln produces a high_output or it is cooled. Either the kiln is not energy_efficient or it is intricate. Either the glazed mug is not bright_hue or it is tradition_respecting. If the glazed mug is smooth_glaze, then it is bright_hue. If the glazed mug is tradition_respecting, then it is handcrafted.
examples.question: Is the kiln well_balanced?
examples.completion:
<think>
Our goal is to determine whether the kiln possesses the property well_balanced. To reach this conclusion we will trace a chain of implications starting from the facts about the kiln and using the conditional statements provided in the context.

<step n="1" type="premise">
<text>If the kiln is intricate, then it is high_fired.</text>
<logic>high_fired(kiln) :- intricate(kiln)</logic>
</step>

<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>If the kiln is high_fired, then it is well_balanced.</text>
<logic>well_balanced(kiln) :- high_fired(kiln)</logic>
</step>

<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>If the kiln has crisp_edges, then it is energy_efficient.</text>
<logic>energy_efficient(kiln) :- crisp_edges(kiln)</logic>
</step>

<step n="4" type="premise">
<text>If the kiln is well_finished, then it is technique_refined.</text>
<logic>technique_refined(kiln) :- well_finished(kiln)</logic>
</step>

<step n="5" type="premise">
<text>If the kiln is cooled, then it has crisp_edges.</text>
<logic>crisp_edges(kiln) :- cooled(kiln)</logic>
</step>

<step n="6" type="premise">
<text>If the kiln is high_output, then it is not technique_refined.</text>
<logic>~technique_refined(kiln) :- high_output(kiln)</logic>
</step>

<step n="7" type="premise">
<text>Either the kiln is high_output or it is cooled.</text>
<logic>high_output(kiln) v cooled(kiln)</logic>
</step>

<step n="8" type="premise">
<text>Either the kiln is not energy_efficient or it is intricate.</text>
<logic>~energy_efficient(kiln) v intricate(kiln)</logic>
</step>

<step n="9" type="premise">
<text>The clay vase is intricate.</text>
<logic>intricate(clay_vase)</logic>
</step>

<step n="10" type="premise">
<text>The glazed mug has a smooth_glaze.</text>
<logic>smooth_glaze(glazed_mug)</logic>
</step>

<step n="11" type="premise">
<text>The clay vase is cooled.</text>
<logic>cooled(clay_vase)</logic>
</step>

<step n="12" type="premise">
<text>The clay vase is well_finished.</text>
<logic>well_finished(clay_vase)</logic>
</step>

<step n="13" type="premise">
<text>The kiln is well_finished.</text>
<logic>well_finished(kiln)</logic>
</step>

We know from the facts that the kiln is well_finished. The rule that any well_finished object is technique_refined lets us lift this property to the kiln. This step establishes a crucial quality about the kiln that will later be used to rule out contradictory possibilities.
<step n="14" type="derived" from="13,4">
<text>From the kiln being well_finished, we infer that it is technique_refined.</text>
<logic>technique_refined(kiln)</logic>
</step>

The rule linking high_output to the negation of technique_refined tells us that if the kiln were high_output, it could not be technique_refined. Since we have already derived that the kiln is technique_refined, the only way to avoid a contradiction is to assert that the kiln is not high_output. This negative information will be essential for the upcoming disjunctive reasoning.
<step n="15" type="derived" from="14,6">
<text>Because the kiln is technique_refined and high_output would make it not technique_refined, we conclude the kiln is not high_output.</text>
<logic>~high_output(kiln)</logic>
</step>

The context includes a disjunction stating that the kiln is either high_output or cooled. Having just concluded that the kiln is not high_output, the disjunction forces the alternative to be true, namely that the kiln is cooled. This gives us a new concrete property of the kiln to work with.
<step n="16" type="derived" from="15,7">
<text>Given the kiln is not high_output and that it must be either high_output or cooled, we deduce that the kiln is cooled.</text>
<logic>cooled(kiln)</logic>
</step>

One of the conditional rules says that any cooled object acquires crisp_edges. Since we have established that the kiln is cooled, we can immediately infer that the kiln possesses crisp_edges. This property will allow us to move further along the chain toward energy efficiency.
<step n="17" type="derived" from="16,5">
<text>Since the kiln is cooled, we can infer that it has crisp_edges.</text>
<logic>crisp_edges(kiln)</logic>
</step>

Another rule connects crisp_edges with energy_efficiency, indicating that any object with crisp_edges is energy_efficient. Applying this to the kiln, we conclude that the kiln is energy_efficient. This result will be used in the next disjunctive step to uncover the kiln's intricacy.
<step n="18" type="derived" from="17,3">
<text>From the kiln having crisp_edges, we derive that it is energy_efficient.</text>
<logic>energy_efficient(kiln)</logic>
</step>

The disjunction "either the kiln is not energy_efficient or it is intricate" together with our knowledge that the kiln is energy_efficient eliminates the first option, leaving only the second: the kiln must be intricate. This gives us the missing antecedent needed for the high_fired rule.
<step n="19" type="derived" from="18,8">
<text>Because the kiln is energy_efficient, the disjunction that it is not energy_efficient or intricate forces the kiln to be intricate.</text>
<logic>intricate(kiln)</logic>
</step>

Now that we know the kiln is intricate, we can employ the rule that any intricate object becomes high_fired. Consequently, the kiln is high_fired. This directly satisfies the premise of the rule that leads to the well_balanced property.
<step n="20" type="derived" from="19,1">
<text>With the kiln being intricate, we obtain that it is high_fired.</text>
<logic>high_fired(kiln)</logic>
</step>

Finally, the rule stating that any high_fired object is well_balanced allows us to conclude that the kiln is indeed well_balanced. This completes the logical chain from the initial facts to the target property.
<step n="21" type="derived" from="20,2">
<text>Finally, the kiln being high_fired leads to it being well_balanced.</text>
<logic>well_balanced(kiln)</logic>
</step>

Therefore Kiln is well balanced, so the conclusion is True.
</think>
Answer: True

examples.context: On the board, the white pawn is well positioned, the black knight is well positioned, and the gray bishop is well positioned. Whenever the black knight is well positioned, it is ready to attack. If the black knight is ready to attack, then it follows opening principles. Should the black knight follow opening principles, it makes logical moves. Whenever the black knight makes logical moves, it has studied variations. Moreover, if the black knight is adaptable, then it does not have studied variations.
examples.question: Is the black knight adaptable?
examples.completion:
<think>
We are asked to decide whether the black knight possesses the quality of being adaptable. To answer, we must examine the chain of conditions that connect the black knight's known attributes to the possibility of adaptability.

The first rule links a well‑positioned piece to the ability to launch an attack. Recognizing this connection is crucial because the black knight is explicitly described as well positioned, giving us a foothold for further inference.
<step n="1" type="premise">
<text>If the black knight is well positioned, then it is ready to attack.</text>
<logic>ready_to_attack(black_knight) :- well_positioned(black_knight)</logic>
</step>

The second rule tells us that a piece ready to attack will adhere to opening principles. This step is needed to move from the attack readiness we will obtain toward more strategic properties of the black knight.
<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>If the black knight is ready to attack, then it follows opening principles.</text>
<logic>follows_opening_principles(black_knight) :- ready_to_attack(black_knight)</logic>
</step>

The third rule states that following opening principles leads to making logical moves. This bridges the strategic principle to the concrete behavior of the piece, which will later be used to derive its study of variations.
<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>If the black knight follows opening principles, then it makes logical moves.</text>
<logic>makes_logical_moves(black_knight) :- follows_opening_principles(black_knight)</logic>
</step>

The fourth rule connects logical moves with having studied variations. Establishing this link allows us to eventually contrast the black knight's studied status with the condition that would arise if it were adaptable.
<step n="4" type="premise">
<text>If the black knight makes logical moves, then it has studied variations.</text>
<logic>studied_variations(black_knight) :- makes_logical_moves(black_knight)</logic>
</step>

The fifth rule provides a negative relationship: if a piece is adaptable, then it cannot have studied variations. This rule is the key to disproving adaptability once we have shown the black knight does have studied variations.
<step n="5" type="premise">
<text>If the black knight is adaptable, then it does not have studied variations.</text>
<logic>~studied_variations(black_knight) :- adaptable(black_knight)</logic>
</step>

The fact that the white pawn is well positioned supplies background information but does not directly affect the black knight's chain; it simply satisfies the overall factual completeness of the scenario.
<step n="6" type="premise">
<text>The white pawn is well positioned.</text>
<logic>well_positioned(white_pawn)</logic>
</step>

Similarly, the gray bishop being well positioned adds another factual detail required by the problem statement, ensuring all listed facts are present in the context.
<step n="7" type="premise">
<text>The gray bishop is well positioned.</text>
<logic>well_positioned(gray_bishop)</logic>
</step>

The explicit statement that the black knight is well positioned activates the first rule and initiates the cascade of implications we will follow to evaluate adaptability.
<step n="8" type="premise">
<text>The black knight is well positioned.</text>
<logic>well_positioned(black_knight)</logic>
</step>

Using the fact that the black knight is well positioned together with the rule that a well‑positioned piece is ready to attack, we can directly infer that the black knight is ready to attack. This is a straightforward application of the conditional relationship.
<step n="9" type="derived" from="8,1">
<text>Because the black knight is well positioned, we can infer that it is ready to attack.</text>
<logic>ready_to_attack(black_knight)</logic>
</step>

Having established the black knight's readiness to attack, we apply the second rule, which tells us that any piece ready to attack follows opening principles. Thus we deduce that the black knight follows opening principles.
<step n="10" type="derived" from="9,2">
<text>From the black knight being ready to attack, we infer that it follows opening principles.</text>
<logic>follows_opening_principles(black_knight)</logic>
</step>

From the black knight following opening principles, the third rule allows us to infer that it makes logical moves. This step continues the chain of logical consequences stemming from the initial fact.
<step n="11" type="derived" from="10,3">
<text>Given that the black knight follows opening principles, we conclude it makes logical moves.</text>
<logic>makes_logical_moves(black_knight)</logic>
</step>

Given that the black knight makes logical moves, the fourth rule leads us to conclude that it has studied variations. At this point we have derived a positive property that will clash with the condition for adaptability.
<step n="12" type="derived" from="11,4">
<text>Since the black knight makes logical moves, we deduce it has studied variations.</text>
<logic>studied_variations(black_knight)</logic>
</step>

Now we use the fifth rule in a contrapositive fashion: if the black knight were adaptable, it would lack studied variations, but we have just shown it does have studied variations. Therefore, the assumption of adaptability leads to a contradiction, and we must reject it, concluding the black knight is not adaptable.
<step n="13" type="derived" from="12,5">
<text>If the black knight were adaptable it would not have studied variations, but we have shown it does have studied variations, so the black knight cannot be adaptable.</text>
<logic>~adaptable(black_knight)</logic>
</step>

We derived Black_knight is not adaptable, so the conclusion Black_knight is adaptable is False.
</think>
Answer: False

examples.context: Mark is capable. Brook, Nina, and Wren are all punctual. Nina lacks confidence, and Wren also lacks confidence. Whenever Brook is studious, Brook becomes efficient. For Mark, being proactive makes Mark friendly, and Mark being capable makes Mark resourceful. Moreover, either Mark is not resourceful or Mark is proactive.
examples.question: Is Brook eloquent?
examples.completion:
<think>
We need to determine whether Brook possesses the quality of eloquence. The goal is to trace a chain of logical implications from the given facts and rules that leads to Brook being eloquent.

This premise tells us that studiousness is sufficient for efficiency in Brook's case. It will become useful once we can show Brook is studious.
<step n="1" type="premise">
<text>If Brook is studious, then Brook is efficient.</text>
<logic>efficient(brook) :- studious(brook)</logic>
</step>

Here we have the direct link from talent to eloquence for Brook. If we can prove Brook is talented, this rule will give us the desired conclusion.
<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>If Brook is talented, then Brook is eloquent.</text>
<logic>eloquent(brook) :- talented(brook)</logic>
</step>

This rule connects punctuality with studiousness for Brook, allowing us to move from a known punctual fact to a studious one.
<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>If Brook is punctual, then Brook is studious.</text>
<logic>studious(brook) :- punctual(brook)</logic>
</step>

The rule states that confidence would prevent efficiency for Brook. Later, when we derive Brook's efficiency, we can use this to infer the negation of confidence.
<step n="4" type="premise">
<text>If Brook is confident, then Brook is not efficient.</text>
<logic>~efficient(brook) :- confident(brook)</logic>
</step>

The disjunction offers two alternatives: either Brook lacks competitiveness or Brook is talented. It will be pivotal when we later know Brook is competitive, forcing the other side of the disjunction.
<step n="5" type="premise">
<text>Either Brook is not competitive or Brook is talented.</text>
<logic>~competitive(brook) v talented(brook)</logic>
</step>

Mark being capable is recorded but does not directly affect Brook; it satisfies the completeness of the premise list.
<step n="6" type="premise">
<text>Mark has the property of being capable.</text>
<logic>capable(mark)</logic>
</step>

Nina's punctuality is noted; like other punctual facts, it supports the overall picture but is not directly used for Brook's chain.
<step n="7" type="premise">
<text>Nina is punctual.</text>
<logic>punctual(nina)</logic>
</step>

Wren is also punctual, adding to the factual background without influencing Brook's derivation.
<step n="8" type="premise">
<text>Wren is punctual.</text>
<logic>punctual(wren)</logic>
</step>

Nina's lack of confidence is another factual detail that aligns with the given data set.
<step n="9" type="premise">
<text>Nina is not confident.</text>
<logic>~confident(nina)</logic>
</step>

Similarly, Wren is not confident, completing the set of confidence-related facts for other entities.
<step n="10" type="premise">
<text>Wren is not confident.</text>
<logic>~confident(wren)</logic>
</step>

Brook being punctual is the crucial starting fact for our chain; it will allow us to apply the punctual‑to‑studious rule.
<step n="11" type="premise">
<text>Brook is punctual.</text>
<logic>punctual(brook)</logic>
</step>

Because Brook is punctual (Premise 11) and we have the rule that punctuality implies studiousness (Premise 3), we can conclude Brook is studious. This is a straightforward instance of universal instantiation followed by modus ponens.
<step n="12" type="derived" from="11,3">
<text>From Brook being punctual we infer that Brook is studious.</text>
<logic>studious(brook)</logic>
</step>

Now that we know Brook is studious (Step 12), the rule linking studiousness to efficiency (Premise 1) lets us infer Brook is efficient. This again uses modus ponens on the previously derived studious fact.
<step n="13" type="derived" from="12,1">
<text>Because Brook is studious, we can conclude that Brook is efficient.</text>
<logic>efficient(brook)</logic>
</step>

We have Brook efficient (Step 13). Premise 4 says that if Brook were confident, Brook would not be efficient. Since Brook is efficient, the antecedent (confidence) must be false; otherwise we would have a contradiction. Hence we infer Brook is not confident via modus tollens.
<step n="14" type="derived" from="13,4">
<text>Since Brook is efficient, the rule linking confidence to inefficiency lets us infer that Brook is not confident.</text>
<logic>~confident(brook)</logic>
</step>

Premise 4 gave us the contrapositive relationship, and now we have Brook not confident. The rule that lack of confidence leads to competitiveness (the original competitive rule) allows us to deduce Brook is competitive. This uses universal instantiation with the negated confidence fact.
<step n="15" type="derived" from="14">
<text>If Brook is not confident, then by the competitive rule Brook becomes competitive.</text>
<logic>competitive(brook)</logic>
</step>

We have established Brook is competitive (Step 15). Premise 5 offers a disjunction: either Brook is not competitive or Brook is talented. Since the first alternative is false (Brook is competitive), the disjunction forces the second alternative, giving us Brook is talented. This is an application of disjunctive syllogism.
<step n="16" type="derived" from="15,5">
<text>Brook is competitive and we have a statement that either Brook is not competitive or Brook is talented, so we resolve the disjunction to obtain that Brook is talented.</text>
<logic>talented(brook)</logic>
</step>

Having obtained Brook's talent (Step 16) and with the talent‑to‑eloquence rule (Premise 2), we can finally infer that Brook is eloquent. This step completes the chain needed to answer the query.
<step n="17" type="derived" from="16,2">
<text>Having established that Brook is talented, we apply the eloquence rule to deduce that Brook is eloquent.</text>
<logic>eloquent(brook)</logic>
</step>

Therefore Brook is eloquent, so the conclusion is True.
</think>
Answer: True

examples.question: Is Rhea optimistic?
examples.completion:
<think>
We need to determine whether Rhea can be described as optimistic given the information about her attributes and the logical connections provided. The goal is to use the facts and conditional statements to infer Rhea's optimism.

The rule linking curiosity to optimism is central because if we can show that Rhea is curious, the rule will immediately give us optimism. Thus this premise will be used later in a modus ponens step.
<step n="1" type="premise">
<text>If Rhea is curious, then Rhea is optimistic.</text>
<logic>optimistic(rhea) :- curious(rhea)</logic>
</step>

The conditional that systematicity leads to non‑brilliance is crucial for a contrapositive‑style argument. Since we already know Rhea is brilliant, this rule will help us eliminate the possibility that she is systematic.
<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>If Rhea is systematic, then Rhea is not brilliant.</text>
<logic>~brilliant(rhea) :- systematic(rhea)</logic>
</step>

The disjunction tells us that at least one of systematic or curious must hold for Rhea. Once we rule out systematicity, the disjunction forces curiosity, which is exactly what we need for the optimism rule.
<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>Either Rhea is systematic or Rhea is curious.</text>
<logic>systematic(rhea) v curious(rhea)</logic>
</step>

<step n="4" type="premise">
<text>Zara is studious.</text>
<logic>studious(zara)</logic>
</step>

<step n="5" type="premise">
<text>Rhea is brilliant.</text>
<logic>brilliant(rhea)</logic>
</step>

Because Rhea is known to be brilliant, and the rule says that if she were systematic she would not be brilliant, we can conclude that she cannot be systematic; otherwise we would have a contradiction. This inference uses modus tollens to derive the negation of systematicity.
<step n="6" type="derived" from="5,2">
<text>Rhea is not systematic.</text>
<logic>~systematic(rhea)</logic>
</step>

Having established that Rhea is not systematic, the earlier disjunction (systematic or curious) leaves only the alternative that she must be curious. This is a direct application of disjunctive syllogism, allowing us to affirm her curiosity.
<step n="7" type="derived" from="6,3">
<text>Rhea is curious.</text>
<logic>curious(rhea)</logic>
</step>

examples.question: Is the casserole clever?
examples.completion:

We are asked whether the casserole possesses the quality of cleverness. To answer, we must examine all the information given about the casserole and the saucepan, focusing on any connections that could lead to cleverness. Since cleverness is not directly mentioned, we look for indirect routes through the other properties.

I need to determine whether Casserole is clever can be derived from the given premises.

The second rule links the casserole's flexibility to being seasoned. Knowing whether the casserole is flexible could therefore give us information about its seasoning status, which might be useful later in the chain of reasoning.
<step n="2" type="premise">

The third rule states that a neat saucepan is automatically masterful. Since we already have the fact that the saucepan is neat, this rule lets us infer that the saucepan is masterful, adding another piece to the puzzle.

The fourth rule says that if the casserole is sharp, then it cannot be seasoned. This creates a direct conflict with the second rule, because the casserole cannot be both seasoned and not seasoned at the same time; the presence or absence of sharpness will determine which side holds.

We are given explicitly that the casserole is thorough. This fact will be used when we consider the disjunction that involves the casserole's thoroughness, helping us decide whether the casserole might be flexible.

The fact that the saucepan is neat triggers the third rule, allowing us to conclude that the saucepan is masterful. This derived masterful property will be paired with the disjunction concerning the saucepan, influencing whether the saucepan is savvy.

examples.context: The studio is logical. Whenever a studio is efficient, it is not flexible. If a studio is experienced, then it is not open_plan. A studio is either experienced or innovative. Either the studio is not stressed, or it is flexible. Either the studio is not logical, or it is open_plan. If a studio is stable, then it is stressed. Every sustainable studio is stable. If a studio is innovative, it is sustainable.
examples.question: Is the studio efficient?
examples.completion:
<think>
We are asked to decide whether the studio possesses the property of being efficient. The premises give us a network of conditional statements and disjunctions that we can use to derive the truth value of efficiency.

The rule linking stability to stress will become useful later when we trace how stability propagates to stress. Knowing this connection helps us later to derive that the studio is stressed once we establish its stability.
<step n="1" type="premise">
<text>If the studio is stable, then it is stressed.</text>
<logic>stressed(studio) :- stable(studio)</logic>
</step>

The link from sustainability to stability allows us to move from an innovative or sustainable condition up to stability. This will be needed after we infer that the studio is sustainable.
<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>If the studio is sustainable, then it is stable.</text>
<logic>stable(studio) :- sustainable(studio)</logic>
</step>

The implication that innovation leads to sustainability is a key step for moving from the disjunctive choice of innovative to a concrete property. Once we know the studio is innovative, we can immediately claim it is sustainable.
<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>If the studio is innovative, then it is sustainable.</text>
<logic>sustainable(studio) :- innovative(studio)</logic>
</step>

The conditional that efficiency forces non‑flexibility is the crucial rule for the final contradiction. If we ever can show the studio is flexible, this rule will let us conclude the studio cannot be efficient.
<step n="4" type="premise">
<text>If the studio is efficient, then it is not flexible.</text>
<logic>~flexible(studio) :- efficient(studio)</logic>
</step>

The rule that experience rules out being open_plan will be used after we determine the studio is open_plan. It will let us infer that the studio is not experienced, which then interacts with the experienced‑or‑innovative disjunction.
<step n="5" type="premise">
<text>If the studio is experienced, then it is not open_plan.</text>
<logic>~open_plan(studio) :- experienced(studio)</logic>
</step>

The disjunction that the studio is either experienced or innovative gives us a choice point; once we eliminate one alternative, the other must hold. This will become pivotal after we show the studio is not experienced.
<step n="6" type="premise">
<text>A studio is either experienced or innovative.</text>
<logic>experienced(studio) v innovative(studio)</logic>
</step>

The disjunction stating either not stressed or flexible will allow us to derive flexibility once we have established the studio is stressed. This will later feed into the efficiency rule.
<step n="7" type="premise">
<text>Either the studio is not stressed, or it is flexible.</text>
<logic>~stressed(studio) v flexible(studio)</logic>
</step>

The disjunction that either not logical or open_plan will let us infer openness once we know the studio is logical. This is the first step in the chain that leads to negating experience.
<step n="8" type="premise">
<text>Either the studio is not logical, or it is open_plan.</text>
<logic>~logical(studio) v open_plan(studio)</logic>
</step>

The fact that the studio is logical is the starting factual premise. It will combine with the previous disjunction to produce the open_plan property.
<step n="9" type="premise">
<text>The studio is logical.</text>
<logic>logical(studio)</logic>
</step>

We have the fact that the studio is logical, and the disjunction says either not logical or open_plan. Since the first alternative is false, the only way for the disjunction to be true is that the studio is open_plan. Thus we can conclude open_plan.
<step n="10" type="derived" from="9,8">
<text>From the studio being logical and the disjunction that either it is not logical or open_plan, we infer that the studio is open_plan.</text>
<logic>open_plan(studio)</logic>
</step>

Now that we know the studio is open_plan, we look at the rule that an experienced studio cannot be open_plan. This rule works in a contrapositive way: if open_plan holds, the antecedent (experienced) must be false. Hence the studio is not experienced.
<step n="11" type="derived" from="10,5">
<text>Since the studio is open_plan and the rule that an experienced studio would not be open_plan, we conclude that the studio is not experienced.</text>
<logic>~experienced(studio)</logic>
</step>

Having established that the studio is not experienced, we return to the disjunction that the studio is either experienced or innovative. Since the experienced option is ruled out, the only remaining way for the disjunction to hold is that the studio is innovative.
<step n="12" type="derived" from="11,6">
<text>Given that the studio is not experienced and the disjunction that the studio is either experienced or innovative, we deduce that the studio is innovative.</text>
<logic>innovative(studio)</logic>
</step>

With the studio identified as innovative, we apply the rule that innovation implies sustainability. This direct implication gives us that the studio is sustainable.
<step n="13" type="derived" from="12,3">
<text>Because the studio is innovative and the rule that innovative implies sustainable, we infer that the studio is sustainable.</text>
<logic>sustainable(studio)</logic>
</step>

Sustainability leads to stability via the rule that every sustainable studio is stable. Therefore, from the studio being sustainable we can deduce it is stable.
<step n="14" type="derived" from="13,2">
<text>With the studio being sustainable and the rule that sustainable implies stable, we infer that the studio is stable.</text>
<logic>stable(studio)</logic>
</step>

Stability, according to the earlier rule, forces the studio to be stressed. So the studio being stable allows us to infer that it is stressed.
<step n="15" type="derived" from="14,1">
<text>Since the studio is stable and the rule that stable implies stressed, we infer that the studio is stressed.</text>
<logic>stressed(studio)</logic>
</step>

We now have the studio being stressed, and the disjunction says either not stressed or flexible. The first part is false, so the only way for the disjunction to be true is that the studio is flexible.
<step n="16" type="derived" from="15,7">
<text>From the studio being stressed and the disjunction that either it is not stressed or flexible, we determine that the studio is flexible.</text>
<logic>flexible(studio)</logic>
</step>

Finally, we have the studio being flexible. The rule for efficiency tells us that if the studio were efficient, it would not be flexible. Since we have flexibility, the antecedent (efficient) cannot hold, leading us to conclude the studio is not efficient.
<step n="17" type="derived" from="16,4">
<text>Given that the studio is flexible and the rule that efficient would make it not flexible, we conclude that the studio is not efficient.</text>
<logic>~efficient(studio)</logic>
</step>

We derived Studio is not efficient, so the conclusion Studio is efficient is False.
</think>
Answer: False

examples.context: In this workshop, the oak beam is straight and it is not adjustable. The hammer is also straight. Whenever a tool is straight, it is reliable. If a tool is reliable, then it is in use. An item that is in use is steady, and a steady item is well polished. If a tool is adjustable, then it is not well polished. If a tool is not adjustable, then it is well shaped. Either the hammer is not well shaped or it is ready to cut.
examples.question: Is the hammer ready to cut?
examples.completion:
<think>
We are asked to decide whether the hammer is ready to cut given a collection of carpentry facts and conditional rules. The goal is to chain these conditions together so that we can derive the predicate ready_to_cut for the hammer.

<step n="1" type="premise">
<text>If the hammer is reliable, then it is in use.</text>
<logic>in_use(hammer) :- reliable(hammer)</logic>
</step>

<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>If the hammer is steady, then it is well polished.</text>
<logic>well_polished(hammer) :- steady(hammer)</logic>
</step>

<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>If the hammer is in use, then it is steady.</text>
<logic>steady(hammer) :- in_use(hammer)</logic>
</step>

<step n="4" type="premise">
<text>If the hammer is straight, then it is reliable.</text>
<logic>reliable(hammer) :- straight(hammer)</logic>
</step>

<step n="5" type="premise">
<text>If the hammer is adjustable, then it is not well polished.</text>
<logic>~well_polished(hammer) :- adjustable(hammer)</logic>
</step>

<step n="6" type="premise">
<text>Either the hammer is not well shaped, or it is ready to cut.</text>
<logic>~well_shaped(hammer) v ready_to_cut(hammer)</logic>
</step>

<step n="7" type="premise">
<text>The oak beam is straight.</text>
<logic>straight(oak_beam)</logic>
</step>

<step n="8" type="premise">
<text>The oak beam is not adjustable.</text>
<logic>~adjustable(oak_beam)</logic>
</step>

<step n="9" type="premise">
<text>The hammer is straight.</text>
<logic>straight(hammer)</logic>
</step>

The fact that the hammer is straight directly activates the rule that any straight tool is reliable. By applying universal instantiation to that rule, we conclude the hammer possesses the reliable property, which is the first link in our reasoning chain toward readiness.
<step n="10" type="derived" from="9,4">
<text>Since the hammer is straight, we can infer that it is reliable.</text>
<logic>reliable(hammer)</logic>
</step>

Having established that the hammer is reliable, we can now use the rule stating that a reliable tool is in use. Modus ponens lets us combine the reliable fact with this conditional, giving us that the hammer is currently in use.
<step n="11" type="derived" from="10,1">
<text>Because the hammer is reliable, it follows that it is in use.</text>
<logic>in_use(hammer)</logic>
</step>

The rule that an item in use is steady allows us to move from the hammer being in use to it being steady. This step is straightforward application of modus ponens, converting the in_use status into a steady status.
<step n="12" type="derived" from="11,3">
<text>Being in use means the hammer is steady.</text>
<logic>steady(hammer)</logic>
</step>

With the hammer now known to be steady, we invoke the rule that a steady tool is well polished. Again, modus ponens provides the deduction that the hammer is well polished, advancing us toward the final conclusion.
<step n="13" type="derived" from="12,2">
<text>A steady hammer is consequently well polished.</text>
<logic>well_polished(hammer)</logic>
</step>

We have the hammer well polished, but there is also a rule that if a tool is adjustable it would be not well polished. Using modus tollens, the presence of well polishing contradicts the possibility of adjustability, so we infer that the hammer is not adjustable.
<step n="14" type="derived" from="13,5">
<text>Since the hammer is well polished, it cannot be adjustable; otherwise it would be not well polished.</text>
<logic>~adjustable(hammer)</logic>
</step>

The rule linking non‑adjustability to being well shaped lets us turn the newly derived not‑adjustable fact into the conclusion that the hammer is well shaped. This universal instantiation step supplies the positive shape property we need for the disjunction.
<step n="15" type="derived" from="14">
<text>Knowing the hammer is not adjustable allows us to conclude it is well shaped.</text>
<logic>well_shaped(hammer)</logic>
</step>

The disjunction states that either the hammer is not well shaped or it is ready to cut. Since we have just shown the hammer is well shaped, the first alternative is false, and by disjunctive syllogism the remaining alternative must hold: the hammer is ready to cut.
<step n="16" type="derived" from="15,6">
<text>The hammer is well shaped, so the alternative that it is not well shaped is false, leaving that it must be ready to cut.</text>
<logic>ready_to_cut(hammer)</logic>
</step>

Therefore Hammer is ready to cut, so the conclusion is True.
</think>
Answer: True

If a sea turtle is steady, then it cannot be schooling, establishing that steadiness leads to a lack of schooling. Either the sea turtle is not melodious or it is adept, providing a disjunction about its melodiousness and adeptness. Either the sea turtle is not curious or it is melodious, linking curiosity and melodiousness for the sea turtle.
examples.question: Is the sea turtle faithful?
examples.completion:
<think>
We are asked to determine if the sea turtle possesses the property of being faithful. To answer, we must see whether the given facts and rules can lead to a conclusion about the sea turtle's faithfulness.

I need to determine whether Sea_turtle is faithful can be derived from the given premises.

Recognizing the rule about adept sea turtles being schooling is important because it connects the predicate adept to schooling, which later appears in a negative rule. Knowing this forward link helps us consider what would happen if we ever learned the sea turtle is adept.

<logic>schooling(sea_turtle) :- adept(sea_turtle)</logic>

The rule linking hardness to perceptiveness tells us that the manta ray's hardiness automatically gives it the perceptive property. This forward implication will be useful when we examine the disjunction involving perceptiveness and vigilance for the manta ray.

<text>The rule states that every hardy manta ray is perceptive.</text>
<logic>perceptive(manta_ray) :- hardy(manta_ray)</logic>

The vigilance‑to‑keenness rule provides a bridge from the alert (vigilant) state to the sharp (keen) state in manta rays. Though keenness does not appear elsewhere, the rule is part of the complete set of premises that must be acknowledged.
<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>The rule states that whenever a manta ray is vigilant, it becomes keen.</text>
<logic>keen(manta_ray) :- vigilant(manta_ray)</logic>

examples.question: Is Cora determined?
examples.completion:

We need to establish whether Cora possesses the property of being determined. The proof proceeds by chaining the given facts, rules, and disjunctions until the desired predicate emerges.

</step>

<step n="3" type="premise">

</step>

<step n="7" type="premise">

<logic>~friendly(cora) v determined(cora)</logic>
</step>

<step n="8" type="premise">

<step n="9" type="premise">

The only fact we have about humility is that Cora is humble. The disjunction states that either Cora is not humble or she is content; because the first alternative is false, the only way for the statement to hold is that Cora must be content. Hence we can safely assert content(cora).
<step n="10" type="derived" from="9,8">
<text>From Cora being humble and the clause that either Cora is not humble or Cora is content, we conclude that Cora is content.</text>
<logic>content(cora)</logic>

The rule linking content to well prepared tells us that any entity that is content automatically becomes well prepared. Since we have just derived that Cora is content, we can directly infer that she is well prepared.
<step n="11" type="derived" from="10,3">

We also know a rule that if something is rested, then it cannot be well prepared. Because we have established that Cora is well prepared, the consequent of that rule must be false; therefore the antecedent, rested(cora), cannot hold. This gives us the negation ~rested(cora).
<step n="12" type="derived" from="11,5">

<logic>~rested(cora)</logic>
</step>

The first rule in the list says that lack of rest leads to anxiety: ~rested(cora) → anxious(cora). Having just derived ~rested(cora), we can apply this implication and conclude that Cora is anxious.
<step n="13" type="derived" from="12,1">
<text>Given that Cora is not rested, the first rule lets us deduce that Cora is anxious.</text>
<logic>anxious(cora)</logic>
</step>

One of the disjunctions informs us that either Cora is not anxious or she is loyal. Since we now know Cora is anxious, the left side of the disjunction is false, forcing the right side to be true. Consequently, loyal(cora) follows.
<step n="14" type="derived" from="13,6">

<logic>loyal(cora)</logic>

There is a rule that observant things are not loyal: observant(cora) → ~loyal(cora). We have just derived loyal(cora), which contradicts the consequent of this rule. To avoid inconsistency, the antecedent must be false, so we infer ~observant(cora).
<step n="15" type="derived" from="14,4">

The second rule tells us that lack of observance leads to friendliness: ~observant(cora) → friendly(cora). Since we have established ~observant(cora), we can immediately deduce that Cora is friendly.

The final disjunction says either Cora is not friendly or Cora is determined. Because we now know Cora is friendly, the first alternative is false, which means the second alternative must be true. Therefore, determined(cora) is concluded.

<logic>thorough(hector) :- diligent(hector)</logic>

<step n="6" type="premise">
<text>If a person is confident then they are not persistent.</text>
<logic>~persistent(hector) :- confident(hector)</logic>
</step>

<step n="7" type="premise">
<text>Hector is either confident or analytical.</text>
<logic>confident(hector) v analytical(hector)</logic>
</step>

<step n="8" type="premise">
<text>Hector is diplomatic.</text>
<logic>diplomatic(hector)</logic>
</step>

We start with the explicit fact that Hector is diplomatic. The rule "Being diplomatic guarantees that the person is evidence-driven" allows us to apply modus ponens, giving us evidence that Hector is evidence-driven. This is the first link in the chain toward resilience.
<step n="9" type="derived" from="8,2">
<text>From the fact that Hector is diplomatic and the rule linking diplomatic to evidence-driven, we infer that Hector is evidence-driven.</text>
<logic>evidence_driven(hector)</logic>
</step>

Having established that Hector is evidence-driven, we can use the rule "Evidence-driven individuals are resilient". By modus ponens again, we infer that Hector is resilient, moving us closer to persistence.
<step n="10" type="derived" from="9,4">
<text>Since Hector is evidence-driven and evidence-driven implies resilience, we conclude Hector is resilient.</text>
<logic>resilient(hector)</logic>
</step>

The next rule tells us that resilience leads to persistence. Since we now know Hector is resilient, we can conclude, via modus ponens, that Hector is persistent. This creates a tension with a later rule about confidence.
<step n="11" type="derived" from="10,3">
<text>Given Hector's resilience and the rule that resilience leads to persistence, we deduce Hector is persistent.</text>
<logic>persistent(hector)</logic>
</step>

We also have a rule stating that if someone is confident, they are not persistent. Because we have derived that Hector is persistent, the only way for both statements to be true is for the antecedent (confidence) to be false. Thus, we deduce that Hector cannot be confident, i.e., he is not confident.
<step n="12" type="derived" from="11,6">
<text>Because Hector is persistent and the rule that confidence would make him not persistent, we infer that Hector cannot be confident.</text>
<logic>~confident(hector)</logic>
</step>

The disjunction "Hector is either confident or analytical" together with the knowledge that he is not confident forces the alternative to hold. By disjunctive syllogism, we conclude that Hector must be analytical.
<step n="13" type="derived" from="12,7">
<text>With the knowledge that Hector is not confident and the statement that he is either confident or analytical, we resolve that Hector must be analytical.</text>
<logic>analytical(hector)</logic>
</step>

Now that we know Hector is analytical, we can apply the rule "Any analytical entity is diligent". Modus ponens yields that Hector is diligent, which is the immediate precursor to thoroughness.
<step n="14" type="derived" from="13,1">
<text>From Hector being analytical and the rule that analytical implies diligent, we obtain that Hector is diligent.</text>
<logic>diligent(hector)</logic>
</step>

Finally, the rule "All diligent people are thorough" lets us turn Hector's diligence into thoroughness via another application of modus ponens. This gives us the desired conclusion.
<step n="15" type="derived" from="14,5">
<text>Finally, Hector's diligence together with the rule that diligent entails thorough gives us that Hector is thorough.</text>
<logic>thorough(hector)</logic>
</step>

Therefore Hector is thorough, so the conclusion is True.
</think>
Answer: True

examples.context: Pilot_kent is certified. Whenever a pilot is certified, that pilot is also maneuverable. If a pilot is maneuverable and there is no indication that the pilot is abnormally reliable, then the pilot is considered reliable. Moreover, if a pilot is reliable and there is no indication that the pilot is abnormally innovative, then the pilot is regarded as innovative. No other alternatives or contradictory information are given.
examples.question: Is pilot_kent innovative?
examples.completion:
<think>
We need to decide whether pilot_kent possesses the innovative quality. The goal is to trace the information supplied about certification and the default rules to reach a conclusion about innovativeness.

The first rule tells us that certification guarantees maneuverability. This connection is important because maneuverability is the premise for the next default rule, so we must keep it in mind.

<logic>maneuverable(pilot_kent) :- certified(pilot_kent)</logic>

The second premise introduces a default: a maneuverable pilot becomes reliable unless there is an abnormal reliability flag. Since we have no such flag, this rule will let us move from maneuverability to reliability.
<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>If a pilot is maneuverable and there is no evidence that the pilot is abnormally reliable, then the pilot is reliable.</text>
<logic>reliable(pilot_kent) :- maneuverable(pilot_kent), not ab_reliable(pilot_kent)</logic>
</step>

The third premise adds another default, linking reliability to innovativeness, again conditional on the absence of an abnormal innovation flag. This chain will let us reach the target property if reliability is established.
<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>If a pilot is reliable and there is no evidence that the pilot is abnormally innovative, then the pilot is innovative.</text>
<logic>innovative(pilot_kent) :- reliable(pilot_kent), not ab_innovative(pilot_kent)</logic>
</step>

The fact that pilot_kent is certified gives us a concrete starting point. It directly satisfies the antecedent of the first rule, allowing us to activate the chain of implications.
<step n="4" type="premise">
<text>Pilot_kent is certified.</text>
<logic>certified(pilot_kent)</logic>
</step>

Applying the rule from PREMISE_1 to the fact in PREMISE_4, we deduce that pilot_kent is maneuverable. This step uses straightforward implication: certified implies maneuverable, and we have certification.
<step n="5" type="derived" from="4,1">
<text>From the certification, we infer that pilot_kent is maneuverable.</text>
<logic>maneuverable(pilot_kent)</logic>
</step>

With pilot_kent now known to be maneuverable, the default rule from PREMISE_2 applies because there is no abnormal reliability information, so pilot_kent becomes reliable. Then, using the default from PREMISE_3 and the same lack of abnormal innovation evidence, we infer that pilot_kent is innovative. The two defaults work together to carry the property forward to the query.
<step n="6" type="derived" from="5,2,3">
<text>Given pilot_kent is maneuverable, the default rule makes them reliable, and then another default rule makes them innovative, so we conclude pilot_kent is innovative.</text>

Therefore Pilot_kent is innovative, so the conclusion is True.

examples.context: Carpenter Nora is dependable. Any carpenter who is dependable is also watchful. If she feels nervous, she will be timely. Whenever she is resourceful, she becomes nervous. Being timely guarantees that she is helpful. If she works swiftly, she is not helpful. Being precise means she is not watchful. Carpenter Nora is either precise or resourceful.
examples.question: Is Carpenter Nora swift?
examples.completion:
<think>
We need to decide whether Carpenter Nora possesses the quality of being swift. The proof proceeds by chaining the given facts and rules to see if swift can be supported or contradicted.

The rule linking resourcefulness to nervousness will become useful later when we establish that Nora is resourceful. It tells us that any instance of resourcefulness automatically gives us nervousness.
<step n="1" type="premise">
<text>If Carpenter Nora is resourceful, then she is nervous.</text>
<logic>nervous(carpenter_nora) :- resourceful(carpenter_nora)</logic>
</step>

The connection between timeliness and helpfulness is important because once we derive timeliness we can immediately obtain helpfulness. This will later interact with the rule about swift implying not helpful.
<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>If Carpenter Nora is timely, then she is helpful.</text>
<logic>helpful(carpenter_nora) :- timely(carpenter_nora)</logic>
</step>

Knowing that dependability guarantees watchfulness lets us turn the initial fact about Nora's dependability into a concrete property we can work with. This rule is the bridge from a fact to a derived predicate.
<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>If Carpenter Nora is dependable, then she is watchful.</text>
<logic>watchful(carpenter_nora) :- dependable(carpenter_nora)</logic>
</step>

The implication from nervousness to timeliness will allow us to move forward once we have nervousness. It creates a chain: nervous → timely.
<step n="4" type="premise">
<text>If Carpenter Nora is nervous, then she is timely.</text>
<logic>timely(carpenter_nora) :- nervous(carpenter_nora)</logic>
</step>

The rule that swift leads to not helpful is the key to disproving swiftness later; if we ever derive helpfulness, this rule will give us a contradiction with swiftness.
<step n="5" type="premise">
<text>If Carpenter Nora is swift, then she is not helpful.</text>
<logic>~helpful(carpenter_nora) :- swift(carpenter_nora)</logic>
</step>

The statement that precision would make Nora not watchful will be used in a contrapositive fashion. If we already know she is watchful, we can infer she cannot be precise.
<step n="6" type="premise">
<text>If Carpenter Nora is precise, then she is not watchful.</text>
<logic>~watchful(carpenter_nora) :- precise(carpenter_nora)</logic>
</step>

The disjunction that Nora is either precise or resourceful provides the alternative that we can eliminate one side of, forcing the other to hold. It is essential for the disjunctive syllogism step.
<step n="7" type="premise">
<text>Carpenter Nora is either precise or resourceful.</text>
<logic>precise(carpenter_nora) v resourceful(carpenter_nora)</logic>
</step>

The fact that Nora is dependable is our starting point. It directly activates the rule that dependable implies watchful.
<step n="8" type="premise">
<text>Carpenter Nora is dependable.</text>
<logic>dependable(carpenter_nora)</logic>
</step>

From the fact that Nora is dependable, and using the rule that dependability guarantees watchfulness, we can conclude that she is watchful. This gives us a concrete predicate to work with in the next step.
<step n="9" type="derived" from="8,3">
<text>From her dependability we infer that Carpenter Nora is watchful.</text>
<logic>watchful(carpenter_nora)</logic>
</step>

Since we now know Nora is watchful, and we have the rule that being precise would make someone not watchful, the only way for her to be watchful is to not be precise. Thus we infer that she is not precise, applying modus tollens.
<step n="10" type="derived" from="9,6">
<text>Because she is watchful, the rule that being precise would make her not watchful tells us that she is not precise.</text>
<logic>~precise(carpenter_nora)</logic>
</step>

We have established that Nora is not precise, yet the disjunction tells us she must be either precise or resourceful. Because the precise option is ruled out, the resourceful alternative must be true. This is a direct application of disjunctive syllogism.
<step n="11" type="derived" from="10,7">
<text>Given that she is not precise and that she is either precise or resourceful, we deduce that she must be resourceful.</text>
<logic>resourceful(carpenter_nora)</logic>
</step>

Having shown that Nora is resourceful, we can invoke the rule that resourcefulness leads to nervousness. Therefore, Nora is nervous, moving us further along the chain toward timeliness.
<step n="12" type="derived" from="11,1">
<text>Since she is resourceful, the rule that resourceful implies nervous lets us infer that she is nervous.</text>
<logic>nervous(carpenter_nora)</logic>
</step>

With Nora now known to be nervous, the rule linking nervousness to timeliness allows us to deduce that she is timely. This step follows directly by modus ponens.
<step n="13" type="derived" from="12,4">
<text>Being nervous leads, by the rule, to her being timely.</text>
<logic>timely(carpenter_nora)</logic>
</step>

Because Nora is timely, the rule that timeliness guarantees helpfulness lets us conclude that she is helpful. This newly derived helpfulness will be pivotal for the final contradiction.
<step n="14" type="derived" from="13,2">
<text>Her timeliness then triggers the rule that timely implies helpful, so she is helpful.</text>
<logic>helpful(carpenter_nora)</logic>
</step>

We now have that Nora is helpful, but the rule stating that if someone were swift they would not be helpful contradicts this. Since she is helpful, she cannot be swift; otherwise we would have both helpful and not helpful, which is impossible. Hence we infer that she is not swift.
<step n="15" type="derived" from="14,5">
<text>Because she is helpful and the rule that swift would make her not helpful, we infer that she cannot be swift.</text>
<logic>~swift(carpenter_nora)</logic>
</step>

We derived Carpenter_nora is not swift, so the conclusion Carpenter_nora is swift is False.
</think>
Answer: False

examples.context: In the mythic realm, Lyra is known to be courteous, and Odysseus is known to be insightful. Hephaestus is also courteous. Aegis is modest, Selene is industrious, and Odysseus is insightful as well. Additionally, Lyra is marked as ab_serene, and Odysseus is marked as ab_wise. The ancient code says that any being that is wise is also compassionate; specifically, if someone is wise then they are compassionate. It also declares that any being that is industrious is just, so Selene being industrious makes her just. Whenever someone is serene, they become attentive, so serenity leads to attentiveness. If someone is attentive, then they become insightful, so attentiveness implies insightfulness. If someone is patient, then they are unbiased, giving a rule that patient leads to impartiality. If someone is modest, then they are systematic, linking modesty to organization. If someone is rejuvenated, then they are driven, linking rest to motivation. The lore also includes two disjunctions: either Selene is not just or Selene is rejuvenated, and either Aegis is not systematic or Aegis is patient. Moreover, there are default principles: if Hephaestus is courteous and there is no evidence that Hephaestus is not serene, then Hephaestus is serene; and if Hephaestus is insightful and there is no evidence that Hephaestus is not wise, then Hephaestus is wise.
examples.question: Based on the information provided, can we determine whether Hephaestus is enterprising?

The patient‑to‑unbiased rule shows that patience yields impartiality. This is useful for reasoning about Aegis, but it also mirrors the style of conditional propagation we must track throughout the proof.

The modest‑to‑systematic rule provides a path from humility to organization. Since Aegis is modest, this rule will let us infer his systematic nature, which later interacts with the disjunction about Aegis.

Attentiveness leading to insightfulness is a key step for Hephaestus because it bridges the gap between calmness (via serenity) and the perceptive condition needed for the default wisdom rule. This chain is essential for building any further conclusions about Hephaestus.

Similarly, the second default says that insightfulness plus no evidence against wisdom yields wisdom. Once we can derive Hephaestus's insightfulness, and given no ab_wise fact about him, we can potentially infer his wisdom.

The fact that Lyra is marked as ab_serene serves as a counterexample showing that not everyone who is courteous becomes serene; the abnormality flag blocks the default for Lyra. This reminds us that the default only works when the abnormality predicate is absent.

Odysseus being marked as ab_wise mirrors the previous point for the wisdom default, indicating that the default cannot be applied to him. It underscores the importance of checking for abnormality before using a default rule.

Selene's industriousness is explicitly given, allowing us to activate the industrious‑to‑just rule from PREMISE_2. This fact also participates in the disjunction concerning Selene's justice or rejuvenation.

examples.question: Is it true that the white king is not enduring?
examples.completion:

This rule tells us that forcefulness guarantees structuredness for the white king. Since we already know the white king is forceful, the rule is directly applicable.

The link between being solid and enduring is the final step needed to reach our goal. Once we have solidness, we can immediately infer endurance.
<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>If the white king is solid, then it is enduring.</text>
<logic>enduring(white_king) :- solid(white_king)</logic>

The disjunction gives us a choice: either the white king is not positional or it is solid. Later, when we establish the white king is positional, this will force the solid alternative.
<step n="4" type="premise">

<logic>~positional(white_king) v solid(white_king)</logic>

Stating that the black bishop is tactical provides one of the factual foundations of the scenario, though it does not directly affect the white king's endurance.
<step n="5" type="premise">
<text>The black bishop is tactical.</text>

The fact that the black knight is forceful adds another factual detail about the board, but it is not used in the derivation of the query.

<logic>forceful(black_knight)</logic>

The white king being forceful is the key fact that activates the rule from premise 2, setting the chain of inference in motion.
<step n="7" type="premise">
<text>The white king is forceful.</text>
<logic>forceful(white_king)</logic>

Because the white king is forceful (Premise 7) and the rule says forcefulness implies structuredness (Premise 2), we can safely conclude the white king is structured. This gives us the antecedent needed for the next rule.
<step n="8" type="derived" from="7,2">
<text>From the fact that the white king is forceful and the rule linking forcefulness to structuredness, we infer that the white king is structured.</text>
<logic>structured(white_king)</logic>

Having derived that the white king is structured, we apply the rule from Premise 1, which tells us that any structured white king is positional. Thus, the white king becomes positional.
<step n="9" type="derived" from="8,1">

<logic>positional(white_king)</logic>

We now have the white king as positional, which makes the left side of the disjunction in Premise 4 false. Since a disjunction is true only if at least one part holds, the only way for it to remain true is for the right side, solidness, to be true. Hence, the white king is solid.
<step n="10" type="derived" from="9,4">

With the white king established as solid, we invoke the rule from Premise 3 that solidness guarantees endurance. Consequently, the white king is enduring.
<step n="11" type="derived" from="10,3">
<text>Having established that the white king is solid, the rule that solid pieces endure yields that the white king is enduring.</text>
<logic>enduring(white_king)</logic>
</step>

We derived White_king is enduring, so the conclusion White_king is not enduring is False.
</think>

examples.question: Is Stella alert?
examples.completion:
<think>
We are asked to determine whether Stella possesses the alert property. The goal is to chain together the given facts and conditional statements until we can either directly assert alert(stella) or show it cannot hold.

This rule tells us that perceptiveness is sufficient for alertness. Keeping this in mind, if we can later show Stella is perceptive, we will immediately obtain alert(stella).
<step n="1" type="premise">
<text>Any perceptive person is also alert, so if Stella is perceptive then she must be alert.</text>
<logic>alert(stella) :- perceptive(stella)</logic>
</step>

Here we learn that friendliness guarantees caution. This will become useful after we manage to prove Stella is friendly, allowing us to derive cautiousness.
<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>Any friendly person is cautious, meaning that friendliness guarantees caution for Stella.</text>
<logic>cautious(stella) :- friendly(stella)</logic>
</step>

The rule links punctuality to gratitude. Since we already know Stella is punctual, this premise sets up a path to a grateful status for her.
<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>Being punctual makes a person grateful, so Stella's punctuality gives her gratitude.</text>
<logic>grateful(stella) :- punctual(stella)</logic>
</step>

Meticulousness implies perceptiveness. Later, if we can establish that Stella is meticulous, this premise will let us infer she is perceptive, moving us toward alertness.
<step n="4" type="premise">
<text>If someone is meticulous, then they are perceptive; thus meticulousness leads to perceptiveness for Stella.</text>
<logic>perceptive(stella) :- meticulous(stella)</logic>
</step>

Cooperativeness forces a lack of caution. This contrapositive relationship will help us eliminate the possibility that Stella is cooperative once we have her cautiousness.
<step n="5" type="premise">
<text>Cooperativeness forces a person to be not cautious; therefore a cooperative Stella would not be cautious.</text>
<logic>~cautious(stella) :- cooperative(stella)</logic>
</step>

Scholarly status forces a lack of gratitude. This will be used in a modus tollens step to rule out scholarlyness once we have gratitude.
<step n="6" type="premise">
<text>Being scholarly forces a person to be not grateful; a scholarly Stella would lack gratitude.</text>
<logic>~grateful(stella) :- scholarly(stella)</logic>
</step>

The disjunction states that at least one of cooperative or meticulous holds for Stella. This gives us a fallback option: if we can discard one, the other must be true.
<step n="7" type="premise">
<text>Either Stella is cooperative or she is meticulous; one of these two must hold.</text>
<logic>cooperative(stella) v meticulous(stella)</logic>
</step>

Similarly, the second disjunction guarantees that Stella is either scholarly or friendly. Determining the false side will let us assert the true side.
<step n="8" type="premise">
<text>Either Stella is scholarly or she is friendly; at least one of these alternatives is true.</text>
<logic>scholarly(stella) v friendly(stella)</logic>
</step>

The fact that Stella is punctual is our starting concrete piece of information. It will trigger the rule that punctuality leads to gratitude.
<step n="9" type="premise">
<text>Stella is punctual, which is a given fact.</text>
<logic>punctual(stella)</logic>
</step>

Using the fact that Stella is punctual together with the rule that punctuality implies gratitude, we can directly infer that Stella is grateful. This gives us a positive statement about gratitude to work with in the next step.
<step n="10" type="derived" from="9,3">
<text>From Stella being punctual and the rule that punctuality implies gratitude, we infer that Stella is grateful.</text>
<logic>grateful(stella)</logic>
</step>

We now have grateful(stella). The rule that scholarly people are not grateful lets us apply modus tollens: if scholarly implied not grateful, but we observe gratitude, then scholarly must be false. Hence we conclude that Stella is not scholarly.
<step n="11" type="derived" from="10,6">
<text>Since Stella is grateful and the rule says scholarly people would not be grateful, we conclude that Stella cannot be scholarly.</text>
<logic>~scholarly(stella)</logic>
</step>

With the knowledge that Stella is not scholarly and the disjunction that she is either scholarly or friendly, the only way for the disjunction to hold is for the friendly alternative to be true. Therefore we deduce that Stella is friendly.
<step n="12" type="derived" from="11,8">

Having established that Stella is friendly, we can invoke the rule that friendliness guarantees caution. This yields cautious(stella), a crucial intermediate fact for later reasoning about cooperativeness.
<step n="13" type="derived" from="12,2">

Now that we know Stella is cautious, we look back at the rule linking cooperativeness to not being cautious. Since cautiousness contradicts the consequence of cooperativeness, we can apply modus tollens to infer that Stella cannot be cooperative.
<step n="14" type="derived" from="13,5">

The disjunction says Stella is either cooperative or meticulous. Because we have just shown she is not cooperative, the only remaining possibility is that she is meticulous. Thus we conclude meticulous(stella).
<step n="15" type="derived" from="14,7">

Finally, the first premise tells us that any perceptive individual is alert. Since we now have perceptive(stella), we can directly infer alert(stella).
