examples.context: Alexander, an ancient commander, is known to be meticulous. Any neutral individual is strategic, and any strategic individual is diplomatic. If someone is inquisitive, then they are not wise. If someone is resolute, then they are not equipped. Moreover, Alexander is either inquisitive or neutral. Also, either Alexander is not diplomatic or he is equipped. Finally, either Alexander is not meticulous or he is wise.
examples.question: Is it true that Alexander is not resolute?
examples.completion:
<think>
We are asked to determine whether Alexander lacks the quality of being resolute. To answer this, we will chain the given facts, rules, and disjunctions step by step, arriving at a conclusion about his resoluteness.

The first premise tells us that neutrality guarantees strategic ability. This will be useful later when we learn that Alexander is neutral, allowing us to infer his strategic status.
<step n="1" type="premise">
<text>The rule says that any neutral person is also strategic.</text>
<logic>strategic(alexander) :- neutral(alexander)</logic>
</step>

The second premise links strategic ability to diplomatic conduct. Once we have Alexander as strategic, this rule will let us assert that he is diplomatic.
<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>The rule says that any strategic person is also diplomatic.</text>
<logic>diplomatic(alexander) :- strategic(alexander)</logic>
</step>

The third premise states that inquisitiveness prevents wisdom. It creates a contrapositive relationship that will help us eliminate the possibility that Alexander is inquisitive once we know he is wise.
<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>The rule says that if someone is inquisitive, then they are not wise.</text>
<logic>~wise(alexander) :- inquisitive(alexander)</logic>
</step>

The fourth premise says that being resolute precludes being equipped. This contrapositive will be the key to deriving that Alexander is not resolute once we establish that he is equipped.
<step n="4" type="premise">
<text>The rule says that if someone is resolute, then they are not equipped.</text>
<logic>~equipped(alexander) :- resolute(alexander)</logic>
</step>

The fifth premise gives a disjunction: Alexander is either inquisitive or neutral. This alternative will allow us to pinpoint his exact status once we rule out one of the options.
<step n="5" type="premise">
<text>It is given that Alexander is either inquisitive or neutral.</text>
<logic>inquisitive(alexander) v neutral(alexander)</logic>
</step>

The sixth premise provides another disjunction: either Alexander is not diplomatic or he is equipped. This will let us derive his equipped status after we confirm he is diplomatic.
<step n="6" type="premise">
<text>It is given that either Alexander is not diplomatic or he is equipped.</text>
<logic>~diplomatic(alexander) v equipped(alexander)</logic>
</step>

The seventh premise offers a disjunction concerning meticulousness and wisdom: Alexander is either not meticulous or he is wise. Combined with the fact that he is meticulous, we can resolve this disjunction.
<step n="7" type="premise">
<text>It is given that either Alexander is not meticulous or he is wise.</text>
<logic>~meticulous(alexander) v wise(alexander)</logic>
</step>

The eighth premise is a straightforward fact: Alexander is meticulous. This fact will be crucial for applying the previous disjunction and moving the proof forward.
<step n="8" type="premise">
<text>It is a fact that Alexander is meticulous.</text>
<logic>meticulous(alexander)</logic>
</step>

We have Alexander being meticulous and the disjunction that he is either not meticulous or wise. Since the first part of the disjunction is false (he is meticulous), the only way for the disjunction to hold is for Alexander to be wise. Thus we conclude he is wise.
<step n="9" type="derived" from="8,7">
<text>Since Alexander is meticulous and either not meticulous or wise, we conclude that Alexander is wise.</text>
<logic>wise(alexander)</logic>
</step>

The rule from premise 3 tells us that inquisitiveness would make someone not wise. Because we have just established that Alexander is wise, the antecedent (being inquisitive) must be false; otherwise we would have a contradiction. Therefore Alexander cannot be inquisitive.
<step n="10" type="derived" from="9,3">
<text>Because being inquisitive would make someone not wise, and Alexander is wise, we infer that Alexander is not inquisitive.</text>
<logic>~inquisitive(alexander)</logic>
</step>

Premise 5 says Alexander is either inquisitive or neutral. Since we have just shown he is not inquisitive, the only remaining possibility is that he is neutral. Hence we infer his neutrality.
<step n="11" type="derived" from="10,5">
<text>Given that Alexander is not inquisitive and that he must be either inquisitive or neutral, we deduce that Alexander is neutral.</text>
<logic>neutral(alexander)</logic>
</step>

With Alexander now known to be neutral, premise 1 allows us to infer that he is strategic, because neutrality guarantees strategic ability. This gives us Alexander's strategic status.
<step n="12" type="derived" from="11,1">
<text>From Alexander being neutral and the rule that neutral implies strategic, we obtain that Alexander is strategic.</text>
<logic>strategic(alexander)</logic>
</step>

Given Alexander's strategic status, premise 2 lets us deduce that he is diplomatic, as every strategic individual is diplomatic. This establishes his diplomatic character.
<step n="13" type="derived" from="12,2">
<text>From Alexander being strategic and the rule that strategic implies diplomatic, we obtain that Alexander is diplomatic.</text>
<logic>diplomatic(alexander)</logic>
</step>

Premise 6 offers a disjunction: either Alexander is not diplomatic or he is equipped. Since we have just proven he is diplomatic, the first option is false, forcing the second option to be true. Consequently, Alexander is equipped.
<step n="14" type="derived" from="13,6">
<text>Since Alexander is diplomatic and either not diplomatic or equipped, we infer that Alexander is equipped.</text>
<logic>equipped(alexander)</logic>
</step>

Premise 4 states that if someone is resolute, they cannot be equipped. We have just shown Alexander is equipped, so the antecedent (being resolute) must be false; otherwise we would have a contradiction. Therefore Alexander is not resolute.
<step n="15" type="derived" from="14,4">
<text>Because being resolute would make someone not equipped, and Alexander is equipped, we conclude that Alexander is not resolute.</text>
<logic>~resolute(alexander)</logic>
</step>

Therefore Alexander is not resolute, so the conclusion is True.
</think>
Answer: True

examples.context: In the robot crew, Diana is known to be open_minded, and whenever someone is open_minded, that person triggers an alert; thus Diana will be alert if she is open_minded. Diana will also be dedicated whenever she is organized. Sky is compassionate, and any compassionate robot is insightful, so Sky becomes insightful. Sage is precise, and any precise robot is observant, making Sage observant. Any inventive robot is empathetic, so if Sage were inventive she would be empathetic. Any organized robot is dedicated, reinforcing Diana's potential dedication. The system also includes the following alternatives: either Diana is not alert or Diana is organized (~alert(diana) v organized(diana)); either Sky is not insightful or Sky is rational (~insightful(sky) v rational(sky)); either Sky is not rational or Sky is industrious (~rational(sky) v industrious(sky)); and either Sage is not observant or Sage is inventive (~observant(sage) v inventive(sage)). Finally, the robots have the concrete properties: Sky is compassionate, Diana is open_minded, and Sage is precise.
examples.question: Is Sky industrious?
examples.completion:
<think>
We are asked to determine whether Sky possesses the property industrious. The knowledge base provides several facts about the robots and logical connections that will allow us to derive Sky's industriousness step by step.

The rule linking compassion to insight is crucial because it gives us a way to move from a known fact about Sky (being compassionate) to a new property (being insightful). Recognizing this bridge sets up the chain of reasoning that will later involve the disjunctions concerning insight and rationality.
<step n="1" type="premise">
<text>The rule states that any robot that is compassionate is also insightful, so Sky being compassionate implies Sky is insightful.</text>
<logic>insightful(sky) :- compassionate(sky)</logic>
</step>

The disjunction ~insightful(sky) v rational(sky) offers a choice: either Sky lacks insight or Sky is rational. Since we will soon establish that Sky is indeed insightful, the first alternative becomes impossible, forcing the second alternative to hold.
<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>The disjunction says that either Sky is not insightful or Sky is rational.</text>
<logic>~insightful(sky) v rational(sky)</logic>
</step>

Similarly, the disjunction ~rational(sky) v industrious(sky) presents two possibilities. Once we have shown that Sky is rational, the negated option (~rational) is ruled out, leaving industrious as the only viable conclusion.
<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>The disjunction says that either Sky is not rational or Sky is industrious.</text>
<logic>~rational(sky) v industrious(sky)</logic>
</step>

Diana being open_minded is recorded as a fact; while it does not directly affect Sky, it fulfills one of the background conditions in the knowledge base and ensures the consistency of the overall scenario.
<step n="4" type="premise">
<text>Fact: Diana is open_minded.</text>
<logic>open_minded(diana)</logic>
</step>

Sage being precise is another factual statement that supports other rules about observance, but it is not needed for the current derivation concerning Sky. Still, it must be mentioned to satisfy the requirement that every premise appears in the context.
<step n="5" type="premise">
<text>Fact: Sage is precise.</text>
<logic>precise(sage)</logic>
</step>

The fact that Sky is compassionate is the starting point for our chain. Without this fact, we could not apply the compassionate‑to‑insight rule, and the subsequent deductions would fail.
<step n="6" type="premise">
<text>Fact: Sky is compassionate.</text>
<logic>compassionate(sky)</logic>
</step>

Applying Modus Ponens, we combine the fact that Sky is compassionate with the rule that compassion implies insight. Because the antecedent (compassionate) holds, we can confidently assert the consequent, giving us that Sky is insightful.
<step n="7" type="derived" from="6,1">
<text>From Sky’s compassion and the rule that compassion leads to insight, we infer that Sky is insightful.</text>
<logic>insightful(sky)</logic>
</step>

Now we have Sky insightful, and the disjunction says either not insightful or rational. Since we know the first disjunct (~insightful) is false, the only way for the disjunction to be true is for the second disjunct, rational(sky), to be true. Thus we deduce that Sky is rational.
<step n="8" type="derived" from="7,2">
<text>Using the insight that Sky is insightful together with the disjunction that either not insightful or rational, we eliminate the false alternative and conclude that Sky is rational.</text>
<logic>rational(sky)</logic>
</step>

With rational(sky) established, we turn to the final disjunction ~rational(sky) v industrious(sky). The left side (~rational) is contradicted by our previous step, so the right side must hold to keep the statement true. Consequently, Sky is industrious.
<step n="9" type="derived" from="8,3">
<text>With Sky being rational and the disjunction that either not rational or industrious, we discard the negated option and deduce that Sky is industrious.</text>
<logic>industrious(sky)</logic>
</step>

Therefore Sky is industrious, so the conclusion is True.
</think>
Answer: True

examples.context: In the workshop, three carpenters—Anne, Ian, and Paul—work together. Anne is cooperative, Ian is cooperative, and Paul is cooperative. Anne is task_focused, and Ian is task_focused. Whenever a carpenter is cooperative, they are steady. Any carpenter who is strong becomes task_focused. If a carpenter is task_focused, then they are ready. A carpenter who is precise cannot be ready. A carpenter needs attention exactly when they are not precise. Moreover, either Paul is not steady or Paul is strong.
examples.question: Can we conclude that Paul does not need attention?
examples.completion:
<think>
We are asked to decide whether Paul does not need attention. The workshop description supplies a network of properties and conditional relationships among the carpenters, especially focusing on Paul. Our goal is to trace the logical consequences of those conditions to see if the negation of needing attention can be established.

<step n="1" type="premise">
<text>If Paul is not precise, then Paul needs attention.</text>
<logic>needs_attention(paul) :- ~precise(paul)</logic>
</step>

<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>If a carpenter is cooperative, then the carpenter is steady.</text>
<logic>steady(paul) :- cooperative(paul)</logic>
</step>

<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>If a carpenter is strong, then the carpenter is task_focused.</text>
<logic>task_focused(paul) :- strong(paul)</logic>
</step>

<step n="4" type="premise">
<text>If a carpenter is task_focused, then the carpenter is ready.</text>
<logic>ready(paul) :- task_focused(paul)</logic>
</step>

<step n="5" type="premise">
<text>If a carpenter is precise, then the carpenter is not ready.</text>
<logic>~ready(paul) :- precise(paul)</logic>
</step>

<step n="6" type="premise">
<text>Either Paul is not steady or Paul is strong.</text>
<logic>~steady(paul) v strong(paul)</logic>
</step>

<step n="7" type="premise">
<text>Anne is cooperative.</text>
<logic>cooperative(anne)</logic>
</step>

<step n="8" type="premise">
<text>Ian is cooperative.</text>
<logic>cooperative(ian)</logic>
</step>

<step n="9" type="premise">
<text>Anne is task_focused.</text>
<logic>task_focused(anne)</logic>
</step>

<step n="10" type="premise">
<text>Ian is task_focused.</text>
<logic>task_focused(ian)</logic>
</step>

<step n="11" type="premise">
<text>Paul is cooperative.</text>
<logic>cooperative(paul)</logic>
</step>

The premise tells us that any cooperative carpenter is steady. Since the context explicitly states that Paul is cooperative, we can directly apply this rule. This gives us the intermediate fact that Paul is steady, which will be useful for later reasoning about the disjunction involving steadiness.
<step n="12" type="derived" from="11,2">
<text>From Paul being cooperative and the rule that cooperative implies steady, we infer that Paul is steady.</text>
<logic>steady(paul)</logic>
</step>

The disjunction says either Paul is not steady or Paul is strong. Because we have just derived that Paul is steady, the first alternative cannot hold. By eliminating the impossible option, we are forced to accept the second alternative, namely that Paul is strong. This step follows the classic disjunctive syllogism pattern.
<step n="13" type="derived" from="12,6">
<text>Using the disjunction that either Paul is not steady or Paul is strong together with the fact that Paul is steady, we resolve that Paul must be strong.</text>
<logic>strong(paul)</logic>
</step>

One of the rules links strength to being task_focused: any strong carpenter becomes task_focused. Since we have established Paul’s strength, we can invoke this rule to infer that Paul is task_focused. This new property will allow us to move forward toward the readiness condition.
<step n="14" type="derived" from="13,3">
<text>Since Paul is strong, the rule that strong implies task_focused gives us that Paul is task_focused.</text>
<logic>task_focused(paul)</logic>
</step>

Another rule states that every task_focused carpenter is ready. With Paul now known to be task_focused, we can apply this implication directly, concluding that Paul is ready. This readiness will later be contrasted with the condition that precise carpenters cannot be ready.
<step n="15" type="derived" from="14,4">
<text>From Paul being task_focused and the rule that task_focused implies ready, we obtain that Paul is ready.</text>
<logic>ready(paul)</logic>
</step>

We have the rule that if a carpenter is precise, then they are not ready. We already know Paul is ready, which contradicts the conclusion that would follow from precision. By modus tollens, the only way to preserve consistency is to assert that Paul is not precise. Thus we derive the negation of precision for Paul.
<step n="16" type="derived" from="15,5">
<text>Because Paul is ready and any precise carpenter would not be ready, we conclude that Paul cannot be precise.</text>
<logic>~precise(paul)</logic>
</step>

The first premise connects the lack of precision to the need for attention: a carpenter who is not precise needs attention. Having just shown that Paul is not precise, we can apply this implication to obtain that Paul needs attention. This completes the chain of derivations leading to the positive assertion of needing attention.
<step n="17" type="derived" from="16,1">
<text>Given that Paul is not precise, the rule that not precise leads to needing attention yields that Paul needs attention.</text>
<logic>needs_attention(paul)</logic>
</step>

We derived Paul is needs attention, so the conclusion Paul is not needs attention is False.
</think>
Answer: False

examples.context: The prima danseuse is well_prepared for the upcoming recital. Whenever a dancer is well_prepared, she becomes stage_nervous. Any dancer who is stage_nervous is also bold. Being bold guarantees that the dancer is skillful. If a dancer possesses artistic_insight, then she cannot be skillful. Either the prima danseuse has artistic_insight or she is driven.
examples.question: Is the prima danseuse driven?
examples.completion:
<think>
We need to decide whether the prima danseuse possesses the quality of being driven. The proof will walk through the chain of conditions supplied in the context to reach that conclusion.

The first premise tells us that preparation leads to nervousness on stage. This connection is essential because it starts the cascade of attributes that will eventually affect the driven status. Recognizing this conditional will allow us to use the fact that the dancer is well_prepared.
<step n="1" type="premise">
<text>The rule that a well_prepared dancer becomes stage_nervous.</text>
<logic>stage_nervous(prima_danseuse) :- well_prepared(prima_danseuse)</logic>
</step>

The second premise links stage nervousness to boldness. It shows how an emotional state translates into a more assertive trait. This rule will be applied once we have established stage_nervousness.
<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>The rule that any stage_nervous dancer is bold.</text>
<logic>bold(prima_danseuse) :- stage_nervous(prima_danseuse)</logic>
</step>

The third premise states that boldness guarantees skillfulness. This bridge moves us from a personality trait to a performance capability, which will later interact with the insight rule. It prepares the ground for deriving skillful.
<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>The rule that boldness leads to being skillful.</text>
<logic>skillful(prima_danseuse) :- bold(prima_danseuse)</logic>
</step>

The fourth premise asserts that having artistic insight prevents a dancer from being skillful. It creates a contradiction between insight and skill, which will be crucial for eliminating the insight alternative later in the proof.
<step n="4" type="premise">
<text>The rule that artistic_insight implies not skillful.</text>
<logic>~skillful(prima_danseuse) :- artistic_insight(prima_danseuse)</logic>
</step>

The fifth premise offers a choice: the prima danseuse either has artistic insight or is driven. This disjunction is the final piece we need; once we can rule out artistic insight, the driven alternative will follow automatically.
<step n="5" type="premise">
<text>The statement that the prima danseuse is either artistic_insight or driven.</text>
<logic>artistic_insight(prima_danseuse) v driven(prima_danseuse)</logic>
</step>

The sixth premise gives us the concrete fact that the prima danseuse is well_prepared. This factual starting point activates the first conditional rule and initiates the logical chain toward the query.
<step n="6" type="premise">
<text>The fact that the prima danseuse is well_prepared.</text>
<logic>well_prepared(prima_danseuse)</logic>
</step>

By applying Modus Ponens to the fact that the dancer is well_prepared and the rule that preparation implies stage_nervousness, we conclude she is stage_nervous. This step translates the concrete preparation into the emotional condition needed for the next inference.
<step n="7" type="derived" from="6,1">
<text>From being well_prepared and the first rule, we infer that the prima danseuse is stage_nervous.</text>
<logic>stage_nervous(prima_danseuse)</logic>
</step>

Now that we know the dancer is stage_nervous, we can use the second rule. Modus Ponens again gives us boldness, because any stage_nervous dancer is bold. This moves the argument forward from emotion to character.
<step n="8" type="derived" from="7,2">
<text>Using the stage_nervous fact and the second rule, we conclude that the prima danseuse is bold.</text>
<logic>bold(prima_danseuse)</logic>
</step>

With boldness established, the third rule tells us that boldness leads to skillfulness. Applying Modus Ponens yields that the prima danseuse is skillful, a key property that will clash with artistic insight later.
<step n="9" type="derived" from="8,3">
<text>Applying the boldness fact with the third rule gives us that the prima danseuse is skillful.</text>
<logic>skillful(prima_danseuse)</logic>
</step>

We have the skillful fact, while the fourth rule says artistic_insight implies not skillful. Using Modus Tollens, the presence of skillful forces us to reject artistic_insight, giving us not artistic_insight. This eliminates one side of the disjunction.
<step n="10" type="derived" from="9,4">
<text>Since the prima danseuse is skillful, the fourth rule forces us to reject artistic_insight, so we have not artistic_insight.</text>
<logic>~artistic_insight(prima_danseuse)</logic>
</step>

Having shown that artistic_insight is false, we return to the disjunction that offered artistic_insight or driven. With the first option ruled out, Disjunctive Syllogism lets us conclude that the prima danseuse must be driven. This directly answers the query.
<step n="11" type="derived" from="10,5">
<text>With not artistic_insight and the disjunction, we eliminate the artistic_insight alternative and deduce that the prima danseuse is driven.</text>
<logic>driven(prima_danseuse)</logic>
</step>

Therefore Prima_danseuse is driven, so the conclusion is True.
</think>
Answer: True

examples.question: Is it true that queen_rook is not reliable?

The first rule connects decisiveness to being tactical. Recognizing this link will allow us to later turn a decisive status into a tactical one.

The fourth rule says that being sportsmanlike guarantees the piece is not tactical. This provides a way to infer the absence of sportsmanship from a tactical status.

The fifth rule indicates that methodical pieces are not focused. This will be employed after we discover the piece is focused.

The first disjunction offers two alternatives: either the piece is not aggressive or it is focused. This will let us eliminate one option once we have information about aggressiveness.

The second disjunction gives that either the piece is not aware or it is reliable. This will be decisive for establishing reliability once we know the piece's awareness.

The third disjunction says either the piece is not confident or it is decisive. Combined with the fact of confidence, it will let us infer decisiveness.

We have confidence and the disjunction that either not confident or decisive. Since the piece is indeed confident, the 'not confident' option is ruled out, leaving decisiveness as the only viable alternative. Therefore, queen_rook must be decisive.

Having established decisiveness, we can invoke the rule that decisiveness implies tactfulness. This direct implication yields that queen_rook is tactical.

Now that queen_rook is tactical, we look at the rule stating that a sportsmanlike piece cannot be tactical. The only way for a tactical piece to exist is if it is not sportsmanlike, so we conclude the piece lacks sportsmanship.

With the knowledge that queen_rook is not sportsmanlike, the rule linking lack of sportsmanship to aggressiveness applies. Consequently, the piece becomes aggressive.
<step n="13" type="derived" from="12,2">

The disjunction tells us the piece is either not aggressive or focused. Since we have just derived that the piece is aggressive, the 'not aggressive' alternative is impossible, forcing the piece to be focused.
<step n="14" type="derived" from="13,6">

examples.question: Is Cyrus loyal?
examples.completion:
<think>
We are asked to determine whether Cyrus is loyal based on the provided facts and logical relationships. The goal is to chain the information together until the loyalty of Cyrus can be established.

</step>

<logic>~healthy(cyrus) v competitive(cyrus)</logic>
</step>

</step>

<step n="8" type="premise">

<logic>creative(hope)</logic>
</step>

<step n="9" type="premise">
<text>Cyrus enjoys good health.</text>
<logic>healthy(cyrus)</logic>
</step>

We start with the fact that Cyrus is healthy. The disjunction "not healthy or competitive" tells us that at least one of those statements is true. Since "not healthy" is false (Cyrus is healthy), the only way for the disjunction to hold is for "competitive" to be true. Hence we derive that Cyrus is competitive.
<step n="10" type="derived" from="9,6">
<text>Because Cyrus is healthy and the statement that either he is not healthy or competitive is true, we can conclude that Cyrus must be competitive.</text>
<logic>competitive(cyrus)</logic>

Now that we know Cyrus is competitive, we can apply the conditional rule that competitiveness implies athleticism. This direct implication lets us infer that Cyrus must also be athletic.
<step n="11" type="derived" from="10,1">
<text>Given that Cyrus is competitive and the rule linking competitiveness to athleticism, we infer that Cyrus is athletic.</text>
<logic>athletic(cyrus)</logic>
</step>

The next piece is the disjunction "not athletic or efficient". Because we have just established that Cyrus is athletic, the "not athletic" part is false. To keep the disjunction true, the other alternative, "efficient", must be true. Therefore we conclude that Cyrus is efficient.
<step n="12" type="derived" from="11,5">
<text>Since Cyrus is athletic and we know that either he is not athletic or efficient, the only way to satisfy the disjunction is to accept that Cyrus is efficient.</text>
<logic>efficient(cyrus)</logic>
</step>

Having established Cyrus's efficiency, we turn to the rule that efficiency guarantees curiosity. By applying this rule, we obtain that Cyrus is curious.
<step n="13" type="derived" from="12,2">
<text>With Cyrus being efficient and the rule that efficiency leads to curiosity, we deduce that Cyrus is curious.</text>
<logic>curious(cyrus)</logic>
</step>

We now have Cyrus as curious, but we also have a rule stating that if Cyrus were active, he would be not curious. Since Cyrus is indeed curious, the antecedent of that rule (Cyrus being active) cannot hold; otherwise it would contradict the known curiosity. Consequently, we deduce that Cyrus is not active.
<step n="14" type="derived" from="13,3">

Finally, the disjunction "active or loyal" must be true for Cyrus. We have just shown that Cyrus is not active, so the only remaining way for the disjunction to be satisfied is for Cyrus to be loyal. Thus we infer that Cyrus is loyal.
<step n="15" type="derived" from="14,4">
<text>Because Cyrus is not active and the disjunction that either he is active or loyal must hold, we conclude that Cyrus is loyal.</text>
<logic>loyal(cyrus)</logic>

Therefore Cyrus is loyal, so the conclusion is True.

Orbital stability is achieved by a body whenever it has data collected. Lack of gravitational binding is inferred for a body whenever it has core fusion. <step n="15" type="derived" from="14,6">

examples.question: Is Jack focused?
examples.completion:
<think>
We are asked whether Jack possesses the property of being focused. To answer, we must chain together the available facts and conditional statements that connect Jack's known attributes to focus.

<step n="1" type="premise">
<text>Whenever Jack is proactive, Jack is cooperative.</text>
<logic>cooperative(jack) :- proactive(jack)</logic>
</step>

<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>Whenever Jack is cooperative, Jack is focused.</text>
<logic>focused(jack) :- cooperative(jack)</logic>
</step>

<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>If Jack is principled, then Jack is judicious.</text>
<logic>judicious(jack) :- principled(jack)</logic>
</step>

<step n="4" type="premise">
<text>If Jack is judicious, then Jack is proactive.</text>
<logic>proactive(jack) :- judicious(jack)</logic>
</step>

<step n="5" type="premise">
<text>If Jack is courageous, then Jack is trustworthy.</text>
<logic>trustworthy(jack) :- courageous(jack)</logic>
</step>

<step n="6" type="premise">
<text>Either Jack is not trustworthy or Jack is principled.</text>
<logic>~trustworthy(jack) v principled(jack)</logic>
</step>

<step n="7" type="premise">
<text>Cole is attentive.</text>
<logic>attentive(cole)</logic>
</step>

<step n="8" type="premise">
<text>Jack is courageous.</text>
<logic>courageous(jack)</logic>
</step>

We start with the fact that Jack is courageous. The rule that courageous individuals are trustworthy allows us to apply modus ponens, turning the courage fact into a trustworthiness conclusion. This gives us a solid base for the next inference.
<step n="9" type="derived" from="8,5">
<text>Because Jack is courageous and courage guarantees trustworthiness, we infer that Jack is trustworthy.</text>
<logic>trustworthy(jack)</logic>
</step>

Now we have Jack as trustworthy, and we also know the disjunction: either Jack is not trustworthy or Jack is principled. Because the first part of the disjunction is false (Jack is trustworthy), the only way the statement can hold is if the second part is true, so Jack must be principled. This uses disjunctive syllogism to extract the principled property.
<step n="10" type="derived" from="9,6">
<text>Since Jack is trustworthy, the disjunction forces the other alternative, so Jack must be principled.</text>
<logic>principled(jack)</logic>
</step>

Having established that Jack is principled, we can invoke the rule that principled people are judicious. Applying modus ponens again, we infer that Jack is judicious. This moves us one step closer to the chain that eventually leads to focus.
<step n="11" type="derived" from="10,3">
<text>Being principled makes Jack judicious, so we conclude Jack is judicious.</text>
<logic>judicious(jack)</logic>
</step>

Judiciousness triggers proactivity according to the next rule. With Jack now judged as judicious, we deduce that he is proactive. This continues the forward reasoning chain toward the target property.
<step n="12" type="derived" from="11,4">
<text>Judiciousness leads to proactivity, therefore Jack is proactive.</text>
<logic>proactive(jack)</logic>
</step>

The rule linking proactivity to cooperativeness tells us that any proactive individual is also cooperative. Since Jack is proactive, we can confidently assert that Jack is cooperative.
<step n="13" type="derived" from="12,1">
<text>Since Jack is proactive, he must be cooperative.</text>
<logic>cooperative(jack)</logic>
</step>

Finally, the rule that cooperative individuals are focused lets us conclude that Jack is focused. By chaining the earlier deductions, we have arrived at the desired conclusion.
<step n="14" type="derived" from="13,2">
<text>Cooperativeness implies focus, so Jack is focused.</text>
<logic>focused(jack)</logic>
</step>

Therefore Jack is focused, so the conclusion is True.
</think>
Answer: True

The fact that Harper is fit is crucial because it activates the rule that fit climbers become equipped. This gives us the starting point for the derivation.

examples.context: Bellamy is fit. Whenever someone is fit, they are humble, so Bellamy being fit makes him humble. If someone is witty then they are courageous; likewise, if someone is cheerful they are witty, and if someone is resilient they are resourceful. Insightfulness would force a person not to be humble, while being strategic would force a person not to be resourceful. Either Bellamy is insightful or he is resilient, and either he is strategic or he is cheerful. These are all the facts and rules that govern Bellamy's character.
examples.question: Based on the given information, is Bellamy courageous?
examples.completion:
<think>
We are given a collection of facts and conditional statements about Bellamy's traits. Our goal is to determine whether the information logically forces Bellamy to be courageous.

<step n="1" type="premise">
<text>Whenever Bellamy is witty, he is courageous.</text>
<logic>courageous(bellamy) :- witty(bellamy)</logic>
</step>

<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>If Bellamy is fit, then he is humble.</text>
<logic>humble(bellamy) :- fit(bellamy)</logic>
</step>

<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>If Bellamy is resilient, then he is resourceful.</text>
<logic>resourceful(bellamy) :- resilient(bellamy)</logic>
</step>

<step n="4" type="premise">
<text>If Bellamy is cheerful, then he is witty.</text>
<logic>witty(bellamy) :- cheerful(bellamy)</logic>
</step>

<step n="5" type="premise">
<text>If Bellamy is insightful, then he is not humble.</text>
<logic>~humble(bellamy) :- insightful(bellamy)</logic>
</step>

<step n="6" type="premise">
<text>If Bellamy is strategic, then he is not resourceful.</text>
<logic>~resourceful(bellamy) :- strategic(bellamy)</logic>
</step>

<step n="7" type="premise">
<text>Either Bellamy is insightful or Bellamy is resilient.</text>
<logic>insightful(bellamy) v resilient(bellamy)</logic>
</step>

<step n="8" type="premise">
<text>Either Bellamy is strategic or Bellamy is cheerful.</text>
<logic>strategic(bellamy) v cheerful(bellamy)</logic>
</step>

<step n="9" type="premise">
<text>Bellamy is fit.</text>
<logic>fit(bellamy)</logic>
</step>

We start with the explicit fact that Bellamy is fit. One of the rules tells us that any fit person is humble, so by applying modus ponens we can assert that Bellamy is indeed humble. This gives us a concrete property to work with in later steps.
<step n="10" type="derived" from="9,2">
<text>From Bellamy being fit and the rule that fit implies humble, we infer that Bellamy is humble.</text>
<logic>humble(bellamy)</logic>
</step>

Now we have Bellamy as humble, but there is a rule stating that if someone were insightful they would not be humble. Since Bellamy is already humble, the only way to avoid a contradiction is to conclude that Bellamy cannot be insightful. This is an instance of modus tollens, turning the conditional around.
<step n="11" type="derived" from="10,5">
<text>Since Bellamy is humble and the rule that insightfulness would make him not humble, we conclude Bellamy cannot be insightful.</text>
<logic>~insightful(bellamy)</logic>
</step>

We have just derived that Bellamy is not insightful. The disjunction supplied says Bellamy is either insightful or resilient. Because the insightful alternative is false, the remaining option must be true, so Bellamy must be resilient. This follows the principle of disjunctive syllogism.
<step n="12" type="derived" from="11,7">
<text>Given that Bellamy is not insightful and that either he is insightful or resilient, we deduce he must be resilient.</text>
<logic>resilient(bellamy)</logic>
</step>

Having established that Bellamy is resilient, we can invoke the rule that resilience guarantees resourcefulness. By modus ponens again, we deduce that Bellamy is resourceful. This adds another positive attribute to his profile.
<step n="13" type="derived" from="12,3">
<text>Because Bellamy is resilient and resilience leads to being resourceful, we infer he is resourceful.</text>
<logic>resourceful(bellamy)</logic>
</step>

There is a rule linking strategic behavior to the negation of resourcefulness. Since we already know Bellamy is resourceful, the antecedent of that rule (being strategic) cannot hold without causing inconsistency. Therefore, we infer that Bellamy is not strategic, using modus tollens.
<step n="14" type="derived" from="13,6">
<text>Knowing Bellamy is resourceful and that being strategic would make him not resourceful, we infer Bellamy is not strategic.</text>
<logic>~strategic(bellamy)</logic>
</step>

The remaining disjunction tells us Bellamy is either strategic or cheerful. Because we have just concluded he is not strategic, the only viable alternative is that he is cheerful. This again is a disjunctive syllogism, allowing us to assert Bellamy's cheerfulness.
<step n="15" type="derived" from="14,8">
<text>Since Bellamy is not strategic and either strategic or cheerful holds, we determine Bellamy is cheerful.</text>
<logic>cheerful(bellamy)</logic>
</step>

With Bellamy confirmed as cheerful, we turn to the conditional that cheerfulness leads to wit. Applying modus ponens, we obtain that Bellamy is witty. This gives us the final link needed for courage.
<step n="16" type="derived" from="15,4">
<text>From Bellamy being cheerful and the rule that cheerfulness leads to wit, we get that Bellamy is witty.</text>
<logic>witty(bellamy)</logic>
</step>

Finally, the rule that any witty individual is courageous lets us conclude Bellamy's courage. Since we have already proven Bellamy is witty, modus ponens yields the desired result: Bellamy is courageous.
<step n="17" type="derived" from="16,1">
<text>With Bellamy witty and the rule that wit leads to courage, we conclude Bellamy is courageous.</text>
<logic>courageous(bellamy)</logic>
</step>

Therefore Bellamy is courageous, so the conclusion is True.
</think>
Answer: True

The disjunction offers two possibilities for the storm_center: it is either fast_moving or ready. By ruling out one option we can deduce the other.

The fact that the high_pressure is stable is part of the broader knowledge base but does not directly affect the storm_center chain; it simply satisfies one of the given facts.

The fact that the rain_cloud shows a creative_pattern is another standalone fact that fulfills the list of premises, though it is not needed for the target query.

We are told explicitly that the storm_center is energized, which activates the rule in PREMISE_1 and starts the reasoning process toward predictiveness.
<step n="9" type="premise">

<logic>energized(storm_center)</logic>
</step>

Using the fact that the storm_center is energized (PREMISE_9) together with the rule that energized implies concentrated (PREMISE_1), we can conclude the storm_center is concentrated. This is a straightforward application of modus ponens.
<step n="10" type="derived" from="9,1">

<logic>concentrated(storm_center)</logic>
</step>

Now that we have the storm_center concentrated (STEP_10), the rule that concentration leads to lasting (PREMISE_3) lets us infer that the storm_center is lasting. Again, this follows by modus ponens.
<step n="11" type="derived" from="10,3">

<logic>lasting(storm_center)</logic>

With the storm_center lasting (STEP_11) and the rule that lasting implies clever (PREMISE_4), we deduce that the storm_center is clever. This continues the chain of implications.

We know the storm_center is clever (STEP_12). The rule that fast_moving would make the storm_center not clever (PREMISE_5) therefore cannot hold, because it would contradict the established cleverness. By modus tollens we infer that the storm_center is not fast_moving.

The disjunction says the storm_center is either fast_moving or ready (PREMISE_6). Since we have just shown it is not fast_moving (STEP_13), the only remaining option is that it must be ready. This is an application of disjunctive syllogism.

If a chef has bold flavor, then the ingredients are fresh, meaning bold_flavor implies fresh_ingredients. Hopeful spice guarantees precise_measure, so any chef who is hopeful_spice will also be precise_measure. A seasoned chef leads to lingering_aroma, therefore seasoned implies lingering_aroma. High heat follows from being marinated, so marinated chefs become high_heat. Exact cuts result from high heat, meaning high_heat implies exact_cut. Either Chef Ivy does not have fresh_ingredients or Chef Ivy is robust. Either Chef Felix is not precise_measure or Chef Felix is seasoned. Either Chef Lee does not have an exact_cut or Chef Lee is a seasoned_chef.
examples.question: Can we conclude that Chef Lee is not a seasoned chef?
examples.completion:
<think>
We are asked whether we can assert that Chef Lee is not a seasoned chef. To answer, we need to see what the premises tell us about Chef Lee's culinary status, especially regarding the seasoned_chef property.

The first premise gives a direct conditional link: any chef who is high_heat must also have an exact_cut. This will let us move from high heat to a concrete preparation quality later in the proof.

<logic>exact_cut(chef_lee) :- high_heat(chef_lee)</logic>
</step>

The second premise connects being marinated to high heat. Since we already know Chef Lee is marinated, this rule will let us obtain high_heat for Chef Lee, setting up the chain toward exact_cut.

<text>The rule states that being marinated guarantees that the chef will be high_heat.</text>
<logic>high_heat(chef_lee) :- marinated(chef_lee)</logic>
</step>

The third premise is a disjunction that says either Chef Lee does not have an exact_cut or Chef Lee is a seasoned_chef. Once we can establish that Chef Lee indeed has an exact_cut, the first part of the disjunction will be false, forcing the second part—seasoned_chef—to be true.
<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>Either Chef Lee lacks an exact_cut or Chef Lee is a seasoned_chef.</text>
<logic>~exact_cut(chef_lee) v seasoned_chef(chef_lee)</logic>
</step>

The fact that Chef Ivy has bold_flavor is recorded, though it does not directly affect the reasoning about Chef Lee. It is included to satisfy the requirement that every fact be mentioned in the context.
<step n="4" type="premise">
<text>It is a fact that Chef Ivy has bold_flavor.</text>
<logic>bold_flavor(chef_ivy)</logic>
</step>

Similarly, Chef Felix's hopeful_spice is a factual statement that appears in the context but does not play a role in the current derivation about Chef Lee.
<step n="5" type="premise">
<text>It is a fact that Chef Felix has hopeful_spice.</text>
<logic>hopeful_spice(chef_felix)</logic>
</step>

Chef Lee being marinated is a crucial fact because, together with Premise 2, it allows us to derive high_heat for Chef Lee, beginning the chain toward the conclusion we need.
<step n="6" type="premise">
<text>It is a fact that Chef Lee is marinated.</text>
<logic>marinated(chef_lee)</logic>
</step>

Combining the fact that Chef Lee is marinated (Premise 6) with the rule that marinated implies high_heat (Premise 2), we can infer that Chef Lee is high_heat. This is a straightforward application of modus ponens, moving one step forward in the logical chain.
<step n="7" type="derived" from="6,2">
<text>From Chef Lee being marinated and the rule that marinated implies high_heat, we infer that Chef Lee is high_heat.</text>
<logic>high_heat(chef_lee)</logic>
</step>

Now that we have established Chef Lee is high_heat, we can apply the rule from Premise 1, which says high_heat leads to exact_cut. By modus ponens again, we conclude that Chef Lee possesses an exact_cut.
<step n="8" type="derived" from="7,1">
<text>Using Chef Lee's high_heat and the rule that high_heat implies exact_cut, we derive that Chef Lee has an exact_cut.</text>
<logic>exact_cut(chef_lee)</logic>
</step>

Having proven that Chef Lee has an exact_cut, we look back at the disjunction in Premise 3: "not exact_cut or seasoned_chef." Since the left side (not exact_cut) is false, the only way for the disjunction to hold is for the right side, seasoned_chef, to be true. Thus we derive that Chef Lee is a seasoned_chef using disjunctive syllogism.
<step n="9" type="derived" from="8,3">
<text>Given that Chef Lee has an exact_cut and the disjunction "not exact_cut or seasoned_chef", we conclude that Chef Lee is a seasoned_chef.</text>
<logic>seasoned_chef(chef_lee)</logic>
</step>

We derived Chef_lee is seasoned chef, so the conclusion Chef_lee is not seasoned chef is False.
</think>
Answer: False

examples.question: Can we conclude that Helen is not industrious?

The first rule connects responsibility to eloquence. It will become useful later once we can establish Helen's responsibility, allowing us to obtain her eloquence, which is needed for the final contradiction with industriousness.
<step n="1" type="premise">
<text>If Helen is responsible, then she is eloquent.</text>
<logic>eloquent(helen) :- responsible(helen)</logic>
</step>

The second rule tells us that vigor guarantees experience. Since we already know Helen is vigorous, this rule gives us a direct route to the experienced predicate, which will be pivotal for eliminating the possibility of wit.
<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>If Helen is vigorous, then she is experienced.</text>
<logic>experienced(helen) :- vigorous(helen)</logic>
</step>

The third rule links energetic behavior to patience. This conditional will be activated after we discover Helen is energetic, enabling us to move toward responsibility.
<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>If Helen is energetic, then she is patient.</text>
<logic>patient(helen) :- energetic(helen)</logic>
</step>

The fourth rule states that patience implies responsibility. Once we have patience, we can climb the ladder to responsibility, then to eloquence, setting up the final step.
<step n="4" type="premise">
<text>If Helen is patient, then she is responsible.</text>
<logic>responsible(helen) :- patient(helen)</logic>
</step>

The fifth rule provides the crucial negative link: if Helen were industrious, she would not be eloquent. This is the key to deriving ~industrious once we have established eloquence.
<step n="5" type="premise">
<text>If Helen is industrious, then she is not eloquent.</text>
<logic>~eloquent(helen) :- industrious(helen)</logic>
</step>

The sixth rule says that wit prevents experience. By showing Helen is experienced, we can use this rule in a contrapositive way to rule out wit.
<step n="6" type="premise">
<text>If Helen is witty, then she is not experienced.</text>
<logic>~experienced(helen) :- witty(helen)</logic>
</step>

The disjunction tells us that at least one of wit or energetic holds for Helen. This will allow us, after eliminating wit, to infer that energetic must be true.
<step n="7" type="premise">
<text>Helen is either witty or energetic.</text>
<logic>witty(helen) v energetic(helen)</logic>
</step>

The fact that Helen is vigorous is our starting point for the chain of deductions; it activates the vigor‑to‑experience rule.
<step n="8" type="premise">
<text>Helen is vigorous.</text>
<logic>vigorous(helen)</logic>
</step>

Applying modus ponens to the fact of vigor and the rule that vigor implies experience gives us the conclusion that Helen is experienced. This new fact will later help us eliminate the possibility that Helen is witty.
<step n="9" type="derived" from="8,2">
<text>From Helen being vigorous and the rule that vigor implies experience, we infer that Helen is experienced.</text>
<logic>experienced(helen)</logic>
</step>

Since we now know Helen is experienced, the contrapositive of the rule 'wit implies not‑experience' lets us infer that Helen cannot be witty; otherwise she would not be experienced. This step uses modus tollens.
<step n="10" type="derived" from="9,6">
<text>Because Helen is experienced and the rule that wit implies not‑experience, we deduce that Helen cannot be witty.</text>
<logic>~witty(helen)</logic>
</step>

With the knowledge that Helen is not witty and the original disjunction that she is either witty or energetic, the only remaining option is that she is energetic. This follows by disjunctive syllogism.
<step n="11" type="derived" from="10,7">
<text>Having ruled out wit and knowing Helen is either witty or energetic, we conclude that Helen must be energetic.</text>
<logic>energetic(helen)</logic>
</step>

Having established Helen's energetic nature, we invoke the rule that energetic leads to patient. Modus ponens yields that Helen is patient, moving us closer to responsibility.
<step n="12" type="derived" from="11,3">
<text>Since Helen is energetic and energetic leads to patient, we obtain that Helen is patient.</text>
<logic>patient(helen)</logic>
</step>

From Helen being patient and the rule that patient implies responsibility, we can again apply modus ponens to obtain that Helen is responsible.
<step n="13" type="derived" from="12,4">
<text>From Helen being patient and the rule that patient leads to responsible, we infer that Helen is responsible.</text>
<logic>responsible(helen)</logic>
</step>

Now that Helen is responsible, the earlier rule connecting responsibility to eloquence lets us deduce that Helen is eloquent. This is a direct application of modus ponens.
<step n="14" type="derived" from="13,1">
<text>Because Helen is responsible and responsibility leads to eloquence, we derive that Helen is eloquent.</text>
<logic>eloquent(helen)</logic>
</step>

We now have Helen being eloquent. The rule stating that industriousness would make her not eloquent creates a conflict: if Helen were industrious, she would have to be not eloquent, contradicting what we just derived. Using modus tollens, we conclude that Helen cannot be industrious.
<step n="15" type="derived" from="14,5">
<text>Given Helen's eloquence and the rule that industriousness would make her not eloquent, we infer that Helen cannot be industrious.</text>
<logic>~industrious(helen)</logic>
</step>

Therefore Helen is not industrious, so the conclusion is True.
</think>
Answer: True

examples.context: The flight plan is clear. If a flight plan is clear, then it follows the standard procedure. Normally, a flight plan that follows the standard procedure and is not known to be abnormal for coordination will be coordinated. Normally, a coordinated flight plan that is not known to be abnormal for being on course will be on course.
examples.question: Can we conclude that the flight plan is not on course?
examples.completion:
<think>
We are asked to determine whether the flight plan fails to be on course. The premises give us a clear fact about the plan and several defeasible rules that can propagate properties, so we need to trace how those rules interact.

The first premise links the property of being clear to following the standard procedure. This conditional will let us move from the known fact of clarity to a new property, which is essential for later defaults.
<step n="1" type="premise">
<text>The rule states that any clear flight plan follows the standard procedure.</text>
<logic>standard_procedure(flight_plan) :- clear(flight_plan)</logic>
</step>

The second premise is a default: if a plan is coordinated and there is no abnormality that blocks being on course, then it is on course. This tells us how the on‑course status can be obtained, provided we can establish coordination first.
<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>The default rule says that a coordinated flight plan without an abnormality preventing it from being on course will be on course.</text>
<logic>on_course(flight_plan) :- coordinated(flight_plan), not ab_on_course(flight_plan)</logic>
</step>

The third premise is another default: if a plan follows the standard procedure and there is no abnormality preventing coordination, then it becomes coordinated. This bridges the gap between the standard‑procedure property and the coordinated property needed for the previous default.
<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>The default rule says that a flight plan that follows the standard procedure and has no abnormality preventing coordination will be coordinated.</text>
<logic>coordinated(flight_plan) :- standard_procedure(flight_plan), not ab_coordinated(flight_plan)</logic>
</step>

The fourth premise gives us the concrete fact that the flight plan is clear. This is the starting point for all forward reasoning, because the rule in PREMISE_1 uses exactly this condition.
<step n="4" type="premise">
<text>It is given that the flight plan is clear.</text>
<logic>clear(flight_plan)</logic>
</step>

Using the fact that the plan is clear together with the rule from PREMISE_1, we can directly infer that the plan follows the standard procedure. This is a straightforward application of modus ponens: the antecedent holds, so the consequent must hold as well.
<step n="5" type="derived" from="4,1">
<text>From the fact that the flight plan is clear and the rule linking clarity to standard procedure, we infer that the flight plan follows the standard procedure.</text>
<logic>standard_procedure(flight_plan)</logic>
</step>

Now that we know the plan follows the standard procedure, the default in PREMISE_3 allows us to conclude it is coordinated, assuming no abnormality for coordination. With the coordinated status in hand, the default in PREMISE_2 then yields that the plan is on course, again assuming no abnormality for being on course. Thus the chain of defaults produces the on‑course conclusion.
<step n="6" type="derived" from="5,3,2">
<text>Applying the two defaults in sequence, the standard‑procedure flight plan is seen as coordinated, and the coordinated plan (with no abnormality) is therefore on course, so we derive that the flight plan is on course.</text>
<logic>on_course(flight_plan)</logic>
</step>

We derived Flight_plan is on course, so the conclusion Flight_plan is not on course is False.
</think>
Answer: False

examples.context: Apollo is clever. Whenever something is clever, it is also insightful. Any insightful being is zealous. If something is hopeful, then it is thoughtful. If something is heroic, then it is not thoughtful. Either Apollo is not zealous or Apollo is hopeful.
examples.question: Is Apollo heroic?
examples.completion:
<think>
We are given several attributes of the figure Apollo and a handful of conditional relationships among those attributes. Our goal is to determine whether Apollo can be considered heroic according to the information provided.

The first rule connects insightfulness to zeal, which will become useful once we know whether Apollo is insightful. Knowing that insight leads to zeal helps us later resolve the disjunctive statement about Apollo's zeal.
<step n="1" type="premise">
<text>The rule that any insightful entity is zealous.</text>
<logic>zealous(apollo) :- insightful(apollo)</logic>
</step>

The second rule tells us that cleverness guarantees insightfulness. Since we already have a fact that Apollo is clever, this rule will let us derive his insightfulness, an essential link in the chain toward zeal.
<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>The rule that any clever entity is insightful.</text>
<logic>insightful(apollo) :- clever(apollo)</logic>
</step>

The third rule links hopefulness to thoughtfulness. This will be needed after we establish whether Apollo is hopeful, allowing us to later confront the rule that heroic beings are not thoughtful.
<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>The rule that any hopeful entity is thoughtful.</text>
<logic>thoughtful(apollo) :- hopeful(apollo)</logic>
</step>

The fourth rule states that any heroic individual cannot be thoughtful. This negative connection is crucial for showing that if Apollo ends up thoughtful, he cannot be heroic, which directly addresses our query.
<step n="4" type="premise">
<text>The rule that any heroic entity is not thoughtful.</text>
<logic>~thoughtful(apollo) :- heroic(apollo)</logic>
</step>

The disjunction says that either Apollo lacks zeal or he is hopeful. This logical alternative will let us infer hopefulness once we can confirm that Apollo does possess zeal, because the first alternative would then be false.
<step n="5" type="premise">
<text>The statement that either Apollo is not zealous or Apollo is hopeful.</text>
<logic>~zealous(apollo) v hopeful(apollo)</logic>
</step>

We are told outright that Apollo is clever. This concrete fact is the starting point for a series of deductions that will eventually allow us to test the heroic claim.
<step n="6" type="premise">
<text>Apollo is clever.</text>
<logic>clever(apollo)</logic>
</step>

Using the fact that Apollo is clever together with the rule that cleverness implies insightfulness, we can conclude that Apollo is insightful. This step bridges the concrete fact to the first conditional rule, giving us the insight needed for the next inference.
<step n="7" type="derived" from="6,2">
<text>From Apollo being clever and the clever‑to‑insightful rule we infer that Apollo is insightful.</text>
<logic>insightful(apollo)</logic>
</step>

Having established Apollo's insightfulness, we apply the rule that any insightful being is zealous, yielding that Apollo is zealous. This deduction moves us closer to the disjunctive premise, where the presence of zeal will force the other disjunct to be true.
<step n="8" type="derived" from="7,1">
<text>Since Apollo is insightful and insightful implies zealous, we conclude Apollo is zealous.</text>
<logic>zealous(apollo)</logic>
</step>

Since we now know Apollo is zealous, the disjunction "either not zealous or hopeful" cannot be satisfied by the first option; therefore, the second option must hold, and Apollo is hopeful. This use of disjunctive syllogism converts the earlier uncertainty into a definite hopeful status.
<step n="9" type="derived" from="8,5">
<text>Given Apollo is zealous and the disjunction that either not zealous or hopeful, we deduce Apollo must be hopeful.</text>
<logic>hopeful(apollo)</logic>
</step>

With Apollo established as hopeful, the rule linking hopefulness to thoughtfulness lets us infer that Apollo is thoughtful. This new property is essential because it directly conflicts with the condition that heroic individuals are not thoughtful.
<step n="10" type="derived" from="9,3">
<text>From Apollo being hopeful and hopeful implies thoughtful, we infer Apollo is thoughtful.</text>
<logic>thoughtful(apollo)</logic>
</step>

We have Apollo being thoughtful, and the rule that any heroic entity is not thoughtful tells us that if Apollo were heroic, he would have to be not thoughtful. Since he is thoughtful, the only consistent conclusion is that Apollo is not heroic, i.e., ~heroic(Apollo).
<step n="11" type="derived" from="10,4">
<text>Because Apollo is thoughtful and the heroic‑to‑not‑thoughtful rule holds, we infer that Apollo cannot be heroic.</text>
<logic>~heroic(apollo)</logic>
</step>

We derived Apollo is not heroic, so the conclusion Apollo is heroic is False.
</think>
Answer: False

examples.context: The white knight is aggressive. The black queen is aggressive, and the black queen is also watchful. The white bishop is determined, which makes it elegant, and if the white bishop were bold it would be studious. The black rook is respectful, and any respectful black rook is methodical. Whenever a piece is patient, it becomes watchful, and aggression guarantees patience. Any watchful piece is reliable. If the black rook were cooperative, it would be confident. Either the white bishop is not elegant or it is bold, and either the black rook is not methodical or it is cooperative.
examples.question: Is it true that the white knight is not reliable?

The black queen being watchful is another factual detail that fulfills the completeness condition for the context paragraph.

The black rook's respectfulness is recorded to cover all given facts; it will not be used in the derivation concerning the white knight.
<step n="7" type="premise">

<logic>respectful(black_rook)</logic>
</step>

Restating that the white knight is aggressive reinforces the premise needed for the aggression‑to‑patience rule and makes the chain of inference explicit.
<step n="8" type="premise">

From the aggressive nature of the white knight (Premise 8) and the rule that aggression implies patience (Premise 2), we can conclude the knight is patient. This step establishes the first link in the chain toward reliability.

Now that we have patient(white_knight) from Step 9, we apply the rule that patient pieces are watchful (Premise 1). This yields watchful(white_knight), moving us closer to the final goal.

<logic>watchful(white_knight)</logic>
</step>

With watchful(white_knight) in hand, we invoke the rule that any watchful piece is reliable (Premise 3). Consequently, the white knight is reliable, directly contradicting the claim that it is not.
<step n="11" type="derived" from="10,3">
<text>Given that the white knight is watchful and every watchful piece is reliable, the white knight is reliable.</text>

examples.context: In the city planning office, Briar is known to be capable, and Frankie is known to be knowledgeable. Whenever someone is capable, they are also cheerful, so any capable person is cheerful. Whenever someone is cheerful, they are empathetic. Whenever someone is resilient, they become witty. If someone is content, then they are not witty. If someone is knowledgeable, they are relaxed. If someone is fair_minded, they are supportive. Additionally, either Briar is not empathetic or Briar is resilient. Also, either Frankie is not relaxed or Frankie is fair_minded.
examples.question: Can we conclude that Briar is not content?
examples.completion:
<think>
Our aim is to determine whether Briar can be shown to be not content. To reach this, we will chain together the given facts and conditional statements that connect capability, cheerfulness, empathy, resilience, and wit.

The rule linking capability to cheerfulness is crucial because it lets us move from the known fact that Briar is capable to a new property, cheerfulness. This connection will be the first step in building our chain of inference.
<step n="1" type="premise">
<text>If Briar is capable, then Briar is cheerful.</text>
<logic>cheerful(briar) :- capable(briar)</logic>
</step>

The second rule tells us that cheerfulness guarantees empathy. Once we have established Briar's cheerfulness, this rule will allow us to infer that Briar is empathetic, moving us closer to the disjunction that involves resilience.
<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>If Briar is cheerful, then Briar is empathetic.</text>
<logic>empathetic(briar) :- cheerful(briar)</logic>
</step>

The third rule converts resilience into wit. Later, when we manage to prove Briar is resilient, we will be able to claim that Briar is witty, which is needed for the final contradiction regarding content.
<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>If Briar is resilient, then Briar is witty.</text>
<logic>witty(briar) :- resilient(briar)</logic>
</step>

The fourth rule states that if Briar were content, then Briar would not be witty. This contrapositive relationship will let us infer the negation of content once we have established wit.
<step n="4" type="premise">
<text>If Briar is content, then Briar is not witty.</text>
<logic>~witty(briar) :- content(briar)</logic>
</step>

The disjunction gives us two alternatives: either Briar lacks empathy or Briar possesses resilience. Since we will soon know that Briar is empathetic, the only way for the disjunction to remain true is for Briar to be resilient.
<step n="5" type="premise">
<text>Either Briar is not empathetic or Briar is resilient.</text>
<logic>~empathetic(briar) v resilient(briar)</logic>
</step>

Frankie's knowledge is recorded as a fact, though it does not directly affect Briar's status. It is included for completeness of the scenario.
<step n="6" type="premise">
<text>Frankie is knowledgeable.</text>
<logic>knowledgeable(frankie)</logic>
</step>

The fact that Briar is capable provides the starting point for the chain of deductions. From this, we will activate the first rule to obtain cheerfulness.
<step n="7" type="premise">
<text>Briar is capable.</text>
<logic>capable(briar)</logic>
</step>

Applying the rule from PREMISE_1 to the known fact that Briar is capable yields Briar's cheerfulness. This is a straightforward use of Modus Ponens, converting a premise into a new attribute.
<step n="8" type="derived" from="7,1">
<text>From Briar being capable and the rule that capable implies cheerful, we infer that Briar is cheerful.</text>
<logic>cheerful(briar)</logic>
</step>

With Briar now known to be cheerful, the rule from PREMISE_2 lets us infer that Briar is empathetic. This step continues the forward chaining from capability to empathy.
<step n="9" type="derived" from="8,2">
<text>Using Briar's cheerfulness and the rule that cheerful implies empathetic, we infer that Briar is empathetic.</text>
<logic>empathetic(briar)</logic>
</step>

We have Briar's empathy, but the disjunction from PREMISE_5 says either not empathetic or resilient. Since Briar is indeed empathetic, the first option is false, so the second must hold: Briar is resilient. This is a classic disjunctive syllogism.
<step n="10" type="derived" from="9,5">
<text>Given that Briar is empathetic and the disjunction that either not empathetic or resilient holds, we conclude that Briar must be resilient.</text>
<logic>resilient(briar)</logic>
</step>

Now that Briar is resilient, the rule from PREMISE_3 converts that into wit. Thus we conclude Briar is witty, preparing us to use the contrapositive rule about content.
<step n="11" type="derived" from="10,3">
<text>Since Briar is resilient and resilience leads to wit, we infer that Briar is witty.</text>
<logic>witty(briar)</logic>
</step>

Having established that Briar is witty, we look at PREMISE_4, which says content would force not witty. Because we have wit, the only way to avoid contradiction is to deny the antecedent, leading to the conclusion that Briar is not content.
<step n="12" type="derived" from="11,4">
<text>Because Briar is witty and the rule that content leads to not witty, we deduce that Briar cannot be content.</text>
<logic>~content(briar)</logic>
</step>

Therefore Briar is not content, so the conclusion is True.
</think>
Answer: True

examples.context: The queen bee is industrious. Whenever the queen bee is obedient, she becomes valiant. If the queen bee is valiant, then she is trustworthy. When the queen bee is ready, she is not productive. If the queen bee is wise, she is not active. The queen bee is either wise or obedient. examples.question: Based on the information provided, can we determine whether the queen bee is strong?

The second rule links valiance to trustworthiness, so any conclusion about the queen bee being obedient will also give us information about her trustworthiness. This chain of implications could be useful when we consider the disjunction involving trustworthiness.

The third rule states that readiness forces the queen bee to be non‑productive. Although we do not yet know whether she is ready, this conditional will be relevant if the readiness condition ever becomes established.

The fact that the queen bee is industrious gives us a concrete starting point. This fact will interact with the disjunction that mentions either non‑industriousness or activity, allowing us to draw further conclusions about her activity level.

examples.context: Within the hive, the elder bee is guarded. The forager bee is learned. Any guarded bee is purposeful. Any efficient bee is innovative. If a bee is guarded, it becomes purposeful. If a bee is efficient, it becomes innovative. Whenever a bee is learned, it also shows teamwork. Being faithful guarantees boldness. A well_rest bee makes quick_decision. If a bee makes quick_decision, it is faithful. Boldness leads to methodical behavior. Either the forager bee does not show teamwork, or it is well_rest. Either the elder bee is not purposeful, or it is efficient.
examples.question: Does the forager bee behave methodically?
examples.completion:
<think>
We are given several characteristics of two bees in the hive and a set of conditional statements linking those characteristics. Our goal is to determine whether the forager bee can be shown to be methodical.

<step n="1" type="premise">
<text>Any learned bee shows teamwork.</text>
<logic>teamwork(forager_bee) :- learned(forager_bee)</logic>
</step>

<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>Being faithful guarantees boldness.</text>
<logic>bold(forager_bee) :- faithful(forager_bee)</logic>
</step>

<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>A well_rest bee makes quick_decision.</text>
<logic>quick_decision(forager_bee) :- well_rest(forager_bee)</logic>
</step>

<step n="4" type="premise">
<text>If a bee makes quick_decision, it is faithful.</text>
<logic>faithful(forager_bee) :- quick_decision(forager_bee)</logic>
</step>

<step n="5" type="premise">
<text>Boldness leads to methodical behavior.</text>
<logic>methodical(forager_bee) :- bold(forager_bee)</logic>
</step>

<step n="6" type="premise">
<text>Either the forager bee does not show teamwork, or it is well_rest.</text>
<logic>~teamwork(forager_bee) v well_rest(forager_bee)</logic>
</step>

<step n="7" type="premise">
<text>The elder bee is guarded.</text>
<logic>guarded(elder_bee)</logic>
</step>

<step n="8" type="premise">
<text>The forager bee is learned.</text>
<logic>learned(forager_bee)</logic>
</step>

The forager bee is explicitly stated to be learned. One of the rules tells us that any learned bee also exhibits teamwork. By applying this rule to the known fact, we can conclude that the forager bee shows teamwork. This establishes the first link in the chain of reasoning.
<step n="9" type="derived" from="8,1">

examples.context: Casey is polite. Whenever Casey is brilliant, she is also determined. If Casey is skillful, then she is not determined. Either Casey is not analytical, or she is brilliant. Either Casey is not polite, or she is analytical.
examples.question: Based on the given information, can we conclude that Casey is skillful?
examples.completion:
<think>
We are presented with several facts and conditional statements about Casey's personal attributes. Our goal is to see whether the information logically forces the claim that Casey is skillful.

The first rule tells us that brilliance guarantees determination. This will become useful later when we manage to show Casey is brilliant, allowing us to derive determination.
<step n="1" type="premise">
<text>If Casey is brilliant then she is determined.</text>
<logic>determined(casey) :- brilliant(casey)</logic>
</step>

The second rule gives a contrapositive relationship: if Casey were skillful, she could not be determined. This rule will help us test the possibility of skillfulness once we know whether she is determined.
<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>If Casey is skillful then she is not determined.</text>
<logic>~determined(casey) :- skillful(casey)</logic>
</step>

The disjunction states that at least one of the two statements—‘Casey is not analytical’ or ‘Casey is brilliant’—must be true. This will be a key piece for a disjunctive syllogism after we settle the analytical status.
<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>Either Casey is not analytical or she is brilliant.</text>
<logic>~analytical(casey) v brilliant(casey)</logic>
</step>

The fourth premise offers another disjunction: either Casey is not polite or she is analytical. Since we already have a fact about her politeness, we can eliminate one side and obtain a new fact.
<step n="4" type="premise">
<text>Either Casey is not polite or she is analytical.</text>
<logic>~polite(casey) v analytical(casey)</logic>
</step>

We are explicitly told that Casey is polite. This fact will interact with the previous disjunction to let us infer her analytical nature.
<step n="5" type="premise">
<text>Casey is polite.</text>
<logic>polite(casey)</logic>
</step>

Having the fact that Casey is polite means the clause ‘Casey is not polite’ in the disjunction cannot hold. By disjunctive syllogism we therefore conclude the remaining option, that Casey is analytical.
<step n="6" type="derived" from="5,4">
<text>Because Casey is polite, the alternative that she is not polite must be false, so we infer that Casey is analytical.</text>
<logic>analytical(casey)</logic>
</step>

Now that we know Casey is analytical, the disjunction ‘not analytical or brilliant’ cannot be satisfied by the first part, so the second part must be true. Hence, Casey is brilliant.
<step n="7" type="derived" from="6,3">
<text>Since we have established that Casey is analytical, the option that she is not analytical cannot hold, therefore Casey is brilliant.</text>
<logic>brilliant(casey)</logic>
</step>

With the new information that Casey is brilliant, we can apply the first rule (brilliance implies determination) via modus ponens, giving us that Casey is determined.
<step n="8" type="derived" from="7,1">
<text>From Casey being brilliant and the rule that brilliance implies determination, we infer that Casey is determined.</text>
<logic>determined(casey)</logic>
</step>

We have established that Casey is determined, but the second rule says that if she were skillful she would be not determined. Using modus tollens, the truth of ‘determined’ forces the negation of the antecedent, so we infer that Casey is not skillful.
<step n="9" type="derived" from="8,2">
<text>Given that Casey is determined and that being skillful would make her not determined, we apply modus tollens to conclude that Casey cannot be skillful.</text>
<logic>~skillful(casey)</logic>
</step>

We derived Casey is not skillful, so the conclusion Casey is skillful is False.
</think>
Answer: False

examples.context: In the automotive workshop, the turbo engine undergoes thorough_check testing. The gearbox is compliant, and the chassis is also compliant. The gearbox is not tuned. Whenever a chassis is compliant, it is responsive. If a component is calibrated, then the turbo engine is high_performance. Whenever a chassis is exhaustive, it is well_engineered. If the turbo engine is thorough_check, then it is robust. Whenever a chassis is not tuned, it is systematic. Whenever a chassis is well_engineered, it is exact. Whenever a chassis is responsive, it is exhaustive. If a component is tuned, then the chassis is not exact. Either the turbo engine is not robust or the turbo engine is calibrated. Either the chassis is not systematic or the chassis is reliable.
examples.question: Is the chassis reliable?
examples.completion:
<think>
We need to determine whether the chassis satisfies the property of being reliable. The goal is to chain together the given facts and rules so that we can derive the reliability of the chassis.

This premise connects compliance to responsiveness for any chassis, which will let us turn the known compliance of the chassis into a responsive property later. It is a crucial bridge toward the exhaustive property needed further down the chain.
<step n="1" type="premise">
<text>The rule states that any chassis that is compliant is also responsive.</text>
<logic>responsive(chassis) :- compliant(chassis)</logic>
</step>

Here we learn that exhaustiveness guarantees being well_engineered. Once we obtain exhaustiveness for the chassis, this rule will let us infer the well_engineered status, moving us closer to exactness and eventually reliability.
<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>The rule says that whenever a chassis is exhaustive, it is well_engineered.</text>
<logic>well_engineered(chassis) :- exhaustive(chassis)</logic>
</step>

This rule tells us that a well_engineered component is exact. It will be used after we have shown the chassis is well_engineered, allowing us to reach the exact property that participates in a later contrapositive argument.
<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>The rule indicates that a well_engineered chassis is exact.</text>
<logic>exact(chassis) :- well_engineered(chassis)</logic>
</step>

The premise states that responsiveness implies exhaustiveness. After we derive that the chassis is responsive, we can immediately conclude it is exhaustive, which is needed for the well_engineered step.
<step n="4" type="premise">
<text>The rule asserts that a responsive chassis is exhaustive.</text>
<logic>exhaustive(chassis) :- responsive(chassis)</logic>
</step>

This conditional says that if a chassis were tuned, it would not be exact. By later showing the chassis is exact, we can apply modus tollens to infer that the chassis cannot be tuned, giving us a negative tuned fact.
<step n="5" type="premise">
<text>The rule declares that if a chassis is tuned, then it is not exact.</text>
<logic>~exact(chassis) :- tuned(chassis)</logic>
</step>

The disjunction provides two alternatives: either the chassis is not systematic or it is reliable. Once we manage to prove the chassis is systematic, the first alternative becomes false, forcing the second alternative—reliability—to hold.
<step n="6" type="premise">

<logic>~systematic(chassis) v reliable(chassis)</logic>

The fact that the turbo engine undergoes thorough_check testing is recorded for completeness, though it does not directly affect the chassis reasoning. It illustrates the broader set of properties present in the domain.
<step n="7" type="premise">

<logic>thorough_check(turbo_engine)</logic>
</step>

Stating that the gearbox is compliant supplies another example of the compliant property, reinforcing the pattern used in the rule for chassis compliance. It is part of the background information.
<step n="8" type="premise">

<logic>compliant(gearbox)</logic>
</step>

The gearbox not being tuned gives a concrete instance of a component lacking the tuned property, mirroring the later inference we will make about the chassis not being tuned. It shows the domain's use of the tuned predicate.
<step n="9" type="premise">

<logic>~tuned(gearbox)</logic>

This fact asserts that the chassis itself is compliant, which is the starting point for applying PREMISE_1. It directly enables us to derive responsiveness for the chassis.
<step n="10" type="premise">
